However, for the most part and in order to ensure effective outcomes, organisations should adhere to this principle to a significant extent. According to the scalar principle, there ought to be a transparent chain of command that extends from the position of supreme authority at the very top of the organisation to each and every person working there, connecting all of the managers working at the various levels. It involves a concept known as a gang plank, which allows a subordinate to contact a superior or his superior in an emergency using a method that contradicts the hierarchy of control. However, the immediate superiors need to be briefed on the situation as soon as possible. 4. Delegation: Delegation is yet anothersignificant idea that isintimately connected to the concept of authority. The act of delegating responsibilities or authority at work to other people, such as workers or subordinates, is known as delegation. When managers are unable to delegate, they end up doing all of the job themselves and underutilizing their employees. The capacity to effectively delegate tasks is essential to succeeding in a managing role. When a superior gives discretionary power to a subordinate, this is known as delegating authority to the subordinate. The act of passing on one’s responsibilities and responsibilities to one’s subordinates is known as delegation. No matter how high up in the organisational structure they are, superiors or managers cannot assign authority if they do not themselves possess that authority. The process of delegation includes the creation of desired outcomes, the assignment of tasks, the delegation of authority for performing these tasks, and the exaction of responsibility for the successful completion of the delegated duties. Employees are then empowered to contribute their own ideas and to perform their duties in the most effective manners when they are given the authority to delegate tasks to others. 5. Span of Control: The number of workers who are under a manager’s supervision is referred to as their “span of control,” which is also sometimes referred to as their “span of management.” It refers to the number of people who report directly to a manager and are accountable for the boss’s actions. Understanding an organisation’s organisational design as well asthe dynamics of its working groups requires a solid grasp of the concept of span of control. Within the same company, the breadth of control might shift from one department to another depending on the circumstances. The range might be either broad or specific. When a manager has a high number of people reporting to him, there is said to be a wide span of influence over the organisation. Greater independence is afforded by this kind of organisational structure. A manager is said to have a restricted range of


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0074.html -->
60 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT authority when there are fewer direct reporters under their supervision. The ability for managers to spend more time with their direct subordinates, as well as the promotion of professional growth and advancement, are both benefits of narrow spans. 3.6 TYPES OF ORGANISATIONS Setting strategic goals within a business is a necessary step that should not be ignored. The strategic goals of a company address all areas of the business, such as deciding which markets to operate in, what products and services to offer clients, and how to find and keep outstanding employees. Setting strategic goals for the organisation and ensuring that all business operations contribute toward meeting those goals are the responsibilities of the management of the organisation. When a company has determined its strategic objectives, the next step is to put those objectives into action. Communication and the provision of plans that direct the activities of those in the organisation are required for the organisation to realise its strategic goals and put those goals into action. Based on the nature of authority and its flow, the basic types of organisations may be classified as follows: (1) Centralised and decentralized organisation (2) Line and Staff Organisation (3) Functional Organisation (4) Committee Organisation 1. Centralised and Decentralised Organisation Centralised Organisation: A centralised business structure is one in which key decisions, such as those on resource allocation, are made by a single individual, and that individual also provides the primary strategic direction for the company. Most small firms are run in a centralised manner, in which the business owner is responsible for making all essential choices concerning products, services, strategic direction, and other crucial areas. On the other hand, the size of an Organisation is not necessary for it to be centralised. Advantages of Centralised Organisation: Organisations that are centralised have greater clarity in their decision-making processes, more efficient implementation of policies and projects, and greater control over the organisation’s overall strategic direction. Centralised organisations typically provide less possibilities for staff members to respond, and they run a greater danger of being rigid as a result. These are two of the key disadvantages of centralised organisations. Disadvantages of Centralised Organisation: Many times, centralised organisational systems are less responsive to the localised demands that are applied from the outside. Employees who are not provided the opportunity to participate in the decision-making process may also feel disenfranchised as a result of this factor. Decentralized Organisation: Most of the time, centralised organisational structures are less responsive to locally focused environmental challenges. Additionally, it may be discouraging to employees if they are not given the opportunity to participate in the decision-making process.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0075.html -->
ORGANISING | 61 Advantages of Decentralised Organisation: A decentralised management structure offers a variety of benefits as follows: • Fast decision making and response times – It is critical that decisions be taken and put into action in a timely way. It is essential for enterprises, if they wish to maintain their competitive edge, to seize chances that are consistent with the overall strategic direction of the firm. • An improved capacity to expand the company – It is essential for businesses to always investigate new avenues through which they may supply their clients with the products and services they require. • Skilled and/or specialised management – Organisations have a responsibility to make investments in the training of highly skilled workers who can make decisions that are in the best interest of the organisation and will assist it in achieving its objectives. • Improvement in the morale of employees – The ability of a business to recruit, train, and keep highly motivated workers is critical to that organisation’s long-term success. One strategy for boosting employee morale is to give workers more say in the decisions that affect them. • Link between compensation and Responsibility – There is generally a correlation between increased prospects for advancement and a matching increase in compensation. When working for a decentralised company, a pay raise will frequently be accompanied by an increase in the duties associated with learning new skills, having additional decision-making authority, and supervising other employees. • Amore effective utilisation of lower and middle management – In order to achieve success in an organisation, a great number of tasks need to be completed. Lower and middle management are frequently relied upon by decentralised companies to carry out a significant portion of their responsibilities. As a result, managers can develop significant knowledge and competence in a variety of fields. Disadvantages of Decentralised Organisation: • Difficulties in coordinating activities – It is essential for an organisation to be working toward a unified objective. Because decision-making is delegated in a decentralised organisation, it is frequently difficult to ensure that all divisions of the company are working in a consistent manner to achieve the organisation’s strategic goals. This is one of the challenges that comes with having a decentralised organisation. • Increase in administrative costs due to duplication of efforts – Decentralised organisations are more likely to engage in duplication of efforts, which leads to inefficiency as well as an increase in costs, because similar decisions and activities need to be carried out across all divisions of an organisation. • Inconsistency in operations – When autonomy is distributed throughout an organisation, as it is in decentralised organisations, division managers may be tempted to customise or alter the operations of their division in an effort to maximise productivity and serve the best interest of the division. This can lead to inconsistency in operations. Within the context of this structure, it is essential to make certain that the timesaving measures used by one department of the company do not interfere with or otherwise interrupt the work of another department located within the organisation. • Self-centered ways of functioning – It is not uncommon for individual divisions within an organisation to be measured on the success of the division rather than the performance of the entire firm. This is due to the fact that each department or division tends to be self-centered


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0076.html -->
62 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT and acts as its own fiefdom. It is feasible for division managers in a decentralised organisation to give priority to the goals of their division rather than the goals of the company. The leaders of decentralised organisations have the responsibility of ensuring that the organisation’s goals continue to be the top priority for the accomplishment of all divisions. • Significant level of dependence on the divisional or department managers – Because divisions within decentralised organisations have a high level of autonomy, the division may become operationally isolated from other divisions within the organisation, focusing solely on the priorities of the division. If the managers of a division or department do not have a diverse range of experience or talents, the division may be at a disadvantage since it will have restricted access to other areas of expertise. 2. Line and Staff Organisation In the context of management, the term “line-staff organisation” refers to the method by which authorities (such as managers) formulate objectives and instructions, which are subsequently carried out by employees and other workers. A large and complicated business may use a line-staff organisational structure in an effort to increase their level of adaptability without giving up their managerial authority. Line and staff organisation is a modification of line organisation that is more complicated than line organisation. Line organisation is the foundation of line and staff organisation. In accordance with this administrative organisation, special and auxiliary activities are linked to the command line by assigning staff supervisors and staff specialists attached to the line authority. This allows the activities to be managed in accordance with this administrative structure. Line executives always hold the authority of command, and the primary responsibilities of staff supervisors are to guide, advise, and counsel line executives. A member of the workforce, the Private Secretary to the Managing Director acts in the capacity of staff executive. Formal organisations were characterised as collective enterprises characterised by a clear division of labour and authority according to the classical theory of organisation, which was associated with Henry Fayol, Frederick W. Taylor, and others who pioneered new administrative strategies in the late 1800s and early 1900s. According to these hypotheses, the authority to make decisions is distributed throughout a hierarchical organisation. Lines of authority serve as the foundation for all interactions that take place between individuals, organisations, and divisions. Most of the time, tasks are carried out in accordance with particular functions, and power is exercised in a hierarchical fashion. A small number of executives or managers make decisions in an organisation that is highly centralised, and those decisions are then implemented further down the chain. However, as an organisation expands in both scope and complexity, it becomes increasingly important for management to maintain a flexible approach to the degree to which central coordination and control are exercised. The goal of the line-staff organisation principle is to maintain a cohesive command structure while allowing for some degree of flexibility within the hierarchical lines of authority. Features of Line and Staff Organisation: (i) There are two different kinds of staff members: a. Staff Assistants like Personal Assistants to the Managing Director and Secretary to the Marketing Manager. b. Staff Supervisor-Operations Control Manager, Quality Controller, and PRO Positions (ii) Line and Staff Organisation is a form of line organisation that has been compromised. It is more intricate than just a line concern.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0077.html -->
ORGANISING | 63 (iii) Within the line and staff organisation, work is segmented and specialised in accordance with individual needs. (iv) The entirety of the organisation is broken up into a variety of functional divisions, each of which has staff specialists assigned to it. (v) The characteristics of specialisation make it possible to achieve efficiency in one’s work. (vi) There are two different channels through which authority might flow simultaneously in a company or organisation: a. Line Authority b. Staff Authority (vii) The line executive retains control of the commanding role, while staff members are limited to advisory roles. Advantages of Line and Staff Organisation: 1) Providing relief to line of executives: In an organisation structured along lines of both line and staff, the advice and counselling that is given to line executives by staff is one way in which the job is divided between line and staff. The line executive can focus on the implementation of plans, which frees them from the need to divide their attention among several different areas. 2) Professional counsel: The line and staff organisation are responsible for providing the line executive with access to expert advice whenever it is required. Staff specialists can handle the planning and investigation work that is associated with a variety of issues, which frees up line officers to focus on carrying out the plans instead. 3) Benefits of Specialisation: One of the many advantages of specialisation is that it allows the firm to be segmented into parts and functional regions. This is accomplished by the partition of the entire concern into two distinct sorts of authority. In this way, all officers or officials can focus their attention solely on their assigned sector. 4) Improvement in coordination: Through specialisation, line and staff organisation can provide superior decision making, while authority remains with only a few people.As a result of thisfeature, each official can concentrate on their own domain, which contributes to improved work coordination. 5) Benefits of Research and Development: The advice of specialised staff members allows line executives more time to execute plans and make productive decisions that are helpful for a concern. This opens a lot of doors for the line executive in terms of bringing new ideas to the table and conducting research in those specific areas. The existence of specialists on staff makes this accomplishment feasible. 6) Training: Because of the existence of staff specialists, the line officials can receive expert advice and get trained on the job. As such, line executives can take proper and informed decisions. In and of itself, this serves as a learning environment for them. 7) Balanced decisions: The level of specialisation that can be attained by line personnel is one component that contributes to the achievement of coordination. Because of this relationship, the line official will always be able to make decisions that are more reasonable and fairer.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0078.html -->
64 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 8) Unity of action: Unity of action comes about as a direct outcome of having unified control. When there is coordination inside a concern, control and its successful implementation can take place. Every single official who has a position of line orstaff authority is given the autonomy to make choices on their own. This serves as an efficient control mechanism for the entirety of the organisation. Disadvantages of Line and Staff organisation: 1) A fundamental misunderstanding: There is a simultaneous flow of two different authorities in an organisation structured along line and staff lines. Because of this, there is a lot of confusion between the two. Because of this, the workers are unable to comprehend who the controlling power in their midst actually is. As a result, the issue of comprehension may present a barrier to efficient operations. 2) Lack of reliable guidance: The staff’s experience and guidance become second nature to the line official. There are situations when the staff specialist may provide line executive advice resulting in incorrect decisions. The smooth operation of the business may be hampered because of this. 3) Conflicts between line and staff: Line and staff are two different authoritiesthat are both flowing at the same time.Conflicts between line and staff might arise. The factors such as designations and prestige influence attitudes that are related to their relation might be stressful for employees’ thoughts. Because of this, there is less coordination, which makes it more difficult for a business to function effectively. 4) Expensive: To keep costs in line with staff concerns, the concerns have to keep the high remuneration of staff expert employees. This ends up being expensive for a company that has limited financial resources. 5) Assumption of authority: Although the power of concern lies with the line official, the staff does not like this arrangement because they are the ones who are more involved in mental work. 6) Staff steals the show: In a line and staff concern, the higher returns are thought to be the result of the expert staff advice and counseling. The officials on the line are upset and concerned about the situation and their dissatisfaction sometimes creates problems. It is critical to the achievement of productive results that line officials feel satisfied in their jobs. 3. Functional Organisation A functional organisation is segregated within the company so that specialists can maintain their positions at the highest levels. It is an organisation that we can define as a system in which functional departments are developed to deal with the difficulties of the business at various levels. This organisation is something that we can call a functional department organisation. The functional authority continues to be delegated only to the functional direction provided by the individual departments. It contributes to the preservation of quality as well as uniformity in the performance of a variety of functions across the entire company. FW Taylor was the one who initially proposed the idea of a functional organisation and advocated for the placement of knowledgeable individuals in key roles.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0079.html -->
ORGANISING | 65 Illustratively, the functional heads and marketing directors in a functional organisation are responsible for providing direction to subordinate employees throughout the organisation in their respective fields. This indicates that subordinates receive commands from multiple specialists, who serve as managers above them in the organisational chart. Features of Functional Organisation: a) All the activities of a functional organisation are broken down into their respective functions, which include functions like operations, finance, marketing, and personal relations. b) A more complex form of administrative organisation than the other two. c) There are three different authorities: line, staff, and function. d) Each functional area is assigned to functional specialists who are vested with the authority to make all decisions pertaining to that function anytime that function is carried out anywhere within the organisation. e) The principle of unity of command does not apply to such an organisation because it already has a line structure in place for that purpose. Advantages of Functional Organisation: 1) Specialisation—A better division of labour takes place, which leads to the specialisation of functions and the profit that follows from that specialisation. 2) Efficient Control: - The management control process is made more straightforward by separating the mental processes from the manual functions. The authority is kept within defined bounds using checks and balances. It is possible that specialists will be called to evaluate the work of several departments. 3) Efficiency: The fact that each function is responsible for a constrained number of other tasks contributes to the overall improvement in efficiency. 4) Cost-Effectiveness: When combined, specialisation and standardisation make it possible to achieve maximum output at the lowest possible cost. 5) Expansion: The expert knowledge of the functional manager makes for greater control and monitoring of the business. Disadvantages of Functional Organisation: 1) Confusion: The functionalsystem is difficult to put into operation, particularly when it is carried out at low levels. This is especially true when it is carried out at higher levels. Because of this, coordination becomes more challenging. 2) Lack of coordination: When a worker is controlled not by one person but by a huge number of people, disciplinary control becomes weak. As a result, there is no cohesive command structure. 3) Difficulty in determining accountability: Due to the existence of many authorities, determining blame can be challenging. 4) Conflicts: It’s possible for members of the supervisory staff with equal positions to have disagreements with one another. On some topics, they could have different opinions. 5) Expensive: It is expensive for a company to maintain a staff of specialists of the highest degree. 4. Committee Organisation A group of individuals who have come together to solve issues that are experienced by the organisation as a whole form what is known as a committee organisation. Individuals are provided with the option to discussthe difficultiesthey have in committee through this approach. The term “committee


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0080.html -->
66 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT organisation” refers to a group of individuals who possess various forms of knowledge and who have been properly organised to address the challenges faced by the organisation. A committee can assist in the gathering of collective ideas and information, as well as the accurate analysis of these items, which can assist in the making of sound managerial decisions and the resolution of challenging situations. Throughout the course of operations, the company may face a variety of challenges; to address these challenges, the management establishes committees by recruiting members from the relevant functional areas. And the management makes judgments and finds solutions to the issues based on the facts and ideas that are offered by the members of the committee. Advantages of committee organisation: 1) Improved Quality of Decisions: One of the most significant benefits of using a committee organisation is the improved quality of decisions. Members with expertise and experience gleaned from a wide range of domains come together in this space. They arrive at the best decision for resolving the difficult issues by first collecting the ideas of a collective group and then analysing those suggestions. 2) Establishing goals, plans, and policies: Every organisation is started with specific goals in mind, and to reach those goals, it is necessary to establish goals as well as plans and policies. The committee serves as the organisational structure that underpins the goal-setting process. In addition to this, it offers recommendations and information that can be used to formulate strategies and policies to accomplish the goals that have been set. 3) Participatory Management: The arrangement of work into committees assures that competent people will take part in management activities. In this setting, each member has the opportunity to contribute their thoughts, ideas, and opinions to the process of making decisions. In the process of decision making, the democratic procedure is taken into consideration. 4) Decrease Prejudice and Conflicts: The committee helps enable the development of ties among members and their interaction with the line management, which in turn helps reduce prejudices and conflicts. They contribute to the authorities’ ability to keep in close communication with one another and share information with one another. As a result, the likelihood of making biased decisions or errors in judgement is reduced. In a similar vein, the development of reciprocal ties among members contributes to the resolution of conflicts that arise among them. 5) Dealing with Complicated Problems: The arrangement of problems into committees makes it easier to deal with complicated and tough sorts of issues. In certain circumstances, during an organisation’s functioning, a great number of difficult or crucial challenges may surface. It is far more difficult for those in line of authority, or even upper management, to address difficulties of this nature. In situations like these, committees are formed by collecting specialists from the relevant fields in order to make it simpler to find methodical solutions to the challenges at hand. 6) Commitment to Implementation: To handle complicated and challenging issues, a committee is established. All the committee’s members have significant prior expertise and are recognised authorities in their respective disciplines. In addition, committees are comprised of knowledgeable people from the highest levels of management. They reach their decision after having an in-depth debate and analysing the potential consequences of several different decisions. 7) Sharing of Authority: During the inquiry and research that is being carried out, some of the authority that is held by the top management is being distributed among the members of the committee. If they stay inside the boundaries of the designated area, committee members are


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0081.html -->
ORGANISING | 67 free to carry out their duties autonomously. In the committee, each member carries the same amount of weight in terms of authority. As a result, decisions made at the executive level receive the assistance and consensus of all the members of the relevant committees. Disadvantages of committee organisation: 1) Creating Conflict: The presence of more headsin a committee increasesthe likelihood of conflict. In certain circumstances, the organisation of the committee contributes to the confusion and contention that exists during the decision-making process. There is a possibility that the ideas held by one member are in direct opposition to those held by other members. 2) Delay in Decisions: Committee members take extra time to discuss various aspects of a problem. This causes the decision-making process to be delayed. Discussion take up a significant amount of the members of the committee’s time. The management might have to shoulder an additional financial burden if the decision is delayed. 3) The Possibility of Diversion: The conversation that is taking place within the committee may in some instances be redirected to another topic. Within the allotted amount of time, the members of the committee are unable to arrive at any kind of decision when faced with a scenario like this. They will need to get together more than once to reach a satisfactory conclusion. 4) The Shifting Tendency: The tendency to delegate decision making to several committees is a standard operating procedure in many organisations. When leaders are unable to conclude regarding significant issues, they frequently defer the decision-making process to a committee. This attitude of line executives hinders the initiative and inventiveness of their employees, which in turn has a detrimental effect on the performance of the firm. 5) Lack of Secrecy: It is difficult to keep the secrecy of internal matters in the structure of the committee, which makes it difficult to preserve the committee’s credibility. The issues are dissected in detail by each member of the committee, and in the course of doing so, they learn more about the workings of the organisation as a whole. As a result, there is a greater chance that the organisation’s secret plans and strategies will become public. 6) Distribution of Responsibilities: Most members of the committee are concerned about the process of decision making. It is possible that the committee will make an incorrect judgement in certain circumstances; but, in such a scenario, no one member will assume personal responsibility for the incorrect decision made on behalf of the committee. Everyone on the committee is eager to pass on their responsibilities to the other members of the group. 7) Division of Accountability: In a committee-based organisation, no one is held responsible for the decisions that are reached by a committee. This results in a split in accountability. The decision reached by a committee is referred to as “one’s decision” because it affects everyone equally. If there is even the slightest error in the way decisions are made, there is a possibility that the company will experience difficulties. No one is willing to accept the whole blame for the error that was made by the entire committee, and this is the situation we find ourselves in. 3.7 ORGANISATION STRUCTURE There are, broadly, two types of Organisation Structures: 1. Formal Organisation Structure 2. Informal Organisation Structure


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0082.html -->
68 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT Formal Organisation (Open/Overt) Goals and Objectives Policies and Procedures Job Descriptions Financial Resources Authority Structures Channels of Communication Products and Services Informal Organisation (Hidden/Covert) Beliefs and Assumptions Perceptions and Attitudes Values Feelings-Joy, Fear, Anger, Hatred etc. Group Norms A. Formal Organisation As a result of the planning process, managers create an organisational structure to achieve methodical work and effective utilization of resources. This structure will be the outcome of organising the process. The term “formal organisational structure” refers to this kind of structure. The formal organisational structure describes in detail the functions that should be carried out by everyone, the authority that should be delegated to everyone, the responsibilities that should be assigned to each individual, the superior-subordinate relationship, and the designation that should be given to each person within the organisation. The managers purposefully craft this structure to facilitate the achievement of their organisation’s objectives. Salient Features of Formal Organisation: a. The act of consciously structuring anything results in the formation of an official and formal organisational structure. b. The accomplishment of the organisational goals is intended to serve as the motivation for the formal organisation structure. c. A formal organisational structure delegates distinct responsibilities to each individual member of the group. d. In a formal organisation, each member is given a specific amount of authority or decision-making power, depending on the nature of the organisation. e. The establishment of hierarchical connections between superiors and subordinates is a direct consequence of a formal organisational structure. f. The organisation’s formal organisational structure serves as the backbone of its hierarchical communication network. Advantages of Formal Organisation: 1. Organised Tasks: When an organisation has a formal organisational structure, its operations are more organised and run more efficiently. 2. Successful Completion of Organisational Objectives: To accomplish the goals of the organisation, a formal organisational structure must be developed.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0083.html -->
ORGANISING | 69 3. Avoidance of duplication of functions: Work is typically parcelled out in a methodical fashion across several departments and personnel within an official organisational structure. As a result, there is no opportunity for labour to be performed in a redundant or overlapping manner. 4. Harmony: The formal organisational structure enables the different departments to coordinate their work, which is a positive outcome. 5. Establishing One’s Place in the Chain of Command: The superior-subordinate relationship, often known as who reports to whom, is delineated in an unmistakable manner by the formal organisational structure. 6. Placement of a greater emphasis on work: Work takes precedence over personal connections in the formal organisational structure, which prioritizes productivity above camaraderie. Disadvantages of Formal Organisation: 1. Postponing an Action: When scalar chains and chains of command verbs are followed, the formal structure is pushed back. 2. Does not consider the employees’social requirements: The fact that the formal organisational structure does not give emphasis to the psychological and social needs of the employees may contribute to a decline in the employees’ morale, which in turn may lead to a fall in productivity. 3. Placement of exclusive emphasis on work: Only the task itself is given value by formal organisational structures; human relations, creativity, ability, and other such things are disregarded. B. Informal OrganisationWithin the formal organisational structure, different job responsibilities are delegated to different individuals. Individuals create some social and friendly groups within the organisation because of the interactions that take place between them while working in various employment positions. One more structure exists within the company and is referred to as the informal organisational structure. This structure is formed by a network of social and friendly groups. Unofficial organisational structures spring into existence on their own, and the primary purpose of these structures is to facilitate the attainment of psychological fulfilment. Because the people working in various job positions interact with each other to build the informal structure, the presence of the informal structure is dependent on the existence of the formal structure. This is because the job positions are formed in the formal structure. If there is no formal structure, then there won’t be any job positions, which means there won’t be any people working in those job positions and there won’t be any informal organisation. Features of Informal Organisation: • The managers don’t have to make any conscious efforts to construct the unofficial organisational structure; rather, it just happens naturally. • Employees often build their own informal organisational structures to achieve greater psychological fulfilment. • An informal organisational structure does not have a predetermined chain of command or set way for information to flow through it.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0084.html -->
70 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT • Under an informal structure, it is impossible to determine the origin of information because any individual can contact any other member of the organisation. • The formal organisation structure is necessary for the existence of informal organisational structures. Advantages of Informal Organisation: 1. Effective and Rapid Communication: Because the informal structure does not adhere to a hierarchical chain, there is the potential for communication to be disseminated quickly. 2. Satisfies societal requirements: Informal communication gives the psychological and social needs of the employees the necessary consideration they need, which in turn encourages those individuals. 3. Correct response: The highest-level managers can learn the actual input that their employees have on the numerous policies and strategies thanks to the informal structure. 4. Utilisation of informal organisation as a strategic tool: The following are some of the ways in which informal organisation has the potential to be more advantageous than the formal organisation: a) The information gleaned from the informal group can be used in order to win the support of the employees and enhance their level of performance. b) Grapevine can swiftly communicate significant information to one another. c) Through collaboration with informal groups, managers can effectively exploit both official and informal organisations for their own purposes. Disadvantages of Informal Organisation: 1. Engage in Rumour-Spreading: According to the results of a survey, around seventy percent of the information that is disseminated through the informal organisational structure is rumour, which might lead employees in the wrong direction. 2. No Organised Tasks: An organisation’s need for structure to run efficiently cannot be met by an informal organisational structure. 3. Potential to have unfavourable effects: If the informal organisation disagrees with the decisions and changes made by management, it will be extremely challenging to put such decisions into action inside the organisation. 4. Placement of a greater emphasis on individual interests: When compared to the satisfying of organisational interests, the informal structure places a greater emphasis on the fulfilment of individual interests. 3.8 ORGANISATION CHARTS AND MANUALS Organisation chart is a vital instrument for conveying information about the working relationships inside a company. It is a diagrammatic form that illustrates the primary functions and how those functions are related to one another, as well as the formal channels of power and the degree of authority held by each manager in charge of a related function. According to the definition provided by George R. Terry, an organisation chart is a “graphical form” that illustrates the essential components of an organisation. These components include key functions and


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0085.html -->
ORGANISING | 71 the relationships that are associated with them, channels of supervision, and the degree to which each employee is in charge. In larger firms, organisation charts are frequently accompanied with organisation manuals to provide further context. Organisation manuals are limited to being in the form of brief notes and can only include departmental charts to illustrate the activities of each department and their relationship to the operations of other departments. Manuals for organisations may, on the other hand, also include sections that define the responsibilities and authority of specific roles, as well as the procedures that must be followed when carrying out the various functions. Obviously, the only context in which this makes sense is one in which a high degree of control is not just desirable but also feasible. This is usually the case with several departments of the government. In a nutshell, the Organisation Manual is a pamphlet that outlines all of the specifics of the organisation, including its goals and policies, authority, functions, tasks, and responsibilities of each unit, as well as all of the information related to it. Copies of organisation manuals should be made available for review inside each department and division, in addition to being kept with the top managers who oversee the organisation. Organisation charts can be divided into two parts: (i) Master Charts: This chart displays the entirety of the formal organisation structure. (ii) Supplementary Charts: Supplementary charts provide an in-depth look at the linkages, authorities, and responsibilities that exist inside a particular department or significant component of an organisation’s specified area of responsibility. Organisation charts can also be classified into the following three types based on how organisation charts are prepared: (i) Vertical chart or Top-down chart: Vertical chart, also known as top-down chart depicts the level of organisation as a hierarchical pyramid with a line of command that descends from the highest level to the lowest. In this chart, the position that holds the highest ranking is displayed at the top level, while the position that holds the lowest ranking is displayed at the lower level. (ii) Horizontal Chart or left to right chart: The levels of organisation are shown going from left to right in this diagram. The chain of command is broken up into horizontal sections. Reading progresses from most difficult to least difficult as one moves from left to right. The horizontal chart has many benefits, including the following: • It is written in the standard reading order, which moves from left to right. • Has a clear mental picture of the different organisational levels. • Simplifies the process of understanding how formal authority and responsibility are distributed throughout an organisation. • Provides an indication of the relative length of the formal authority chain. (iii) Circular Chart: A circular form can be used to display a variety of jobs or functions that are held inside an organisation. A concentric or circular chart illustrates the vertex by placing it at the centre of a series of concentric circles. Locations that are regarded as having approximately the same level of significance can be found on concentric circles that are the same distance from the centre. Channels of formal power are represented visually by lines connecting various blocks of functions or positions. Its primary deficiency is that it frequently causes confusion.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0086.html -->
72 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT Meaning of Organisation Manual There is a possibility that organisation charts will not offer all of the facts that are often wanted and required to comprehend a company. However, an organisation manual adds to the information that is provided in an organisation chart by providing more data and serving as a supplement. A compact book that contains information about the aims of the organisation, the authority and responsibilities of various positions, as well as the processes and procedures that are to be followed is called an organisation manual. Manuals can be drafted for the entirety of an organisation or for certain divisions within that organisation. Information such as organisational goals and policies, job descriptions of important employees, organisational procedures, methods, and rules are all included in the organisation manual. The organisation manual can be broken up into the following four sections: i. Policy Manual ii. Operational Manual iii. Organisational Manual iv. Rules and Regulations Manual Advantages of Organisation Chart and Manual: i. Management Tool: Both the Organisation Chart and the Organisation Manual contain information about the structure, positions, and authorities inside the organisation. As a result, an organisation chart and manual are regarded as essential components of an effective management tool. ii. Avoidance of Overlapping and Duplicate Activities: The organisation chart and manual are prepared after careful analysis of the jobs and positions requirements in the organisation. As a result, this ensures that all activities are covered properly by various positions, and they will bring out very easily the organisational weaknesses. As a result, they contribute to the reduction of overlapping authority and responsibility, as well as redundant labour. iii. Resolution of Organisational Conflicts: An Organisation Chart and Manual Both clearly show Positions, Authority Relationships, and Procedures as a result, they serve as an easily accessible resource for resolving organisational problems. Take, for instance, promotions. iv. Training Guides: The organisation chart and manual both serve to evolve the work that is expected of employees in the organisation. As a result, they have the potential to serve as a method of onthe-job training. v. Reference to Outsiders: Because the organisational manual and chart clearly indicate the position, authority, and responsibility, outsiders who are unfamiliar with the organisation may know very easily with whom they must interact for a particular work task because the manual and chart provide this information in a clear and concise manner. Disadvantages of Organisation Chart and Manual: i. Rigidity: The organisational functioning is made more rigid with the help of charts and manuals. While the organisation chart and manual are being produced, there are several changes that take place within the organisation. As a result, the incorporation of modifications requires some time. In addition, written papers have the power to move others to make changes. William H. Newman, the celebrated Management Author, saw quite some time back that “the sanctity of printed words tends towards inflexibility and increased difficulty.”


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0087.html -->
ORGANISING | 73 ii. A Partially Completed Picture: The organisational chart and manual only show a portion of the full picture of how the organisation operates. They only depict official ties and operating procedures. To exert more effective control over the actions of the organisation’s members, managers need to be familiar not only with formal but also with informal patterns of interaction. iii. Inadequate Description: An organisational chart just illustrates the reporting linkages, or who is responsible for what. The figure does not reveal the precise proportion of authority and responsibility that each person holds. As a result, the Organisation chart offers an inaccurate representation of authority. iv. Potential for Psychological Issues: The organisation chart may result in potential psychological issues for employees working for the organisation. Because the chart places individuals in either a higher or lower position, respectively. Therefore, a feeling of superiority or inferiority may arise, which may be counterproductive to the development of team spirit. It is possible that those at lower levels of an organisation won’t be happy with the chart being maintained. Advantages of Organisation Manual: (i) Because it contains rules, regulations, and a variety of instructions, the organisation manuals make it possible for employees to quickly learn the various procedures and practices. (ii) Because both instructions and policies are laid out in an understandable manner, making decisions is both simple and expedient. Disadvantages of Organisation Manual: (i) Preparation of an Organisation Manual requires a significant financial investment and a lot of time. (ii) There is very little room for initiative and discretion on the part of individuals when there are predetermined rules, regulations, and other factors. 3.9 THE ORGANISATION CULTURE The values, attitudes, beliefs, and behaviours that characterise and contribute to an organisation’s one-of-akind social and emotional work environment are referred to as the organisation’s culture. Organisational culture is also referred to as corporate culture. The organisational culture is one of the things that is the most difficult to change because it is unique to each company and is comprised of both written and unwritten rules that have been developed over the course of time. The underlying beliefs, assumptions, values, and modes of interaction that contribute to the one-of-a-kind social and psychological environment of an organisation are what make up what is known as the “organisational culture.” Every organisation has a company culture, regardless of whether this culture was intentionally cultivated. Innovative ResultOriented Aggressive People/ Team Oriented Stable DetailOriented Organisational Culture Profile


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0088.html -->
74 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 3.9.1 Types of Organisational Culture There are primarily four distinct cultures that can be found within an organisation. These are as follows: 1) The Clan Culture: This culture has its foundation in working together. Members have a lot in common with one another and have the perception that they are part of a large family that is very involved in activities. The organisation is held together by its commitments and traditions, and leadership takes the form of mentoring for its members. 2) The Adhocracy Culture: This culture is characterised by a high level of energy and inventiveness. It is expected of leaders to be innovative and entrepreneurial, and employees are actively encouraged to take calculated risks. Experimentation, with an emphasis on individual ingenuity and freedom, is the primary means by which the organisation maintains its cohesion. 3) The Culture of the Market: This culture is founded upon the competitive nature of the market and the pursuit of tangible success. The focus is on achieving the goals, and the leaders are known to be strict and demanding. The organisation’s members are all working toward the same objective, which is to achieve an edge over all of their competitors. Market share and profitability are the primary factors that determine value. 4) The Hierarchy Culture: This culture is characterised by its emphasis on hierarchy and control. The atmosphere at work is very formal, and there are stringent protocols established by the institution to provide direction. A culture that places an emphasis on productivity and predictability is conducive to leadership because it requires organised coordination and monitoring. 3.10 AUTHORITY AND RESPONSIBILITY In the context of a specific job, function, orsituation, the term “authority” refersto the formal, institutional, or legal power that gives the holder of that job, function, or position the ability to successfully carry out the responsibilities associated with that job, function, or position. A subordinate’s duty is known as responsibility, and it is delegated to them by his or her superior. Authority: a) A superior has the legal right to issue commands to those under his or her supervision. b) The position of the boss within the organisation is the primary factor that, in most cases, determines who has authority. c) A superior can give his subordinate the authority to do something under his supervision. d) The chain of command moves from superior to subordinate in a downward direction. e) Authority can be defined as the legitimate power that an individual or group possesses over other individuals. Responsibility: a) A subordinate is obliged to his or her superior authority to complete the tasks that have been delegated to them. b) Responsibility is an outcome of the superior-subordinate relationship, in which the subordinate accepts the obligation to carry out the responsibilities that have been delegated to him. c) The subordinate cannot delegate this responsibility to anyone else. d) The chain of responsibility moves from the subordinate to the superior position in an ascending order. e) It means making a moral commitment to complete the work that has been allotted.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0089.html -->
ORGANISING | 75 3.10.1 Relationship between Authority and Responsibility A superior has the authority, or the right, to give orders and instructions to his subordinates in order to get things done. This right is known as “authority.” The term “responsibility” refers to the responsibilities that are placed on a person because of the delegation of authority. The obligation that rests on the shoulders of the subordinate to carry out the duty to the best of his or her ability is another aspect of responsibility. It results from the hierarchical structure of superiors and subordinates. It is possible to delegate authority, but responsibility cannot be passed on to another party. The chain of command moves downward. It is always possible for a superior to delegate some of his authority to his subordinates, but he cannot absolve himself of the responsibility or obligation to carry out his duties. The individual’s superior is the one to whom responsibility is owed. As a result, a subordinate cannot get out of his responsibilities by passing some of their authority down to another subordinate. Because responsibility is never transferred horizontally, it can never be delegated. The relationship between authority and responsibility is very close. Equal treatment ought to be given to both. A subordinate will only be accountable for the responsibility associated with the authority that has been delegated to him. 3.10.2 Sources of Authority The various sources of authority are listed below: 1) Legitimate Authority: This type of power typically originates from the status quo and the cultural system of obligations that govern our rights and responsibilities. As a result, the “position” is accepted by the people as being “legitimate.” The social institution of private property is the primary contributor to the formation of the status of a private company. In the realm of politics, this right derives from the political system known as representative government. 2) Coerciveness: It is based on the ability of one person to instil fear in another person and is founded on the subordinate’s expectation that punishment will be met for not agreeing or complying with superior orders or beliefs. It is derived from the ability of one person to instil fear in another person. 3) Rewarding Authority: When compared to the power of coercion, the power of reward is more desirable. This results from the fact that some individuals possess the ability to reward you. Purchasing positioning, which has low position power, might be able to use considerable influence from their ability to either speed up or delay the delivery of an essential spare part. In a similar vein, professors at universities have significant power to award you, as they can award or withhold higher grades. Permitting Company paid vacations is another example of awards. 4) Expertise: This refers to the power that comes from having knowledge, abilities, and expertise in a particular field. Because superiors are in possession of this knowledge,subordinatesstrive to acquire it and carry out their instructions. Due to the high regard in which their specialised knowledge is held, professionalssuch as doctors, lawyers, and university professors can wield significant influence over the people around them. 5) Referent Authority: The recognition of a person’s faith as a leader who is held in high esteem, admiration, and often imitation by those who are subordinate to them is the foundation upon which referent power is built.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0090.html -->
76 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 3.10.3 Characteristic of Authority Some of characteristics of authority are as follows: 1) Foundation for getting things done: Within an organisation, authority serves as the foundation upon which the work can be completed. It is the right to influence the actions and behaviours of others within an organisation, as well as the performance of certain activities, with the intention of achieving predetermined goals. 2) Legitimacy: Authority is recognised because it possesses some legitimacy, which means that it is accorded the right to ensure its own safe performance by other people. A right of this kind may be recognised by law or in a formal setting, or it may have historical backing. Authenticity as measured by conventional or generally recognised criteria. Based on the position or office that he holds within the organisation, a manager is granted the authority to exert some level of influence over the actions of the people who report to him. 3) Decision making: The ability to make decisions is a prerequisite for holding power. When a manager evaluates the performance of his subordinates in relation to the flow of activities, he may decide to issue directives to his employees instructing them to act in a particular manner or prohibiting them from acting in that manner. 4) Subjectivity in Implementation: Even though authority is partially objective, its exercise is heavily influenced by subjective factorssuch asthe personality of the manager who is empowered to exercise authority, in the context of a subordinate or group of subordinates, are major influences on how authority is exercised. 3.10.4 Forms of Responsibility There are two forms of responsibility: 1) Operating responsibility: • Individuals are held accountable for their own actions in the workplace through the concept of “operating responsibility.” • The person who is doing the work isthe one who isresponsible for, or obligated to fulfil, operating responsibility. 2) Ultimate Responsibility: • The manager is ultimately responsible for fulfilling his or her final task, which is to see to it that the work is carried out effectively by the staff members. • The manager, who is ultimately responsible because he or she is the one who ensures that the work gets done, bears the ultimate accountability. 3.11 KEY ISSUES IN ORGANISATION STRUCTURE When businesses strive to enhance and better manage their day-to-day operations, overcoming organisational challenges can be a difficult obstacle for some of them to surmount. Recognising that there is a problem and pinpointing its origin is the first stage in the process of finding solutions to challenges that arise inside an organisation. It takes time to discover where the problems are coming from and create appropriate remedies for each problem, but it is a crucial step for any organisation that wants to grow and grow in a healthy way. Any organisation that wants to grow must take this step.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0091.html -->
ORGANISING | 77 3.11.1 Important Issues in Organisational Structure i. Turnover: A problem that arises in an organisation when a significant number of workers quit their jobs on a regular basis is referred to as having a high turnover rate. To make up for this, a company has to continually bring in new employees to replace those positions. This could tie up resources at the organisation and cause delaysin the workflows at the company. The following are some potential causes of high turnover rates: • Employees are unhappy with management and the officials that represent them in leadership roles. • They are unhappy with their employment and do not believe it provides any satisfaction to them. • Members of the staff are underpaid and seek increased recompense for the work that they do. • Employees are under the impression that their employer does not pay attention to their opinions, ideas, or problems. • The employees of the team do not see any opportunities for advancement inside the organisation. ii. Productivity: Productivity is measured by the amount of work that is successfully completed by personnel while adhering to the established timeline. When a company has high productivity, it suggests that they are hitting their production quotas, their business operations are running smoothly, and they are fulfilling all orders within the allotted time frame. Losses in productivity could be experienced by an organisation for the following reasons: • There are not enough people on the teams. • The workers either get disinterested in their work or become distracted. • Alterations in structure and practice made suddenly might be disorienting. • The difficulty of one’s task might be increased by factors such as unachievable deadlines and ineffective management. iii. Process Management: Process management is a tool that managers use to guarantee that their teams are adhering to industry standards so that they may accomplish their task in a timely and effective manner. The manager is responsible for establishing norms and guidelines, selecting which practices are to be kept, and determining which practices do not offer value. It is possible for managers to put up procedures that are difficult to understand and complicated, which can lead to poor process management. • The leadership of the company is rigid and pays no attention to the opinions of its workers. • The task that their teams are responsible for and the resources they require to complete it are not well understood by managers. iv. Role Specification: The process of employing the individual who is the most competent for a position and then giving that job to the employee who is the most fit for the role is called role specification. A deficiency in the quality of the role specification can cause disruptions in the workflow, decreases in efficiency, and barriers in communication between members of the team. Problems with role specifications may occur due to the following reasons: a) There is a possibility that managers will act in a biased manner towards or against persons. b) A hiring manager will not put in the effort to conduct a comprehensive interview with a candidate’ c) It’s possible that leadership doesn’t fully comprehend the capabilities and unique characteristics of their team. d) There is a risk that nepotism will result in an unqualified new hire.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0092.html -->
78 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT v. Customer satisfaction and relationships: The satisfaction of one’s clientele and the quality of the relationships one cultivates with those clients is among the most essential components of a prosperous business. Customers who are pleased with the products or services they receive are more likely to return for additional business and make additional purchases. Customers could be upset with a company either because the workers who serve them deliver bad customer service or because the product itself does not meet their expectations. Retraining personnel to give the highest level of customer service possible and engaging customers through polls, social media, and other research methods may be one approach to addressing the problem of low customer satisfaction that arises within an organisation. vi. Innovation: Innovation is the process by which businesses generate new ideas and grow their product and service offerings. An organisation that is not just forward-thinking but also receptive to new possibilities, one that stays current with technological advances, would be one that rises to the top of its field. Because of the following factors, organisations experience less innovation and become more stagnant: a) They have a culture at their organisation that does not encourage employees to be creative. b) The organisation continues to employ archaic business strategies that stifle rather than foster innovation. vii. Teamwork: Teamwork requires members of an organisation to cooperate with one another toward the accomplishment of a certain objective. Productivity and revenue can both be increased through effective teamwork, which also makes it simpler for everyone to complete their assigned tasks. When members of a team have contrasting personality traits, it can be difficult to maintain effective teamwork within a company. a) There are certain people who give significantly more to initiatives than others. b) Certain employees receive preferential treatment from their managers. c) The organisation suffers from ineffective lines of communication. 3.12 ORGANISATIONAL CHANGE The term “organisational change” refers to the actions that are taken by a company or business to modify a significant aspect of their organisation. These aspects may include the company’s culture, the underlying technologies or infrastructure that it makes use of to function, or their internal procedures. The transition of an organisation from one state to another is what is meant by the term “organisational change.” Alterations to an organisation can take on a variety of forms. Changes could be made to the organisation’s structure, strategy, rules, processes, technology, or culture as part of this process. The process of utilising change to bring about a successful resolution is referred to as organisational change management, and it typically involves three primary steps: planning, implementation, and follow-up. The need for organisational transformation is driven by a variety of variables. The following are some of the most typical ones that managers are confronted with: a) New management at the helm of the business entity or in other areas or departments within the organisation b) Alterations to the organisational structure of teams c) The introduction of innovative technologies d) The adoption of novel business models


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0093.html -->
ORGANISING | 79 There are three primary categories of organisational changes, which are as follows: 1. Developmental Change: Any modification to an organisation that results in an improvement to the processes and procedures that have already been established. 2. Changes during Transition: This type of change moves an organisation away from its existing state and into a new state to address a problem, such as implementing a merger and acquisition or automating a task or process. Examples of this type of change include merging and acquiring other companies or automating a task or process. 3. Transformational changes: Such changes refer to the transformation of an organisation’s culture and way of doing business that is both drastic and fundamental in nature. When undergoing transformative change, it is possible that the final effect will not be known. For instance, a company might branch out into whole new product categories or market niches. 3.12.1 Management of Change A methodical and systematic strategy for addressing the transition or change of an organisation’s objectives, procedures, or technologies, is referred to as “change management.” The goal of change management is to put into action tactics that will bring about change, control change, and assist individuals in becoming accustomed to change. The evaluation of each change request in terms of its potential effect on the project is an essential part of project management, and thus plays an important part in change management. Examining how a change in one part of the project may affect other parts of the project as well as the impact that change may have on the project is the responsibility of project managers as well as senior executives who oversee change control. Included on the list of facets of the project that require specific attention from specialists in change control are the following: • Scope: Change requests should be examined to determine how they will affect the scope of the project. This evaluation should take place with regard to the scope of the project. • Timing: When evaluating change requests, it is important to consider how such requests will affect the overall timing of the project. • Costing: Change requests need to be examined to establish how they will affect the overall cost of the project. Since the cost of labour is often the single highest expense associated with a project, rushing through the completion of project activities can result in significant and immediate shifts in the total cost of the endeavour. • Evaluation of Change Requests Regarding Quality: Change requests should be examined to determine how they will affect the overall quality of the project once it is finished. An acceleration of the project timetable in particular has the potential to impair quality, as does the rapid completion of work, which can lead to flaws.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0094.html -->
80 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT • Human Resources: In the area of human resources, change requests should be assessed to see whether or not there is a requirement for additional or specialised labour. When the timeline of the project needs to be adjusted, the project manager runs the risk of losing valuable resources for use on other tasks. • Communications: Change requests that have been approved should, in the appropriate amount of time, be notified to the relevant stakeholders. • Risk: Change requests need to be assessed in order to determine the risks that are posed by the requests. Even very insignificant alterations might have a cascading effect on the project and increase the danger of logistical, financial, or even physical complications. • Recovery: If the scope of the project changes, it may be necessary to re-evaluate the procurement of supplies and contract personnel. 3.12.2 Resistance to Change The act of rejecting or battling against modifications or changesthat affect the status quo is what we mean when we talk about change resistance. This resistance may show itself in a single worker or permeate the entire workplace. A lack of readiness to adjust one’s behaviour in response to changing conditions can be defined as resistance to change. It can be done in an indirect or direct manner, organised or on a personal level. Employees may come to the realisation that they do not like or desire change and protest it publicly, which can be a very disruptive action. There is a clear indication of resistance to change in functions such as: • Denunciation or Fault Finding • Nit-picking over tiny details • Offensive or Insulting remarks • Absence in meetings • Dishonouring commitments • Continuous arguments • Disruptive behaviour 3.12.3 Approaches to Change Management The implementation and operation of change management is a process that is ongoing and requires a significant amount of time, knowledge, devotion, and effort. Because it involvesthe participation of people or employees of the organisation, these people may also be affected by the changes that are being made. An organisation must first determine why it needs changes and how it will benefit from the changes before adopting one of the many effective and popular techniques or models for change management. The following is a list of some of the most effective techniques and methods for putting change management into action: 1. Lewin’s Change Management Model: Kurt Lewin (1890-1947) was a social psychologist whose extensive work covered studies ofleadership styles and their effects, work on group decision-making, the development of force field theory, the


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0095.html -->
ORGANISING | 81 unfreeze/change/refreeze change management model, the action research approach to research, and the group dynamics approach to training, especially in the form of T-Groups. (Source: https://www. bl.uk/people/kurt-lewin). The change management model developed by Lewin is one of the most widely used and successful models available to us today, making it possible for us to gain an understanding of both structural and organisational change. Kurt Lewin conceived of and initially constructed this model in the 1940s, which continues to be still applicable. Lewin used the transition from solid to liquid to a block of ice to illustrate the concept of structural or organisational change. Their model consists of three primary phases, which are Unfreeze, Change, and Refreeze, which are discussed in detail as under: • Unfreeze: According to Lewin’s methodology, the process of transformation begins with the preparation of the change. This stage is the first in the process. This indicates that the organisation must be prepared not only for the possibility of change but also for the fact that change is both significant and essential. It is crucial to get over this phase because most people all over the world tend to fight change, and it is also essential to get past the status quo. Making people realise why there is a need to change the existing method and how change can offer benefits is the most important thing that can be done here. In addition, the transfer required an organisation to investigate its history and conduct an in-depth analysis of it. • Change: The true transformation or change happens during this step of the process. This process can take some time to complete because people typically need some time to adjust to new happenings, advancements, and changes in their environment. At this point, strong leadership and assurance are essential because they not only point the process in the right path but also make it simpler for the individuals or workers who are participating in the process. The successful completion of this phase relies heavily on clear communication as well as precise scheduling. • Refreeze: At this stage, the people in the company or organisation have shown that they are willing to accept, embrace, and work to put the change into effect. As a result, the firm or organisation starts returning to its previous state. Because of this, the stage is referred to as the recycling stage. This is the point in time when workers and processes begin to re-freeze, and things start getting back to the normal speed and routine that they were previously in. To make sure that changes are always harnessed and put into action even after the goal has been accomplished, this phase requires the assistance of the people. The employees gradually feel more at ease and confident about the adjustments that have been implemented because of the sense of stability that has been established. 2. McKinsey 7S Model: One of the few models that has managed to remain relevant even as others have come and gone out of style is the McKinsey 7-S Framework or Model. It was developed by consultants working for McKinsey & Company in the late 1970s, and it has seven stages, or phases, for managing change. The Seven Stages of the model: • Strategy: A company’s strategy is the game plan it devises to overcome its rivals and achieve its objectives. According to McKinsey’s 7-S framework, this is the first stage of change, and it involves the development of a step-by-step procedure or future plan.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0096.html -->
82 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT • Structure: Structure is the stage or characteristic of this model that pertains to the division of the organisation or the structure it follows. • Systems: This stage is related to the manner in which the day-to-day activities are carried out in order to complete a task, and it focuses on how the task is carried out. • Values that are shared: Shared values are the fundamental or primary principles that an organisation bases its operations and decisions on in order to function effectively. • Style: The term “style” refers to the manner in which changes in leadership and organisational structure are accepted or put into effect. • Staff: The term “staff” can refer to either the workforce as a whole or individual employees, as well as their individual capacities for work. • Skills: The employees of the organisation possess a variety of skills in addition to their core competencies, which are described here. Advantages of the model: a) This model provides a variety of approaches and strategies for understanding an organisation and gaining a profound comprehension of the functioning of the organisation. b) This model integrates both the emotional and the practical aspects of change, which is something that is important to do in order to create ways to enable employees to deal with transition in an easier manner. c) This model considers all components to be important and equally worthy of being addressed, and as a result, it does not omit any aspect that may be of significance in some contexts. d) Additionally, this model provides a directional factor for organisational change. Disadvantages of the model: a) The fact that every component is connected to and dependent upon every other component means that the failure of any one component results in the failure of the entire model; this is the most significant drawback of this model. b) When compared to the other models, this one is more complicated, and it does not place an emphasis on the differences between them. c) There have been more instances of failure experienced by organisations that have used this model, and this can also be one of the negatives that are associated with it. 3. Kotter’s Change Management Principles: The Change Management Principle developed by Kotter is widely regarded as one of the most influential and influential principles in the world. John P. Kotter, a professor at Harvard Business School and author of several books on the topic of change management, is the brains behind the formulation of this theory. His theory of change management is structured in eight phases, with each phase concentrating on a key principle that addresses people’s reactions to change.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0097.html -->
ORGANISING | 83 Stages of Kotter’s model: 1) A rising sense of urgency: Creating a sense of urgency among the people is the focus of this step. The goal is to encourage people to act and move closer to achieving their goals. 2) Putting together the team: In this stage of Kotter’s theory of change management, you are going to make sure that you have the right people on the team by choosing individuals with a diverse set of abilities, levels of knowledge, and levels of commitment. 3) Getting the vision right: This part of the process involves getting the vision right by not only considering the strategy, but also considering creativity, emotional connection, and goals. 4) Communicating: Kotter’s theory on the management of change places a significant emphasis on communication with individuals concerning the change itself and the necessity for it. 5) Getting things off the ground: To get things moving or to empower action, one needs to get support, remove the roadblocks, and implement feedback in a constructive way. This is necessary to get things moving or to empower action. 6) Concentrating on goals for the near term: A good strategy for achieving success without feeling too much pressure is to concentrate on short-term objectives while also breaking down the larger objective into more manageable subgoals. 7) Not giving up: It is essential to refrain from giving up during the process of change management, no matter how challenging the circumstances may appear. Perseverance is the most important factor in achieving success. 8) Adapting to new circumstances: In addition to effectively managing change, one of the most important things to do is to reinforce it and incorporate it into the culture of the workplace. Advantages of Kotter’s Model: • This is a model that can be followed in a step-by-step fashion and is simple to implement. • Instead of attempting to alter oneself in response to the change, the focus here is on embracing it and preparing for it. Disadvantages of Kotter’s Model: • Because this is a model that can be followed step-by-step, there is no other step that needs to be completed to get to one. • The entire process described in this model can be very time consuming. 4. Nudge Theory: Nudge Theory, also known simply as “nudge,” is a concept that has applications in the fields of behavioural science, economics, and political theory.
