However, it is also applicable to the process of change management in organisations and businesses. American Legal Scholar Cass R. Sunstein and American Economist Richard H. Thaler deserve the lion’s share of the credit for developing this theory.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0098.html -->
84 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT The essence of this principle consists of gently nudging or pushing someone, as well as encouraging and motivating them to make a change. The Nudge Theory is helpful not only in locating and comprehending existing influences, but also in providing an explanation for those influences, with the goal of either removing them entirely or modifying them to the point where they allow for the achievement of positive outcomes. 5. ADKAR Model: The ADKAR model or theory of change is a goal-oriented tool or model that enables various change management teams to concentrate on the steps or activities that are directly related to the goals it wants to achieve. This makes it possible for change management to be more effective. The model produces cumulative and sequential results, both in terms of the goals achieved and the results that are defined by using the model. When using this model, one must obtain each result or outcome in a specific order for the change to be able to be maintained and implemented. This means that the order in which each result or outcome is obtained is very important. Change managers can use models to identify various gaps or gaps in the process of change management in order to provide efficient training to employees. This is possible thanks to the use of models. ADKAR model basically stands for: a) Consciousness - regarding the requirement and prerequisite for change b) A strong desire to both be a part of and contribute to the process of change c) Knowledge - how to effect this transformation d) Capability - the ability to integrate change on a consistent basis e) Reinforcement, both to maintain its current position and to add additional support later. Advantages of ADKAR Model: • The model provides the capability of identifying the reasons why the changes that have been made are not working and why the desired results are not being obtained and evaluating those reasons. • Using the model, one is able to dissect the changesinto their component parts, and then pinpoint the area in which the transformation may not be as successful as originally envisioned. • It provides both a business and a people-oriented perspective on the changing environment. 6. Bridges’Transition Model: The Bridges’ transition model was developed by William Bridges, an American author, speaker, and organisational consultant (Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William Bridges). The theory was brought to the attention of the general public after it was published in the book “Managing Transitions.” This approach, model, or theory is distinguished by the fact that it concentrates on progression rather than modification and, as a result, remains static. Although there is often only a fine line between the two, it is essential to be aware of the distinction between change and transformation. Change is something that occurs to people, even if they are unaware of it happening to them, even though on the one hand the transition is something that occurs from the inside out. The model centres on three primary steps, which are described in the following order: • End, give up and let go • Neutral zone • New beginning


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0099.html -->
ORGANISING | 85 7. Kubler-Ross Five Stage Model: After conducting research on death and dying, Elizabeth Kubler-Ross came up with the idea for the Five Stage Model that she later named after herself. This model is also known as the grief model because it discusses the various emotional states and stages that people go through when they become aware that their time on earth is ending. The model is adaptable to a variety of other aspects of life, including but not limited to the loss of a job or a shift in one’s line of work and conditions of a lesser severity. The model, which assists in comprehending and coping with one’s own personal trauma, has gained widespread acceptance all over the world. The Kubler-Ross model can be broken down into several distinct stages, which are as follows: 1. Denial: When people are unable to accept the news about their diagnosis, they are said to be in the first stage of the model, which is called denial. Because of their incapacity to process information, some people develop what is like a buffer or hedge around themselves. During this stage, one may experience tremors as well as a feeling of numbness. This is since everyone is fighting the change and does not want to believe what is taking place. 2. Anger: After carefully processing the information, one’s initial reaction is frequently one of anger. When a person realisesthat the change will in fact influence them, their initialstate of denial transitions into anger. During this stage, the individual begins looking for someone else to blame for their situation. Anger can be channelled in a variety of different ways depending on the person. 3. Bargaining: Bargaining is involved in the subsequent stage or stage in order to obtain the optimal answer to the problem or problem that has been presented. People engage in bargaining as a means of warding off the worst-case scenario and as a natural response to limit their exposure to excessive change. 4. Depression: When someone realises that their attempts at bargaining are fruitless, it can cause them to experience feelings of depression and a loss of confidence. At this point, the individual is no longer bothered by anything and has entered a state of mind that is both depressing and hopeless. Depression can be viewed or identified in a variety of ways, and some of these ways include having low energy, not committing to anything, having low motivation, and not having any enthusiasm or joy in any form. 5. Acceptance: Acceptance of what is occurring and the beginning of resigning oneself to it can eventually occur when a person realises that being depressed or fighting change serves no purpose and that there is no point in doing either. People approach this stage in their lives in a variety of different ways. Others, meanwhile, may come to the conclusion that they have no other options available to them and may, as a result, give up trying to improve their circumstances and instead give in to the predetermined outcomes of their lives.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0100.html -->
86 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 3.13 CONFLICT DYNAMICS To have a full comprehension of what is meant by the phrase “dynamics of conflict,” one must first comprehend what each individual word means. There are two primary ways that the term “conflict” can be defined. The argument that different stages of the conflict process fundamentally alter the environment in which interactions between actors take place is what is meant by the concept of conflict dynamics at the core of a stage. 3.13.1 Conflict Management The process of resolving conflicts through conflict management aims to achieve a balance between minimising the potential for negative outcomes and maximising the potential for positive outcomes. This essential management ability entails negotiating, thinking creatively, and using a range of different strategies according to the context of the interaction. The goal of conflict management is to improve learning and group outcomes, such as an organization’s efficiency or performance in a given environment. Some Examples of Skills needed for conflict management are given below: • Clear and effective communication- Communication skills are one of the most important skills you need to have for conflict management because they are the key to successfully resolving conflicts. • Listening attentively • Engaging in the practice of empathy. • Problem solving. • Positive attitude. • Setting the priority levels. • Being patient. • Understanding others’ body language. 3.13.2 Conflict Resolution Strategies The following is a list of the top five strategies for conflict resolution: 1. Refrain from Ignoring the Conflict: If you’re the type of person who prefers to avoid confrontation, the thought of burying your head in the sand and acting as if a problem doesn’t exist in the hopes that it will go away on its own may make you feel like giving in to temptation. Although this does occur on occasion, the reality is that in most cases, doing so will make the problem even more severe. Conflicts that are ignored have a propensity to worsen over time and resurface at inopportune times; therefore, whenever you recognise it, your team should take a side and the conflict should be resolved as soon as you recognise it. 2. Explain the nature of the issue: It is imperative that you gather all the relevant information if you are tasked with mediating a dispute between two members of your team. Find out what the real issue is by having a discussion with everyone who is affected by it. How does everyone else make sense of what’s going on here? Are the requirements going unfulfilled? What does each party consider to be an acceptable way to resolve the conflict? Make sure that all the parties involved are aware that you are acting as an impartial mediator and assure them that they can feel free to discuss sensitive information with you.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0101.html -->
ORGANISING | 87 3. Organise a meeting between the various parties involved: After you have had the opportunity to speak with each of the parties involved separately, you should then bring them all together in a meeting so that they can resolve their differences in an atmosphere that is impartial. Now is the time to generate ideas, pay attention to what others have to say, and be willing to consider a wide range of points of view. The objective is to figure out what the issue is, what part everyone is playing in the disagreement, and what kinds of solutions might be available. 4. Identify a solution: After both parties have had the opportunity to discuss the current situation, it is time to determine what sort of solution might be satisfactory, as well as the steps that need to be taken to arrive at that solution. In an ideal world, by this point, both parties will understand the position of the other, and in many cases, the conflict can only be resolved through civilised and honest conversation. In any case, if the circumstance calls for further resolution, you will be required to negotiate with the other party and assist them in developing an appropriate solution. This stage may require some time and effort on both parties’ parts because it requires them to put aside their differences and preferences to locate some ground they can work on together (which may include accepting the fact that they will not get everything they want out of the situation). The next step is to collaborate with both parties to produce a detailed plan of action that will lead to a resolution of the issue. 5. Maintain vigilance and follow up on the situation regarding the conflict: Even though the problem has been pinpointed and addressed, this does not mean that the problem will vanish completely. As a manager, it is your duty to check in with both sides of the conflict to ascertain whether the issue has been resolved and whether or not the steps that have been outlined are being carried out in order to find a solution. If everything seems to be going smoothly, just keep in mind that it is important to take a step back and assess the situation on a regular basis to determine whether things are truly going smoothly or if there is still tension that can be resolved. This is a prerequisite for conflict resolution. If it is obvious that the solution was not working or was not the appropriate solution for the situation, make sure that a dialogue is continued with both parties to adjust expectations, identify alternative solutions, and create solutions that are positive and healthy. LET US SUM UP Organisation is the cornerstone that all other aspects of management are built upon to create their overall structure. It is the pillar upon which management is built. The process of forming relationships between the various participants in an enterprise is referred to as “organising.” The authority and responsibilities of each party play a role in the formation of the relationships. The formal organisation is supplemented by the informal organisation, which does not appear on the organisation chart, to assist in the accomplishment of organisational objectives in an effective and efficient manner. Organisation requires the establishment of structural relationships between the various departments and the individuals who are employed there in order to achieve the objectives that have been set. The distribution of responsibilities and the handing down of authority are the two primary concerns of an organization’s structure. The establishment of formal relationships among the individuals working in the organisation is very important for the purpose of making clear the lines of authority in the organisation and for the purpose of coordinating the efforts of different individuals in an effective manner. The current era is one that features intensely competitive business environments. For businesses to thrive and remain profitable in the current economic climate, it is essential for them to outperform their rivals in every facet of their customer interactions. To accomplish this goal, various


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0102.html -->
88 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT businesses have opted to implement either differentiation or integration as their strategy. Differentiation provides customersin a niche market with a product orservice that istailored to theirspecific requirements. Integration provides access to a wide range of products through a single entity. It enables them to gain an advantage over their competitors and cultivate a dedicated customer base. KEYWORDS Specialisation, Delegation of Authority, Chain of Command, Unity of Command, Span of Control, Centralisation and Decentralisation, Line and Staff Organisation, Committees, Organisation Chart, Organisation Manual, Organisational Culture, Formal and Informal Organisation, Management of Change, McKinsey 7S Model, Kotter’s Model, Nudge Theory, ADKAR Model, Kubler-Ross Five Stage Model, Conflict Management CHECK YOUR PROGRESS 1. Which of the following does not constitute a part of the process of organising? (a) Establishing reporting relationships (b) Determining who will be responsible for a particular activity (c) Specialising (d) Dividing and assigning work 2. Part of this process involves organising the activities into categories according to the product lines. (a) Functional Structure (b) The divisional hierarchy (c) Organisation with official standing (d) An unofficial grouping or organisation 3. It is possible to delegate decision-making power to individuals at lower levels. This is known as (a) Assignment of Responsibility (b) Centralisation (c) Decentralisation (d) None of the preceding options 4. The expression “span of management” refers to (a) The scope of a manager’s duties and responsibilities (b) The responsibility of a manager (c) The maximum number of direct subordinates that can be effectively managed by a single supervisor. (d) None of the preceding options 5. When we talk about centralisation, we mean (a) The preservation of one’s right to make decisions (b) Extending into new territories (c) The division or categorization of different levels (d) The spread of decision-making power throughout the organisation SOLUTIONS 1. (c) 2. (b) 3. (c) 4. (c) 5. (a)

STRUCTURE 4.0 Objectives 4.1 Introduction 4.2 Functions of Staffing 4.3 Objectives of Staffing 4.4 Nature of Staffing 4.5 Facets of Staffing 4.6 Significance of Staffing 4.7 System Approach to Staffing 4.8 Recruitment 4.9 Selection 4.10 Training 4.11 Retention and Development 4.12 Knowledge and Learning Management 4.13 Performance Appraisal 4.14 Human Resource Development Let Us Sum Up Keywords Check Your Progress Solutions U N I T 4 STAFFING

<!-- Page Start: text/part0104.html -->
90 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 4.0 OBJECTIVES After studying this unit, you will have better understanding of: • Functions, objectives and nature or staffing • Different facets of staffing • Significance of staff training • Systems approach to staff training • Retention and development of staff • Knowledge and learning management • Performance appraisal • Human Resource Development (HRD) 4.1 INTRODUCTION People working in an organisation play a vital role in achieving its goals or objectives. It is not only the number of people which matters but also their skill level and motivation. Proper recruitment and training policies of the organisation play an important role in ensuring that right quality of people in right number are put for carrying out a particular type of assignment. Appropriate career development policies should also be adopted to tackle the problem of attrition of skilled staff. The effective administration of human resources is the foundation of the managerial function of staffing.Amethod or procedure, such as recruiting, selection, placement, training, and development, giving remuneration, etc., are all examples of activities that fall under the purview of effective human resource management. The process of recruiting and training suitable individuals to fill the available jobs in a company is an important part ofstaffing. One definition of this processis asfollows: “It entails making an estimate of the quantity and type of personnel that will be required, as well as recruiting and developing the said personnel, in addition to maintaining and increasing the competence and performance of said personnel”. In other words, the process of locating, analysing, placing, cultivating, and reviewing persons for employment opportunities may be referred to asstaffing. The process ofstaffing an organisation entail determining the manpower requirements of the business and then providing it with sufficient and competent individuals at all its levels. Staffing therefore encompasses activities such as personnel planning, procurement (also known as selection and placement), training and development, performance evaluation, and compensation of workers. Managers are responsible for building an organisation through the process of recruiting, selection, and development of individuals as capable employees. This process is known as staffing. The process of staffing plays an important part in the planning of human resources. It guarantees that the organisation is making the greatest possible use of its human resources. It may also be noted that the hiring process is the most important part of any other managerial role. It is beneficial to an enterprise’s efforts to keep a workforce at a decent level. 4.2 FUNCTIONS OF STAFFING The primary functions of the staffing are: i. Obtaining qualified individuals for various job positions inside the organisation is the primary objective of the staffing function, which has several other responsibilities as well.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0105.html -->
STAFFING | 91 ii. The process of staffing ensures that the most qualified candidates are selected for open positions, which results in greater levels of both productivity and performance. iii. It contributes to the promotion of the most effective and efficient usage of human resources in a variety of ways. iv. The successful recruitment of the right person raises the level of job satisfaction and morale experienced by workers. v. The process of staffing serves to guarantee that human resources are used more effectively. vi. It secures the organization’s continued existence as well as its continued expansion using development managers. vii. Proper people can be placed in the right jobs with the help of staffing services. viii. Staffing is a function that is used in many different contexts. It is the responsibility of managers at every level of management to carry it out. The process of management includes staffing as one of its essential components. It is possible to define it as the process of recruiting and cultivating the necessary personnel to fill the available roles in an organization’s numerous departments. This is so because when it comes to effective and efficient management of the business, every business entity is extremely concerned with the quality of the people available to them. According to Peter Drucker, “man, of all the resources accessible to man can grow and develop.” It is concerned with the recruitment, selection, placement, utilisation, and development of an organisation’s staff members. 4.3 OBJECTIVES OF STAFFING The important objectives of staffing are: i. To get the appropriate employees for the appropriate positions. ii. To educate and cultivate the available human resources. iii. To design policies for personnel matters, such as transfer, promotion, and other related work. iv. To effectively shape the available human resources and to motivate those resources toward better levels of performance. v. To create a positive and productive working connection between employers and employees as well as between different groups of employees. vi. To ensure that the demands of the workers are met to the workers’ satisfaction so that they will become dedicated and loyal to the organisation. vii. To keep positive human interactions in place in order to foster strong morale among the workforces. 4.4 NATURE OF STAFFING 1) Concentrated on Individuals: The process of Staffing is concerned with the effective utilisation of an organisation’s Human Resources. It encourages and incentivises each worker to contribute to the fullest extent possible toward the accomplishment of the organization’s goals and objectives. 2) Oriented Towards Development; This means that the focus is on maximising the potential of the employees working for the organisation. Their interests, personality, and talents will all mature as a result of this. It makes it possible for


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0106.html -->
92 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT workers to derive the greatest possible satisfaction from their employment. Employees are given the tools they need to reach their full potential as a result. Employees are given the opportunity to enhance their careers through various educational and training programmes provided by the company. 3) An all-pervasive function: Staffing is essential to the operation of every business. It is a significant component of the overall managementsystem that hasthe capability of being implemented in both for-profit and not-for-profit businesses. It is necessary for all personnel at all levels of the business, regardless of their type. 4) An ongoing procedure The process of hiring new employees is an ongoing one that never comes to a conclusion. It involves constant vigilance and an acute awareness of the significance of human relations in every aspect of the operation. 5) Human objectives: This helps employees reach their full potential so that they can experience the greatest amount of satisfaction from the work that they do. It fosters an environment in which workers are willing to collaborate voluntarily toward the accomplishment of the organisation’s stated objectives. 6) Focused on both individuals and groups to achieve goals: Staffing is concerned with employees in both their individual and group capacities in order to achieve goals. It creates the appropriate organisational framework to meet the requirements of both individual efforts and collective endeavours. It does this by combining the pursuit of individual goals with those of the group in such a way that the employees experience a sense of involvement with the organisation. 7) Creating an environment conducive to productive work: It creates an environment conducive to productive work inside the firm, one in which every worker puts his or her best efforts to the accomplishment of organisational goals. It makes for an atmosphere that is not just physically but also mentally pleasant to be in while at work. 8) Its multidisciplinary character: Staffing is a field that originated in the social sciences. It incorporates theories and ideas derived from a variety of academic fields, including sociology, anthropology, management, and psychology. In addition to this, it has taken some ideas from the behavioural sciences. It might be thought of as the science of “human engineering.” 9) An essential component of general management: The hiring and firing of employees are essential components of general management. It is an essential aspect of the responsibilities of each line manager. Effective people administration skills are required of each member of the management group, from the most senior member on down. It is helpful to other functional areas of management and provides support for them. 10) A blend of art and science: The process of hiring new employees is grounded in the field of human engineering. It can be thought of as an organised body of knowledge that is made up of principles and methods. It is also an art because it requires the ability to interact with different types of individuals. Because it handles employees and finds solutions to their problems in a methodical manner, it is considered one of the creative arts. It is considered a management philosophy because of its emphasis on the dignity and worth of individual human beings.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0107.html -->
STAFFING | 93 4.4.1 Salient Features of Staffing The following facts clearly bring out the salient features of staffing as a function of management: 1. Involvement with Human Beings: The interaction that staffing has with living individuals is the first and most crucial important aspect of staffing. This indicates that, in contrast to planning and organising, it is not merely a matter of filling out paperwork, but rather entails the nomination of qualified individuals to a variety of jobs. Planning determines not only what work is to be done but also when, how, and by whom it is to be done. In a similar manner, the organising process involves the preparation of an organisational structure chart. On the other hand, in the process of staffing, competent people are picked and provided training while keeping in mind the importance of the role, and this process does not simply involve doing paperwork. All the operations that are carried out to complete this task involve dealing with human beings in some capacity, whether it be recruitment, selection, training, promotion, etc. 2. Distinct Managerial Function: The fact that staffing is seen as a separate managerial job is the second significant aspect of this process. A separate management function indicates that rather than being a significant component of another function, it is in fact a significant function on its own. The other categories of managerial functions, such as planning, organising, leading, and controlling, all include staffing as one of its subcategories. A short while ago, several management specialists believed it to be a component of organising. However, in modern times, it is acknowledged as a significant part of management thanks to the findings of several distinct research projects. 3. A Necessary Activity at All Managerial Levels: The process of staffing is critical at every administrative level. For instance, appointing the General Manager is one of the ways in which the Board of Directors fulfils its responsibility for staffing. The staffing role is carried out by the General Manager, who is responsible for appointing departmental managers; departmental managers, in turn, are responsible for carrying out this function by appointing their subordinates. It is imperative that this point be made clear: the creation of a separate personnel department does not absolve the responsible managers of their duty to perform this vitally important function. The establishment of this department was done with the intention of assisting managers of all levels in the performance of the staffing responsibilities that are assigned to them. It is essential to emphasise the fact that the managers in charge are ultimately responsible for the hiring decisions that are made. 4. Associated to Social Responsibility: Since staffing involves human beings, it is important to remember that man is a social animal. The social responsibility that comes along with this role is born out of the fact that it is related to human beings. For managers to fulfil this role, they need to exercise caution and maintain objectivity while they move through the various ancillary functions, such as recruitment, selection, promotion, and so on. 5. Impact of Internal and External Environment: The enterprise’s internal and external environments both have an impact on the performance of the personnel department. The promotion policy, the demotion policy, the transfer policy, and so on are all examples of employee-related policies that are part of the enterprise’s internal environment. If the vacant roles are going to be filled up by promotion as a matter of company policy, then the employees who are currently working for the company will have the possibility to attain higher posts, while persons who come from outside the company will only be appointed to lower posts.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0108.html -->
94 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT When viewed in this light, the function of staffing is indeed impacted by the internal policy of the business. The policies of the government and the educational environment are two components of the external environment that the firm is subject to. It’s possible for the government to make it their official policy that employees ofspecific businessesshould only be recruited through job exchanges. The organisation of specialised training camps is one way that educational institutions can contribute to the growth and development of their workforce. In this manner, the external environment has an impact, as well, on the function of staffing. 4.5 FACETS OF STAFFING There are three primary facets of staffing, which are as follows: i. Recruitment: Recruitment is a positive process that seeks to attract a bigger number of people with ideal profiles to apply for positions that are empty in the business. The organization’s goal with recruitment is to fill positions as quickly as possible. The more people that apply for a job, the better your chances are of finding an applicant who meets your requirements. ii. Selection: Selection is a procedure that eliminates candidates by carefully reviewing their applications and choosing those who are the best fit for the open position. The selection process is different from the recruitment process in that it rejects applications. iii. Training: Training is another constructive activity that improves the employees’ knowledge and abilities, as well as their capacity to do their jobs more effectively. The processes of recruitment, selection, and training are followed by organisations to ensure that all positions in the organisational hierarchy are filled with people who are competent and talented in their respective fields. On the other hand, the nature of the corporate environment affects the manner in which these operations are carried out. 4.6 SIGNIFICANCE OF STAFFING Finding the appropriate candidates for open positions in an organisation’s hierarchy is the responsibility of the staffing department. Because the human resource is what propels a company to greater heights, it is imperative for management to ensure that they have the appropriate kind of personnel in place. This is the most fundamental and important job of management. In the past, “staffing” meant appointing individuals to get things done. However, as a result of the rapid changes that have taken place in the corporate environment, “staffing” has taken on a higher role as a management function. Staffing must appoint people who not only have the specialised knowledge but also the appropriate attitude, aptitude, commitment, and sense of loyalty for the organisation in order to keep up with the continuously advancing technology, changing human behaviour, and growing size of the business. This is required in order for staffing to be effective. The caliber of an organisation’s workforce is directly proportional to the company’s level of success. Thus, staffing is an important managerial function and its significance can be further explained as under: i. Recognizes Competent Staff: The process of determining the number of workers needed to fill positions in an organisational structure and assigning those positions to the appropriate individuals is referred to as “staffing.” It makes certain that only those individuals with the necessary skills are hired.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0109.html -->
STAFFING | 95 For instance, the HR Head of your Company identifies the departments that are facing shortage of staff at various levels and then works toward finding people who are suitable for those departments. ii. Enhancement of Overall Performance: When businesses put the right people in charge of the right jobs, they are able to use their physical resources in the most effective way possible, which ultimately results in increased productivity, enhanced efficiency, and enhanced performance. For instance, in the interest of achieving the best possible outcomes, the Purchase Department should only appoint persons who have integrity, understand the material and capital goods acquisition procedures and have knowledge about the industry and the Suppliers. iii. Ongoing Capability for Survival and Development: Training and development programmes for employees that are done correctly keep managers up to date on the changes that have occurred in the business environment. The continuity of the business’s survival and expansion can be ensured by conducting succession planning for managerial positions. For instance, whenever there is a change in the systems, procedures and the technology used by the Company or the organisation is restructured, staff needs to be trained and educated so that they are aware of the modifications and are able to incorporate them into their working. iv. The Optimal Employment of the Available Human Resources: The needs ofthe workforce are determined during the staffing process, and appointments ofindividuals are planned accordingly. This ensures that there are sufficient personnel available to avoid underutilization of workers or disruptions to work caused by inadequate staffing levels. For instance, if there are enough teachers working in each department, then each individual class will have a teacher assigned to it. v. Increases Job Satisfaction and Contributes to a Positive Morale: Staffing recognisesthe value of its employees’contributions and expresses gratitude and appreciation for those contributions through ongoing performance reviews and evaluations. The contentment and morale of the workforce are maintained in this way. For instance, after the financial year is closed, a company might plan evaluations for each individual employee in order to select an “employee of the year.” This not only rewards the hard-working employee with a sense of satisfaction but also encourages others to work even more diligently. You might also notice that the Managing Director of your company was a Junior Manager at one point in his/her career. As an appreciation of her/his hard work, she/he has been elevated to this position. It is necessary for the success and growth of an organisation that positions in the organisational structures be filled at the right time with the right number and the right kind of people to be a successful and growing organisation. People who aren’t qualified to do their jobs will cause a waste of resources, time, energy, and effort. Appointing individuals who have the necessary knowledge and experience is necessary to make the most efficient use of resources, accomplish the highest possible level of productivity, and produce products or provide services of a high quality. Training should be provided to workers to enable them to adjust to shifting conditions in the workplace. It is necessary to provide suitable compensations and incentives for reaching one’s goals to maintain full employment.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0110.html -->
96 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 4.7 SYSTEM APPROACH TO STAFFING A method based on an open system is required for staffing. It is carried out within the company, which, in turn, is linked to the environment outside the company. For this reason, it is necessary to take into consideration the internal factors of the company, which include the personnel policies, the organisational climate, and the reward system. The total management system is related to the managerial function of staffing that is performed by managers. In particular, enterprise plans end up serving as the foundation for organisation plans, both of which are essential in order to accomplish the enterprise’s objectives. The current organisation structure, as well as any plans for the structure, will determine the number and types of managers needed. Using the management inventory, a comparison is made between the needs for managers and the available talent. According to the findings of this investigation, both external and internal sources are used in the procedures of recruitment, selection, placement, promotion, and termination. A performance review, a strategy for one’s career, as well as training and development opportunities for managers are also vital components of staffing. Asthe model demonstrates,staffing has an impact on both leading and controlling. For instance, managers who have received adequate training are able to cultivate an atmosphere in which members of the workforce can simultaneously achieve both the enterprise’s goals and their own personal objectives by cooperating in groups. In other words, having the appropriate number of staff makes leading easier. In a similar manner, choosing quality managers influences controlling, for instance, by preventing a large number of undesirable deviations from developing into major issues. The external environment is also something that cannot be ignored; high technology calls for managers who possess a high level of education, training, and expertise. It is possible that an organisation will not be able to expand at the desired rate if it is unable to satisfy the demand for managers of this type. 4.7.1 Factors Affecting the Requirement of Quantum and Types of Managers • The number of managers that are required in an organisation is dependent not only on the size of the business but also on the complexity of the organisation structure, the plans for expansion, and the rate of turnover among managerial personnel. There is no regulation that governs the relationship between the number of managers and the total number of staff members. • It is possible to modify a structure so that the number of managers in a given instance will increase or decrease regardless of the size of an operation by either expanding or contracting the delegation of authority. This can be accomplished by modifying a structure so that it either expands or contracts. • Even though it has been emphasised here how important it is to determine the number of managers that are required, it is abundantly clear that numbers are only one part of the picture. Specifically, it is necessary to determine the qualifications for each individual position in order to select the managers who are the most suited to their roles. A detailed analysis of this kind is necessary in order to satisfy the requirements for the managerial position. 4.8 RECRUITMENT Staffing refers to the process of hiring individuals who are the most qualified for a job whereas recruiting is the process of finding potential applicants for a job and encouraging them to apply for the vacant post. Recruiting and staffing are two different processes but are often used interchangeably.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0111.html -->
STAFFING | 97 The process of recruitment and staffing is what constitutesthe method by which companies find employees to fill open positions. Recruitment takes place at a variety of stages throughout a company’s existence, although it is at its most intense when a new business is launched or when an existing business undertakes expansion or new directions. The procedure starts with extensive planning that occurs behind closed doors and continues with the recruitment, interviewing, and selection of staff members. There are a few different paths that can be taken by small businesses during the process of recruitment and staffing. The following is a rundown of the primary stages involved in the recruitment process: • Determining the need for hiring • Conceiving a recruitment strategy • Drafting a job description • Publicising the position • Recruiting candidates for the position • Examining applications • Conducting a phone interview or initial screening • Conducting interviews • Evaluating candidates • Conducting a background check • Making a decision • Checking references • Making an offer of employment • Hiring candidates • On boarding of candidates 4.8.1 Types of Recruitment 1) Internal Recruiting: The process of filling open positions within a company with current staff members from that organisation is known as internal recruiting. 2) Retained Recruiting: When a company engages a recruiting firm, they can do so in numerous different ways; one of the most prevalent ways is retained recruiting. When an organisation hires a recruiting firm to fill a vacant position, the organisation is responsible for paying an upfront fee to the recruiting firm. Up to the point that the post is filled, the company is responsible for locating potential candidates. In addition, the organisation commits to forming an exclusive partnership with the company. In other words, companies are unable to use multiple recruiting agencies for the purpose of filling the same vacancy. 3) Contingency Recruiting: This type of recruiting, like retained recruiting, requires the assistance of an outside firm. In contrast to retained recruiting, contingency hiring does not require an upfront payment. Instead, the recruitment company is only compensated when one of the candidates they represent is offered and accepts a position within an organisation. 4) Recruiting for Staffing Agencies: Staffing recruiters are employed by staffing agencies. Staffing and recruiting involve pairing skilled job seekers with open positions that meet their qualifications. In addition, most of the jobs that staffing companies fill are either temporary or only available for a limited time. 5) Outplacement Recruiting: When it comes to recruitment, outplacement is a type of advantage that is often sponsored by employers and assists former employees in making the transition into new


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0112.html -->
98 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT jobs. The purpose of outplacement recruiting is to equip people who have lost their jobs with the tools necessary to locate new employment or pursue other lines of work. 6) Reverse Recruiting: This is a procedure in which an employee is urged to seek employment with a new businessthat offers a better fit for theirskillset. This processrefersto the practice of encouraging an employee to seek employment with a different organisation. Workers who need assistance with this procedure can take advantage of our Reverse Recruiting service, which offers reviewing of resumes, holding mock interviews, and providing in-depth explanations of various job roles and responsibilities. 4.8.2 Method for Conducting Successful Recruitment Procedure When it comes to producing high-quality personnel on a consistent basis, recruitment is a sophisticated process that demands significant research, comprehensive processes, and finesse. Keeping this in mind, the following are the three most important pieces of advice for successful recruitment: a) Search within your organisation before looking outside: There is a significant probability that the most qualified individual for the post is already employed by your company. Candidates from within your company are already aware with your company’s culture and goals, and they contribute to achieving those goals. It is reasonable to anticipate that, given their history of accomplishments inside your organisation, they will continue to be successful in their new role. b) Contact applicants considered passive: There is a good probability that the person who would be perfect for your open position is not currently seeking for a new job and will not react to the advertisement that you post on the job board. Why? because it is most likely that they are working somewhere else already. After all, there might not be any reason that your competitors should not also be interested in hiring your perfect applicant. Therefore, if you want to be successful in recruiting, you need to hunt for elite talent outside of your current candidate pool. It is crucial to create contacts with local university business schools (or other relevant departments), checking social media sites like LinkedIn for strong resumes from applicants who might not be explicitly looking for a new job, encouraging your employees to suggest people they know or are linked to, and encouraging your employees to attend industry conferences and participating in professional organisations. All of these are important avenues through which we can grow our recruitment network. c) Hire the person already excelling in similar jobs: Several Management Gurus and Experts are of the view that we should hire a person who is the sure thing and has already made a name in similar jobs. To put it another way, a history of success is the single best predictor of future achievement. 4.9 SELECTION The phase of the staffing process known as selection is the component of the hiring procedure that entails selecting an employee to hire from a shortlist of exceptional applicants who have been reduced. Throughout the process of recruiting, there are multiple opportunities for selection to take place. Managers shortlist the applicants to be contacted based on the credentials of their applications and decide about the applicants who are to be called for interviews, and ultimately which applicants to be hired for available positions. If you have a good understanding of the many levels of selection and what to look for at each level, you will be better able to choose the right job candidates for your company’s long-term success.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0113.html -->
STAFFING | 99 The Selection Process: It is necessary for an organisation to develop its own selection procedures because each company has its own standards. and requirements. Nevertheless, the most important steps are as follows: 1) Preliminary Interview: This is a very generic and basic interview that is held to exclude the candidates who are utterly unfit to work in the organisation. The purpose of this interview is to determine who will move on to the next stage of the interview process. As a result, the company now has access to a pool of applicants who could be suitable for open positions in the company. 2) Taking Applications: Candidates for open positions submit their resumes and cover letters to the business in order to be considered for employment. The application provides the interviewers with information about the candidates, such as their biographical data, their work experience, their hobbies and interests. 3) Examining the Applications: Once the applications have been received, a special screening committee examines them in order to select potential candidates from among the applications who will then be contacted to schedule interviews. Candidates could be chosen based on specific criteria such astheir qualifications, previous work experience, and so on. 4) Employment Tests: For a company to determine whether or not an individual is qualified for a certain job, the company must first evaluate the individual’s abilities. This is accomplished by the administration of numerous job exams, including IQ tests, aptitude tests, competence tests, personality tests, and so on. 5) Formal Interview: The fifth and last stage of the selection process is the employee interview. This stage is the next step in the selection process. Interviews for jobs are conducted to determine in great depth a candidate’s skill set and whether they have the capacity to work in the business. The purpose of an employment interview is to determine whether the candidate is suitable for the position, as well as to provide the candidate with information regarding the work profile and the responsibilitiesthat would be expected of the potential employee. Interviews are an essential part of the hiring process because they help employers choose the most qualified candidates for open positions. 6) Verification of References: The individual who provides a possible employee›s reference is also a very important source of information, so we must check with them. The referee will be able to provide information regarding the individual›s capabilities, experience in the prior firms, leadership, and managerial skills, and more. The Human Resources department is responsible for maintaining the confidentiality of the information provided by the referee. 7) Medical Tests: The seventh and finalstep in the selection processisthe medical examination, which is another highly essential part of the procedure. Employers can determine whether or not any of the potential applicants are physically and psychologically fit to undertake the tasks associated with their professions with the help of medical examination. There would be fewer incidents of absenteeism, accidents, and employee turnover if there is an effective system of medical check-ups in place. Additionally, the health standards of the employees will be greater.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0114.html -->
100 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 8) Final Selection and Appointment: This is the last phase in the selection process, and it consists of a letter announcing the final selection and appointment. An appointment letter is delivered or emailed to the employee to advise his selection for the job once the candidate has demonstrated that they are qualified for the position by passing all written tests, interviews, and medical examinations. The terms of employment, including working hours, remuneration, and leave benefits, are listed in their entirety in the letter of appointment. It is common practice to hire workers on a conditional basis or on probation, with the understanding that they will be hired permanently or confirmed in service if the company is satisfied with their performance. Importance of the Selection Process: • The expansion and success of the company are directly attributable to the careful selection and placement of staff. In a similar vein, the capabilities of the company’s workforce are the only real measure of how successful the company can be. • The rapid accomplishment of company goals is directly attributable to the recruitment of workers who are talented and skilled. • Industrial accidents will substantially fall in numbers when the right technical staff is engaged for the right jobs. • People’s levels of job satisfaction, as well as the quantity and quality of their output, tend to rise when they find employment in fields in which they excel since this leads to more personal fulfilment. • People that are content with their jobs frequently tend to have good morale and motivation to do better. 4.10 TRAINING The staff will participate in training following the completion of the orientation. Training is the process of increasing the skills, capacities, and knowledge of workers so that they are better suited to perform certain job duties. The training process shapes the way people think and ultimately leads to improved work performance from those individuals. 4.10.1 Importance of Training Training is necessary for the growth and success of a company. It is beneficial to the organisation’s employees as well as the employers of that organisation. If an employee receives adequate training, he or she will become more effective and productive over time. The following are the primary reasons why training is provided: a) Training is provided to new candidates that join an organisation. Through this training, they become acquainted with the mission and vision of the organisation, as well as the laws and regulations, and the working circumstances. b) The current staff members receive training to update their skills and expand their knowledge base. c) Training is provided to accommodate for any updates or alterations that may be made to the technology to keep up with those changes. Examples of this include the purchase of new machinery, alterations to the manufacturing process, and the installation of computers. Training is provided to employees on how to use updated machinery and methods of doing job. d) When advancement in one’s career and getting promotions become priorities, training is provided to employees to get them ready to share the responsibilities that come with working at a higher level.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0115.html -->
STAFFING | 101 The benefits of training can be summed up as: The following are some benefits that can be attributed to training: I. Boosts Employee Morale: Training helps employees achieve job stability and job satisfaction, which in turn boosts employee morale. When an employee’s needs are met and his or her morale is high, that individual will make a greater contribution to the overall performance of the firm, and employee absenteeism and turnover will be reduced. II. Decreased requirement for supervision: An employee who has received adequate training will be familiar with his duties and details of his work and will require less oversight. As a result, there will be a reduction in the amount of time and effort that is wasted. III. Reduction in the number of accidents: Employees who lack the knowledge and abilities necessary to perform their duties are more likely to make mistakes. When a person has received additional training, there is a decreased likelihood of them being involved in an accident while on the job, and they also become more proficient. IV. Increased Promotion Chances: Training helps employees improve their abilities and effectiveness, which increases their chances of being promoted. Employees, thus, develop into something of value for the organisation. V. Increased productivity: Training helps employees become more productive and efficient, which is one of the many benefits of employee education. Employeesthat have received adequate training demonstrate both quantity and quality in their work. If staff are given the appropriate training, there will be less waste of time, money, and other resources. 4.10.2 Types of Training 1) On-the-jobTraining: This type of training refers to the methods that are used to instruct workers while they are performing their regular duties at an organisation. The training method is straightforward and efficient in terms of cost. By utilising such a training strategy, it is possible for employees who are neither fully nor partially adept to receive adequate instruction. The personnel receive their training using real-world scenarios from the workplace. “Learning by doing” is the guiding principle behind this type of instruction. On-the-job training can take many forms, such as job rotation, mentoring, and even temporary promotions, among other things. 2) Off-the-job Training: Training that takes place in a setting that is not the employee’s normal place of employment Training that takes place in a setting that is not the employee’s normal place of employment is referred to as “off the job training.” It is typically utilised if new workers are hired. Workshops, seminars, conferences, and other similar events are all examples of non-work-related training opportunities. A system like this is expensive, but it is effective if and only if a big number of people need to be trained in a relatively short amount of time. Off the job training is also known as vestibule training, which means that the employees are trained in a different place (which could be a vestibule consisting of a corridor, an entrance, a reception room, or something else) where the actual working conditions are mimicked. 4.11 RETENTION AND DEVELOPMENT Practices that contribute to retention arise in every area of HR, and all positions within an organisation will need to collaborate with one another to develop and implement retention plans that incorporate multiple facets. It is possible that depending on the circumstances, both broad-based and focused techniques, or even a combination of the two, may be appropriate.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0116.html -->
102 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 4.11.1 Effective Practices in Retention It is possible that effective practices in a variety of areas can be especially potent in assisting an organisation in achieving its retention goals. These areas include the following: 1. Hiring: Practices regarding talent acquisition can have a significant impact on turnover, and a significant body of research indicatesthat one way to reduce turnover among newly hired employees is to give applicants an accurate preview of the work they will be doing as part of the recruitment process. 2. Possibilities of Socialising: New employees have a rather high rate of turnover. New employees have a greater chance of remaining with an organisation if they are exposed to socialisation practices, which are often given as part of an integrated on boarding and integration programme. These practices consist of shared as well as individualised learning experiences, official and informal events that assist individuals get to know one another, and the assignment of more experienced employees as role models for new employees in the company. 3. Training and development: Employees who are not provided with the opportunity to regularly upgrade their skill sets are more likely to leave a company that does not provide these possibilities. 4. Compensation and rewards: Even though pay levels and job satisfaction are only moderate predictors of whether an employee would leave an organisation, a business has three options available to it in order to retain its workforce: a) Establishing the company as the industry leader in terms of salary and awards. b) Using a person-based compensation structure to allow for the customization of rewardsto specific requirements. c) Making an obvious connection between retention and rewards (e.g., tying vacation hours to seniority, offering retention bonuses orstock optionsto longer-term employees, or linking defined benefit plan pay-outs to years of service). 5. Fair Supervision: Fair treatment by a supervisor is the single most critical factor in determining whether an employee will remain in their position, according to several studies. Because of this, a corporation can decide to concentrate on improving its supervisory and management practices as well as its communication abilities. 6. Employee involvement: Involved and engaged employees are content with their jobs, love their work and the organisation in which they are employed, believe that their job is significant, take pride in their company, and believe that their employer values the contributions that they make. According to the findings of one study, employees who were highly engaged in their work had a probability of quitting that was five times lower than employees who were not engaged in their work. 4.11.2 Strategies used in Retention and Development 1. Comprehensive Strategies: To address the overall retention rates, wide ranging tactics are directed either at the entire organisation or at majorsubsystems, and they can either target the entire organisation or big subsystems. Examples of this include implementing changes to the hiring process that incorporate criteria linked to employee retention, enhancing the quality of the working environment, and increasing base salaries based on market rates. Typically, there are three sources where a corporation can obtain the data required to assist it in selecting which comprehensive strategy to put into action: • Research on employee retention can give useful insights on the major factors that contribute to employee turnover. Access to the most recent findings from research on employee turnover and


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0117.html -->
STAFFING | 103 retention can be gained through participation at conferences and membership in professional associations like SHRM. • The term “effective practices” refers to the techniques that other organisations are implementing and determining to be either effective or ineffective. • Benchmarking surveys can provide information on how a company compares to competitors on areas such as wages, perks, bonus schemes, and other similar topics. 2. Strategies Directed at Retention Data from numerous important sources, such as organisational exit interviews, post-leave interviews, stay interviews, employee focus groups, predicted turnover studies, and other qualitative studies are used as the foundation for targeted tactics. An organisation can use this information to determine more precisely where a problem occurs and to design plans that are highly relevant and linked to address the issue by following the steps outlined in this article. For instance, if a significant number of female professionals are leaving the organisation, the organisation could conduct research on the most common reasons that women give for leaving a company and develop strategies to specifically deal with this group of workers. This would be the case if female professionals are leaving in significant numbers. When it comesto the effective and efficient administration of an employee retention strategy, people managers play a crucial role. It is essential to the success of the programme to have a management team that is informed about employee motivation, retention methods, benchmarking, and best practices. 4.11.3 Implementation When it comes to the effective and efficient administration of an employee retention strategy, people managers play a crucial role. It is essential to the success of the programme to have a management team that is informed about employee motivation, retention methods, benchmarking, and best practices. 1. Laying the groundwork The information that an organisation requires to evaluate the magnitude of its problem and to assist in shaping the retention measures that are executed as a reaction can be obtained if the following actions are followed together to give the desired results: • Determining whether employee turnover is a problem: You can accomplish this step by doing a turnover analysis, benchmarking, and a needs assessment (both external and internal). • Creating a plan to improve retention: After analysing the turnover analysis, benchmarking data, and needs assessment, an action plan for retention of employees should be prepared. This step involves determining whether broad-based or tailored techniques should be implemented (or whether a combination of both should be used). • Carrying out a plan for customer retention: This includes putting into action the techniques that have been determined to be suitable for the particular issue. • Examining the results: After putting the strategy into action, you should evaluate the results to determine the extent of the impact in comparison to the expense. 2. Benchmarking When getting ready to put in place a strategy for retaining employees, the first step that should not be skipped is the establishment of appropriate benchmarks, both external and internal. 3. Comparative analysis with other organisations: Is a turnover rate of 15% per year too high? It is not possible to provide a satisfactory response to this question in isolation. When trying to determine


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0118.html -->
104 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT whether employee turnover is a problem for a business, benchmarking and needs assessments can provide valuable information that can help with the determination. The process of comparing a company’s turnover rates to those of its industry as well as its competitors is known as external benchmarking. These numbers show annual and monthly quit rates expressed as a percentage of total employment for all non-farm employment in the United States. The data are split down by industry, geographic region, sector, and other categories as well. 4. Internal benchmarking: With this type of benchmarking, a company monitors its employee turnover rate over the course of a given period of time. Indicative of the existence of a potential issue is an upward trend in the rate, whether it be generally or within certain demographics. 4.11.4 Dealing with Common Problems Programs designed to keep current employees on board come with their fair share of the usual challenges encountered in strategic endeavours. These are the following: 1. Absence of support from senior management: It is doubtful that supervisory staff will concentrate on issues relating to people if top leadership does not convey to managers and supervisors the message that employees are essential to the long-term survival of the organisation, and if this message is not communicated, If senior management does not take an active role in the process of employee retention and does not accept primary responsibility for it, managers and employees will continue to be in the dark regarding the genuine value that employees bring to senior management as well as the organisation as a whole. If senior management does not take an active role in the process of employee retention and does not accept primary responsibility for it, managers and employees will continue to be in the dark regarding the genuine value that employees bring to senior management as well as the organisation as a whole. 2. The impression that participating in the programme entails a lot of pointless “busywork”: It is possible that managers may dismiss efforts to improve employee satisfaction as “nice” or “simply busywork” if the company as a whole isn’t committed to the initiative and doesn’t have a clear grasp of how it contributes to the organisation’s long-term success. 4.12 KNOWLEDGE AND LEARNING MANAGEMENT Knowledge Management is such an essential concept, that the term has even been given its own acronym, which is KM for short. The processes of generating, exchanging, utilising, and managing information and knowledge inside an organisation are collectively referred to as “knowledge management.” 4.12.1 Benefits of Knowledge Management There are a variety of unique reasons why companies would like to employ the most effective strategies for knowledge management within their organisation. According to various KM theories, some of these causes include the following: • Making knowledge accessible in order to support the development of products and services that are more innovative • Shorter development cycles • Managing creative endeavours and educational growth • Getting the most out of the experience and knowledge of staff members


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0119.html -->
STAFFING | 105 • Facilitating employees’ access to pertinent concepts that are necessary for them to perform their jobs in a satisfactory manner • Problem Solving • Management of both physical assets and intellectual property Additionally, there is a connection between knowledge management and various technologies. Some examples of these technologies are as follows: • Software designed for group work that includes features such as threaded conversations and the ability to share documents • Workflow management systems • Content management systems • Software for organising and arranging schedules • Software for electronic forms of communication E-learning, also known as learning management system, is what paves the way for a solid foundation of knowledge management. This is because these systems enable businesses to develop individualised education and training programmes. A good many of the technologies described above can communicate with one another. There is a model of KM that has been formally specified, and it consists of four different parts. These aspects are referred to as socialisation, combination, externalisation, and internalisation, respectively. This paradigm, which comes under the heading of the SECI Model for Knowledge Management, was established by Nonaka and Takeuchi. 4.12.2 Learning Management Systems and the SECI Model • Socialisation: The initial component of knowledge management is known as socialising. The use of a learning management system or eLearning in conjunction with socialisation is highly beneficial. True knowledge management requiresthat there be a constant flow of information and knowledge amongst employees, and this flow should not be restricted in any way. The utilisation of chat rooms and the opportunity for employees to communicate with one another while they are studying are two features that are made possible, thankfully, by learning management systems (LMS). • Externalisation: Within the context of the business theory of knowledge management, we may observe several distinct manifestations of the concept of externalisation taking place. One approach is to connect the material covered in online learning courses to events that are taking place in actual workplaces. For instance, workers can participate in training and then be directly asked to apply what they’ve learned to their jobs outside of the context of a virtual learning environment. Testing the employees’ expertise is yet another method for integrating externalisation into the process. Then, based on how well individuals do their jobs, externalisation can be utilised as a method to develop new forms of training and learning. • Combination: The combination component of the knowledge management theory has many potential applications in the world of business. These applications can be carried out in a variety of different ways. One approach to achieving this goal is to make use, in the process of regularly updating and streamlining training, of both the feedback of employees and the input of subject matter experts.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0120.html -->
106 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT • Internalisation: The ultimate purpose of utilising a learning management system is for employees to internalise the information that they have gained. They should be taught essential skills in a manner that enables them to immediately put those skills to use in their work. Some other methods to be kept in mind while using a learning management system to implement knowledge management include: • Determining the success of training that was delivered using a learning management system by using metrics to measure that success. When it is possible, we should use numbers that are specific and concrete. • The participation of managers is one of the most important aspects of a training and development strategy that is based on the principles of knowledge management. In the end, it is going to be the managers who are going to be responsible for determining whether employees are successfully meeting key performance metrics. • The fact that managers are not entirely clear how to get the most out of a learning management system is one of the most significant issues that arise in the context of knowledge management and learning management systems. Managers and other leaders in an organisation need to have a solid understanding of how to maximise the benefits gained from the implementation of a learning management system. • If you get the impression that your company is not putting the principles of knowledge management into practice in an efficient and measurable manner, you may wish to examine the training content to determine whether it is applicable and practical. 4.13 PERFORMANCE APPRAISAL An employee’s work and contributions to the organisation are evaluated on a regular basis as part of a process called a performance evaluation. Companies conduct performance reviews to ascertain which individuals have made the most significant contributions to the expansion of the firm, evaluate the level of progress made, and recognise the efforts of high-achieving personnel. The phrase “performance appraisal” refers to the periodic examination of an employee’s contribution to a company in terms of both job performance and overall contribution. An employee’s abilities, achievements, and growth—or lack thereof, depending on the results—are analysed during a performance assessment, which is also known as annual review, performance review or evaluation, or employee appraisal. Performance reviews are used by businesses to provide employees with comprehensive feedback on their work, as well as to justify salary raises and incentives, as well as choices regarding employment termination. They can be carried out at any time, but annually, semi-annually, or quarterly are the most common intervals for doing so. 4.13.1 How Performance Appraisals Work Employees can advance their careers using performance reviews, which are often developed by the departments of Human Resources (HR). They offer feedback to employees regarding the performance of their work responsibilities. It ensures that employees are managing and fulfilling the goals that are required of them, and it provides them with direction on how to accomplish those goals if they fall short of meeting them. Performance reviews assist in the decision-making process regarding the distribution of financial resources within an organisation. This is necessary since most businesses only have a finite amount


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0121.html -->
STAFFING | 107 of money available from which to pay out incentives like pay raises and bonuses. They give a method for businesses to discover which individuals have contributed the most to the growth of the firm. This enables the business to appropriately reward the employees who have demonstrated the highest levels of performance. Appraisals of an employee’s performance not only help employees and their managers create a plan for employee development that may include additional training and increased responsibilities, but they also help employees, and their managers identify ways in which an employee may improve and advance in their career. In an ideal situation, the performance review should not be the only moment during the course of the year when managers and employees discuss about the contributions made by the individual. Regular communication helps to ensure that everyone is on the same page, fosters the development of stronger connections between employees and managers, and reduces the stress associated with annual reviews. 4.13.2 Types of Performance Appraisals Most performance reviews are conducted from the top down, which means that managers evaluate their employees without receiving any feedback from those being evaluated. However, there are also several other types: • Self-evaluation is when individual employees evaluate their own behaviour and performance on the job. • Peer assessment is a rating of an individual’s performance given by their workgroup or other co-workers. • 360-degree feedback assessment incorporates comments from the participant, as well as those of their supervisor and peers. • Negotiated appraisal is a more recent trend that involves a mediator and aims to minimise the adversarial aspect of performance reviews by allowing the subject to present first. This is done to moderate the impact of performance evaluations on employees. Also places an emphasis on the positive aspects of the individual before offering any constructive criticism. When there are disagreements between subordinates and supervisors, this structure is often helpful in mediating those disagreements. 4.13.3 Objectives of Performance Appraisals It is common practice to refer to the primary purpose of the employee performance appraisal as a comparison of the actual performance of the employee to the standard performance that has been established by the organisation. Nevertheless, the employee performance appraisal system accomplishes several additional significant goals, some of which are listed below for your perusal: • The performance appraisal system is responsible for maintaining the records that are needed to establish the employee’s compensation and the structure of their salary structure. • It is quite helpful to discover the strengths and limitations of the employees, as this assists in assigning the appropriate individual to the appropriate position. • It gives one access to the potential that already exists inside them for future expansion and development. • It provides the essential feedback regarding the performance of the employee as well as their genuine contribution to the development of the organisation.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0122.html -->
108 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT • It provides the HR department with direction regarding the training requirements of the staff members. • It shapes the employees’ approach to their work and instils a sense of responsibility for their work. 4.13.4 Advantages and Disadvantages of Performance Appraisal The employee performance appraisal system is an essential component of the HR functions, and it is put to effective use on a consistent basis for the advantage of the company. However, each method has its own advantages as well as its own disadvantages. The following is a list of positives and downsides associated with performance reviews. Advantages: • It makes it easier for managers to determine which staff member deserve promotions and which should be let go because of their lack of productivity. • It is useful information for the company to consider when determining the employee’s salary. The records of the employee’s performance review can also be used to make decisions regarding additional perks and allowances, and these decisions can be based not only on the employee’s performance but also on the employee’s additional efforts. • Particular measures can be adopted in order to foster the growth of the staff members. The employee’s areas of weakness will be brought to light through the performance review process, providing the organisation with the information it needs to design and implement an appropriate training programme. • The performance review also makes recommendations regarding the improvements that should be made to the selection process so that better staff may be hired. • An employee’s performance can be effectively communicated to management through the use of a performance review as it is an efficient method. It is a method for providing feedback to employees regarding how well they are performing their job responsibilities. • The feedback received on one’s performance might serve as a source of inspiration and drive. It paints a picture of the employee’s efficiency and inspires the individual to better their own performance in the workplace.
