Disadvantages: • The elements that are used to evaluate an employee’s performance have a direct and decisive impact on the performance appraisal. The failure of performance evaluation can be caused by the application of elements that are inaccurate or inappropriate to the situation. • When evaluating someone’s performance, it’s possible to gloss over important aspects that are ultimately accountable for that performance. • Aspects like as one’s attitude, capabilities, and level of initiative are exceedingly nebulous and challenging to evaluate. • There are times when the managers who carry out the performance appraisal do not have the necessary qualifications to make an accurate evaluation of the employees’ capabilities. As a result, it results in the acquisition of irrelevant data and the failure of performance evaluation. 4.13.5 Performance Appraisal and Career Strategy The practice of evaluating an individual’s performance on the job in order to arrive at objective conclusions regarding human resource management is known as performance appraisal. In this process, the performance of an employee on the job is formally evaluated, feedback is provided to the employee, and a conclusion is made regarding whether or not and how the performance may be improved. The process of evaluating someone’s performance might take place in either a formal or an informal setting.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0123.html -->
STAFFING | 109 A manager may provide an employee with an informal assessment by commenting on how well or how poorly a specific job was performed either while the employee is performing the job or soon after the employee has completed the job. It is carried out on a daily basis in its entirety. On a regular schedule, formal evaluations are carried out either year or twice yearly. It is used primarily for the following four purposes: 1) to inform employees how their current performance is being formally rated; 2) to determine which employees are eligible for merit raises; 3) to determine which employees need additional training; and 4) to determine which employees should be considered for promotion. Formal appraisal can be done using one of these four primary methods: 1) the evaluation of subordinates by a superior; 2) the evaluation of subordinates by a group of superiors; 3) the evaluation of a colleague by a group of peers; and 4) the evaluation of bosses by subordinates. All feedback control methods are embodied in an evaluation of the employee’s performance appraisal system. Information regarding employees, their performance on the job, and the outcomes of the task can be obtained by managers through the use of this system. The quality of the control measures that are utilised, such as defining standards, gathering information, and taking remedial action, is directly related to the efficiency of an evaluation of performance. Because performance is measured across multiple dimensions, the methods used to evaluate it must take into account the many facets of a given work. The evaluation of performance can either focus on the behaviours of the employee or the results of their work. Graphic rating scales and behaviorally anchored rating scales are two major evaluation methods that fall under the genre of behavior-oriented assessment methods. Both the manager’s performance in achieving goals and plans, as well as his performance in his role as a manager, should be evaluated as part of the performance review. The evaluation of a manager’s effectiveness as a manager ought to be an additional component of the system that compares performance to objectively measurable goals. When evaluating a person’s performance, it is necessary to take into account both their strong points and their problematic areas. The process of identifying one’s interests and skills serves as the basis for developing a professional strategy. There are a few steps involved in developing a plan for one’s professional life. These include the construction of a personal profile, the formulation of long-range personal and professional goals, an analysis of the risks and opportunities posed by the environment, the identification of personal strengths and weaknesses, the development of strategic career alternatives, consistency testing and strategic choice, the development of short-range career objectives and action plans, the development of contingency plans, the implementation of career plans, and the monitoring of progress. 4.13.6 Methods of Performance Appraisal Numerous methods have been devised to measure the quantity and quality of performance. Each of the methods is effective in a relative manner for various organisations. Broadly, all methods of appraisals can be divided into two different categories.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0124.html -->
110 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT • Past-Oriented Methods • Future-Oriented Methods (A) Past-Oriented Methods 1. Rating Scales: Rating scales consists of several numerical scales representing job related performance criterions such as dependability, initiative, output, attendance, attitude, etc. Each scale ranges from excellent to poor. The total numerical scores are computed and final conclusions are derived. Advantages – Adaptability, easy to use, low cost, every type of job can be evaluated, large number of employees covered, no formal training required. Disadvantages – Rater’s biases. 2. Trait Analysis: Under this method, checklist of statements of traits of employee in the form of Yes or No based questions is prepared. Here the rater only does the reporting or checking and HR department does the actual evaluation. Advantages – economy, ease of administration, limited training required, standardization. Disadvantages – Raters biases, use of improper weights by HR, does not allow rater to give relative ratings. 3. Forced Choice Method: The series of statements arranged in the blocks of two or more are given and the rater indicates which statement is true or false. The rater is forced to make a choice. HR department does actual assessment. Advantages – Absence of personal biases because of forced choice. Disadvantages – Statements may be wrongly framed. 4. Forced Distribution Method: Here employees are clustered around a high point on a rating scale. Rater is compelled to distribute the employees on all points on the scale. It is assumed that the performance is conformed to normal distribution. Assumption of normal distribution, unrealistic, errors of central tendency may occur. 5. Critical Incidents Method: The approach is focused on certain critical behaviours of employees that makes all the difference in the performance. Supervisors as and when they occur record such incidents. Advantages – Evaluations are based on actual job behaviours, ratings are supported by descriptions, feedback is easy, reduces recent biases, chances of subordinate improvement are high. Disadvantages – Negative incidents can be prioritized, forgetting the positive ones, overly close supervision. 6. Confidential Records: Mostly used by government departments, and in older organisations where the concept of self-assessment is not encouraged. Here the report is given in the form of Annual Confidential Report (ACR) and may record ratings with respect to following items; attendance, self-expression, team work, leadership, initiative, technical ability, reasoning ability, originality and resourcefulness, etc. The system is highly secretive and confidential. Feedback to the assessee is given only in case of an adverse entry. Rather negative in impact as the ratings are highly subjective and can be manipulated to suit an occasion. 7. Pen portrait: The assessor pictures in writing about the assessee, regarding his qualities and performance as well as his potential. Armies use this method in evaluating cadets. (B) Future-Oriented Methods (i) Management by Objectives: A concept popular till recently and introduced by the management Guru, Peter Drucker, where performance is rated against the achievement of objectives stated by the management. MBO process goes as under.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0125.html -->
STAFFING | 111 • Establish goals and desired outcomes for each subordinate in a conference between the management and the concerned subordinate. • Set performance standards. • Assess performance achieved against goals set for the employee through frequent performance review meetings between the manager and the subordinate. • Identify reasons for shortfall and give feed-back for improvement. • Establish new goals and new strategies for the coming year. (ii) Assessment Centre Approach Method: Under this method, many evaluators join together to judge employee performance in several situations with the use of a variety of criteria. It is used mostly to help select employees for the first level (the lowest) supervisory positions. Assessments are made to determine employee potential for the purposes of promotion. The assessment is generally done with the help of a couple of employees and involves a paper-and-pencil test, interviews and situational exercises. Some of the other features of this system are: (i) The use of situational exercises (such as an in-basket exercise, management games, role-playing, critical incident and leaderless group discussion, etc.), (ii) Evaluators are drawn from experienced managers with proven ability at different levels of management, (iii) They evaluate all employees, both individually and collectively and each candidate is given one of the four categories: more than acceptable, less than acceptable and unacceptable, (iv) A summary report is prepared by the members and a feedback on a face-to-face basis is administered to all the candidates who ask for it. Assessment Centre Approach Procedure: First, a leadership group is established, each member supporting a predefined position, but the group must arrive at consensus. Then a task force is used with an appointed leader, who decides on a course of action. Simulation games and in-basket exercises are used to test organisational and planning abilities. Oral report is made by the candidate, which tests the communication skills and insights into the present position. Personal interviews and projective tests are used to assess work motivation, career orientation and dependence on others. Paper and pencil tests measure intellectual ability. To make Assessment Centre Programme successful, it is necessary that heavy emphasis is placed on clear statement of goals, the obtaining of top management commitment, job analysis, assessor training and programme audit and evaluation. (iii) Behaviourally Anchored Rating Scales (BARS) This is a modern appraisal technique which supports the claim that it provides better, more equitable appraisals as compared to other techniques. The procedure for BARS is usually five stepped as under: • Generate Critical Incidents: Persons with knowledge of the job to be appraised (job holders/ supervisors) are asked to describe specific illustrations(critical incidents) of effective and ineffective performance behaviour. • Develop Performance Dimensions: The people then divide the incidents into smaller set (say 5 or 10) of performance dimensions. Each cluster is then defined clearly. • Reallocate Incidents: These critical incidents are then reallocated to any group of people who also know the job. They are given the cluster’s definitions and asked to redesign each incident to the dimension it best describes. Typically, a critical incident is retained if some percentage (generally 50 to 70%) of this group assigns it to the same cluster as the previous group did.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0126.html -->
112 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT • Scale of Incidents: This second group is generally asked to rate on a given scale, the behaviour described in the incident as to how effectively or ineffectively it represents performance on the appropriate dimension. • Develop Final Instrument: A subset of incidents (usually 6 or 7 per cluster) are used as ‘Behaviour anchors’ for the performance dimensions. The BARS technique has advantages like – More accurate judgment, establishes clear standards, provides good feedback to the people being appraised, make the dimensions more independent of each other and it provides independence to the rater. 4.13.7 Key Result Areas (KRAs) In the context of Performance Management process, the role of Key Result/Responsibility Areas (KRAs) is very important. Key Result Areas are set of activities derived out of short term objectives. KRAs refer to general areas of outcomes or outputs for which the department’s role is responsible. The Individual Performance and Development Plan has two component parts, the Performance Plan and its related Individual Development Plan. The Performance Plan is constructed by the manager and employee together, focusing on priority-setting for the performance management cycle and, working co-operatively through a four-step process: 1. Agreeing upon Key Results Areas 2. Agreeing upon Performance Objectives 3. Agreeing upon Key Performance Indicators and their associated Performance Targets 4. Agreeing upon Action Plans KRAs refer to general areas of outcomes or outputs for which the sector/department is responsible. 4.14 HUMAN RESOURCE DEVELOPMENT The purpose of human resource development is to enhance the efficiency of individuals, groups, and organisations through the coordinated implementation of various training, organisation, and career advancement initiatives. Through carefully orchestrated learning experiences, HRD helps individuals in companies acquire the fundamental skills necessary to accomplish their current responsibilities as well as those that will be created in the future. The training of an individual after he or she has been hired, the provision of opportunities to learn new skills, the distribution of resources that are beneficial for the employee’s tasks, and any other activities that contribute to the employee’s growth are all included in human resource development. It is essential for both the onboarding of new employees and the continuation of their employment that the HR department follow certain procedures. Employees cannot achieve their goals without the appropriate training. Employees will become stale and unmotivated if they are not given opportunities to gain new skills and advance their career and personal development. 4.14.1 Benefits of Human Resource Development The development of an organization’s human resources is generally seen as the most important factor in achieving higher levels of profitability, greater productivity, and improved relations. Appropriate human resource development confers an infinite number of benefits. The following are some of the most important advantages:


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0127.html -->
STAFFING | 113 • Human Resource Development, sometimes known as HRD, helps people become more capable. The workforce receives new skills, knowledge, and attitudes as a result of HRD’s efforts. • People are able to become more engaged to their jobs when an adequate HRD programme is implemented. It is necessary to have a reliable performance appraisal system in order to evaluate individuals according to their level of achievement. • With the assistance of human resource development, a culture of trust and respect can be established in a given setting. • With the assistance of HRD, an accepting attitude toward change can be developed. The employees discovered that they had improved capabilities in terms of being able to solve problems. • It contributes to the overall improvement of the employees’ progress. The organisation’s sense of teamwork is also boosted by HRD’s efforts. • Additionally, it assists in the development of a “efficiency culture” within the firm. It results in an increase in the efficiency of the organisation. Both the utilisation of the available resources and the accomplishment of the set objectives are improved. • It encourages greater participation from staff members. When they do a good job, workers have a sense of pride and a sense of having accomplished something. • It also helps to collect data on employee programmes and policies that is helpful and objective, which further facilitates better planning for human resources. 4.14.2 Types of Human Resource Development The process of developing an organization’s human resources typically starts as soon as an employee is hired and continues for as long as that employee is employed by the organisation. HRD can take many forms, such as on-the-job training or work shadowing, classroom education or education obtained online, opportunities for professional development and growth, and training to ensure compliance with laws and regulations. i. Learning the facets of a work while actually performing the tasks associated with that employment is what is meant by “on-the-job training.” On-the-job training may be required for a worker even though they are familiar with the fundamental requirements of their position. This training may cover specifics such as which forms to use, where materials are housed, and how to access the computer systems. ii. Another related method is “work shadowing”, in which an employee watches another employee perform the duties of their job in order to acquire the necessary abilities. Another type of growth is intellectual or professional growth, which can take the form of attending classes at a university or certification programmes, as well as job-specific trainings and seminars that focus on how to perform one’s job more effectively. Asignificant amount of capital is put out by a variety of businessesin the form ofsubstantial investments in the form of training and development opportunities for their staff members. Even though a significant portion of this training is now offered virtually, for example, through webinars and online courses due to the proliferation of online learning, it is still very common practice to conduct trainings in person or to attend training seminars or conferences with other professionals in the field. 4.14.3 Managing Different Generations The human repercussions of these stereotypes include misunderstandings between millennials and their bosses, which results in people viewing each other differently than they actually are. These


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0128.html -->
114 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT misunderstandings can be avoided. The economic repercussions include millennials leaving their jobs out of a sense of frustration at being misunderstood. By looking beyond the labels and making an effort to better grasp the situation, managers can sidestep this trap. The top five best practicesfor working well with employees of varying agesin the workplace are as under: 1) Do not make any assumptions: Do not make any assumptions: It’s been said that millennials are adept with technology. Employers can count on baby boomers to be devoted employees. Gen Xers are cynical. Traditionalists are known for their thriftiness. Maybe if you were retired, you would also be doing the same thing. One person cannot be responsible for starting a trend! Even though members of the baby boomer generation, may have a statistically greater tendency to remain in their positions for longer periods of time than millennials, this does not mean that your baby boomer employee is not a flight risk. It is not necessary to assume that every millennial you know fits this basic profile simply because they are a member of this generation. Let us look at an example. A millennial works in the technology industry. On paper, it is reasonable to assume that the millennial possesses a certain level of technical prowess because of the work that he or she does. However, if the person does not use Facebook or Instagram, people will assume that he or she is technically incapable. In a nutshell: Never make any assumptions. 2) Avoid using labels: Even if your intentions are good, using generational designations puts you at danger of alienating co-workers. This is true even if you have good intentions. For instance, Mr. X is employed by a technology start-up that only recently filled a position in the marketing department with a millennial. Mr. X, in the course of extending a warm welcome to the newest member of the team, stated that due to the fact that the new employee is a millennial, she may bring a fresh viewpoint to the work that the team is currently performing. He meant to offer praise to the new employee, but inadvertently insulted a generation X marketer who worked for the company and who afterwards lodged a formal complaint. With his action Mr. X hinted that past generations were not as innovative as millennials by making the statement that millennials are innovative. It would not have been exclusive if he had merely eliminated the generational designations from the equation before making the statement. 3) Ensuring regular review of your own biases: Given how pervasively generational stereotypes are used, we may probably be excused for falling for the hype. Nevertheless, overcoming preconceived notions is not as easy as one might think it would be. Even if we are aware of our generational biases, we may still have the tendency to pass judgement on others around us in an unconscious manner. For instance, a baby boomer working in a Public Sector Undertaking, complained to HR about a young co-worker who constantly had headphones in while they were at work. He characterised the co-worker as a millennial who “always needed to be connected outside of work and didn’t know how to converse socially face-to-face.” The actual facts however were different as the millennial stated that he finds that listening to classical music helps him concentrate on his work. So in this case, the more experienced worker has to understand that he may have been applying his own unconscious prejudice to his colleague, despite the fact that he did not completely understand him.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0129.html -->
STAFFING | 115 4) Focus on the people around you rather than the trends: Once you understand how inaccurate generational stereotypes are, you will start to become aware of how much content there is on the internet pertaining to this topic. It is a waste of time to read articles with headings like “How to Manage Millennials” and “What Millennials REALLY Want,” because these articles continue to reinforce broad assumptions. Ignore those people. Please hold off on clicking. Stay away from other people. Refrain from giving in to the desire to buy books or sign up for seminars that exaggerate generational stereotypes in the workplace. Also, resist giving in to the urge to buy publications that highlight distinctions between generations in the workplace. People’s motivation to write about it will decrease in direct proportion to our collective lack of interest in the topic. Instead, communicate with the other people on your team and your co-workers. Ask them how to handle themselves if you want to know how to handle them. Talking to them about it is the best way to find out what it is that they truly want. When it comes to trying to understand the people in your life, there is no replacement for just asking them questions. 5) Acknowledge and celebrate the differences that exist throughout each generation: The generation gaps are a hard reality and need to be acknowledged and embraced gracefully. 4.14.4 Managing Diversity The workforce is becoming increasingly diverse in terms of generations represented. The workforce is growing increasingly diverse in terms of age, with members of generations ranging from the seasoned Baby Boomers who have years of expertise to the fresh-faced Generation Z members who are always connected. Companies routinely engage employees from as many as four different generations in today’s world, and it should come as no surprise that each generation has its own distinct mannerisms, requirements, objectives, and characteristics that should be taken into consideration by employers. Employing a staff that spans many generations comes with a number of advantages, despite the fact that managing a workforce that covers such a wide range of ages presents its fair share of obstacles. Advantages of generation diversity: a. Problem-solving: Having a multigenerational workforce can be quite advantageous when it comes to identifying potential solutions and new methods of handling day-to-day concerns. This is due to the fact that each generation has a different approach to problem-solving, and as a result, each generation has a different perspective on how problems should be solved. b. Understanding diverse audiences: Each generation has its own distinct characteristics, which can be a real advantage to your company. Employing people that belong to each of these generations allows you to gain a deeper understanding of the various demographic groups that you are aiming to communicate with. c. Occasions for educational growth: Learning opportunities are presented to all employees, not just the younger workers, when there is generational diversity in the workplace. Employees of different generations might teach one another new ways of thinking about things and ways of conducting business that are more effective. For instance, a worker who is more proficient with technology may be aware of a quicker way for a Baby Boomer to handle a laborious physical activity at their place of employment.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0130.html -->
116 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT d. Mentorship: An atmosphere with employees of varying ages is ideal for fostering mentoring relationships. Many businesses make the decision to launch a mentorship programme in order to give their staff members the chance to learn from one another. Employees not only benefit from the acquisition of new knowledge and abilities as a result of this, but also see improvements in the way they collaborate with one another. 4.14.5 Healthy Industrial Relations We can define industrial relations as the relations and interactions that take place within an industry, particularly between the labour and management, as a direct result of their combined attitudes and approaches with regard to the management of the affairs of the industry, for the purpose of improving relations between the management and the workers, and also improve the industrial environment and the economy as a whole. The term “industrial relations” also refers to the interaction that exists between workers and management, which can either directly or indirectly be traced back to the relationship between unions and employers. Industrial relations has become one of the most delicate and complex problems of modern industrial society. Industrial progress is impossible without cooperation of labours and harmonious relationships. Therefore, it is in the interest of all to create and maintain good relations between employees (labour) and employers (management). Concept of Industrial Relations The term ‘Industrial Relations’ comprises of two terms: ‘Industry’ and ‘Relations’. “Industry” refers to “any productive activity in which an individual (or a group of individuals) is (are) engaged”. By “relations” we mean “the relationships that exist within the industry between the employer and his workmen.” The term industrial relations explains the relationship between employees and management which stems directly or indirectly from union-employer relationship. Industrial relations are the relationships between employees and employers within the organizational settings. The field of industrial relations looks at the relationship between management and workers, particularly groups of workers represented by a union. Industrial relations are basically the interactions between employers, employees and the government, and the institutions and associations through which such interactions are mediated. To summarise, the management and the trade union both need to approach the building of good industrial relations with a positive attitude if they want to see it succeed. Healthy industrial relations require a number of important characteristics to be present, including mutual respect, understanding, goodwill, and acknowledgement of dignity. When there is widespread growth and wealth, there is typically harmony in industrial relations. During times of economic prosperity, there is a general uptick in employment levels, salary growth, and a general improvement in worker happiness. However, when a recession is present, there is a decrease in salaries as well as a fall in the employment level. These unfavourable economic conditions poison otherwise positive labour relations. As a result, business cycles are also a factor in whether or not there are strong industrial relations. The following are some of the areas that fall under the purview of industrial relations: a) The relationship between employees and their superiors or supervisors, as well as the relationship amongst employees themselves. b) Collective ties between various labour unions and the management of an organisation. The term for this dynamic is “union-management relations.” c) Collective relations among employers’ associations, labour unions, and the government.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0131.html -->
STAFFING | 117 4.14.6 Management of Change Managing change means managing the conversation between the people leading the change effort and those who are expected to implement the new strategies, managing the organizational context in which change can occur, and managing the emotional connections that are essential for any transformation. Management of change involves managing the interaction between the people who are leading the change effort and those who are expected to implement the new strategies. It also involves managing the organisational context in which changes can take place and managing the emotional bonds that are essential for any transition. Theories from a wide range of fields, such as psychology, behavioural science, engineering, and systems thinking, are included into change management. In addition, there is a diverse selection of models from which to select. For instance, Lewin’s Change Management Model divides the process of change into three main stagesthat are known as “unfreeze-change-refreeze,” whereas Kotter’s 8-Step Change Model offers a more thorough guide through the process of change. The notion that change never occurs in a vacuum is fundamental to every single theory put forward to explain how to control it. The entirety of the organisation, including each and every member of it, will be affected in some way, shape, or form by the change. However, if you have effective change management, you will be able to persuade everyone to adjust to and accept your new method of operation. The following points and strategies can help for better management of change within a workplace. 1) Gaining an Understanding of the Change Process: There is no such thing as a cookie-cutter approach to transformation. On the other hand, the vast majority of those that are successful adhere to the processes that make up the change management process. Each and every change process has a starting point, denoted by “Point A,” as well as a destination point (Point B). The change process consists of everything that takes place between the two points mentioned above. It entails a number of processes, most of which are categorised as belonging to one of three stages: preparation, execution, and subsequent action. The following are the events that take place throughout each phase: • Preparation: It is the responsibility of the change manager to get both the organisation and its workers ready for the transition. In order to do this, it is necessary to explain to the staff members the long-term goal that must be accomplished as well as the strategy that will be used to get there. • Implementation: It is the responsibility of the change manager to ensure that changes are carried out in a manner that is consistent with the long-term goals of the organisation. • Follow-through: It is the responsibility of the change manager to make certain that the change is sustainable and that it is incorporated into the culture and practices of the organisation. 2) Acquiring an Awareness of the Drivers of Change: In order for managers to successfully manage change, they must first understand why it is required. If you don’t do this, it will be impossible for you to design a strategy that addresses fundamental issues and pressing challenges like the following: • Which pressures are driving the shift that is occurring? • Are these pressures from within, such as the introduction of new leadership? • Are these pressures from the outside world, such as the creation of new technology, a shift in your business, or the appearance of a new competitor?


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0132.html -->
118 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 3) Formulating a Strategy: Once you have an understanding of the reason for the shift, the next step is to devise a plan. This plan should include a high-level overview of the reasons for change, describe the scope of the project, identify the main stakeholders, organise a team, and present a thorough roadmap of the tasks that will be required to finish the project. It is much simpler to explain the change to the members of your team when you already have a clear strategy in place, and it is also much simpler to track progress toward important milestones and targets when you already have a defined plan. 4) Clarity of Communication: Your ability to communicate in a way that is both clear and focused is one of the most effective weapons at your disposal when it comes to guiding both your company and your staff through a period of considerable change. In the end, you will need to be competent of explaining change to two audiences that couldn’t be more different from one another. The first category includes all of yourstaff members and other members of your team. These personnel need to understand not just why the change is necessary but also how their job responsibilities will be affected by the change. You also need to acknowledge the significance of listening to their issues so that you can alleviate any worries that they may have. 5) Be Ready to Overcome Obstacles: No matter how well prepared you are for the possibility of change, there is no guarantee that everything will go according to plan. You must ensure that you are prepared for a variety of possible outcomes. 4.14.7 Formulating Career Strategy The following is a rundown of the seven steps that make up the construction of the career strategy: 1. A career development plan (CDP) is a list of long and short-term goals that employees set for themselves in either their current role or pre-emptively for their future job. The first step is to do an in-depth analysis of your values, strengths, and motivators. Creating a plan for one’s professional life is analogous to erecting a structure. 2. The second step is to be aware of your advantages. 3. Conduct research into the many possibilities and make the most of the opportunities that present themselves is the third step. 4. Developing Expertise is the fourth step. 5. The fifth step is developing a network. 6. The sixth step entails Considering and evaluating your alternatives. 7. The seventh step is to taking action. 4.14.8 Stress Management The term “stress management” refers to a wide range of treatments and psychotherapies that are aimed at regulating the degree of stress experienced by a person, particularly the level of chronic stress, typically with the intention and the goal of enhancing day-to-day functioning. There are a wide variety of mental and physical symptoms that can be brought on by stress, and these symptoms can change depending on the person and the circumstances. These can involve a general deterioration in one’s physical condition in addition to feelings of depression. In today’s modern world, effective stress management is considered to be one of the most important factors in leading a happy and successful life. The demands of life are frequently numerous and can be tough to manage; however, there are a variety of techniques to deal with anxiety and preserve overall well-being that can be learned through stress management.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0133.html -->
STAFFING | 119 The levels of stress can be easily measured by utilising a variety of physiological tests that are quite similar to those that are used in polygraph examinations, despite the common misconception that stress is a personal experience. There are many different models of stress management, and each of these models offers a unique explanation of the mechanisms that can be used to control stress. A significant amount of additional research is required in order to gain a better understanding of which mechanisms truly operate and are efficient in practice. LET US SUM UP Companies and their human resources departments are open systems, and as such they are susceptible to the external environment in which they function. The workforce these days, is becoming increasingly diverse, and organisations have to strive for addressing employee concerns and to take the maximum out of their employees who come from different geographical regions, and have multifarious educational, cultural, and religious backgrounds. At the same time, there is also an increase in the number of employees who are expats. The process of finding and enticing people who might be interested in applying for jobsthat are currently open or will become available in the future is known as recruitment. When it comes to selecting a recruitment source, managers are constrained by a variety of factors, including the following: the image of the firm, the desirability of the job, internal policies, budgetary assistance, and government policies, among other things. Selection is the process of analysing an applicant’s qualifications, experience, skills, and knowledge and matching it with the requirements of the job in order to evaluate whether or not the applicant is suitable for the position being filled. The performance review procedure affords an opening for the implementation of organisational transformation. It makes the process of bringing about change in the organisation’s culture easier to accomplish. The organisation is able to transform into a learning organisation as a result of the interactive sessions that take place between the management and the employees, the mutual goal setting, and the efforts that are made towards the career development of the employees. It is easier for performance reviews to become an ongoing part of everyday practice when they are conducted on a frequent basis. Additionally, regular performance reviews encourage employees to take responsibility for their work and contribute to their professional growth. It is generally agreed upon that the growth of an organisation’s human resources is the single most essential aspect in terms of achieving improved profitability, greater levels of production, and improvements in industrial relations. The management of diversity has emerged as a topic of increasing significance in recent times. The growth of an organisation issignificantly impacted by the quality of the relationships within the industrialsector. It is necessary to manage the relationship between the people who are leading the change effort and those who are expected to implement the new tactics in order to effectively manage change. In addition to this, it entails the management of the organisational context within which changes might occur, as well as the management of the emotional attachments that are necessary for any shift. It has become very important to manage job related stress in modern times. Stress management refersto a wide variety of treatments and psychotherapies that are intended to regulate the level of stress experienced


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0134.html -->
120 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT by a person, particularly the level of chronic stress, often with the objective and goal of improving dayto-day functioning. KEYWORDS Human Resources, Job Satisfaction, Systems Approach, Training, Employee Retention, Benchmarking, Knowledge Management, Learning Management Systems, Performance Appraisal, Generation Diversity, Industrial Relations, Change Management, Communication, Stress Management CHECK YOUR PROGRESS 1. Which of the following does not qualify as an important aspect of staffing? (a) Developing talents in workers (b) Reasonable distribution of responsibilities among staff members (c) Contentment on the part of employees (d) Effective management of available human resources 2. Which test is most helpful in determining an individual’s potential to pick up new abilities quickly and easily?
