<!-- Page Start: text/part0203.html -->
U N I T 9 CAPITAL INVESTMENT DECISIONS STRUCTURE 9.0 Objectives 9.1 Introduction 9.2 Objective of Capital Investment Decisions 9.3 Estimation of Project Cash Flows 9.4 Social Cost Benefit Analysis Let Us Sum Up Keywords Check Your Progress Solutions


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0204.html -->
190 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 9.0 OBJECTIVES After studying this unit, you will have better understanding of: • Objective of capital investment decisions • Estimation of cash flows of a project • Estimation of project cost • Methods of Investment appraisal • Payback period • Accounting Rate of Return • Net Present Value • Internal Rate of Return • Modified Internal Rate of Return • Profitability Index • Social Cost Benefit Analysis 9.1 INTRODUCTION No entrepreneur will take a deep dive into any venture just because he orshe has money and risk appetite. No one is alone in any big or small project. Therefore, conviction of the team is essential. How much initial capital will be required is a starting point. The decision has to be a considered one and the process and approach has to be purely professional and scientific. Let us see what goes into this process. 9.2 OBJECTIVE OF CAPITAL INVESTMENT DECISIONS The goal of management is to increase the wealth of the shareholders as much as possible. In order to achieve this goal, the Finance Manager is responsible for analysing potential investment possibilities and identifying those that can boost the value of the company. Consider the following scenario: three companies, Company A, Company B, and Company C, all have the same assets and opportunities for investment. However, the management of Company A does not take advantage of its investment opportunities and instead distributes all of its earnings to its shareholders; the management of Company B only makes the investments necessary to replace deteriorating plant and equipment and distributes any leftover earnings to its shareholders; and the management of Company C invests in all of those possibilities that generate a return that is higher than what the shareholders could have received if they had invested the funds themselves. This allows them to earn a higher return. In the instance of Company A, the return on investment for the owners of the company will not be as beneficial as it would have been if the company had made use of more favourable investment prospects. Company A will gradually decrease in size to the point where it has no more assets since it will not invest in anything, not even the replacement of ageing plant and equipment. The management of Company B is passing up a number of opportunities to make valuable investments. This indicates that there will be missed chances and that the wealth of the stockholders will not be maximised. However, the management of Company C is making all investments that are successful and is therefore increasing the wealth of the owners. The expansion of Company C will continue so long asthere are lucrative investment opportunities and the company’s management is able to take advantage of those opportunities. The objective of the capital investment decision is to first assess the requirement and then think about the sources, the cost, the form and the time schedule.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0205.html -->
Capital Investment Decisions | 191 9.3 ESTIMATION OF PROJECT CASH FLOWS Cash requirement of any project can be safely analyzed in terms of long term, short term, owned, borrowed, fixed cost, working capital, inflows and outflows. All this can be appropriately included in a projected cash flow statement. The process requires the following; • Identify the elements of cash flow:Abusinesstypically will have three elements of cash flows: initial, operating and terminal. Initial Investment will be the capital expenditure and the contribution for net working capital to start the project. The operating one will comprise of the outflow and resulting inflow of the operations of the business. The terminal is the one what remains that is net inflow after paying off all the realizations of the assets on liquidation of the business when the economic life comes to an end. All the flows are post tax for the reasons that that element does not belong to the business or the owners. • How to estimate and basis of each such element: There are four principles to be kept in mind. The first is the separation principle which means we have to separate the investment side from finance side which simply means separating assets for servicing cost of it. Cost flows in to investment side and the interest, if any, flows in to financing side. Then there is incremental principle which means estimate separately the cost which will be incurred even if the project is not run from the cost which we incur while running it. We can also say it is fixed versus variable cost. While considering the cash flows, considering the post- tax cash flows always, is advisable since the tax payments cannot be ignored. This is the third principle. If you ignore, you will have to discount pretax flows with a discount rate which may or may not be reliable. The last principle is consistency principle. • Collate all the components of cash flow: All the components of the cash flow namely fixed capital, working capital, own capital (Equity), long term borrowing, outflow for operationssuch as purchases, production expenses, operating and administration expenses, selling expenses, interest cost, and inflow on account of sales and services are collated and net flow or the cash balance is found out to complete the cash flow statements. This is prepared for the period of the entire project period or for reasonably long enough period to reach a break even or achieve other objectives such as debt free status of closure or hive off etc. 9.3.1 Forecasting and its Relation to Regulation of Capital for Short, Medium and Long Term Periods The forecast will be guided by the following factors. • Requirements of the fixed assets like land, factory building, office premise, plant and equipment, electrical installations, tools,spares, furniture and fixtures, office equipment, computers and embedded or basic software. This will be under the head long term and requires long term funding. • The working capital will include raw materials, work in progress, stores, finished goods in stock, receivables, advances and cash balance to meet manufacturing, operating, marketing, administrative expenses. • The working capital will also be required to provide for business advances and gap between the statutory dues payment and collection and collection as well as payment of income tax. • The duration for which the working capital is required will depend upon the operating cycle of the concerned business. • While most of the long-term fundsrequirements will be met through equity and term financing, a part of the working capital is also required to be funded by the promoters as the margin and the balance, generally 75% can be sourced from banks. Term finance can be achieved through various means as discussed in the preceding chapter.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0206.html -->
192 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT • A part of the working capital can be sourced from the payables or sundry creditors • The regulations or the business practices many times play important part, as far as raising of the finance is concerned. Equity, preference shares, debentures can be raised as per the provisions of the Companies Act, 2013. The manner, the procedures and compliances prescribed need to be taken care of. The business norms with regard to debt equity ratio and rating are also to be kept in mind. 9.3.2 Relationship between Sales, Production and Other Functional Budgets As and when the project is set up, the business activities will start. Forecasting, therefore, has to include funds that will be required for running it and the funds that will flow in due to sales and services. These activities also need support of many other functions orservices which include accounts, finance, marketing, administration, HR, maintenance, public relations, legal and compliance, Information technology, research and developments, logistic, procurement and supply chain etc. No business can be run smoothly without provision of cost of such in-house services and functions. Expenses relating to all these departments are also required to be estimated so that the cash forecast for working capital is appropriately made. 9.3.3 Cash Forecasts Cash forecasts shall also include the inflow on account of sales and services. Forecasting sales will be an elaborate exercise and will take into account various factors such as estimated demand, production capacity planned, procurement or availability of raw materials, fixing price of the product, keeping in mind the competition and need to penetrate the market in the face of current market conditions and market leaders, availability or required engagement of working capital funds, discounts, incentives to marketing team, cost of sales campaigns, exhibitions, touring, advertisements etc. Statement showing the calculation of Cash Inflow After Tax (CFAT) Particulars (Rs.) (Rs.) Sales value xxx Less: Variable Cost xxx Contribution xxx Less: Fixed Cost (a) Fixed Cash Cost xxx (b) Depreciation xxx xxx Earning Before Tax (EBT) xxx Less: Tax xxx Earning After Tax (EAT) xxx Add: Depreciation xxx Cash Inflow After Tax (CFAT) xxx ILLUSTRATION 1 XYZ Ltd is evaluating the purchase of a new machinery with a depreciable base of Rs. 1,00,000; expected economic life of 4 years and change in earnings before taxes and depreciation of Rs. 45,000 in 2021,


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0207.html -->
Capital Investment Decisions | 193 Rs. 30,000 in year 2022, Rs. 25,000 in year 2023 and Rs. 35,000 in year 2024. Assume straight-line depreciation and a 20% tax rate. You are required to compute relevant cash flows. SOLUTION: Depreciation = 1,00,000 divided by 4 = Rs. 25,000 per year Amount in (Rs.) Years 2021 2022 2023 2024 Earnings before tax and depreciation 45,000 30,000 25,000 35,000 Less: Depreciation (25,000) (25,000) (25,000) (25,000) Earnings before tax 20,000 5,000 0 10,000 Less: Tax @20% (4,000) (1,000) 0 (2,000) Earnings after tax 16,000 4,000 0 8,000 Add: Depreciation 25,000 25,000 25,000 25,000 Net Cash flow 41,000 29,000 25,000 33,000 9.3.4 Cost Analysis for Projects What is the project cost? It is the cost incurred for setting up a project. Total project cost consists of total investment in the fixed assets that will enable the entity to start the production. If we divide it in to various sections, we have a break up as under: Fixed assets: Fixed assets consist of all the tangible assets such as land, buildings, plant and machinery, electrical installations, office equipment, computers, vehicles, staff quarters, etc. Let’s now see what constitutes the cost of some of these fixed assets; • Land cost will be different for freehold land or lease land, the former being costlier because it gives lifetime rights to enjoy it. The cost will include stamp duty, legal fees, leveling and developing. • Like land, the cost of factory building will vary based on whether is bought, constructed or on rental basis. Whatever it is, the cost of acquisition,stamp duty, legal cost, cost of construction and alterations or additions to the lease premises are some of the major elements of such cost. • Plant and machinery will cost you as much as its purchase price, duties, freight inward, clearing and customs and installations expenses. • Power line cost, internal cabling for connecting and electrifying the plant, transformers, sub-station, equipment and installations will be part of the proper power supply set up. • Depending upon the nature of the plant activity, the company may be required to invest in environment clearance, maintenance and protections. Such cost is a fixed one and can include equipment for prevention of air and water pollution, planting of trees and creating clean and hygienic environment. • The cost of other tangible fixed assets does not require elaboration. • Apart from the above, the company may also have to invest in creating or acquiring initial intangible assetssuch asregistrations, patents, trademarks, inventions, discoveries, data,software,systems etc.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0208.html -->
194 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT Installation cost: Such cost will vary from industry to industry. A simple engineering unit will not incur installation cost as much as a refinery or pharmaceutical or a chemical process plant will be required. A heavy engineering unit or auto component unit may require installation of overhead cranes or goods movement facility. Moreover, apart from the cost, the installation requires deep planning including taking care of all the logistics and time and motion study. All direct cost including the cost of the manpower directly and wholly concerned with the installation will be accounted under this head. Direct cost relating to setting up of the business: It is advisable to maintain records of expenses incurred by the company during construction period in such a way that the expense can be linked to the asset or a class of asset. At the end of the year or completion of the construction whichever is earlier, the expenses should be classified and be debited to the asset or class of the asset and credited to the Expenditure during Construction Period Account. Other cost: This cost relates to general operating and administration cost the company incurs during the setting up of the project. This can also be capitalized appropriately among the capital assets that have been created. The basis has to be on sound accounting principles. The analysis of this total cost: Although it is necessary that during the construction period there has to be continuous monitoring from various angles, the final cost analysis will be centering on the following points or issues. The necessity: The analysis should reveal that the asset acquired was absolutely necessary; be it type, brand, size, capacity or power, and the numbers. This information can be found in the project report and final analysis should confirm the conformation. The best combination: Many processes or methods of production have alternate suggestions and we need to find that the combination used is the best one. Competitive bargaining: This means that the procurement process is so planned that the price paid for the asset is the best bargained one. Cost versus recoverable duties: The duty structure of the country is such that there is not tax on the tax. In other words, the duty paid on an asset is allowed to be off set against the duties or taxes collected. This advantage is also called Input Tax Credit (ITC). Therefore, in the final analysis, it should be concluded that all the taxes paid on acquisition of the assets, to the extent recoverable, are not included in the cost of the asset. Leveraging: There are many alternatives for fund raising whether it is for long term or for working capital. Similarly, with respect to assets too, one can go for buying or leasing. While carrying out the cost analysis, one should also consider that the company has taken into consideration optimal leverages while sourcing the finance and the assets. The ultimate object of such leveraging is to optimize the cost of operation and make the business more competitive or profitable. Life span of the productive assets: While choosing the asset for business set up, the company will also take into account the life span of the asset to be acquired. If the life span is too long, the cost of repairs and maintenance will be high and the company will be saddled with an asset with outdated technology. If the life span is too short, the company will have to go for frequent replacement disrupting production. Based on the business plan and life cycle of the business, it will decide about the lifespan and the asset. This will also decide the depreciation rate and the period.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0209.html -->
Capital Investment Decisions | 195 Justification: No asset should be acquired unless there is enough justification. No one can afford idle or unfit for use assets on your fixed asset register. Environmental impact: Basically, the type of the plant, the technology, the process and the product do have impact on the environment. It may be power consumption, water or air pollution or the impact on the green surroundings or life of the sea creatures or the working conditions of the employees and workers. The company has to provide for the protection and accordingly spend on technology or assets to eliminate or minimize the impact on the environment. Growth considerations: The cost analysis will also take into account whether the project cost takes care of future growth whether short term or long term. The cost incurred should be futuristic meaning the growth considerations are inbuilt. Capacity enhancement and provision research and development are important ingredients of future growth and the project cost should be found growth compliant in the ultimate cost analysis, Sustainability: The project is to be so structured that it will be self-sustainable in the long-term and accordingly the provision of assets, manpower, technology, research and development etc. are forming part of it The returns that are projected: The cost analysis should finally end with the yield that is proved on paper. Based on the risk, term of the project and the resources deployed, a rate of return is fixed as a target and costing structure should enable the management to achieve it. 9.3.5 Methods of Investment Appraisal A loan appraisal is done to decide whether to give a loan or not. Similarly, an investment appraisal helps to decide whether to invest or not. Basically, an investor would like to know whether he will get his money back and if yes, how much and when. There are various methods used by the business community to carry out the appraisal; the major and in vogue are as follows. 9.3.5.1 Payback period, discounted payback period Simply speaking, payback period is the period at the end of which the initial investment is entirely returned to the investor. Return of investment isthe net cash flow for each of the years of operation. When the cumulative cash flow is equal to the investment, the investment is considered to be returned and no further. It is to be remembered that both the positive or negative cash flows are to be added till the total net cash flow equals the amount of the initial investment. In other words, it is the cumulative cash flow which has to be equal to the investment. The formula, in those cases where the Net Cash Inflow every year is same, can be read as under; Payback Period = Amount to be initially invested/ Estimated Annual Net Cash Inflow per year In those cases, where the Net Cash Inflow every year is not the same, we have to add up Net Cash Inflows from the first year till the total is equal to the amount invested initially. This may involve Net Cash Inflow during a part of any year and that part is calculated based on the assumption that cash flow is uniform during the year. This is elaborated in Illustration. 3. In the case of discounted payback period, instead ofsimply adding the cash flow asit is, it isthe discounted cash flow that is considered.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0210.html -->
196 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT The following points are worth noting: • This method simply identifiesthe period in which the initial investment in the project isfully recovered. • It does not take time value of money into account unless it is discounted payback period method. • The method is a simple concept. • This is suitable for small businesses where it is not worth spending more time and money to make more detailed or scientific analysis. • The shorter the period for recovery of initial investment, lesser is the risk and better or quicker is the probability to earn profits. • Payback Period method ignores the cash flow beyond the payback period and hence ignores profit aspect. • While payback period method is used across the line, the other more sophisticated methods are used in larger sized budgeting exercise. ILLUSTRATION 2 Suppose a project costs Rs. 20,00,000 and yields annually a profit of Rs. 3,00,000 after depreciation @ 10% (straight line method) but before tax at 50%. The first step would be to calculate the cash inflow from this project. The cash inflow is calculated as follows: Particulars (Rs.) Profit before tax 3,00,000 Less: Tax @ 50% 1,50,000 Profit after tax 1,50,000 Add: Depreciation written off 2,00,000 Total cash inflow 3,50,000 While calculating cash inflow, depreciation is added back to profit after tax since it does not result in cash outflow. The cash generated from a project therefore is equal to profit after tax plus depreciation. The payback period of the project shall be: Payback period = Rs. 20,00,000 Rs. 3,50,000/Year = 5.71 Years ILLUSTRATION 3 Suppose ABC Ltd. is analyzing a project requiring an initial cash outlay of Rs. 2,00,000 and is expected to generate cash inflows as follows: Year Annual Cash Inflows (Rs.) 1 80,000 2 60,000 3 60,000 4 20,000


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0211.html -->
Capital Investment Decisions | 197 Its payback period shall be computed by using cumulative cash flows as follows: Year Annual Cash Inflows (Rs.) Cumulative Cash Inflows (Rs.) 1 80,000 80,000 2 60,000 1,40,000 3 60,000 2,00,000 4 20,000 2,20,000 In 3rd year, cumulative cash inflows equal to initial cash outlay i.e., Rs. 2,00,000. Hence, payback period is 3 years. Suppose if in above example, the initial outlay is Rs. 2,05,000, then: Payback period shall lie between 3 to 4 years. Since up to 3 years, a sum of Rs. 2,00,000 shall be recovered and balance of Rs. 5,000 shall be recovered in the part (fraction) of 4th year, computation is as follows: Part of 4th year = Balance cash outlay Cash Inflow in 4th Year = 5,000 20,000 = 0.25 year Thus, total cash outlay of Rs. 2,05,000 shall be recovered in 3.25 years’ time. ILLUSTRATION 4 Rs. 30,000 cash outlay for a project with annual cash inflows of Rs. 6,000 would have a payback period of 5 years (Rs. 30,000/ Rs. 6,000). The problem with the Payback Period is that it ignores the time value of money. In order to correct this, we can use discounted cash flows in calculating the payback period. Referring back to our example, if we discount the cash inflows at 15% required rate of return, we have: Year Cash Flow (Rs.) PVF@15% PV (Rs.) Cumulative PV (Rs.) 1 6,000 0.870 5,220 5,220 2 6,000 0.756 4,536 9,756 3 6,000 0.658 3,948 13,704 4 6,000 0.572 3,432 17,136 5 6,000 0.497 2,982 20,118 6 6,000 0.432 2,592 22,710 7 6,000 0.376 2,256 24,966 8 6,000 0.327 1,962 26,928 9 6,000 0.284 1,704 28,632 10 6,000 0.247 1,482 30,114


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0212.html -->
198 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT The cumulative total of discounted cash flows after ten years is Rs. 30,114. Therefore, our discounted payback is approximately 10 years as opposed to 5 years undersimple payback. Itshould be noted that as the required rate of return increases, the distortion between simple payback and discounted payback grows. 9.3.5.2 Accounting Rate of Return (ARR) The foregoing method of appraisal does not help you to know the rate of return because we stop once the investment is recovered. The methods to determine the rate of return are many but the most common, easy, simple and practical is the accounting rate of return. This method involves estimating the revenue and expenses for say over three years and to find out the average rate of return which can be considered as the rate for appraising the investment. This method scores over other methods because: • The DCF method calls for estimates for the entire project life making it more uncertain. • We find many assets outlive the rated life which makes DCF working away from reality. • In the long run, the estimating exercise turns out to be unreal with regard to revenue and cost. • This method uses a limited data but is based on accounting information readily available and familiar to the business community. However, the method is profit centric and not cash flow centric. It does not take into account the time value of money. The example given below will explain the difference. Let us consider two investment proposals X and Y, both of which require an outlay of Rs. 2,00,000. Both the proposals have an expected life of 4 years and Nil salvage value after the expected life is over. Figures in Rs. X Y Year Book Value Depreciation Profit after tax Cash Flow Book Value Depreciation Profit after tax Cash flow 0 2,00,000 0 0 (2,00,000) 2,00,000 0 0 (2,00,000) 1 1,50,000 50,000 80,000 1,30,000 1,50,000 50,000 20,000 70,000 2 1,00,000 50,000 60,000 1,10,000 1,00,000 50,000 40,000 90,000 3 50,000 50,000 40,000 90,000 50,000 50,000 60,000 1,10,000 4 0 50,000 20,000 70,000 0 50,000 80,000 1,30,000 From the perspective of the accounting rate of return, the similarities between the two proposals, both of which have a rate of return on investment equal to forty percent, are apparent. Nevertheless, project X is significantly more desirable given that it delivers benefits at an earlier stage. The payback period criterion does not place any weightage on more distant benefits, whereas the accounting rate of return criterion appears to place an excessive amount of weightage on these benefits. Moreover, there is internal inconsistency in the sense that the return part (numerator) belongs to the owners(Equity and Preference shareholders), the denominator representstotal investment which include equity as well as debt. Accounting rate of return (ARR) = Average annual net income Investment


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0213.html -->
Capital Investment Decisions | 199 ILLUSTRATION 5 Suppose A Ltd. is going to invest in a project a sum of Rs. 3,00,000 having a life span of 3 years. Salvage value of machine is Rs. 90,000. The profit before depreciation for each year is Rs. 1,50,000. The Profit after Tax and value of Investment in the Beginning and at the End of each year shall be as follows: Year Profit Before Depreciation (Rs.) Depreciation (Rs.) Profit after Depreciation (Rs.) Value of Investment In (Rs.) Beginning End 1 1,50,000 70,000 80,000 3,00,000 2,30,000 2 1,50,000 70,000 80,000 2,30,000 1,60,000 3 1,50,000 70,000 80,000 1,60,000 90,000 The ARR can be computed by following methods as follows: (a) Version 1: Annual Basis ARR = Profit After Depreciation Investment in the beginning of the year Year 1 80,000 3,00,000 = 26.67% 2 80,000 2,30,000 = 34.78% 3 80,000 1,60,000 =50% (b) Version 2: Total Investment Basis ARR = Average Annual Profit Investment in the beginning ×100 = (80,000+80,000+80,000)/ 3 3,00,000 × 100 = 26.67% (c) Version 3: Average Investment Basis ARR = Average Annual Profit Average Investment × 100 Average Investment = (Rs. 3,00,000 + Rs. 90,000)/2 = Rs. 1,95,000 Or, Average Investment = ½ (Initial Investment – Salvage Value) + Salvage Value = ½ (Rs. 3,00,000 – Rs. 90,000) + Rs. 90,000 = Rs. 1,95,000 ARR = 80,000 1,95,000 × 100 = 41.03%


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0214.html -->
200 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT ILLUSTRATION 6 A project, requiring an investment of Rs. 10,00,000, yields profit after tax and depreciation which is as follows: Years Profit after tax and depreciation (Rs.) 1 50,000 2 75,000 3 1,25,000 4 1,30,000 5 80,000 Total 4,60,000 Suppose further that at the end of the 5th year, the plant and machinery of the project can be sold for Rs. 80,000. Determine the Accounting Rate of Return. SOLUTION: In this case the rate of return can be calculated as follows: Total Profit ÷ No. of years Average investment or Initial Investment × 100 (a) If Initial Investment is considered then, = 4,60,000 ÷ 5 years 10,00,000 × 100 = 9.2% (b) If Average investment is considered, then, = Rs. 92,000 Avg Investment × 100 = Rs. 92,000 Rs. 5,40,000 × 100 = 17.04% Where, Average Investment = ½ (Initial investment – Salvage value) + Salvage value = ½ (Rs. 10,00,000 – Rs. 80,000) + Rs. 80,000 = Rs. 4,60,000 + Rs. 80,000 = Rs. 5,40,000 9.3.5.3 Net Present Value (NPV) • The meaning of NPV: A bird in hand is better than two in the bush! A rupee now is more valuable than the same rupee a year later. Thisis what the present value means. Net present value used in terms of cash flow means the present value of all future cash flows. Cash flows mean net flow; difference between the inflow and outflows and the net flow after setting off the negative flows. The present year cash flow gets full weightage without any discount. The future years’ flows are discounted at a rate considered the expected rate of return which is subjective and may vary based on the expectation of the investor which again is based on the duration and the risk, mainly. If the NPV is equal to 0, then the project will neither add value nor are you likely to lose. Positive NPV means project can be given go ahead and the negative is red signal.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0215.html -->
Capital Investment Decisions | 201 • Strengths and limitations of NPV method: The main strength of the NPV is that it takes into account the time element and brings the rate of return nearer to the reality. It helps you quickly evaluate the surplus you will end up with at the end of the project period and whether it matches your expectations all things considered. The biggest limitation is that it is all estimate and surreal. The discounting factor applied is very subjective. With uncertainties galore in the long term, the risk factors add up which are not simply captured here. Moreover, this method does not provide the overall result of profit or loss over the period of the project. • The working capital adjustment in an NPV analysis: A cash flow is a combination of out flow on account of investment and expenses and inflow on account of capital, borrowings and revenue. The working capital, apparently does not figure in any of the above combinations. However, working capital does have an impact on cash flow. Working capital is the difference between the current assets and current liabilities. Generally, a business will have more assets than liabilities under this head. In other words, net current assets will represent net out flow on account of working capital engagement. An increase in working capital is an outflow and decrease is an inflow. In order to have a correct picture of cash flow, the working capital adjustment is necessary. • Capital rationing: Rationing implies restriction on use or supply. In terms of capital budgeting, this is based on the universal truth that resources are limited. Capital available for investment is no exception. Capital rationing therefore takes various forms e.g. no fresh investments during a year or for a period, no use of retained profitsfor investment, not to borrow orseek external fundsfor capital investment. To trigger the rationing, certain norms are fixed like not to take up part of a project, enforce higher IRR or profitability index, fixed money discount rate, inflation linked rate, risk free interest rate etc. • Equivalent Annual Costs (EAC): This cost of the asset is other than that for acquisition. In other words, it is an annual cost of owning, operating and maintaining an asset over its life time. In terms of capital budgeting and cost analysis, EAC enables us to compare the cost effectiveness of two or more assets with different lifespans. Price of the asset, expected life span and the discount rate used to work out the EAC. Net present value = Present value of net cash inflow - Total net initial investment Decision Rule: If NPV ≥ 0 Accept the Proposal If NPV ≤ 0 Reject the Proposal The NPV method can be used to select between mutually exclusive projects; the one with the higher NPV should be selected. ILLUSTRATION 7 Compute the net present value for a project with a net initial investment of Rs. 1,00,000. The net cash flow for year one is Rs. 55,000; for year two is Rs. 80,000 and for year three is Rs. 15,000. Further, the company’s cost of capital is 10%. [PVIF @ 10% for three years are 0.909, 0.826 and 0.751]


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0216.html -->
202 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT SOLUTION: Year Net Cash Flows (Rs.) PVIF @ 10% Discounted Cash Flows (Rs.) 0 (1,00,000) 1.000 (1,00,000) 1 55,000 0.909 49,995 2 80,000 0.826 66,080 3 15,000 0.751 11,265 Net Present Value 27,340 Recommendation: Since the net present value of the project is positive, the company should accept the project. ILLUSTRATION 8 XYZ Ltd. is analysing capital expenditure proposals for the purchase of equipment; the company uses the net present value technique to evaluate projects. The capital budget is limited to Rs. 500,000 which XYZ Ltd. believesisthe maximum capital it can raise. The initial investment and projected net cash flows for each project are shown below. The cost of capital of XYZ Ltd is 12%. You are required to compute the NPV of the different projects. Project 1 (Rs.) Project 2 (Rs.) Project 3 (Rs.) Project 4 (Rs.) Initial Investment 200,000 190,000 250,000 210,000 Project Cash Inflows: Year 1 50,000 40,000 75,000 75,000 2 50,000 50,000 75,000 75,000 3 50,000 70,000 60,000 60,000 4 50,000 75,000 80,000 40,000 5 50,000 75,000 100,000 20,000 SOLUTION: Calculation of net present value: Period PV factor Project 1 (Rs.) Project 2 (Rs.) Project 3 (Rs.) Project 4 (Rs.) 0 1.000 (2,00,000) (1,90,000) (2,50,000) (2,10,000) 1 0.893 44,650 35,720 66,975 66,975 2 0.797 39,850 39,850 59,775 59,775 3 0.712 35,600 49,840 42,720 42,720


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0217.html -->
Capital Investment Decisions | 203 4 0.636 31,800 47,700 50,880 25,440 5 0.567 28,350 42,525 56,700 11,340 Net Present Value (19,750) 25,635 27,050 (3,750) 9.3.5.4 Internal Rate of return (IRR) In the context of investment, an Internal Rate of Return means an annual rate of growth in investment a business is going to generate. The concept of calculating NPV and IRR is the same. However, while calculating IRR, the NPV is set to zero; The formula and calculation used to determine this figure are as follows: 0=NPV= T∑t=1 Ct /(1+IRR)t −C0 Where: Ct=Net cash inflow during the period t C0=Total initial investment costs IRR=the internal rate of return T= the number of time periods t=a particular time period in the total time periods varying from 1 to T You ought to know the initial total investment which is an outflow (-) (C0). T isthe total number of periods of the life of the project. Then we have the period (t) which represents a particular time period, within the total time periods T, and the net cash flow during this period is represented by Ct. The calculation will give you the result which is the discount rate or the IRR. However, the formula is such that the resultant IRR will not be generated easily and therefore permutations and combinations through excel sheet on trial and error basis will give you the result which finally can be tested by simple calculations. If this rate of return is higher than basic or required RR(RRR), then only the investment is worth. RRR is equivalent to the cost of funds. • Limitations of the IRR method: Although this is an ideal way of appraising an investment proposal, it may not work well in an inappropriate case. Moreover, if the factors comprising the IRR calculations are difficult to predict, it may be misleading. In case cash flows intermittently turn positive as well as negative, there can be multiple rates. If the estimates in IRR and NPV differ drastically from actual results, the analysts will have to choose to combine IRR analysis with scenario analysis. Scenarios can show different possible NPVs based on varying assumptions. If studied in conjunction with weighted average cost of capital (WACC) and or Required Rate of Return (RRR), the results can be more authentic. • Multiple IRRs: This situation arises when the project has non-conventional or casual or interruptive cash flows. 9.3.5.5 Modified internal Rate of Return (MIRR) The formula used for calculating IRR presumes that the net cash inflows of the project during the intermediate years are reinvested at the IRR of the project. This presumption is not realistic as the market is not concerned with what the IRR of our project is. Therefore, a Modified IRR(MIRR) approach is


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0218.html -->
204 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT adopted. MIRR also takes care of the problem faced in IRR method regarding more than one value of IRR for projects with alternating positive and negative cash flows leading to confusion and ambiguity. MIRR method finds only one value for the project. Definition and explanation of MIRR: A Modified IRR is the one calculated to correct aberrations arising out of disruptive or alternatively positive and negative cash flows as also to correct the unrealistic assumption of investing intermittent cash inflows at project IRR. Under this approach, any negative cash flow in any period, during the life of the project, is treated as the cost of the project and added to the initial cost of the project by discounting at the cost of the capital. Thisis called the Present Value of Costs (PVC). Also, the project inflows are compounded at the cost of capital to arrive at the total compounded terminal value (TV) of the inflows. Then an appropriate rate of discount for this compounded terminal value is found out so that this discounted terminal value is equal to the total present value of the cost of the project (PVC). This discount rate is called MIRR. The process for calculating MIRR: The procedure for calculating MIRR is as follows: • Step 1: Calculate the present value of the costs (PVC) associated with the project, using the cost of capital (r) as the discount rate: PVC = t n t t cash Outflow r ∑ = + 0 () 1 • Step 2: Calculate the terminal value (TV) of the cash inflows expected from the project: TV = t n ∑ =0 Cash Inflowt (1 + r) n–t • Step 3: Obtain the MIRR by solving the following equation: PVC = TV (1 + MIRR) n • n is the number of equal periods at the end of which the cash flows occur (not the number of cash flows), • Spreadsheet applications, such as Microsoft Excel, have inbuilt functions to calculate the MIRR. In Microsoft Excel this function is “=MIRR(...)”. Strengths of the MIRR approach: As mentioned above, MIRR approach is an improvement over the IRR approach and provides a more realistic and undisputed rate of return of the project. However, both IRR and MIRR are not valid for ranking projects of different sizes, because a larger project with a smaller modified internal rate of return may have a higher net present value. However, there exist variants of the modified internal rate of return which can be used for such comparisons. ILLUSTRATION 9 The following example will illustrate the calculations involved in arriving at the IRR. Following are the cash flows of the project of XYZ Ltd. Year 0 1 2 3 4 Cash Flow (2,00,000) 60,000 60,000 80,000 90,000


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0219.html -->
Capital Investment Decisions | 205 The IRR is the value of r which satisfies the following equation: 2,00,000 = 60,000 (1 + r)1 + 60,000 (1 + r)2 + 80,000 (1 + r)3 + 90,000 (1 + r)4 The calculation of r involves a process of trial and error. We try different values of r till we find that the right-hand side of the above equation is equal to 2,00,000. Let us, to begin with, try r = 14 percent. This makes the left-hand side equal to: 2,06,085 = 60,000 (1.14)1 + 60,000 (1.14)2 + 80,000 (1.14)3 + 90,000 (1.14)4 This value isslightly higher than our target value, 200,000. So, we increase the value of r from 14 percent to 15 percent. (In general, a higher r lowers and a smaller r increases the left-hand side value). The lefthand side becomes: 2,01,601 = 60,000 (1.15)1 + 60,000 (1.15)2 + 80,000 (1.15)3 + 90,000 (1.15)4 This value is still slightly higher than our target value, 200,000. So, we increase the value of r from 15 percent to 16 percent. (In general, a higher r lowers and a smaller r increases the left-hand side value). The left-hand side becomes: 1,97,272 = 60,000 (1.16)1 + 60,000 (1.16)2 + 80,000 (1.16)3 + 90,000 (1.16)4 Since this value is now less than 200,000, we conclude that the value of r lies between 15 percent and 16 percent. For most of the purposes, this indication suffices. If a single point estimate of r is needed, the following interpolation procedure is to be used: 1. Determine the net present value of the two closest rates of return. (NPV/ 15 percent) 1,601 (NPV/ 16 percent) (2,728) 2. Find the sum of the absolute values of the net present values obtained in step 1: 1,601 + 2,728 = 4,329 3. Calculate the ratio of the net present value at the smaller discount rate, identified in step 1, to the sum obtained in step 2: 1,601/ 4,329 = 0.37 4. Add the number obtained in step 3 to the smaller discount rate: 15 + 0.37 = 15.37 percent When done in this manner, the internal rate of return that is determined is an approximation of the actual internal rate of return that is extremely near to being accurate. The following is the decision rule that should be used for IRR: Accept: If the IRR is greater than the cost of capital Reject: If the IRR is less than the cost of capital


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0220.html -->
206 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT ILLUSTRATION 10 The calculation of MIRR can be illustrated through the following example. Square Limited is evaluating a project which has the following initial investment and cash inflows: Year 0 1 2 3 4 5 6 Cash Flow -240 -160 40 120 160 200 240 The cost of capital for Square Ltd is 15 percent. Present value of cost = 240 + 160/(1.15) = 379.13 Terminal value of cash inflows = 40(1.15)4 + 120(1.15)3 + 160(1.15)2 + 200(1.15) + 240 = 69.96 + 182.51 + 211.60 +230 + 240 = 934.07 MIRR is computed as follows: 379.13 = 934.07 /(1 + MIRR)6 (1 + MIRR)6 = 934.07/379.13 = 2.463 1 + MIRR = 2.4631/6 = 1.162 MIRR = 1.162 – 1 = 0.162 = 16.2 percent Conclusion: As the MIRR is higher than the cost of capital, the project is acceptable. 9.5.3.6 Profitability Index This is an index that either explains or represents the relationship between the cost and the benefit of a project proposal. It is also called value investment ratio or profit investment ratio. PI is calculated by dividing the present value of future expected cash flows by the initial investment amount in the project. Higher the Index better is profitability of the project. Anything below 1 indicates that the project is unprofitable. Mathematically: The Profitability Index (PI) is calculated as below: Profitability Index (PI) = Sum of discounted cash inflows Initial cash outlay or Total discounted cash outflow (as the case may be) ILLUSTRATION 11 Suppose we have three projects involving discounted cash outflow of Rs. 5,50,000, Rs. 75,000 and Rs. 1,00,20,000 respectively. Suppose further that the sum of discounted cash inflows for these projects are Rs. 6,50,000, Rs. 95,000 and Rs. 1,00,30,000 respectively. Calculate the respective Profitability Index (PI) for the three projects. SOLUTION: The respective Profitability Index for the three projects would be as follows: 1. PI = Rs. 6,50,000 Rs. 5,50,000 = 1.18 2. PI = Rs. 95,000 Rs. 75,000 = 1.27


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0221.html -->
Capital Investment Decisions | 207 3. PI = Rs. 1,00,30,000 Rs. 1,00,20,000 = 1.001 It can be seen that in absolute terms, project 3 gives the highest cash inflows yet its Profitability Index is low. This is because the outflow is also very high. The Profitability Index factor helps us in ranking various projects. 9.4 SOCIAL COST BENEFIT ANALYSIS Central and State Governments and local bodies or designated corporations take up many projects of infrastructure developments, airports, ports, bridges, dams etc. To support decision making process, SCBA is carried out. On one hand we have total cost of the project and on the other hand the social cost as well as benefits. One has to attach or allot value to each such impact or benefit. Adverse impact will have negative value and the benefits will have positive value. Adverse impact is the social cost. Loss of mangroves, generation of pollution, impact on plants, extinction of some birds and rare species, water level going down, health hazards are some of the social cost and environment impact. Benefits are ease of travel, fuel savings, time savings, employment generation, uplifting of living standards and bringing order in traffic management and safety, among others. If net result of positive and negative values is positive and equal or more than the cost of the project, the project is considered as beneficial. LET US SUM UP Identifying, screening, and choosing investment projects are part of capital budgeting. These projects provide benefit to a business entity by increasing itsfuture cash flows. Future cash flows must be estimated with and without the investment. How does the investment affect cash flow? We evaluate these changes in cash flows in our investing analysis. Estimating incremental cash flows entails anticipating sales, costs, taxes, and net working capital. Cash flows from buying and selling assets must incorporate (1) direct cash flows, (2) tax ramifications, and (3) foregone cash flows. Changes in net working capital in an investment project are caused by a bigger requirement for cash, accounts receivable, or inventory, or by adjusting accrual-based accounting assumptions to a cash basis. We calculate future net cash flows by combining these predictions with cash flows from acquiring and selling project assets. Using multiple evaluation methodologies, we determine if this investment maximises owners’ wealth. Payback period, discounted payback period, net present value, profitability index, internal rate of return, and modified internal rate of return are used to evaluate investment cash flows. None of these six strategies consider all cash flows, their timing, or their risk, nor do they maximise owners’ wealth. Payback period and discount payback period measure how long future cash flowstake to repay the initial investment.The payback period just considersfuture cash flows’amounts, whereasthe discounted payback period considers their present value. Both methods provide some information about an investment’s desirability, but they don’t tell us if a project would increase the owners’ wealth. Net present value is the amount the business entity’s value increases if the investment is made. It’s the difference between operating and investment cash flows. An investment profile depicts a project’s NPV for multiple discount rates to show a project’s sensitivity.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0222.html -->
208 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT The profitability index compares projected operating cash inflows to investment cash inflows. The profitability index shows us if an investment will improve the shareholders’ wealth. We can’t use the profitability index to choose between mutually exclusive initiatives because it doesn’t quantify value growth in dollars. When cash is scarce, the profitability index helps prioritise projects. IRR isinvestment yield. Discountrate equals zero net present value.IRR isrisky when comparing mutually exclusive projects or limiting capital spending. The modified internal rate of return is an investment yield assuming cash inflows are reinvested at a different pace. This solution solves the IRR method’s unreasonable reinvestment rate assumptions. MIRR is risky when choosing between mutually exclusive projects or when spending is limited. Each method has pros and cons. NPV, PI, IRR, and MIRR are better methods than payback period and discounted payback period methods. We must be vigilant while evaluating mutually exclusive or capitalrationed projects. Net present value maximises owner wealth for mutually exclusive enterprises or capital rationing. In practice, businesses use discounted cash flow techniques, with IRR being the most common. Net present value is also one of the more popular methods. KEYWORDS Capital Budgeting, Forecasting, Investment Appraisal, Payback period, Net Present Value, IRR, MIRR CHECK YOUR PROGRESS 1. Acapital budgeting technique which does not require the computation of cost of capital for decision making purposes is: (a) Net Present Value method (b) Internal Rate of Return method (c) Modified Internal Rate of Return method (d) Payback Period method 2. IRR method would favour project proposals which have: (a) Heavy cash inflows in the early stages of the project. (b) Evenly distributed cash inflows throughout the project. (c) Heavy cash inflows at the later stages of the project. (d) None of the above. 3. While evaluating capital investment proposals, time value of money is used in which of the following techniques: (a) Payback Period method (b) Accounting rate of return (c) Net present value (d) None of the above 4. Depreciation is included as a cost in which of the following techniques: (a) Accounting rate of return (b) Net present value (c) Internal rate of return (d) None of the above

If the total income from the project is expected to be Rs. 60,00,000 and recognition is given to the effect of straight-line depreciation on the investment, the average rate of return is: (a) 12% (b) 24% (c) 60% (d) 75% SOLUTIONS 1. (d) 2. (a) 3. (c) 4. (a) 5. (b)

STRUCTURE 10.0 Objectives 10.1 Introduction 10.2 Foreign Investment Analysis 10.3 International Capital Budgeting 10.4 Issues Involved in Evaluation of Overseas Projects 10.5 Approaches for Evaluation of Overseas Projects 10.6 Evaluation Methods 10.7 Impact of Transfer Pricing Let Us Sum Up Keywords Check Your Progress Solutions U N I T 10 CAPITAL BUDGETING FOR INTERNATIONAL PROJECT INVESTMENT DECISIONS


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0226.html -->
212 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 10.0 OBJECTIVES After studying this unit, you will have better understanding of: • Concepts of international investments • Channels of international investment • Exchange and country risks • Analysis involved in international investments • International capital budgeting • Issues involved in evaluation of overseas projects • Approaches for evaluation of overseas projects • Impact of transfer pricing 10.1 INTRODUCTION The capital budget is determined by the company management to plan expenses on fixed assets. The objective of capital budgeting is to make long-term expenditure decisions about whether particular projects will result in sustainable growth and provide the expected returns. Using the capital budgeting approach, each suggested capital investment decision is given a quantitative analysis in order to have a rational approach towards the business decision. On the similar lines, making investments abroad and that too in a project, requires serious considerations of many issues especially because the investment need to factor in multiple aspects of a foreign territory where environment, laws, culture, political stability are unknown and unpredictable. Capital budgeting, therefore, for such projects or investment requires deep analysis of the Investment. The issues faced by an international entity are quite different compared to a domestic businessman. These arise out of differences in work culture, legal system, currency system, business traditions, accounting standards, trade barriers, local language, and so on. Therefore, we need to analyse these major issues as a part of capital budgeting for international project decisions. 10.2 FOREIGN INVESTMENT ANALYSIS 10.2.1 Special Considerations-Foreign & Home Currency Cash Flows You are sitting in India and making investment or setting up a project abroad. Your spending will be abroad in that country’s currency. Your entire projections will be in foreign currency. Adoption of a currency conversion rate, particularly for cross currency transactions, will need care and efforts. If the project implementation time is long, the foreign currency valuation can pose problem because of the rate volatility. Your imports and exports, borrowings and repayments, dividends and repatriation; all will be at rates fluctuating from time to time. Elaborate workings and spread sheets, including for estimates, will be required. One may have to keep updating or revising in case of major exchange rate fluctuation. One may have to work on multiple scenarios. Moreover, the foreign investment cash flows, made in the relevant foreign currency, may need integration with Indian cash flows if the activities are integrated or the project cost benefits are shared both between the overseas outfit and local entity. One has to make a choice about the currency, on the basis of which one is dominant from the project point of view. 10.2.2 Foreign Currency Discount Rates Computation Converting local currency into a foreign currency is done at a prevailing rate which is called spot rate. Even spotrate variesfrom one form or mode ofthe currency to anothersuch as currency notes, travellers’cheques,


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0227.html -->
Capital Budgeting for International Project... | 213 telegraphic transfer etc. However, if you want to contractfor purchase at a future date, the rate called forward contract rate is to be adopted. If you want to sell your local currency to buy say USD at a future date, there will be discount to the value of your currency because you will have to pay future premium. Future contract rate needs to be adjusted by the interest rate and the time factor. To calculate the forward rate, we have to multiply the spot rate by the ratio of interest rates and adjust for the time until expiration. Thus, this aspect of currency ratesis an important aspectwhile conducting the exercise of capital budgeting forforeign investments. 10.2.3 International Portfolio Investment and Institutional Constraints With increasing globalisation, the trade and business have spread much beyond the domestic barriers. People look beyond their countries to set up projects and invest in profitable businesses. Countries like India have started allowing the residents to send money under simpler guidelines to abroad for various purposes including investment. Presently in India, an individual can annually send an amount equivalent upto USD 2,50,000 per FYunderthe LiberalisedRemittance Scheme (LRS).Although, underthe liberalised scheme, a lot more purposes are allowed, we will discuss only the topic of International Portfolio Investment. The introduction of investment alternatives,such asinternational mutual funds, country funds and internationally listed stocks, which allow investors to achieve international diversification without incurring excessive costs, has also contributed to buoyancy in portfolio investments. • We need to know and understand; The International Portfolio Investment (IPI) means and includes investment into the foreign securities which can be in various forms such as Treasury bills, mutual funds, listed equities, debt instruments etc. Thisinvolves cross country remittance through designated international fund transfer channels. The remittance will always be in the local currency of the country where the investment takes place. • Benefits; Developed and industrialised markets or fast developing markets offer good opportunities. There are varieties of products available. Information, analysis and studies too are largely and easily available. The stability of exchange rates of developed countries provides safe value and lesser risk of exchange fluctuations or erosion of investment value. Investors may be able to achieve higher returns with reduced risk by diversifying their investible resources internationally • Diversification: Investor will usually follow the principle of diversification to ensure a reasonable and steady return along with safety of funds and benefits of capital appreciation. This is easier in case of international scenario. Diversification of investible resources and building of investment portfolio, comprising securities of different companies across national markets, offers the opportunity of achieving a better risk-return trade-off than by investing solely in domestic securities. • Barriers: We may sometimes find impediments and barriers in capital flow. In long term investments, the risk is higher because events, creating uncertainty in the political or economic field, may likely crop up. Foreign country may impose price controls with a view to achieve their fiscal and monetary policy or social objectives. Even language barrier may result into disadvantage in obtaining quick and reliable information. • Asset allocation: Efficient management of foreign portfolio quite heavily depends on asset allocation. Once you decide the investment objective, you need to select right mix of risk and return to achieve targeted return during investment tenure. Such skilful selection will allow the management to passively mange it rather actively. Required liquidity to quickly honour redemption request shall be taken into account and yet avoid idle funds. Securities having less liquidity in the market may create problems at the time of exit. Nowadays, there are adequate market mechanismsto easily identify high rated securities.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0228.html -->
214 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT There are numerous classes such as small cap, mid cap, large cap, high P/E, low P/E, value stock (high book to market price ratio) growth stocks (low book to market price stock), rated, unrated, treasury and government bonds, debt securities, listed, unlisted, private placement, IPO, FPO, OFS to choose to suit investment objective. • Hedging strategies: Market index and Exchange rate index are not necessarily linked to each other. Exchange rate fluctuations also occur for reasons other than stock performance or economic growth. Forward exchange rate contract is one way out to hedge against such risks, based on your planned acquisitions or exits or liquidation. Moreover, a separate hedge strategy needs a combination of the world market portfolio, risk-lessforeign and domestic currency bonds and a currency-specific hedge portfolio, that stripsforeign bonds of their currency risk. There are a number of waysto construct a hedge portfolio. The preferred and simple method is to hedge the entire amount of funds, exposed to currency risk. ILLUSTRATION 1 CCS Technologies Ltd., an Indian IT company is planning to make an investment through a wholly owned subsidiary in a software project in China with a shelf life of two years. The inflation in China is estimated as 8 percent. Operating cash flows are received at the year end. For the project, an initial investment of Chinese Yuan (CN¥) 30,00,000 will be in land. The land will be sold after the completion of project at estimated value of CN¥ 35,00,000. The project also requires an office complex at cost of CN¥ 15,00,000 payable at the beginning of project. The complex will be depreciated on straight-line basis over two years to a zero-salvage value. This complex is expected to fetch CN¥ 5,00,000 at the end of project. The company is planning to raise the required fundsthrough GDR issue in Mauritius. Each GDR will have 5 common equity shares of the company as underlying security which are currently trading at Rs. 200 per share (Face Value = Rs. 10) in the domestic market. The company has currently paid the dividend of 25% which is expected to grow at 10% p.a. The total issue cost is estimated to be 1 percent of issue size. The annual sales are expected to be 10,000 units at the rate of CN¥ 500 per unit. The price of unit is expected to rise at the rate of inflation. Variable operating costs are 40 percent of sales. Fixed operating costs will be CN¥ 22,00,000 per year and expected to rise at the rate of inflation. The tax rate applicable in China for income and capital gain is 25 percent and as per GOI Policy no further tax shall be payable in India. The current spot rate of CN¥ 1 is Rs. 9.50. The nominal interest rate in India and China is 12% and 10% respectively and the international parity conditions hold. You are required to (a) Identify expected future cash flows in China and determine NPV of the project in CN¥. (b) Determine whether CCS Technologies should go for the project or not assuming that neither there is restriction on the transfer of funds from China to India nor any charges/taxes payable on the transfer of funds. Solution: 1. Calculation of Cost of Capital (GDR) Current Dividend (D0) 2.50 Expected Dividend (D1) 2.75 Net Proceeds (Rs. 200 per share – 1%) 198.00 Growth Rate 10.00% Ke = 2.75 + 0.10 = 0.1139 i.e., 11.39% 198

Calculation of Expected Exchange Rate as per Interest Rate Parity YEAR EXPECTED RATE 1 = 9.50 × (1+ 0.12) (1+ 0.10) = 9.67 2 = 9.50 × (1+ 0.12)2 (1+ 0.10)2 = 9.85 3. Realization on the disposal of Land net of Tax CN¥ Sale value at the end of project 3500000.00 Cost of Land 3000000.00 Capital Gain 500000.00 Tax paid 125000.00 Amount realized net of tax 3375000.00 4. Realization on the disposal of Office Complex (CN¥) Sale value at the end of project 500000.00 WDV 0.00 Capital Gain 500000.00 Tax paid 125000.00 Amount realized net of tax (A) 375000.00 5. Computation of Annual Cash Inflows Year 1 2 Annual Units 10000 10000 Price per unit (CN¥) 540.00 583.20 Annual Revenue (CN¥) 5400000.00 5832000.00 Less: Expenses Variable operating cost (CN¥) 2160000.00 2332800.00 Depreciation (CN¥) 750000.00 750000.00 Fixed Cost per annum (CN¥) 2376000.00 2566080.00 PBT (CN¥) 114000.00 183120.00 Tax on Profit (CN¥) 28500.00 45780.00 Net Profit (CN¥) 85500.00 137340.00 Add: Depreciation (CN¥) 750000.00 750000.00 Cash Flow 835500.00 887340.00

216 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT (a) Computation of NPV of the project in CN¥ Year 0 1 2 Initial Investment -4500000.00 Annual Cash Inflows 835500.00 887340.00 Realization on the disposal of Land net of Tax 3375000.00 Realization on the disposal of Office Complex 375000.00 Total -4500000.00 835500.00 4637340.00 PVF @11.39% 1.000 0.898 0.806 PV of Cash Flows -4500000.00 750279.00 3737696.00 NPV -12,025 (b) Evaluation of Project from CCS Point of View (i) Assuming that inflow funds are transferred in the year in which same are generated i.e. first year and second year. Year 0 1 2 Cash Flows (CN¥) -4500000.00 835500.00 4637340.00 Exchange Rate (Rs./ CN¥) 9.50 9.67 9.85 Cash Flows (Rs) -42750000.00 8079285.00 45677799.00 PVF @ 12% 1.00 0.893 0.797 -42750000.00 7214802.00 36405206.00 NPV 870008.00 (ii) Assuming that inflow funds are transferred at the end of the project, i.e. second year. Year 0 2 Cash Flows (CN¥) -4500000.00 5472840.00 Exchange Rate (Rs/ CN¥) 9.50 9.85 Cash Flows (Rs) -42750000.00 53907474.00 PVF 1.00 0.797 -42750000.00 42964257.00 NPV 214257.00 Though in terms of CN¥ the NPV of the project is negative but in Rs. it has positive NPV due to weakening of Rs. in comparison to CN¥. Thus, CCS Technologies can accept the project.
