<!-- Page Start: text/part0231.html -->
Capital Budgeting for International Project... | 217 10.2.4 Direct and Indirect Channels for International Portfolio Investment Domestically, we have direct equity, equity MF, debt MF, NPS, PPF, bank FDs, SCSS, (senior citizen’s saving scheme), company deposits etc. Internationally, we have American Depository Receipts (ADRs), Global Depository Receipts (GDRs), Foreign Direct Investing, Global Mutual Funds, Exchange-Traded Funds (ETFs) and Multinational Corporations (MNCs). In case of indirect channel, the investment is made into certain intermediary like mutual funds who, in turn, invest into various sectors. By opting for direct channel, we use ADR or GDR issued by corporates, FDI (Foreign Direct Investment) route where the investee company uses the funds for its own financial requirements. There are number of sites which allow you to invest online. 10.2.5 Exchange and Country Risk These risks are typical to foreign investment. Exchange risk relates to the value of your investment going down in terms the local currency due to depreciation of local currency or appreciation of foreign currency of the investee country. Currency valuations are affected by many local or international events or economic performance of the concerned countries.Wars,sanctions, embargo, pandemic, fiscal policies, etc. are some of the factors. Country risk includes political developments, liberalisation or restrictions on free trade, local laws, regulations and legal system, social or political turbulence or instability, revolts, elections and change of governments. 10.2.6 Return and Risk of Foreign Investment The Return of foreign investment, like any other investment, depends on the type of the asset viz. liquid, growth or long term with risk. More liquid the investment, lesser is the return because liquidity ensures less risk. It also depends on the product as well as structuring of the same. While secured assets give less return, the unsecured one gives you better return. An investment in a country with stable or strong foreign exchange rate performance, will provide better returns. Dividend accumulations or reinvestment plans provide higher returns than from those where dividends are distributed. Compared to domestic one, an international investment can offer better returns as the countries of investment, generally, are well or better managed economies. Apart from the usual Risk any investment poses, the international investment is also subject to exchange and country risk.War, economic policies,sanctions, form of country governance, social goals etc. determine economic and trade policies, having bearing on the investment climate and returns, as well as risk. 10.2.7 Capital Asset Pricing Model The abbreviated term CAPM describes the relationship between the risk and the returns or specifically, between the systemic risk and expected returns. The returns are always based on the risk and the time value of money. For pricing of a particular security or investment product, one has to undertake quite a few analyses. CAPM is one such method. This is generally done for risky assets, so that the price paid is appropriated to generate expected returns. Basically, one would like to find out risk free return over a time. A beta is generated, which is a measure of volatility or the systemic risk compared to the market as a whole. A security beta is calculated by dividing the product of the co-variance of the security’s returns and the market returns by the variance of the of the market returns over a specified period. If it was possible to accurately forecast future cash flows, this type of derivative method would not be necessary. However, that not being the case, an investor would like to depend on such pricing method.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0232.html -->
218 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT Calculation of CAPM Cost of Equity (re) = Risk-Free Rate + Levered Beta x Market Risk Premium Cost of Equity (re) = rf + βL x (rm – rf) where: rf = risk-free rate βL = levered beta rm = expected return on the market rm – rf = market risk premium Risk-Free Rate (rf): The expected rate of return on an investment in a security considered to have no inherent risks is referred to as the risk-free rate. The actual risk-free rate that is used in CAPM shifts depending on the yields that are currently available for the selected security. Market Risk Premium (rm – rf or mrp) The difference between the expected return on an investment and the risk-free rate is known as the market risk premium. Beta (β) The S&P 500 index has traditionally been used as a stand-in for the market when calculating beta, which is a measure of the co-variance between the rate of return on a company’s stock and the return on the overall market (systematic risk). 10.2.8 Arbitrage Pricing Theory This is an alternative method to CAPM. While CAPM takes into account security returns and market returns, this method or theory goes beyond it, thinking that market sometimes misprices securities. APT, therefore, tries to take advantages of any or many arbitrage opportunities or derivatives in the market or the economy. It uses the linear relationship between the asset’s expected return and a number of macroeconomic factors or variables that affect or capture the systemic risk. GDP, Domestic Inflation Rate, Stock Indices, Gold Prices, risk free rate of interest are such factors. ILLUSTRATION 2 For example, the following four factors have been identified as explaining a stock’s return and its sensitivity to each factor and the risk premium associated with each factor have been calculated: (RP means Risk Premium) • Gross domestic product (GDP) growth: ß = 0.6, RP = 4% • Inflation rate: ß = 0.8, RP = 2% • Gold prices: ß = -0.7, RP = 5% • Sensex index return: ß = 1.3, RP = 9% • The risk-free rate is 3% Using the APT formula, the expected return is calculated as: • Expected return = 3% + (0.6 x 4%) + (0.8 x 2%) + (-0.7 x 5%) + (1.3 x 9%) = 15.2% Using APT, arbitrageurs can take advantage of any deviations from fair market value


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0233.html -->
Capital Budgeting for International Project... | 219 10.3 INTERNATIONAL CAPITAL BUDGETING Having learnt about international investment, risk and return management and pricing, let’s now turn to the capital budgeting in international scenario. Capital budgeting will be necessary while considering establishing a project there. The reasons for a project abroad can be like tapping the local market of a foreign country, manufacturing something there which can be consumed here as consumption item or raw material or by using the local resources abroad, capture international market. In all the cases, however, the requirement of capital budgeting will surely arise. 10.3.1 Project Evaluation Project evaluation is a strategy that can be used to determine whether a project, programme, or policy is going to be successful and what impact it shall have. In order to conduct an analysis of the process and results of a particular project, it is necessary for the evaluator to gather relevant information. Evaluation of a project may lead to changes being made to the internal workflows, the identification of patterns among the project’s intended audience, the formulation of plans for future projects, or the reporting of the value of projects to external stakeholders. Let’stake an example. The demand of our product in the US has been steadily increasing and we feel that setting up a manufacturing plant in the US may help us supply goods smoothly and in large numbers. Let us assume further that estimated investment is about 100 lakh USD, the risk-free return in the US market is 4% and the risk-free return in India or Indian currency is 7%. We have to evaluate the project and decide whether it is worthwhile to take it up or not. In next few paragraphs, we will see how to evaluate such a project and how this evaluation is different from the evaluation of domestic projects, which we have discussed in detail, in the previous chapter. 10.4 ISSUES INVOLVED IN EVALUATION OF OVERSEAS PROJECTS While the methods of evaluation of overseas projects are the same as for the domestic projects, the following issues are involved: 1. Calculation of Discount rate: Arriving at an appropriate discount rate is essential for applying the discounting methods of project evaluation. While the risk-free interest/discount rate isreadily available in both India and the foreign country, we have to arrive at the relevant risk-adjusted discount rate. The method applied for this will be clear from the following Illustration: ILLUSTRATION: The following data is provided: a. The risk-free discount rate in USA is 4% b. The risk-free discount rate in India is 7% c. The risk-adjusted discount rate, required by the company in India is 12% We have to calculate the risk-adjusted discount rate in USA, which will be acceptable to the company. Solution: The formula used is, (1 + ra) = (1 + rf) * (1 + rp) Where, ra is the risk-adjusted discount rate, rf is the risk-free discount rate and rp is the risk premium


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0234.html -->
220 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT Applying this formula to the rupee discount rates, we calculate the risk premium, required by the company, as under: (1 + 0.12) = (1 + 0.07) * (1 + rp) or, (1 + rp) = 1.12/ 1.07 = 1.0467 Applying the same formula and using the value of rp, as arrived above, we can calculate the riskadjusted discount rate for US$, as under: (1 + ra) = (1 + rf) * (1 + rp) or, (1 + ra) = (1 + 0.04) * 1.0467 = 1.0888 So, the risk-adjusted discount rate, applicable for cash flows in US$, will be 8.88% Here we have assumed that the risk premium, required by the company for the US project is same as acceptable to it for a similar Indian project. However, in practice, it may require a higher risk premium in view of the additional risks involved like, trade barriers, currency fluctuations, stringent laws etc. 2. Estimating the exchange rate in future years of the project’s life: While evaluation of a domestic project issame as cash flows are in rupees and the discounted value is also calculated in rupees. But, in case of an overseas project, the cash flows are in foreign currency. So, before discounting, we will have to convert these cash flows in to rupees at the prevailing exchange rate, to arrive at the present value. This is because our initial investment in the project is valued at the present spot rate. The method applied for this will be clear from the following Illustration: ILLUSTRATION: The following data is provided: a. The notional risk-free interest rate in USA is 4% b. The notional risk-free interest rate in India is 7% c. Current Spot rate of 1 US$ is Rs. 80 We have to calculate the estimated spot rate of US$ at the end of each of the years of the project life. Solution: The formula used is, St = S0 * [(1 + rh)/ (1 + rf)]t Where, St is the spot rate of US$ at time t, S0 is the spot rate today. rh is the notional risk-free interest rate in India, rf is the risk-free interest rate in Foreign country (USA in this case) Applying this formula, we calculate the estimated spot rate for 1 US$, as under: S1 = 80 * (1 + 0.07)/ (1 + 0.04) or, S1 = 80 * (1.07)/ (1.04) = 80 * 1.0288 = Rs. 82.310 For second year, S2 = 80 * (1.0288)2 = 80 * 1.0585 = 84.6820 We can similarly calculate the estimated spot rate for the third year and further. 10.5 APPROACHES FOR EVALUATION OF OVERSEAS PROJECT There are, basically, two approaches for foreign project evaluation, viz. Home Currency Approach and Foreign Currency Approach. 1. Home Currency Approach Under this approach, all the cash flows of the project are converted in to home currency (rupee)


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0235.html -->
Capital Budgeting for International Project... | 221 by applying the actual/estimated spot rate at the time of the cash flow. These cash flows are then discounted using the domestic risk-adjusted discount rate. This approach will be clear from the following Illustration: ILLUSTRATION: The following data is provided: a. The cash flows of the project are as under (in US$, lakh): Initial investment 100 First year net cash inflow 30 Second year net cash inflow 40 Third year net cash inflow 50 Fourth year net cash inflow 50 b. The risk-adjusted rupee discount rate, required by the company, which is envisaging project in USA, is 12% c. The notional risk-free interest rate in USA is 4% d. The notional risk-free interest rate in India is 7% e. Current Spot rate of 1 US$ is Rs. 80 We have to calculate the PV of the cash inflows of the project which has a useful life of 4 years, using the Home Currency approach. Solution: We have to first calculate the estimated spot rate for 1 US$, as under: S1 = 80 * (1 + 0.07)/ (1 + 0.04) or, S1 = 80 * (1.07)/ (1.04) = 80 * 1.0288 = Rs. 82.310 For second year, S2 = 80 * (1.0288)2 = 80 * 1.0585 = 84.6820 For third year, S3 = 80 * (1.0288)3 = 80 * 1.0889 = 87.1130 For fourth year, S4 = 80 * (1.0288)4 = 80 * 1.1203 = 89.6250 Therefore, the cash inflows, converted in to rupees, will be, 30 *82.310, 40 *84.6820, 50 * 87.1130, and 50 * 89.6250 Or, Rs. 2469, v 3387, Rs. 4356, and Rs. 4481 respectively in the first,second, third and the fourth year. These cash inflows will have to be discounted by the rupee risk-adjusted discount rate to arrive at their Present Value (PV). So, PV = 2469/1.12 + 3387/(1.12)2 + 4356/(1.12)3 + 4481/(1.12)4 Or, PV = 2204 + 2700 + 3100 + 2848 = 10,852 So, the Present Value of all the cash inflows of the project is Rs. 10,852 2. Foreign Currency Approach Under this approach, the cash flows of the project remain in the foreign currency only and are not converted in to home currency (rupee). These cash flows are then discounted, using the risk-adjusted discount rate of the foreign currency. The present value of the discounted cash flow, thus arrived, is converted in to home currency by applying the present spot rate. This approach will be clear from the following Illustration: ILLUSTRATION: The following data is provided: a. The cash flows of the project are as under (in US$, lakh): Initial investment 100 First year net cash inflow 30


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0236.html -->
222 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT Second year net cash inflow 40 Third year net cash inflow 50 Fourth year net cash inflow 50 b. The risk-free discount rate in USA is 4% c. The risk-free discount rate in India is 7% d. The risk-adjusted discount rate, required by the company, in India, is 12% e. Current Spot rate of 1 US$ is Rs. 80 We are required to calculate the PV of the cash inflows of the project, which has a useful life of 4 years, using the Foreign Currency approach. Solution: We will have to first calculate the risk-adjusted discount rate for US$, which will be acceptable to the company. Applying the formula, given in the earlier paragraph, we calculate the risk premium, required by the company, as under: (1 + 0.12) = (1 + 0.07) * (1 + rp) or, (1 + rp) = 1.12/ 1.07 = 1.0467 Applying the same formula and using the value of rp, as arrived above, we can calculate the riskadjusted discount rate for US$, as under: (1 + ra) = (1 + rf) * (1 + rp) or, (1 + ra) = (1 + 0.04) * 1.0467 = 1.0888 So, the risk-adjusted discount rate, applicable for cash flows in US$, will be 8.88% or say, 8.9%. Now, we will discount the cash inflows in US$, using this risk-adjusted discount rate for US$, to arrive at the present value of the cash inflows PV = 30/1.089 + 40/(1.089)2 + 50/(1.089)3 + 50/(1.089)4 = 27.55 + 33.73 + 38.72 + 35.55 = 135.55 So, the Present Value of all the cash inflows of the project is US$ 135.55 lakh Applying the spot rate given, this is equal to 80 * 135.55 = Rs. 10,844 10.6 EVALUATION METHODS We will not go into the details of the methods as these are already discussed in the previous chapter. We will only illustrate, how the Home Currency approach and the Foreign Currency approach are applied to these methods. For this, we will use the same project which is mentioned in the above illustrations. 10.6.1 Evaluation methods using Home Currency Approach 1. Pay- back period method Step 1. Estimate the spot rate for each year of the project’s life by using the formula, St = S0 * [(1 + rh)/ (1 + rf )]t Step 2. Convert foreign currency cash inflows in to home currency inflows, by using the spot rates arrived at in step 1 Step 3. Convert initial investment in foreign currency in to home currency by using actual present spot rate Step 4. Find out the period during which the cash inflows in home currency are equal to the initial investment in home currency. This is the pay-back period.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0237.html -->
Capital Budgeting for International Project... | 223 ILLUSTRATION: In the example given in 10. 5 above, the cash flows in home currency are calculated as under: Initial investment Rs. 8,000 lakh First year cash inflow Rs. 2469 lakh Second year cash inflow Rs. 3387 lakh Third year cash inflow Rs. 4356 lakh Fourth year cash inflow Rs. 4481 lakh So, the pay-back period is 2 + (1144/ 4356) =2. 26 years or, 2 years and 3.15 months 2. NPV method Step 1. Estimate the spot rate for each year of the project’s life by using the formula, St = S0 * [(1 + rh)/ (1 + rf)]t Step 2. Convert foreign currency cash inflows in to home currency inflows, by using the spot rates arrived at in step 1 Step 3. Convert initial investment in foreign currency in to home currency by using actual present spot rate Step 4. Discount the cash inflows using the domestic risk-adjusted discount rate and find their PV. Step 5. Calculate NPV by subtracting initial investment from the PV ILLUSTRATION: In the example given in 10.5 above, the PV of the cash flows in home currency is calculated as Rs. 10,852 lakh. So, NPV is 10,852 - 8000 = Rs. 2,852 lakh 3. Internal Rate of Return Method Step 1. Estimate the spot rate for each year of the project’s life by using the formula, St = S0 * [(1 + rh)/ (1 + rf)]t Step 2. Convert foreign currency cash inflows in to home currency inflows, by using the spot rates arrived at in step 1 Step 3. Convert initial investment in foreign currency in to home currency by using actual present spot rate Step 4. By trial and error, find that discount rate which makes the PV of cash inflows equal to the initial investment ILLUSTRATION: In the example given in 10. 5 above, the cash flows in home currency are calculated as under: Initial investment Rs. 8,000 lakh First year cash inflow Rs. 2469 lakh Second year cash inflow Rs. 3387 lakh Third year cash inflow Rs. 4356 lakh Fourth year cash inflow Rs. 4481 lakh So, use the following formula to calculate IRR (r)of the project: 8000 = 2469/(1 + r) + 3387/(1 + r)2 + 4356/(1 + r)3 + 4481/(1 + r)4 By trial and error, the rate is about 27% 4. Profitability Index Method Step 1. Estimate the spot rate for each year of the project’s life by using the formula, St = S0 * [(1 + rh)/ (1 + rf )]t


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0238.html -->
224 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT Step 2. Convert foreign currency cash inflows in to home currency inflows, by using the spot rates arrived at in step 1 Step 3. Convert initial investment in foreign currency in to home currency by using actual present spot rate Step 4. Discount the cash inflows using the domestic risk-adjusted discount rate and find their PV. Step 5. Calculate PI by dividing PV by the initial investment ILLUSTRATION: In the example given in 10. 5 above, the PV of the cash flows in home currency is calculated as Rs 10,852 lakh. So, PI = PV/ Initial investment = 10,852/ 8000 = 1.356 10.6.2 Evaluation methods using Foreign Currency Approach 1. Pay- back period method Step 1. Find out the period during which the cash inflows in foreign currency are equal to the initial investment in foreign currency. This is the pay-back period ILLUSTRATION: In the example given in 10.5 above, the cash flows in foreign currency are calculated as under: Initial investment US$ 100 lakh First year cash inflow US$ 30 lakh Second year cash inflow US$ 40 lakh Third year cash inflow US$ 50 lakh Fourth year cash inflow US$ 50 lakh So, the pay-back period is 2 + (30/ 50) =2. 6 years or 2 years and 7.2 months 2. NPV method Step 1. First calculate the risk-adjusted discount rate for foreign currency, which will be acceptable to the company, by using the formula; (1 + ra) = (1 + rf) * (1 + rp) Step 2. Discount the cash inflows in foreign currency, using this risk-adjusted discount rate for foreign currency, to arrive at the present value of the cash inflows in foreign currency. Step 3. Convert the figure, arrived at Step 2 to home currency by multiplying it by the present spot rate. Step 4. Convert the initial investment to home currency by multiplying it by the present spot rate Step 5. Calculate NPV by subtracting initial investment from the PV ILLUSTRATION: In the example given in 10.5 above, the risk-adjusted discount rate, applicable for cash flowsin US$, is calculated to be 8.88% or say, 8.9%. The PV, of the cash inflows, discounted by 8.9% is: PV = 30/1.089 + 40/(1.089)2 + 50/(1.089)3 + 50/(1.089)4 = 27.55 + 33.73 + 38.72 + 35.55 = US$ 135.55 lakh. Applying the spot rate given, this is equal to 80 * 135.55 = Rs. 10,844 lakh The initial investment is 100 *80 = Rs. 8000 lakh So, the NPV is 10,844 – 8000 = Rs. 2,844 lakh


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0239.html -->
Capital Budgeting for International Project... | 225 3. Internal Rate of Return Method Step 1. First calculate the risk-adjusted discount rate for foreign currency, which will be acceptable to the company, by using the formula; (1 + ra) = (1 + rf) * (1 + rp) Step 2. By trial and error, find out that rate of discount which makes PV of cash inflows in foreign currency, equal to initial investment in foreign currency. This is the IRR of the project. Step 3. If the rate calculated in step 2 is more than that in step 1, the proposal is acceptable. ILLUSTRATION: In the example given in 10.5 above, the risk-adjusted discount rate, applicable for cash flowsin US$, is calculated to be 8.88% or say, 8.9%. For finding IRR (r), use the formula; 80 = 30/(1 + r) + 40/(1 + r)2 + 50/(1 + r)3 + 50/(1 + r)4 By trial and error, the value of r can be found, which will be about 35% 4. Profitability Index Method Step 1. First calculate the risk-adjusted discount rate for foreign currency, which will be acceptable to the company, by using the formula; (1 + ra) = (1 + rf) * (1 + rp) Step 2. Discount the cash inflows in foreign currency, using this risk-adjusted discount rate for foreign currency, to arrive at the present value of the cash inflows in foreign currency. Step 3. Convert the figure, arrived at Step 2 to home currency by multiplying it by the present spot rate. Step 4. Convert the initial investment to home currency by multiplying it by the present spot rate Step 5. Divide the figure of step 3 by the figure of step 4 to arrive at the PI ILLUSTRATION: In the example given in 10.5 above, the risk-adjusted discount rate, applicable for cash flowsin US$, is calculated to be 8.88% or say, 8.9%. The PV, of the cash inflows, discounted by 8.9% is: PV = 30/1.089 + 40/(1.089)2 + 50/(1.089)3 + 50/(1.089)4 = 27.55 + 33.73 + 38.72 + 35.55 = US$ 135.55 lakh. Applying the spot rate given, this is equal to 80 * 135.55 = Rs. 10,844 lakh The initial investment is 100 *80 = Rs. 8000 lakh So, the PI is 10,844/ 8000 = 1.356 10.7 IMPACT OF TRANSFER PRICING One cannot forget to ascertain the impact of Transfer Pricing on the cross-country investment decisions. Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Deployment (OECD) has been trying and successfully so, to eliminate unfair trade practices in the international trade or transactions. Many wealthy and powerful multinational corporations have managed to earn huge amount of profits and parked them in countries or domains having minimum or no taxes. These are known as tax havens. One of the measures taken by OECD is to recommend Transfer Pricing rules. These rules apply to all international transactions that take place between two related parties. Most of the countries have incorporated such rules in their tax laws. It is believed that all corporates will so plan that in internal transitions with related parties, they will manipulate the rates or charges in such a manner that the entity located in tax haven will amass more


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0240.html -->
226 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT profits. The Transfer Pricing Rules put all such transactions under strict scrutiny and impose financial burden if the rules are breached. The financial cost can sometimes be prohibitive. Therefore, suitable study and analysis are needed while making international investment. LET US SUM UP Capital budgeting for international projects or investment requires deep analysis of the Investment. The issues faced by an international entity are more and quite different as well as difficult compared to a domestic businessman. These arise out of culture, legal system, currency denomination, accounting standards, trade barriers, local language, and so on. The International Portfolio Investment (IPI) involves investment into the foreign securities which can be in various forms such as Treasury bills, mutual funds, listed equities, debt instruments etc. This involves cross country remittance through designated international fund transfer channels. The remittance will always be in local currency of the country where the investment takes place. In short term investments, the risk is higher because of events creating uncertainty in the political or economic field may likely crop up. Foreign country may impose price controls with a view to achieve their fiscal and monetary policy or social objectives. Exchange rate fluctuations also occur for reasons other than stock performance or economic growth. The risks of investing abroad include Exchange and Country Risk as well as Return and Risk of Foreign Investment. Also Liquidity and Return of foreign investments depend on the type of asset viz. liquid, growth or with risk. An investment in a country with stable of strong foreign exchange rate performance will provide better returns. Dividend accumulations or reinvestment plans provide higher returns than from those where dividends are distributed. International investment can offer better returns asthe country of investment generally are well or better managed economies. A beta is generated which is a measure of volatility or the systemic risk compared to the market as a whole. The actual risk-free rate that is used in CAPM shifts depending on the yields available for the selected security. Using APT, arbitrageurs can take advantage of any deviations from fair market value. It usesthe linear relationship between an asset’s expected return and a number of macroeconomic factors such as GDP, inflation rate, gold prices and the risk free rate of interest. Project evaluation is a strategy that can be used to determine whether a project, programme, or policy is going to be successful and what impact it shall have on the society as a whole. If we find the NPV is equal to 0, then the project will neither add value nor are you likely to lose. Positive NPV means project can be given go ahead and the negative is red signal. The Profitability Index is the next step forward after NPV. When a project has a positive net present value (NPV), it should be accepted. If the IRR is lower than the cost of capital, the project should be abandoned. The IRR rule states that if the internal rate of return on a project is greater than the minimum required rates of return, then the project or investmentshould proceed. One cannot forget to ascertain the impact of Transfer Pricing on the cross-country investment decisions. Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Deployment (OECD) has been trying and successfully so, to eliminate unfair trade practices in the international, trade or transactions.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0241.html -->

STRUCTURE 11.0 Objectives 11.1 Introduction 11.2 Sources & Perspectives on Risk 11.3 Sensitivity Analysis 11.4 Scenario Analysis 11.5 Hillier Model 11.6 Simulation Analysis 11.7 Decision Tree Analysis 11.8 Corporate Risk Analysis 11.9 Managing Risk 11.10 Project Selection under Risk 11.11 Risk Analysis in Practice Let Us Sum Up Keywords Check Your Progress Solutions U N I T 11 ADJUSTMENT OF RISK AND UNCERTAINTY IN CAPITAL BUDGETING DECISION


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0244.html -->
230 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 11.0 OBJECTIVES After studying this unit, you will have better understanding of: • Sources & Perspectives on Risk • Sensitivity Analysis • Scenario Analysis • Simulation Analysis • Decision Tree Analysis • Corporate Risk Analysis • Risk Analysis in Practice 11.1 INTRODUCTION Capital budgeting is required for project implementation which generally takes long time. Budgeting means estimating cost and revenue to be incurred and earned in the future period whether it is a project to be established or is a running enterprise. The word capital budgeting is for new projects generally. Cost includes land, plant, labour, materials and engagement of working capital. Capital or cash has its own cost depending on time, volume, source including foreign sourcing. Revenue estimating exercise is also notsimple where it is dependent on many factorsincluding market behaviour and competition. Each element of cost and revenue, therefore, poses many questions and uncertainty. Moreover, if the investment is cross country, complications multiply. All these factors increase the intensity and impact of risk on the project, which one must consider, while preparing the capital budget. The risk and uncertainty that exist in multiple forms need to be considered. Such risk analysis is the most complex and a very slippery aspect of capital budgeting. Certain riskslike scenario, decisions, breakeven,situation are standalone and certain are contextual which arise due to method, mode or form such as corporate risk or market risk. 11.2 SOURCES & PERSPECTIVES ON RISK Some projects like those depending on high technology, or in a politically unstable country, using hazardous chemicals or polluting ones, have specific risks. Risks faced in such situations are called project specific risks. When we opt for a project for products having huge demand that involves no rocket science, there is every possibility of immense competition once the demand starts increasing exponentially. There are areas of business which are season based or dependent on policy decisions by the authorities, where risk exists in a different form. If you produce on a large scale, you will have to go out from local to national to international arena, which will pose marketing risk. Moreover, going international will confront you with unknown rules, regulations, culture, habits and market sensitivity. In such scenario, the exchange risk peeps in. Thus, one has to perceive the risks in various standalone forms and various sources like market or forms or method of conducting business. All the risks have the propensity to impact sales, cost and profitability. For example; a plant using hazardous chemicals will have to submit for strict scrutiny before, as well as during operations, and in case of any laxity, it may be ordered to close down immediately like we saw in Vedanta’s Sterlite Copper Plant in Tamil Nadu. A plant generating pollution may have to spend huge amount on pollution control equipment apart from intermittent scrutiny and looming threat of suspension of activity. Corporate fights and takeover bids also affect the performance of a unit e.g. Indigo Airlines or Future Retail. Having generally discussed and acknowledging the importance of accounting for risk while capital budgeting, we will now discuss various perspectives.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0245.html -->
Adjustment of Riskand Uncertaintyin Capital... | 231 11.3 SENSITIVITY ANALYSIS The uncertainty of future movements of certain variables can move either way, that puts all our present estimates and the project or investmentsto jeopardy. We need to safeguard against this. The variables and important components of capital budget are cost, revenue and net profits. Taking all possible variables into account will be a difficult task, making calculations complex. Sensitivity analysis aims to assess the impact of changes in each of these important variables on our projections or estimates. To explain the sensitivity analysis due to such variations, let’s take a simple example of a project to be implemented. Table A below gives brief but important details of a PET Tube manufacturing project. Table A Rs. in Lakhs Year 0 Years 1-10 Investment (40,000) Sales 36,000 Variable Costs 24,000 Fixed Costs 2,000 Depreciation @10% of project cost 4,000 Profit before Tax 6,000 Tax @ 33.33% 2,000 Profit after Tax 4,000 Cash flow from operations 8,000 Net cash flow (40,000) 8,000 On a total investment of Rs. 40,000 lakhs, targeted sales are Rs.36,000 lakhs. The PAT (Profit After Tax) is Rs.4,000 lakhsresulting into net cash flow from operations at Rs.8,000 lakhs. Considering discounting rate of 12% and project term as 10 years, the Net Present Value (NPV) works out to Rs.5,200 lakhs. This is a normal scenario without getting in to risk arising out of uncertainty. Now if we consider the best and the worst scenarios in the case of investment, sales, variable cost and fixed cost; the results give you surprises. To calculate the impact of changes in one variable, we presume that the other variables remain constant. The Table B below shows that in the worst scenario, if the investment cost goes up to Rs.48,000 lakhs, the NPV nose dives to -1300 (negative). Similarly, if the sales fall to Rs.30,000 Lakhs, the NPV falls to -2340(negative).


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0246.html -->
232 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT Table B Rs. (Lakhs) Rs. (Lakhs) Range NPV Key Variable Pessimistic Expected Optimistic Pessimistic Expected Optimistic Investment 48,000 40,000 36,000 -1,300 5,200 8,440 Sales 30,000 36,000 42,000 -2,340 5,200 12800 Variable cost as% of sales 70 66.67 65 680 5,200 7460 Fixed Costs 2600 2000 1600 2940 5,200 6706 We can, therefore, say that the sensitivity analysis is a good method of assessing the risk, enabling us to find out how robust or weak the project is. If we find such impact and identify the vulnerable aspects, we can change our plan or make amendments to contain the risk. This method can thus be employed to evaluate the budget and the project to a higher degree of risk management. The limitation of this method is that it only shows the NPV and only one variable is changed at a time. To an extent this method is also subjective because it depends on the perception of the decision maker of the analyst. Steps involved in Sensitivity Analysis The steps that need to be taken in order to perform a sensitivity analysis are as follows: 1. Identifying the factors that have an impact on the NPV (or IRR) of the project. 2. Developing a mathematical understanding of the connections between the different variables. 3. Conducting an analysis to determine how the changes in each of the variables will affect the net present value (or internal rate of return) of the project. ILLUSTRATION 1 A Ltd. is considering its new project with the following details: Sr. No. Particulars Figures 1 Initial capital cost Rs. 400 Cr. 2 Annual unit sales 5 Cr. 3 Selling price per unit Rs. 100 4 Variable cost per unit Rs. 50 5 Fixed costs per year Rs. 50 Cr. 6 Discount Rate 6% Required: 1. Calculate the Net Present Value (NPV) of the project. Assume that the tax rate is zero. 2. Compute the impact on the project’s NPV considering a 2.5 per cent adverse variance in each variable. Which variable shall have the maximum effect? Consider Life of the project as 3 years.

F Fixed cost per year Rs. 50 Cr. G Net cash inflow per year (E - F) Rs. 200 Cr. Calculation of Net Present Value (NPV) of the Project Year Year Cash Flow (Rs. in Cr.) D Number of units sold per year (units in Crores) 5 5 5 5 5 4.875 E Total Contribution (C × D) 250 250 237.5 243.75 250 243.75 F Fixed cost per year 50 50 50 50 51.25 50 G Net Cash Inflow per year (E - F) 200 200 187.5 193.75 198.75 193.75 H PV of Net cash Inflow per year (G × 2.673) 534.60 534.60 501.19 517.89 531.26 517.89 I Initial capital cost 400 410 400 400 400 400 J NPV (H - I) 134.60 124.60 101.19 117.89 131.26 117.89 K Percentage Change in NPV - -7.43% -24.82% -12.41% -2.48% -12.41% ILLUSTRATION 2 Analyse how sensitive a project isto changesin initial project cost, annual cash inflow, and cost of capital using the following information regarding the project.: Initial Project Cost (Rs.) 1,20,000 Annual Cash Inflow (Rs.) 45,000 Project Life (Years) 4 Cost of Capital 10% Identify which of the three factors, the project is most sensitive to, if the variable is adversely affected by 10%? Adjustment of Riskand Uncertaintyin Capital... | 235 Situation NPV Changes in NPV Base (present) Rs. 22,605 - If initial project cost is varied adversely by 10% i.e. it becomes Rs. 1,32,000 (Rs. 1,20,000 × 1.10). (1,42,605 – 1,32,000) = Rs. 10,605 (22,605 – 10,605)/Rs. 22,605 = (53.08%) If annual cash inflow is varied adversely by 10% i.e. it becomes Rs. 40,500 (Rs. 45,000 × 0.9). [40,500 × 3.169) – (1,20,000)] = Rs. 8,345 (22,605 – 8,345)/Rs. 22,605 = 63.08% If cost of capital is varied adversely by 10% i.e. it becomes 11%. (45,000 × 3.103) – 1,20,000 = Rs. 19,635 (22,605 – 19,635)/22,605 = 13.14% Conclusion: Project is most sensitive to ‘annual cash inflow’. 11.4 SCENARIO ANALYSIS This method is an extension of or a step forward compared to the sensitivity analysis where only one variable was changed at a time. In this method, one plans for say, three scenarios namely, normal or the expected, optimistic and the pessimistic scenario. In the normalscenario, all the variablesshow expected values and the best values are taken for the optimistic scenario. It is in the pessimistic scenario the worst values are placed. Thus, all the variables move in the same directions at the same time. To explain the analysis in figures, the following table is presented. Table C (Rs. in lakhs) Pessimistic Scenario Expected Scenario Optimistic Scenario Investment 48,000 40,000 36,000 Sales 30,000 36,000 42,000 Variable Costs 21,000 (70%) 24,000 (66.67%) 27,300 (65%) Fixed Costs 2,600 2,000 1,600 Depreciation 4,800 4,000 3,600 Pre-Tax profit 1,600 6,000 9,500 Tax 540 2,000 3,160 Profit after Tax 1,060 4,000 6,340 Annual Cash flow from operations 5,860 8,000 9,940 Net present value Cash Flow X PVIFA (12%, 10 years) (14,900) 5,200 10,080 However, someone may ask, will all the variables move in the same direction at the same time? The answer may not be in affirmative.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0250.html -->
236 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT ILLUSTRATION 3 XYZ Ltd. is considering a project “A” with an initial outlay of Rs. 14,00,000 and the possible three cash inflow attached with the project as follows: (Rs. ‘000) Particulars Year 1 Year 2 Year 3 Worst case 450 400 700 Most likely 550 450 800 Best case 650 500 900 Determine the net present value of each scenario based on the assumption that the cost of capital is 9 percent. If XYZ Ltd is sure about the most likely result in the first two years but uncertain about the cash flow in the third year, then analyse what the NPV will be assuming the worst case scenario in the third year. SOLUTION: The possible outcomes will be as follows: Year PVF @ 9% Worst Case Most likely Best case Cash Flow PV Cash Flow PV Cash Flow PV (Rs. ‘000) (Rs. ‘000) (Rs. ‘000) (Rs. ‘000) (Rs. ‘000) (Rs. ‘000) 0 1 (1,400) (1,400) (1,400) (1,400) (1,400) (1,400) 1 0.917 450 412.65 550 504.35 650 596.05 2 0.842 400 336.80 450 378.90 500 421.00 3 0.772 700 540.40 800 617.60 900 694.80 NPV -110.15 100.85 311.85 If XYZ Ltd. is certain about the most likely result in first two years but uncertain about the third year’s cash flow, then, NPV, expecting worst case scenario in the third year, will be as follows: = (Rs. 14,00,000) + Rs. 5,50,000 X 1/(1.09) + Rs. 4,50,000 X 1/(1.09)2 + Rs. 7,00,000 X 1/(1.09)3 = (Rs. 14,00,000) + Rs. 5,04,587 + Rs. 3,78,756 + Rs. 5,40,528 = Rs. 23,871 11.5 HILLIER MODEL An investor applies varioustechniques of analyzing the risk involved in any particular investment decision. Hillier’s Model is one amongst many of these risk analysis techniques. This model is also based on NPV like earlier methods. Prof. Fredrick. S. Hillier of Stanford University suggested this model. Hillier’s view wasthat the risk associated with the cash inflowsisreflected in the standard deviation of the cash inflows. Lesser the deviation of cash flows from the mean value, the lesser would be the risk and vice versa. He argued that for risk analysis, value of standard deviation of net present value may be obtained through analytical deviation of the cash inflows. Two cases can be considered forsuch analysis viz. no correlation among cash flows and perfect correlation among cash flows. When cash flows for different years are perfectly correlated, the behaviour of cash flows in all years is alike. But, if they are not correlated, it


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0251.html -->
Adjustment of Riskand Uncertaintyin Capital... | 237 implies that cash inflow in any particular year will be independent of the cash inflow in any other year during the life of the project. The formulae, though bit complicated, are presented below. Perfectly Correlated Cash Flows NPV = + − = ∑ C i I t t t n 1 () 1 NPV = + − = ∑ C i I t t t n 1 () 1 σ σ () () / NPV = +       = ∑ t t t n i 2 2 1 1 2 1 σ σ () () NPV = = + ∑ t t t n 1 i 1 Uncorrelated Cash Flows where Ct is the expected cash flow for year t, σt is the standard deviation of cash flow for year t, i is the risk-free rate, and I is the initial investment. 11.6 SIMULATION ANALYSIS Simulation means not real, imitation or deception. It is a process of creating a similar but artificial situation. What we do here is that firstly, we conduct analysis by finding out sensitivity of certain criteria of merit such as NPV, IRR, or any such criterion, to variation in basic factors. Then as a next step, we do simulations to find out likelihood under various scenarios to enable us to take the best suited decision. The steps involved can be briefly stated as under: • Prepare model project report on expected lines. • Calculate NPV. (it can be IRR too) • Find out how the NPV is influenced by or related to the parameters and the exogenous variables. • Parameters are input variables specified by the decision maker who is in charge of the project. • Exogenous variables are those whose value is determined outside the model and imposed on the model. These are not in control of the decision maker. This may probably depend on random event. For example, if we use seeds, fertilisers and water and then depend on rains to give us say 100 tons of a crop; the seeds, fertilisers, water and yield are endogenous and the rainfall is the exogenous factor which is not influenced by other variables. • Fix the values of the parameters and also estimate the probability of distributions of exogenous variables. • Select a value at random from the probability distributions of each of the exogenous variables. • Determine the NPV with relation to the randomly generated values of exogenous variables and the predetermined or specified parameter values. • Simulate the above exercise to any number of times to generate large number of simulated NPV. • Plot the frequency distribution of the NPV and then consider which simulation is most probable or suitable. Most of the simulations are done using computers because of computational tedium and the multiplicity. Manual calculations become lengthy and bit difficult. Thistool is versatile. Its capacity to handle multi factor project is a greatscoring point. It can deal with complex interrelationships among parameters and exogenous variables, which are otherwise difficult to deal with manually. Despite heavily relying on computer computations, it does not replace skilled


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0252.html -->
238 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT judgement which is required in selecting variables and combinations. Although this is a powerful tool, the treatment of correlations between variables remains a major problem. If correlations are not handled properly, it can give misleading conclusions. 11.7 DECISION TREE ANALYSIS Like scenario analysis, another method to help in corporate decision making is Decision Tree method, which a graphical representation of possible outcomes(with their associated probability), attached to each decision. So, there are two elements in a decision tree: 1. Branch, which represents a decision (which is an alternative course of action) and 2. Node, at the end of the branch, which represents the reward of the decision along with the probability attached to that reward. Example: A finance manager has to select either project A or project B. The reward of project A (NPV) is Rs. 5 lakh and the probability attached to it is 40%. The reward of project B (NPV) is Rs. 4 lakh and the probability attached to it is 40%. It is graphically represented by a simple decision tree which has only two branches. First branch represents the decision to go for project A and the second branch represents the decision to go for project B. The nodes at the end of each branch represent the outcome (NPV) and the probability. This decision tree will further grow, i.e. there will be more branches emanating from node of first and/or second branch, if there are further uncertainties associated with each decision (represented by branch). For example, project A can be executed either by the company itself or it may be executed by a contractor on turnkey basis. The cost associated with each decision, and the probability of its completion in time, are different in both the cases. So, graphic representation will be through two more branches out of the node of first branch, one representing decision of internal execution and the other turnkey contract. The nodes at the end of each of these additional branches will show the probability and cost of each decision. As further uncertainties are taken into account, the decision tree will grow bigger and bigger. The management has to decide the factors to be taken into account depending upon the size and importance of the project. The above process of preparing the tree is called “Drawing (or delineating) the decision tree”. Thisis first of the two stepsinvolved in the whole process of “Decision TreeAnalysis”. The otherstep is “Evaluating the outcome”. This process of evaluation startsfrom the last branch of the tree till we come to the starting point of the tree. We have already learnt in ScenarioAnalysis, the way to evaluate a situation. For example, if the probability associated with NPV of Rs. 5 lakh is 40% and that associated with NPV of Rs. 4 lakh is 60%, the estimated NPV is: (5 * 0.4) + (4 * 0. 6) = Rs. 4.4 lakh. We can continue to move backward in the decision tree, calculating the estimated NPV at each node, selecting the decision of higher value and discarding the other, till we come to the starting point. 11.8 CORPORATE RISK ANALYSIS Every existing business firm has its own unique risk profile for its cash flows. When a new project/ investment is envisaged, which has its own risk profile, the combined risk profile of the firm is likely to undergo a change. Corporate RiskAnalysisisthe evaluation of thisimpact of the new project/investment on the combined risk profile of the firm. A corporate faces variety of risks like economic, competition, financial, reputation, operational, compliance, security etc. It is to be evaluated what type of corporate risk analysis one hasto do while embarking up on a project and preparing capital budget. The project may


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0253.html -->
Adjustment of Riskand Uncertaintyin Capital... | 239 have impact, good or bad, on the corporate. For example, in a portfolio ofsecurities, when a new security is to be added, it may impact the overall profile of the portfolio due to a different standard deviation of its returns and the correlation of its returns with the returns on the other securities in the portfolio. Similarly, we may analyse what the risk connected with a project will mean in terms of corporate risk? A project is like a product in a bouquet of other projects carried out by the corporate.Aproject on a standalone basis or by itself may look risky or notso profitable. However, if you look in conjunction with the overall product or project profile of the corporate, it may be a complimentary one. Diversification, backward integration or a captive power plant cannot be viewed or reviewed independently. For example, a company engaged in toiletries may well find to also sell personal care products because the target customers are the same. Or, a continuous process plant, facing power shortages or frequent breakdowns, may find it absolutely necessary to start a captive power plant. It may be argued that a diversification strategy reduces the overall risk and sometimes protects the business from cyclical downfall when one project faces slump but the other has a healthy turnover. It has become a tendency, now a day, not to depend on one source of income. If not all the eggs in one basket, have three eggs in one. The corporate may enjoy a reasonable degree of stability.And, who knows the laggard of today may become a leader of tomorrow. However, in a corporate structure, this theory of diversification with an object of risk mitigation, may not find favour with all. The shareholders may try themselves to reap benefit of diversification by investing into other company who is a leader in that segment rather than allowing the present company venturing and risking into that field. You may find pressure on the share pricesif the disapproving shareholders decide to exit or offload the equity. The debate can go on. One has to decide based on the ground realities; risk, resources, expertise, competition, existing leaders, available in-house talent, cost of hiring or outsourcing are some of the issues that require consideration. After all, the corporate profile should not lose its identity and image and not substantially add risk to the profile. 11.9 MANAGING RISK Risk Management requires special skill, apart from the in-depth knowledge of the business. There are internal risks, as well as, external risks. There are controllable risks and also uncontrollable ones. While many risks can be eliminated by means of technology or change in system or methods, certain risks can only be mitigated. Based on the cost benefit analysis and the resource and technology options, a separate department, exclusively dealing with risk, may be formed. In the following paragraphs, we will discuss various elements or the factors or sources that determine the risk perception. The cost: This is the biggest variable that poses the highest degree of the risk. The cost can be fixed or variable. The fixed cost is plant, facilities, technology, or even manpower. Instead of building our own plant, we can get the product manufactured outside, like many Pharma companies do. Instead of employing permanent labour or staff we can hire through manpower agencies. Land and building can be owned or be acquired on lease. Variable cost, like raw material, can be efficiently managed by proper mix and replacements. You need deep knowledge about product, labour market, compensation packages, quality difference when raw materials are inter changed, labour laws compliance, reliability and availability of outsourcing agencies, cost escalations when hire or own options are taken. One has to identify many such factors which pose a risk and then plan a relevant risk strategy. Financial Leverage: Buy or lease isthe best example ofsuch strategy. They say don’t buy a buffalo when you can get uninterrupted milk supply from outside. Similarly, the manner or method of fund raising is important. The equity appears cheap or free of cost but servicing it by post tax profits and dividend is not


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0254.html -->
240 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT easy. Moreover, large equity poses a risk of dilution of control over management and ownership. On the other hand, too much of borrowing upsets the ideal debt equity ratio and brings down the profitability. All this can be managed if one fairly knows the finance cost, profitability and the promoter’s capacity and risk-taking abilities. Pricing: This is an issue which has internal as well as external factors to be studied and analysed. While the cost structure of the company may not allow pricing the product below a certain level, the competitors and market conditions may force you to peg down the price. One has to skilfully and accurately calculate the breakeven point and then determine a risk perceived price of the product. Sequential Investment: Based on current trend and business growth, many a times the management is tempted to decide about fresh investment. While this augmentsthe capacity and enhancesthe competitive strength, the strategy may hurt financially if the excess capacity turns into a bane instead of boon when market gets flooded with the goods or, the consumer preference suddenly changes due to innovation or other disruptive events or new enterprises entering the market. A decision tree analysis may help here. Information Intelligence: Never test the depth of the water with both the feet! Whether it is a new product or new technology or new territory, don’t jump start before you have done all your homework. Take help of expert or specialist to ensure that there is nothing amiss. Perfection is the only answer. Strategic Alliance: Risk taking capacity or appetite is never unlimited, however big the business group is. The sayings ‘keep the powder dry’ works here. An opportunity may come and you may have little time to think lest it may disappear. Therefore, one such strategy is to keep in your armour an array of connections and networks that are willing and capable of quickly partner with you to exploit such opportunities. You can keep the list of areas of interest and capabilities or expertise at instant access. Sometimes, it is worth keeping SPV with appropriate name and objects ready for quick decision. Here what one has to plan is to identify strength of such willing partners and keep handy draft MOU which can also help in hunting for the target. Insurance: Never miss any opportunity to hedge or insure all known risks, be it fire, flood, earth quake, riot, war, cybercrime. This is also an antidote and gives peace of mind. Supply chain management: To remove uncertainties of prices or supply and improve cost control, build up long term relations and have long term sustainable arrangements for sourcing key inputs. This is sometimes necessary for commodities where price fluctuations are wide and volatile. In case of imported materials, this is very necessary to insulate you from vagaries of international market mechanism and manipulations. Detailed study and deep knowledge about the market are a key to better planning and risk management. Derivatives: This is applicable to investment products and commodities. Futures and options are such products. One who buys an option is called the owner of the option. He has right or option to buy or sell an underlying product or asset, before a given date, at a predetermined price. Of course, he pays a premium while acquiring the right. An option to buy is a call option and sell option is called a put option. The owner can exercise the option in best possible way to minimise the cost of purchase or maximise sell price. Such derivatives can also be embedded in debt instrument where the borrower can opt for early repayment. These are innovative toolsto manage the risk but are riskier themselves, if not managed well, because of high leveraging. Shorter time to market: This is applicable when you innovate and launch a new product in the market. Research has proved thatshorter the time to market, higher the benefits you reap from the market, because


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0255.html -->
Adjustment of Riskand Uncertaintyin Capital... | 241 you will be early bird taking all the advantage. If average period for improved and new models for automobile product is four years and you come out within two years, you meet the market expectations that have built up in two years rather than that may build up in four years, which is less predictable. You also face less competition. Contingency planning: It will not always be possible to avoid or take care of all the risks. Therefore, you either try for risk mitigation or prepare a contingency plan to face and provide for it to a manageable extent. A detailed action plan, to deal with the events that may possibly happen, adversely affecting the working, and to cope with these events, can be drawn up with assigned duties to identified staff. This may keep the management ready to face and mitigate the risk. 11.10 PROJECT SELECTION UNDER RISK A project report, once ready, will tell us about what we can expect from the implementation which may include an NPV or IRR or any other criterion of merit. Further analysis will also reveal the major deviations under various scenarios, finally leading us to either go ahead with or reject the project. Thus, we face a situation where we have to take a decision under various risk probabilities. Under such circumstances, we follow the following process: a) Evaluation using our judgemental capabilities; though highly subjective, this method is generally the first to be resorted to. We are always proud and confident of our senior management which is generally a pool of talented executives having worked for decades in responsible positions, steering companies to success. It may be a project evaluation committee or budgeting committee or senior executive committee or the Board itself. No special methods or complicated calculations are deployed. It’s just brain storming. Some may be highly optimistic, some pessimistic, some having pecuniary interest or someone a devil’s advocate. All will debate and will be heard. Under discussion will be pros and cons, risk and rewards, side effects, collateral damages, competitor’s response, capital constraints, reputation risk and host of things. It will be free for all but finally a judgemental all things considered. This is what we in our personal life do choosing a career or a spouse! b) How quickly we become risk free: This is an important aspect of any large or risky project. We are always eager to be risk free which indirectly means when we get our money back. Larger the risk, shorter the period we would like to get out of it because longer the term, uncertainties increase and new unexpected events upset our plans. In other words, we presume that risk is a function of time. Therefore, the payback period determination guides us about acceptability of a project. By following this method for decision making, we are trying to control the risk by bringing it closer to reality and also bring an end to it. The opponents of this theory, particularly in investments in securities, argue that it is in the long term that a risk is evened out and the one who stays put has the last laugh. Hence this method may not be the only or the best method. c) Risk adjusted discount rate: When we make an investment in a project, we try to first find out what will be the return on it. For that purpose, we calculate NPV which has to be positive to fund the project. How do we work out NPV? We use a discount rate to measure the NPV. In the normal circumstances, if we do not anticipate any risk, we will adopt the average cost of capital as the discount rate. However, if we find that there is a possibility of certain risk occurrence, we need to provide for the same and we enhance the rate. This rate is the risk adjusted discount rate. We calculate the same in the following manner.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0256.html -->
242 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT rk = i + n + dk where rk represents the risk-adjusted discount rate for project k, i represents the risk-free interest rate, n represents the adjustment for the company’s normal risk, and dk represents the adjustment for project k’s differential risk. The firm’s cost of capital is measured by (i + n). The differential risk, dk, refers to the difference between the risk perception of project k and the existing risk profile of the firm. It may be positive or negative which is dependant on the outcome of the comparison between the risk of the project being considered and the risk presently faced by the firm. In a manner that is completely comprehensible, the adjustment for the differential risk of project k is contingent on how the management perceives the risk involved in the project as well as the attitude of the management toward risk (risk-return trade-off).A large Company like Reliance, for example, may use various risk-adjusted discount rates for various types of investments, which may include investments for replacement of plant and machinery, investments for expansion of the plant capacity, investment in similar lines of business and investment in new types of businesses. After the risk-adjusted discount rate (rk) for the project has been determined, the project will only be approved if the risk-adjusted discount rate allows for a positive net present value when the rate is used in the calculation. In actual businesssituations, the risk-adjusted discount rate istypically put into use.When considering various investment opportunities, businesses employ a variety of discount rates, each of which is supposedly correlated to a specific risk factor. When it comesto normal replacement investments, the discount rate is typically low; when it comes to expansion investments, the discount rate is moderate; and when it comes to new investments, the discount rate is typically high. In spite of its widespread use, the risk-adjusted discount rate method has two significant drawbacks: (i) It is difficult to reliably estimate dk, and as a result, its value is frequently arrived at in a manner that is very ad hoc and arbitrary. (ii) The second assumption made by this approach is that the level of risk remainsrelatively stable throughout time. It is possible that this assumption is not particularly valid. The adjustments can go on for specific risks like a greenfield project, expansion, new invention, price sensitivity of the product, cost related uncertainties etc. To the extent the risk related discount calculation poses difficulties and problems; this method is considered less reliable or practical. Moreover, this method presumes that the risk increases at a constant rate with time which may not be valid at all times. d) Certainty Expectations: When we face uncertainty or odds while expecting certain returns, it is possible that we may prefer certainty and settle down for a lower return. For example, we have two options on investments one 11% returns on 10000 invested and second 20% return on 100000 invested. Though both look good, we may settle down for a certain return of say 10% on any investment not exceeding 25000. This is called certainty equivalent method. This method deals with variability of outcomes and our attitude towards risk and we decide about an amount of return which is equivalent to certainty. Under the certainty equivalent method, the net present value is calculated as follows: CEC(NPV) = ÷ () + − , = ∑αt t t t t n A R 1 I 1  Where, CEC(NPV) = certainty equivalent value net present value;


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0257.html -->
Adjustment of Riskand Uncertaintyin Capital... | 243 t = a time index which varies from one to n; αt = certainty equivalent co-efficient in period t; At = annual expected after-tax cash flow in period t (which may have either positive or negative values) Rf = risk free rate of return which functions as the cost of capital n = the project’s expected life Io = the initial investment For calculating NPV using the above formula, we need to first calculate the certainty equivalent coefficient for the project. For this, we have to know the probability attached to each set of cash inflows and that amount of cash inflows with high certainty, which is acceptable for the project. Suppose, the first set of cash inflows of Rs. 10,000 has a probability of 0.6 and the second set of Rs. 15,000 has a probability of 0.4, and the cash inflow acceptable with high certainty is Rs. 11,000, then the certainty equivalent coefficient will be calculated as under; 11,000/ (10,000 * 0.6 + 15,000 * 0.4) = 11,000/ 12,000 = 0.92 The certainty equivalent method does not assume that risk increases with time at a constant rate. It is, therefore, conceptually better than the risk-adjusted discount rate method. In certainty equivalent method, each year’s certainty equivalent coefficient is calculated based on the level of risk characterising that particular year’s cash flow. But, the risk-adjusted discount rate method is more popular than the certainty equivalent method. This is perhaps because it is simple to adjust the discount rate but inconvenient and difficult to specify a series of certainty equivalent coefficients. 11.11 RISK ANALYSIS IN PRACTICE We have several methods of risk analysis. These contain various ways and complicated mathematical formulae, some of which are quite complex. However, while applying any such method, what we keep common in our mind is certain features of compilation. We will now coversome of these in the paragraphs to follow. 1. Revenue Estimation: The principle of accounting is conservatism. Revenues are not in our complete control and therefore one should be a bit conservative while estimating it. If we put in the expected revenue figures but certain unfavourable but unexpected events take place resulting into lower revenue, the whole project may get jeopardised. Therefore, one needs to justify the revenue budgets and avoid the optimism. 2. Cost Estimates: Here we follow the opposite thinking. We need to keep a safety margins from 10% to 30% depending up on the nature or type of cost. Raw material prices are volatile particularly if it comprises commodities. Prices of imported ones are too very unpredictable. The staff and labour cost depend upon the industry standards and the attrition cost as well as skill development are the factors to be considered. Therefore, the cost aspect is a committed amount unlike the revenue and requires margins for escalations. Above all there is a contingency cost which needs to be considered. Based on experience and prevailing circumstances,10% to 30% cushioning may be required to provide safety. 3. Flexibility in investing: A project, where to begin with, you had an X amount as investment in mind, may end up with X plus as we go and consider all aspects of revenue, cost and risk coverage. Therefore, the promoter has to take decision about a cut-off point beyond which he may not fund. However, these are management decisions and perceptions which may depend on risk taking abilities


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0258.html -->
244 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT and return expectations. It also depends on how long isthe wait for the return. Many times, therefore, short payback period is preferred to minimise the risk and also a cut-off point fixed in advance. The management can fix minimum attainable objectivesfor risk and return and a time frame for payback. 4. Sensitivity Analysis: It is time we put in practice what we have learnt in the forgoing paragraphs. Such analysis will help us to find out how robust or vulnerable the project is if we work on variables to test the impact of upward movement in cost and fall in revenue and add little more probable risk. The analysis will change our NPV or IRR and will help us to come to a conclusion in all fairness. 5. Scenario Analysis: Here too, we create variousscenariossuch as normal, optimistic and pessimistic onesstriking at the roots of our estimates. We discover a position which may or may not be acceptable or may need substantial adjustments in our expectations of returns or investment levels. Depending up on the industry or the product or the market or the location or the competition we need to create different scenarios of our estimates to reinsure that ultimately, we get a reasonable return, take lesser risk and have the quickest exit window. Based on a survey, the order of preference of the business for various techniques for project evaluation are Sensitivity analysis, Scenario analysis, and Risk adjusted discount rate and decision tree analysis. 6. Decision Making: (i) Decision Making using Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Analysis (ii) Decision Making using Relevant Cost Concepts (iii) Decision Making using Activity Based Costing (iv) Ethical and Non-Financial Considerations Relevant to Decision Making LET US SUM UP Capital budgeting is a must for project implementation. Budgeting means estimating cost and revenue to be incurred and earned in the future periods. Cost includes land, plant, labour, materials and engagement of working capital. Risk analysis is the most complex and a very slippery aspect of capital budgeting, for many projects. Some projects, such as in a politically unstable country, using hazardous chemicals or polluting ones, have specific risks. One has to perceive the risks in various standalone forms and various sourceslike market or forms or method of conducting business.All the risks have the propensity to impact sales, cost and profitability. Sensitivity Analysis and Scenario Building is an important part of Capital Budgeting decisions because risk and uncertainty are very important factors which need to be accorded appropriate weightage and significance while taking a call on capital investments. The model on risk analysis suggested by Prof. Fredrick. S. Hillier of Stanford University is quite important in this regard. Simulation means not real, imitation or deception and the Simulation techniques are also used for evaluating different scenarios. What we do here is that we conduct analysis by finding out sensitivity of certain criteria of merit such as NPV, IRR, or any such criterion to variation in basic factors. Then we do simulations to find out likelihood under various scenarios. A decision tree is the graphical depiction of all the possibilities or outcomes to solve a specific issue or avail a potential opportunity. It is widely used for making crucial decisions like project selection, cost management, operations management, production method, and to deal with various otherstrategic issues. A corporate risk is the risk a corporate may face. It is to be evaluated what type of corporate risk analysis one has to do while embarking up on a project. A project is like a product in a bouquet of other projects carried out by the corporate. The corporate risk of a project adds to the corporate risk profile of the


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0259.html -->
Adjustment of Riskand Uncertaintyin Capital... | 245 company. It may be argued that a diversification strategy reducesthe overall risk and,sometimes, protects the business from cyclical downfall, when one project faces slump but the other has a healthy turnover. The shareholders may try themselves to reap benefit of diversification by investing into other company, who is a leader in that segment, rather than allowing the present company venturing into that field. Risk Management requires special skill apart from the in-depth knowledge of the business. Based on the cost benefit analysis and the resource and technology options, a separate department, exclusively dealing with risk, may be formed. Other issues that management should be aware of are: Never test the depth of the water with both feet and never jump start before you have done all your homework. A project report, once ready, will tell us about what we can expect from the implementation which may include an NPV or IRR or any other criterion of merit. Further analysis will reveal the major deviations under various scenarios, finally leading us to either go ahead with or reject the project. When we make an investment in a project, we try to first find out what will be the return on it. For that purpose, we calculate NPV which has to be positive to fund the project. We use a discount rate to measure the net present value (NPV). The certainty equivalent method deals with variability of outcomes and our attitude towards risk. It is conceptually superior to the risk-adjusted discount rate method because it does not assume that risk increases with time at a constant rate. Despite its conceptual soundness, it is not as popular as the risk related discount rate calculation.
