Adjustment of Riskand Uncertaintyin Capital... | 247 STRUCTURE 12.0 Objectives 12.1 Introduction 12.2 Decision Making using Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Analysis 12.3 Decision Making using Relevant Cost Concepts 12.4 Decision Making using Activity Based Costing (ABC) 12.5 Ethical and Non-Financial Considerations Relevant to Decision Making Let Us Sum Up Keywords Check Your Progress Solution U N I T 12 DECISION MAKING


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0262.html -->
248 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 12.0 OBJECTIVES After studying this unit, you will have better understanding of: • Decision Making using Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Analysis • Decision Making using Activity Based Costing • Ethical Decision-Making in Finance • Character-Based Decision-Making Model 12.1 INTRODUCTION Capital budgeting decision is not the only financial decision, required to be taken by a business organisation. Financial decisions also involve various other decisionslike, what products/servicesshould be manufactured/provided by the organisation, what should be the price of each product/service, whether it is profitable to produce internally or source from vendors, whether a special order should be accepted or not, and so on. All these decisions are based on analysis of data available and/or the estimates made. This analysis may involve various techniques depending on the complexity of the data and the choice of the management. It is also very important here to keep in view the fact that, in today’s society, every financial decision of a business organisation, cannot be based on the consideration of profit alone. Ethics, social responsibility, or other not-profit considerations, also often affect the financial decisions. For example, installing a pollution control unit in a cement plant is not an economically viable proposal but may be approved by the management on environmental and social considerations. 12.2 DECISION MAKING USING COST-VOLUME-PROFIT (CVP) ANALYSIS In practical terms, the cost, volume and the price are the important ingredients of any profit analysis. Cost has two main components namely, fixed and variable. The fixed cost per unit will go down if volume of production or sales increases. Variable cost generally varies with volume but here too the variance will depend on the product mix and the processes adopted. Higher volume of productions will generally reduce the cost of production due to economy of scale, but incremental cost due to upscaling the facilities may again change the cost structure. Higher volume in sales may accompany with disproportionate increase in marketing cost, some of which will be for brand building and the rest may commensurate with the sales volumes. While pricing will have direct impact on profitability, it in fact determines the breakeven point. Higher the sales realisation, earlier the breakeven point. Volume will also have direct impact on absolute profits, which too will be a determining factor in calculating the breakeven point. An investor will have many constraints and criteria while taking a decision to invest. Some important points affecting investment decisions due to cost, volume and price, which ultimately determine profits and the breakeven point, are noted below. i. When we introduce a new product in the market, we cannot expect high volumes and therefore the costing will be higher. ii. In the aforesaid scenario, we will have to keep the price affordable or practically low to attract new set of buyers, which will delay the breakeven point. iii. In case of a consumer products having large market, we will have to plan for big volumes which will require large capital investment. iv. In the aforesaid scenario, to carve out a reasonable market share, huge advertisement and brand building expenses will have to be incurred affecting the cost and profitability.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0263.html -->
Decision Making | 249 v. Moreover, building large capacity will need huge investment delaying the payback period. vi. A speciality product, on the other hand, can be launched with high price and good margins but may need brand building and huge R&D expenditure. We can go on giving dozens of such examples to emphasise the point that cost, volume and price remain the most important and constant factors affecting the profits, breakeven point, and payback period and ultimately the decision making process for any project investment. Any change in any one or more of these factors will need reworking and reanalysis of the decision-making process. We should also note that these factors have influence inter se. For example, a higher fixed or variable cost will require us the increase the price to keep the project viable. On the other hand, if the market or customers cannot afford or bear high price, which is beyond our control, we need to revisit costing to fit into the revenue estimates. Therefore, one has to first identify what are uncontrollable factors which have impact on CVP analysis and then try to adjust the controllable ones. The CVPanalyst will also have to consider unforeseen events which may affect price, cost or volume such as war, floods, pandemics, political upheaval, trade treaties, quota restrictions etc. To conclude, we can say that CVP analysis is a good tool to assess the impact of changes in price, volume, variable costs or fixed costs on benefits that we plan to reap up on implementation of a project. In addition to being helpful in decision making for project implementation, the CVP analysis helps in answering various financial questions like: • How the profits will be affected if we reduce the selling price and increase the volume? • If the fixed costs undergo a change due to advertising campaign or other sales promotion measures, what should be the additional sales volume to meet these costs? • Whether the sales staff should be paid on the basis of fixed salary, commission or, a combination of both? • What will happen to the financial results of the organisation, if there is fluctuation in the sales, price, output or costs? Illustration 1 A company manufactures cycles for both adults and children. Given below is information about cycles made for children– Particulars Traditional CVP Analysis Activity Based CVP Analysis Monthly Demand and Production 10,000 units 10,000 units Selling Price Rs. 8,000 per unit Rs. 8,000 per unit Variable Cost per unit Rs. 7,500 per unit Rs. 7,500 per unit Fixed Cost p.m. (as identified under each cost system) Rs. 10,00,000 p.m. Rs. 8,00,000 p.m. In the context of a traditional CVP study, fixed costs are defined as costs that do not change in proportion to the amount of product or service being purchased. After conducting an analysis using activity-based costing, it was determined that the monthly fixed costs that do not change, based on factors such as volume or any other cost driver, are Rs. 8,00,000 per month. According to the findings of the analysis, a milling machine is utilised to cut metal into steer support. The production of these steer supports occurs in batches of twenty-five units. After completing a batch intended for the children’s cycle, the following


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0264.html -->
250 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT batch would be intended for the adult cycles. As a result, there would be a change in the set-up following the completion of each batch. If 10,000 children’s bicycles need to be manufactured, the needed number of set-ups is equal to 10,000 steer support divided by 25 each batch. This results in a total of 400 set-ups. Each set-up costs Rs. 500, which includes charges for items such as changing the oil and purchasing jigs, among other things. In the past, the standard CVPanalysis combined this cost with the fixed cost altogether. You are required to (i) Determine the break-even point and profit per month using both the classic(traditional) CVPmethod and the Activity Based CVP method. (ii) As the manager of the plant, it is in your best interest to limit the amount of times that the machine needs to be set up so that it can operate at full capacity. Imagine for a moment that the milling machine has the capability of producing different types of cycles, such as adult cycles, sports cycles, and so on at any time. As a result, you suggest that the batch size of the children’s steer support be increased to 50 individual units in a single batch. The current number of set-ups, 400 (10,000 units divided by 25 units), will be reduced to 200 (10,000 units divided by 50 units). The fabrication of larger batches will necessitate the leasing of more inventory storage space, which will result in an additional expense of Rs. 50,000 per month for the business. Analyse the impact on BEP (units per month) and PPM (earnings per month). (iii) When should the cost of labour be included in the overall estimate of how much a set-up will cost? Explain. (iv) How shall the number of set-ups on the milling machine and the cost of each set-up affect the machine’s overall flexibility? Explain. Solution: (i) (a) Break-even point (units per month) and profit per month under classical(traditional) CVP analysis: Selling Price per unit Rs. 8,000 Variable Cost per unit Rs. 7,500 Contribution per unit Rs. 500 Fixed Cost per month Rs. 10,00,000 Break-even Point (per month in units) = Fixed Cost p.m./ Contribution p.u.= Rs. 10,00,000/ Rs. 500 per unit 2,000 units Monthly Demand (units) 10,000 units Profit per month = {Monthly demand (units) × Contribution per unit} – Fixed Cost per month = (10,000 × Rs. 500 per unit) – Rs. 10,00,000 Rs. 40,00,000 (b) Break-even point (units per month) and PPM (profit per month) under Activity Based CVP method. Number of units produced per batch is 25. Therefore, number ofset-ups will be 10,000 units/ 25 units = 400 per month.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0265.html -->
Decision Making | 251 Selling Price per unit Rs. 8,000 Variable Cost per unit Rs. 7,500 Contribution per unit Rs. 500 Fixed Cost per month (per Activity Based method) Rs. 8,00,000 Break-even Point (per month in units) = {Fixed Cost p.m. + (number of set-ups × cost per set-up)}/ Contribution p.u. = {Rs. 8,00,000 + (400 × Rs. 500 per set-up)}/ Rs. 500 per unit = Rs. 10,00,000/ Rs. 500 per unit 2,000 units Monthly Demand (units) 10,000 units Profit per month = {Monthly demand (units) × Contribution per unit} – (Fixed Cost per month + Set-up cost per month) = (10,000 × Rs. 500 per unit) – (Rs. 8,00,000 + Rs. 200,000) = Rs. 50,00,000 – Rs. 10,00,000 Rs. 40,00,000 Despite the fact that the BEP units and the earnings each month are identical when using either method, theActivity-Based method has brought to light the fact that there are 400 set-ups being conducted each month. This would provide management with additional data to work with in order to enhance the functioning of the operations. (ii) Break-even point (units per month) and profit per month underActivity Based CVP analysis: Batch size increased from 25 to 50 units; monthly set-ups reduce from 400 to 200 per month. Selling Price per unit Rs. 8,000 Variable Cost per unit Rs. 7,500 Contribution per unit Rs. 500 Fixed Cost per month (per Activity Based method) Additional cost p.m. for inventory storage = Rs. 50,000 Rs. 8,50,000 Break-even Point (per month in units) = {Fixed Cost p.m. + (number of set-ups × cost per set-up)}/ Contribution p.u. = {Rs. 8,50,000 + (200 × Rs. 500 per set-up)}/ Rs. 500 per unit = Rs. 9,50,000/ Rs. 500 per unit 1,900 units Monthly Demand (units) 10,000 units Profit per month = {Monthly demand (units) × Contribution per unit} – (Fixed Cost per month + Set-up cost per month) = (10,000 × Rs. 500 per unit) – (Rs. 8,50,000 + Rs. 1,00,000) = Rs. 50,00,000 – Rs. 9,50,000 Rs. 40,50,000 Analysis A conclusion can be drawn that the capacity of the machine can be enhanced if the batch size is increased. The time that has been saved as a result of the reduction in set-ups from 400 per month to 200 per month is now available to be employed in the production of parts for other cycles. As


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0266.html -->
252 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT a result of there being fewer set-ups, the monthly expenditures associated with the set-ups will go down. Even after taking into account the rise in the cost of storing the goods, profits have gone up by Rs. 50,000 per month (40,50,000 – 40,00,000). As a direct result of this, the number of units per month required to reach the point where operations are profitable has come down from 2,000 to 1,900. This decrease is attributable to the savings in the overall set-up costs that were achieved as a result of there being fewer set-ups. (iii) Inclusion of labour cost in the cost of set-up would depend on their availability: (a) The cost of hiring temporary labour or the cost of outsourcing operations necessary for set-up would be included in the total cost of setting up. (b) The cost of permanent labour used for set-up who are otherwise idle would not be included in set-up costs because the salaries given to them have to be incurred even otherwise; this cost is a sunk cost. (c) The opportunity cost of labour must be taken into account, in addition to the hourly labour rate, in situations where permanent labour is used for setup, who are otherwise fully engaged in the production process, additional labour supplies are unavailable in the short term, and no further overtime work is possible. (iv) Setup affects a machine’s production efficiency. Reduced number of set-ups or reduced set-up time can help increase the machine’s utilisation. This also provides the organisation with the freedom to alter the batches produced by the milling machine to meet the needs of both children’s and adults’bicycles. Setup expenses are another issue that affectsthe flexibility of production. Reduced set-up costs result in increased adaptability to alter the milling machine’s production batches to accommodate different types of cycles. 12.3 DECISION MAKING USING RELEVANT COST CONCEPTS In decision making, one of the other ways is to classify the costs according to whether they are relevant or not to a particular decision. This concept is called Relevant Cost Concept and is valid and applicable for not only while planning an investment, but also while running a business, on the premises that decision making is a constant process and cost is an integral part of it. Why we call it a Relevant Cost is because the cost is not a fixed or onetime concept but a concept relevant at a given time for a given situation. It variesin total from one alternative to another. In fact, every business decision hasits cost whether known, unknown, direct or indirect. Let’s now discuss in more detail various cost elements. Relevant costs are those future costs which will be affected by a decision whereas, irrelevant costs are those which are not affected by the decision. To give a simple example, if one owns both, a diesel and a petrol car, and he has to undertake a long journey, the decision about using diesel or petrol car will take into account the costs of petrol and diesel but not the cost of road tax and insurance, as these costs are already incurred and will remain the same, irrespective of the decision. So, the costs of petrol and diesel are relevant costs, while the costs of road tax and insurance are irrelevant cost. In this example, the costs of road tax and insurance are called “Sunk Costs” as these are made even before the decision making process starts. Sunk cost does not mean that it is a wrongly incurred expenditure or has no benefit. In our above example, road tax and insurance costs have to be incurred and have their benefits. The only point is that these are irrelevant to the decision of making a choice of using which of the cars. Relevant costs are also categorised as Avoidable costs, while the irrelevant costs are categorised under Unavoidable costs. This is because avoidable costs are incurred only if a specific business decision is


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0267.html -->
Decision Making | 253 made while the unavoidable costs will have to be incurred irrespective of the outcome of the decision. In our above example, the road tax and insurance costs are unavoidable costs while the cost of petrol and diesel are the avoidable costs. The relevant cost concept helps the decision making process by discarding the irrelevant cost data and thus, make the decision making process less complicated. While using the concept of Relevant costs, it is worthwhile to examine the so called “Opportunity Costs”. When you conceive a project, you had an alternative use available or wasin mind which could have given you some X return. When you use the resources for another project, you will lose that opportunity and potential income. That lost income is the opportunity cost. Opportunity cost thus does not require the payment of cash or its equivalent. It is a potential benefit or income that is given up as a result ofselecting an alternative over another. It may be noted that opportunity cost, as a part of decision making, will arise only when use of scares resources is involved. For example, if the forging machine in an engineering workshop is being utilised at only 60% of its capacity for existing products, the decision to add another product, which uses 10% of the potential capacity of the forging plant, no opportunity cost is involved as we are not sacrificing any production of existing products. However, if it is being used, say at 95% of its capacity, adding the item to the production line will result in some sacrifice of the quantity of production of existing items, and, therefore, will involve an opportunity cost. Some of the important areas of decision making, which involve the Relevant Cost Concept, are as under: i. Add or drop a product line or segment: Such decisions are taken on the basis of incremental income that new product will have after offsetting the loss of income from the discontinued product. One will have to consider the cost of additional investment and recovery, if any, from the disbanded plant. There may cost of acquisition of new customers over and above losing the goodwill of the deprived customers. ii. Make or buy decision: Ice required for the production process, or power requirements for the continuous running plant, are generally outsourced from ice factory or the power company, as the case may be. However, sometimes, if the actual cost of production of ice or a unit of power, is substantially lower than the price presently paid; it will make sense to have plants for captive consumption. iii. Setting price of a product: If the product is for mass consumption, the price and demand elasticity is too high even to risk a marginal increase in the price; the rivals will deliver a blow by reducing their price and you may suffer a permanent loss of customer base. To fix the price of a new product, more important than the costing and the expected margins, you will have to study the market conditions like consumer preference, strength of the competitors, price sensitivity and the quality of the product. Thus, in such cases the relevant cost is not just the book cost or numerical cost but the invisible costs of other factors stated above. iv. Accepting or rejecting special orders: A large order may primarily look attractive because it will reduce inventory and make way for new production line. However, if the large order is accompanied by conditions such as heavy discount or extended credit period of stringent goods return; it may not be an offer worth accepting. Also, sometimes, a client could ask a company to provide them with a one-off product. In such cases, disruptions to supplies to the present customers may not be a good idea. Hence the relevant cost analysis may help you to take appropriate decision.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0268.html -->
254 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT v. Heavy discount offers from suppliers: A probable heavy discount and the taking care of continuous raw materials flow may be good objectives to be achieved, but the curtailed credit and disproportionate engagement in inventory may offset the advantages. One may also have to ensure proper quality of the materials received under such offers. vi. Import Substitutes: Good quality and lower prices are generally considered as the criteria for such decision. However, bulk purchase requirements, exchange fluctuations, remedies in case of supply failure or shortage and stringent (LC) payment terms may deter you from taking the decision and hence the relevant cost analysis. vii. Raw material mix: The cost reduction may be the main reason why the businessmen tend to think of this alternative. However, the quality concerns will have to be weighed before finalising such decision. There may be issues with regard to the availability of the substitute material. viii. Sale and Deals: Stock clearance and store space may induce such decisions but the costing and the recovery of all costs prior to sell of goods through deals will require consideration. ix. Outsourcing an activity or service: Security, watch and ward, payroll accounting, IT management, statutory compliance services, tax management are some of the services many organisations think of outsourcing mainly because of identifiable cost and specialised functions. Proper study of the requirements and in-house cost estimates will be required apart from confidentiality commitment and reliability aspects of the outsourcing agent. To conclude, the relevant cost concept is a very wide concept and each case of management decision will need special and specific study and analysis. There are costs which are indirect, hidden, unimaginable, remote, over a period of time and difficult to even estimate. This needs deep knowledge of business and a thoughtful process and analysis. Relevant cost analysis plays a significant role in decision-making. Let us check out some relevant cost examples: Example1. The ABC Company plans to launch a self-care portal, which will result in a reduction of five positions within the company’s customer support department. In this case, the cost that is significant and relevant is the payment for the five personnel positions. Example 2. A business has received orders from buyer X for 3 materials A, B and C. It already has some old inventory of products B and C, as specified in the following table. We have to arrive at the relevant cost of each material for making the decision of whether to sell or not. Material Inventory Units Required sale price per unit A Nil 50 Rs. 10 p.u B 150 @ Rs. 15 p.u 200 Rs. 17 p.u Inventory units can be sold at Rs. 13 C 90 @ Rs. 30 p.u 100 Rs. 23 Material A: With zero inventories, they will buy all 50 units at Rs. 10. Hence, relevant costs = 50 units × Rs. 10 = Rs. 500


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0269.html -->
Decision Making | 255 Material B: We assume that if the order of buyer X is not accepted, the units in inventory will have to be sold at the discounted selling price of Rs. 13/unit, as there are not many buyers in the market for this product. Our purchase price of Rs. 15/unit has no meaning. As there are only 150 units in the inventory, we will have to purchase 50 units @ 17/unit from the market to fulfil the order. Hence, relevant cost of material B = Rs. 13 × 150 + Rs. 17 × 50 = Rs. 1,950 + Rs. 850 = Rs. 2800 Material C: Rs. 30 per unit is not relevant since the current price is Rs. 23. Therefore, Relevant cost of Material C = 100 units × Rs. 23 = Rs. 2,300 Example of Make or Buy Decision using Relevant Cost Concept A company that specialises in the production of completed items needs to have certain components. It must choose between manufacturing the components in-house or obtaining them from a third party. Naturally, the one with the lowest cost is the one to choose. In the case of a make or buy decision, some examples of associated costs are direct materials, direct labour, and other overhead expenses. Let’s say a business needs a component for a machine. They have the option of procuring the part from a third party or producing it in-house at the factory. In the event that the business chooses to outsource certain functions, it will need to free up some space that can be rented out. If the management decides to outsource work it can generate additional cash from rented premises. Thus, the company is able to reach the conclusion with the help of relevant cost analysis that purchasing the part is a more financially sound choice. For example; The ABC Company is in the business of producing auto parts, some of which require very precise pieces of machinery. When purchasing from a supplier, the unit price is Rs. 5 (five rupees). However, the identical component can also be manufactured by the company itself. The company needs a total of 50,000 individual units of spare parts each year. The following costs are incurred by the company when the goods are produced internally: Direct materials = Rs. 2/unit Direct labour = Rs. 4/unit Overhead costs = Rs. 1/unit Special tools = Rs. 40,000 Item Cost per Unit (Rs.) Total cost for 50,000 units Direct Materials 2 1,00,000 Direct Labour 2 1,00,000 Overhead Costs 1 50,000 Special Tools 40,000 2,90,000


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0270.html -->
256 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT According to the above illustration, it will cost ABC Rs. 2,50,000 to buy from a supplier. And it will cost Rs. 2,90,000 to make the same internally. Therefore, ABC should continue outsourcing. Example of Continue Production or Close Business Unit decision, using Relevant Cost Concept The question of whether or not to continue operations or to shut down individual business units, inevitably arises at some point in the life of every company. In this case, the management needs to assess whether or not the units produced are generating the desired income and whether or not the maintenance cost of the plant and machinery is high. When it comes to making that decision, having the appropriate cost analysis form is of the utmost importance. For example; The company Amol makes cheese worth Rs. 10,000 per month. Maintenance cost for machinery is Rs. 3,000, Rs. 2,000 for material, Rs. 2,500 for labour, and Rs. 2,500 for miscellaneous costs. Overall expenses amount to Rs. 10,000 for an income of the same amount at Rs. 10,000. So, the company might think of discontinuing the cheese unit. Amol might continue with cheese production if the expenses are lower, like Rs. 7,500/-. 12.4 DECISION MAKING USING ACTIVITY BASED COSTING (ABC) Prior to the emergence of ABC, companies typically calculated profitability using the allocation method. This allocation method involved allocating costs to a product or customer using metrics such as the total number of units produced, accounts, customers, or transactions. Activity Based Costing (ABC) is used for estimating the cost which in turn is used for decision making. It has been widely used to help the management in taking important decisions like pricing, outsourcing etc. The method is used for costing of products, service or even a customer who is being serviced, all termed as objects under this method. The method is named after activity, which is the focus of the process. ABC method of costing is based on the fact that the products and services, provided by a company to its customers, involves various such activities which are not exclusively related to one product or service. For example: The quality control department ensures that all the products and services provided by the company are of desired quality. But the service provided by this department is not equally spread over all such products and services. Some products may require higher amount of time and resources of the department, compared to the other products.Activity-Based Costing (ABC) istechnique of appropriately assigning the costs of such activities to various products and services of the company. ABC involves identification of each cost driving activity and apportioning its cost to different products or jobs. The basis for this allocation is the quantity of each such cost driving activity required for their completion. Under this technique, the overhead costs of the company are identified with each cost driving activity. Examples of such cost driving activities may be issue of purchase orders, quality control, receipt and issue of materials, etc. To clarify further the concept of ABC, let us take a simple example: Example: Company SW Ltd purchases CKD (Completely Knocked Down) packs of 2 wheelers and 3 wheelers and assembles them to sell in the market. The material and labour cost of each pack, till it reaches the assembly line, is Rs. 50,000 and Rs. 80,000 respectively. Total cost incurred by assembly line, during the year, is Rs. 20,00,000, utilising 20,000 labour hours. Assembly of a 2 wheeler takes, on an average, 20 labour hours while the assembly of a 3 wheeler takes 30 labour hours. We have to find the cost of each 2-wheeler and 3wheeler using the ABC costing method.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0271.html -->
Decision Making | 257 Solution: Direct cost of material and labour for each pack is known, viz. Rs. 50,000 and Rs. 80,000 respectively. The issue is how to allocate the cost of assembly, an activity which is applied to both types of products. For this, we use the term, ‘cost driver of the activity’, i.e. the most prominent constituent of the cost of the activity. In the activity of assembly, the main cost constituent (cost driver) is the labour, which is paid on hourly basis. In this case, the rate of the cost driver is total assembly cost/labour hours used = 20,00,000/ 20,000 = Rs. 100 per labour hour. So, the assembly cost allocated to each 2-wheeler is Rs. 20*100 and for 3-wheeler, it is Rs. 30*100. So, the total cost of each 2-wheeler is Rs. 52,000 and that of each 3 wheeler, Rs. 83,000. Methodology of Activity Based Costing Method The manufacturing or the service is first subdivided into discrete and quantifiable activities, also called work units. The cost is then allocated or attributed to each such quantifiable work unit. The basis of attribution of cost can be the benefit received from the indirect activates. The cost attribution can also be based on the activities undertaken to produce each product or service. The following terms are used while operating ABC System: i. Cost Object. This is an item for which the cost measurement is required and it can be a product, service or a customer. ii. Cost Pool. This term is used for grouping of the costs incurred on a particular activity which drives them iii. Cost Driver. This is any factor that causes a change in the cost of activity. These are further classified into Resource Cost Driver and Activity Cost Driver. A Resource Cost Driver measures the quantity of resources consumed by an activity and the Activity Cost Driver is a measure of production, marketing, research etc. Measuring units can be number of units produced, number of sales personnel, number of research projects, number of hours spent on project etc. There are various stages or steps for the ABC System which are briefly stated below. 1. Identify the project for costing exercise. 2. Identify different activities within the organisation. 3. Identify the direct cost of the products. 4. Identify the overheads to be related to the activities. 5. Spread the support services across the primary activities. 6. Determine the activity cost drivers. 7. Compute the total cost of the products or the objects. In the traditional approach, we allocate the overheads based on the volume of production unlike in ABC system where the allocation is activity based calculated on the basis of cost drivers of the activities. ABC method can give us product profitability as well as customer profitability. It can also throw light of process efficiency. In short, activity-based cost information is both intuitive and logical. To conclude, it makes sense to those charged with the task of improving performance and the method provides them with transparent information on the cost ramifications of their decisions. Activity cost driver rate = Total cost of activity Activity driver


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0272.html -->
258 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT ILLUSTRATION 2 Let’s say that the management of a company that manufactures certain electronic devices has taken a decision to install an ABC system. The management comes to the conclusion that there should only be three cost drivers for all overhead expenses, and those are direct labour hours, machine hours, and the quantity of purchase orders. The following are the company’s overhead costs, as shown in the general ledger: – General Ledger Amount (Rs.) Payroll taxes 1,000 Machine maintenance 500 Purchasing Dept. labour 4,000 Fringe benefits 2,000 Purchasing Dept. Supplies 250 Equipment depreciation 750 Electricity 1,250 Unemployment insurance 1,500 Total 11,250 Differentiate which overheads are driven by direct labour hours? Solution: Payroll taxes Rs. 1,000 Fringe benefits Rs. 2,000 Unemployment insurance Rs. 1,500 Total Rs. 4,500 Similarly, overheads driven by machine hours include Machine maintenance, depreciation and Electricity totalling Rs. 2,500 and finally overheads driven by number of purchase orders include purchasing department labour and purchasing department supplies totalling Rs. 4,250. Now, overhead rate is calculated by the formula Total cost in the activity pool ÷ Base, base being the total number of labour hours, machine hours and total number of purchase orders in the given case. Assume that the total number of labour hours be 1,000 hours, machine hours be 250 hours and total purchase orders be 100 orders. So, Cost driver rate would be Cost Driver Rate (Rs.) Rs. 4,500/ 1,000 Rs. 4.50 per labour hour Rs. 2,500/ 250 Rs. 10 per machine hour Rs. 4,250/ 100 Rs. 42.50 per purchase order


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0273.html -->
Decision Making | 259 ILLUSTRATION 3 XYZ Ltd. is a multiproduct company, manufacturing three products X, Y and Z. The budgeted costs and production for the year ending 31st March are as follows: X Y Z Production quantity (Units) 4,000 3,000 1,600 Resources per Unit: - Direct Materials (Kg.) 4 6 3 - Direct Labour (Minutes) 30 45 60 The budgeted direct labour rate was Rs. 10 per hour, and the budgeted material cost was Rs. 2 per kg. Production overheads were budgeted at Rs. 99,450 and were absorbed to products using the direct labour hour rate. XYZ Ltd. followed the Absorption Costing System. XYZ Ltd. is now considering to adopt an Activity Based Costing system. The following additional information is made available for this purpose. 1. Budgeted overheads were analysed into the following: (Rs.) Material handling 29,100 Storage costs 31,200 Electricity 39,150 2. The cost drivers identified were as follows: Material handling Weight of material handled Storage costs Number of batches of material Electricity Number of Machine operations 3. Data on Cost Drivers was as follows: X Y Z For complete production: Batches of material 10 5 15 Per unit of production: Number of Machine operations 6 3 2 You are requested to: Prepare a statement for management,showing the product costs of each product using theABC approach.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0274.html -->
260 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT Solution: Activity Based Costing X Y Z Total Quantity (units) 4,000 3,000 1,600 - Material Weight per unit (Kg.) 4 6 3 - Total material weight 16,000 18,000 4,800 38,800 Machine operations per unit 6 3 2 - Total operations 24,000 9,000 3,200 36,200 Total batches of Material 10 5 15 30 Material handling rate per kg. = Rs. 29,100 ÷ 38,800 Kg = Rs. 0.75 per Kg Electricity rate per machine operations = Rs. 39,150 ÷ 36,200 = Rs. 1.081 per machine operations Storage rate per batch = Rs. 31,200 ÷ 30 batches = Rs. 1,040 per batch Unit Costs: X (Rs.) Y (Rs.) Z (Rs.) Direct Costs: Direct Labour Direct material Production Overheads: Material Handling Electricity Storage Total unit costs Number of units Total costs 5.00 8.00 3.00 (Rs.0.75 X 4) 6.49 (Rs.1.081 X 6) 2.60 Rs. 10X 040/4000) 25.09 4,000 Rs. 1,00,360 7.50 12.00 4.50 (Rs.0.75 X 6) 3.24 (Rs.1.081 X 3) 1.73 (Rs. 5 X 1040/3000) 28.97 3,000 R s. 86,910 10.00 6.00 2.25 (Rs.0.75 X 3) 2.16 (Rs.1.081 X 2) 9.75 (Rs. 15 X 1040/1600) 30.16 1,600 Rs. 48,256 12.5 ETHICAL AND NON-FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS RELEVANT TO DECISION MAKING All our previous discussions on methods to be followed, while making decisions, were based on minimising the cost and maximising the returns. It all related to pecuniary aspect of the thing and the benefits to the


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0275.html -->
Decision Making | 261 self. However, in all this maze of lectures and theories, we forget that there is something beyond money and self. No human being can live or survive in isolation. We all need society that is worth living. The society can be worth living provided all in the society are happy and there is peace around. Therefore, wellbeing of all is equally important. Human qualities such as fairness, compassion, respect, duties, law abidance, and mutual accommodation, do need consideration while taking business decisions. Last but not the least, preserving the earth and the environment are equally important, for the mankind and other lives on the earth to survive. All these considerations are considered as ethical and non-financial ones, important in business decisions. Ethical Decision-Making in Finance The decisions based on ethics are driven by a different set of considerations, such as: • Is it the truth? The company should always stick to the Truth. When you speak a truth, you don’t have to remember what you have said because the truth, as fact, will always remain stored in your memory and will come out wheneverso required. On the other hand, a lie will alwaysrequire a cover up. A small slip and you will be exposed. Thus, it is always better to write, speak and express truth and truth only • Is it a breach of trust? If someone has trusted you, never misplace it. Trust is a foundation of many relations, including business relations and dealings. Breach of trust can be a legal ground for making a contract null and void. A criminal breach of trust can land you in jail. A party, who is not trustworthy, will lose all its goodwill and consequently, will be out of business. • Is it respectful?An organisation should always ensure that it isrespectful of all the men and matters. An employee, a colleague, a customer, or for that matter anyone, with whom you have to deal, should be respected. Everyone has his own status and a place in the eyes of the people around him. Do not brush him aside saying that he needs our business and earns fortune through us. Respect begets respect and hence it will be beneficial for our own business. • Is it responsible? All of us have to act as a responsible person or citizen. The word responsible means having an obligation to do something, or having control over or care for someone as a part of one’s own job. Hence, while taking decisions, we should act with sense of responsibility. If we do so, all around will benefit and we will add to our goodwill. • Is it fair? We should not always look at the things from the view point of our advantage. Or that is it advantageous to us? To be a successful, as well as reputed company, we should adopt policy of fairness in our dealings. Our action should be such that fairness is visible through it. Fairness means treating people or matter with equality without any favouritism or discrimination. For example, when we constitute a committee for employee welfare, we should ensure that every segment of workforce isfairly represented on it. When we are negotiating wage revision, we should always keep in mind the views, feelings and requirements of the workers. Even while dealing with competitors, the element of fairness should not be given a go by, under any circumstances. In fact, fairness should be in our nature and blood and this policy will go a long way for the success of the business. • Is it compassionate? Compassion is a fine human quality. It is a feeling or showing a sympathy and concern for the fellow beings. We should not hesitate taking certain decisions on compassionate ground when the rules and procedures do not cover certain situations. An example can be giving extended maternity leave to a female employee whose child is born with certain problems and the mother needs some more time to attend to it. Or, giving extra increment to an employee, who has a bright child and needs extra money for her education


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0276.html -->
262 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT • Is it civil? We keep complaining about unfair treatment meted out to us every now and then, but keep doing the same to others! We need not always wait for others to be civil to us but do a bit from our side voluntarily. Being civil also means being courteous or polite in our behaviour. If this happens, we will create an atmosphere of brotherhood around us. Create an ethical Framework within an organisation: This will need special efforts on the part of the decision-making authority to make constant endeavour to inculcate the following qualities in each and every member of the organisation; Trust: Action on everyone’s part becomes the action of the company. Therefore, all should act in a way that inspirestrust and this willsurely have positive impacts and enhance reputation. Integrity and honesty are the foundations which will remove the trust deficiency, if any. With this quality, the company will improve itsreliability in the market. Thisshould be constantly followed. Meeting the commitments, even in difficult situation, will add to the reputation of the company. Respect: Respect for everyone and respect for all should be the mantra. Be it a customer, an employee, a competitor; respectshould never be wanting.You may disagree at times, but let it be respectfully. Conflict, differences, intolerance, differing ideologies, if any, should coexist with respect and honour. The feeling for oneness and friendliness should be spread all around. Promoting good and moral behaviour should be decision maker’s top priority. Responsible: To be responsible means to be considerate and also accountable. Never forget that every action comes with consequence and, therefore, never hurt or insult others. A sort of discipline should be developed around, to avoid rash and erratic behaviour. Fair: A company is stoic, but not its members. A company can endure pain or hardship, but not the people running it. Like good times, bad times too will come. The point is that the company’s culture will adopt policies that will make people learn good qualities like fairness, which will keep it away from such unbearable future pains. Take success with humility and defeat with grace. Keep the mind open and do not overreact adversely to a change, which sometime is necessary. Fairness in dealings and behaviour will help you remain attached to the ground, during unfair whether. Care: Respect and care should always be universal. Display you care to every one whether he is a worker or a customer or a competitor. You care more for your reputation and not just profits. Forget and forgive nature will help you move forward faster. Do not care for past mistakes or reverses but alwayslearn from it. Civil: The Company should exhibit a sense of civic duty. It should treat its surroundings with care and respect. The company should also protect the local culture, environment and the mother earth. The company should recognize its debt to the public and respect the laws of the land Character-Based Decision-Making Model There are many models which suggest framework for deciding the ethical soundness of a decision. Prominent among them is the Character-Based Decision-Making Model, developed by Josephson Institute of Ethics. It provides a framework that can be used to decide whether a decision is morally and ethically sound. The six pillars of this model are: Trustworthiness, Respect, Responsibility, Fairness, and Citizenship. The model suggests a seven-step path to better decisions. These steps are: 1. Stop and Think 2. Clarify Goals


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0277.html -->
Decision Making | 263 3. Determine Facts 4. Develop Options 5. Consider Consequences 6. Choose 7. Monitor and Modify The model suggests the rationalization of obstacles to ethical decision making, as under: rationalizations • If It’s Necessary, It’s Ethical • The False Necessity Trap • If It’s Legal and Permissible, It’s Proper • It’s Just Part of the Job • It’s All for a Good Cause • I Was Just Doing It for You • I’m Just Fighting Fire with Fire • It Doesn’t Hurt Anyone • Everyone’s Doing It • It’s Ok if I Don’t Gain Personally • I’ve Got It Coming • I Can Still Be Objective The model involves the Golden Rule – “Help when you can and avoid harm when you can.” It also involves the principle that, in general, the company should make decisions that promote the greatest amount of moral justness. ILLUSTRATION 4 Sprinter Sportswear is a multi-national firm that currently operates in 23 different countries across the globe. However, the corporation does not even have a single factory that it owns and operates. The production process has been completely subcontracted out to a total of 175 manufacturers located in countries and regions with relatively cheap operating costs. Sprinter’s procurement requirements are met in their entirety by the factories. These factories function entirely on their own, and Sprinter has no part in their day-to-day business. The goods that are purchased from this supplier network, are brought into distribution centres, where they are stocked, and from there they are sent out to wholesalers of sportswear and apparel. Recent news reports from several of the nations, that are considered to be part of the Third World, have shown that high levels of child labour are being employed.Although it is against the law in some nations, businesses often resort to using child labour in order to keep their costs as low as possible. These nations do not have any factoriesthat employ minorsin a direct capacity. Instead, they hire independent contractors to carry out the work. These contractors, in turn, illegally employ youngsters without the knowledge of local officials charged with upholding employment laws in the area. In addition, the working conditions in these factories are extremely unpleasant and unsanitary. Since Sprinter only deals with these factories in its capacity as a customer, the company initially pretended that the problem did not exist. Sprinter, as a corporation, has not engaged in any activity that may be considered unlawful, in the course of its business operations. The members of the Board, however, have been compelled to contemplate taking action against such factories, as a result of the increased emphasis placed on corporate social responsibility. You are required to


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0278.html -->
264 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT (i) Discuss why Sprinter sportswear should attempt to address this issue. (ii) Suggest some of the actions that the company can take to address this issue. Solution: (i) In order to get a competitive advantage in terms of cost, work may be contracted out to regions and nations on the opposite side of the world. A company might not be held directly accountable for the unethical business conduct of its vendors. Nevertheless, contemporary businesses have a moral obligation to provide care to a larger variety of stakeholders, some of whom may not be directly connected to the company. In this instance, it has a responsibility to exercise reasonable care for the workers who have been engaged by the factories that are part of its supply chain. The problem that it is trying to solve, is one that has to do with the exploitation of child labourers in factories, which occurs when work is outsourced to employees of third parties. Despite the fact that Sprinter sportswear has not engaged in any activity that would be considered criminal, the company has a moral obligation to these children. Because children have the right to an education, it is against the law in most nations to have them work in factories or mines. Sprinter ought to make an effort to address this problem given that children are being directly engaged as a direct result of the labour that has been outsourced. Additionally, any negative news concerning the manufacturing process of its products has the potential to harm the company’s business. (ii) Sprinter should have the objective of making its products responsibly. Some actions which can be taken by Sprinter are: Sprinter can create a Code of Conduct that outlines the appropriate criteria for operating a business and provides specific examples. These requirements could relate to hiring processes, in which case it could stipulate that people needed to manufacture a product should be over a certain age to be considered for employment in that capacity. Others may have to do with the atmosphere of the workplace, the protection of employees, or the long-term viability of the environment. Sprinter must make it mandatory for its suppliers to adhere with all applicable laws and to execute the aforementioned codes of conduct. Itshould insist that the supplier is available for periodic inspection by Sprinter to ensure that the supplier is complying with the standards outlined in its Code. Sprinter can establish an audit team that routinely inspects manufacturers during the pre-sourcing and follow-up stages of the manufacturing process. Only those factories that meet Sprinter’s requirements shall be considered for commercial partnerships by the company. Any individuals who violate the Code of Conduct throughout the follow-up stages should, as is fitting, be subject to a penalty, and more major violations should result in the contract being terminated. Sprintershould list, according to geography, the suppliersfrom which it acquiresitsitems, on its website. It should also supply information about the items that are created by each of its suppliers, as well as information like worker diversity, average age, and so on. This will make it possible for watch groups to learn the identities of the suppliers and provide the company with advance warning if there are any labour problems or other labour related issues within the plants. LET US SUM UP The cost, volume and price are the key ingredients of any profit analysis. Cost has two main components namely, fixed and variable. Variable cost generally varies with volume but here too the variance will depend on the product mix and processes adopted. Price also has direct impact on profitability as it determines the breakeven point. Cost, volume and price remain the most important and constant factors affecting the


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0279.html -->
Decision Making | 265 profits, breakeven point, and payback period. Any change in any one or more of these factors will need reworking and reanalyse the decision-making process for any project investment or any other financial decision of the organisation. Every business decision has its cost whether known, unknown, direct or indirect. Relevant Cost is not a fixed or onetime concept but a concept relevant at a given time for a given situation. It varies in total from one alternative to another and can be applied to all business decisions. If the product is for mass consumption, the price and demand elasticity is too high even to risk a marginal increase in the price; the rivals will deliver a blow by reducing their price and you may suffer a permanent loss of customers’ customer base. Bulk purchase requirements, exchange fluctuations, remedies in case of supply failure or shortage and stringent (LC) payment terms may deter you from taking the decision and hence the relevant cost analysis by way of outsourcing an activity or service to a third-party company are some of the key considerations. Activity Based Costing (ABC) is used for estimating the cost of products, services and customers. The method runs on the philosophy that it is better to be approximately right than precisely wrong. It is based on the activities undertaken to produce each product or service. Cost of any product is changed when there is a change in the cost of an activity. Activity Cost Resource Driver is a measure of production, marketing, research etc. for an organisation. It can give us product profitability as well as customer profitability and process efficiency. Ethical and Non-Financial Considerations Relevant to Decision Making are considered as ethical and non-financial ones important in business decisions. These include but are not limited to: ‘Is it the truth? Is it a breach of trust? Does the company always stick to the Truth?’. An organisation should always ensure that it is respectful of all the men and matters. Fairness means treating people or matter with equality without any favouritism or discrimination. Compassion is a fine human quality and this policy will go a long way for the success of the business. Being civil also means being courteous or polite in our behaviour. Integrity and honesty are the foundations which will remove the trust deficiency, if any. Meeting the commitments even in difficultsituation will add to the reputation of the company. The feeling for oneness and friendliness should be spread all around. Take success with humility and defeat with grace. Display you care to every one whether he is a worker or a customer or a competitor. Fairness in dealings and behaviour will help you remain attached to the ground during unfair whether. The company should also protect the local culture, environment and mother earth. KEYWORDS CVP Analysis, Sunk Cost, Opportunity Cost, Relevant Cost, Activity Based Costing, Cost Object, Cost Pool, Cost Driver, Trust, Responsibility, Fairness, Compassion CHECK YOUR PROGRESS 1. A cost driver is: (a) An item of production overheads (b) A common cost which is shared over cost centres (c) Any cost relating to transport (d) An activity which generates costs

<!-- Page Start: text/part0280.html -->
266 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 2. In activity-based costing, costs are accumulated by activity using: (a) Cost drivers (b) Cost objects (c) Cost pools (d) Cost benefit analysis 3. Which of the following statements is correct? (a) Good quality and lower prices are generally considered as the criteria for decision on imports substitutes. (b) If the product is for mass consumption, the price and demand elasticity is normally too high (c) Relevant Cost is not a fixed or onetime concept but a concept relevant at a given time for a given situation (d) All of the above 4. MODULE – C VALUATION, MERGERS & ACQUISITIONS Unit 13. Corporate Valuation Unit 14. Discounted Cash Flow Valuation Unit 15. Other Non-DCF Valuation Models Unit 16. Special Cases of Valuation Unit 17. Merger, Acquisition & Restructuring Unit 18. Deal Structuring and Financial Strategies
