<!-- Page Start: text/part0309.html -->
STRUCTURE 14.0 Objectives 14.1 Introduction 14.2 Estimating Inputs 14.3 Approaches to Discounted Cash Flow Models 14.4 Various Discounted Cash Flow Models 14.5 Dividend Discount Model 14.6 Applicability of the Dividend Discount Model Let Us Sum Up Keywords Check Your Progress Solutions U N I T 14 DISCOUNTED CASH FLOW VALUATION


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0310.html -->
296 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 14.0 OBJECTIVES After studying this unit, you will have better understanding of: • Discounted cash flow model of valuation • Inputs for Discounted cash flow model and their estimation • Various types of Discounted cash flow models • Various Dividend Discount Models • Applicability of the Dividend Discount Models 14.1 INTRODUCTION Even though discounted cash flow valuation is only one of several ways to approach valuation, and even though the vast majority of valuations done in the real world are relative valuations, discounted cash flow valuation serves as the basis upon which all other valuation techniques are constructed. In order to accurately do relative valuation, it is necessary for us to have a fundamental understanding of discounted cash flow valuation. It is normal practice to start with a discounted cash flow valuation before applying option pricing models to the process of asset appraisal. That is why understanding the discounted cash flow analysis is important. Any person who has a fundamental understanding of this subject will be able to analyse and apply any of the other methods. This section examines the many sub-approaches to discounted cash flow valuation as well as the foundation of this method, which is a philosophical justification for discounted cash flow valuation, and an evaluation of the distinct sub-approaches to discounted cash flow valuation. This approach is grounded in the concept of present value, which statesthat the value of any asset is equal to the sum of the asset’s predicted future cash flows, expressed as a present value. The cash flows will be different for each asset, such as dividends for stocks, coupons (interest) and the face value of bonds, and cash flows after taxes for real estate projects. The level of risk associated with the expected cash flows will determine how the discount rate is calculated; higher rates will be applied to riskier assets, while lower rates will be applied to safer projects. The discounted cash flow valuation method seeks to arrive at an estimate of an asset’s true worth, by basing that estimate on the asset’s underlying characteristics. What exactly does “intrinsic value” mean? In the absence of a more precise definition, you can think of it as the value that would be attached to the company if it were evaluated by a neutral analyst who not only makes accurate estimates of the expected cash flows for the company given the information that is available at the time, but also uses the appropriate discount rate to value these cash flows. Even if the task of estimating intrinsic value appears to be impossible, particularly when valuing young companies with a great deal of uncertainty about the future, it is still worthwhile to make the best estimates that you are capable of and to persevere in attempting to estimate value, because markets occasionally make errors. Even though prices on the market can be different from an asset’s intrinsic value (which can be calculated based on its fundamentals), you are keeping your fingers crossed that the two will eventually align. There are literally many different variations of discounted cash flow models that can be used. It is widespread practice at investment banks and consulting firms, to assert that their valuation models are superior to, or more complex than, those utilised by their contemporaries in the industry. However, at the end of the day, discounted cash flow models can only change along a number of factors.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0311.html -->
DISCOUNTED CASH FLOW VALUATION | 297 14.2 ESTIMATING INPUTS For applying the DCF method of valuing a firm or any other asset, we need the following inputs: a. The predicted cash flows in future b. The discount rate that is suitable for the given level of risk associated with these cash flows c. The estimated cash flow growth rate and d. The estimated pattern of growth. These are discussed, in detail, below: 14.2.1 Expected Cash Flows The dividend is the only source of cash flow that an equity investor receives from a publicly traded company, at least in the most literal sense; financial models that use dividends as cash flows are referred to as dividend discount models. A broader definition of cash flows to equity would be the cash flows that are left over after the cash flow claims of nonequity investors in the firm have been met (interest and principal payments to debt holders and preferred dividends), as well as after enough of these cash flows have been reinvested into the firm to sustain the projected growth in cash flows. This would be an example of how a broader definition of cash flows to equity could be used. This concept is known as the free cash flow to equity (FCFE), and the types of models that make use of this concept, are referred to as FCFE discount models. The total cash flow to all claim holders in the company is what is referred to as the cash flow to the firm. One technique to calculate this cash flow isto add the free cash flowsto equity to the cash flowsto lenders (debt) and preferred stockholders. Estimating the cash flows prior to payments on debt and preferred dividends is a much simpler way to reach the same number. To do so, take the after-tax operating income and remove the net investment that is required to maintain growth. This gives us an estimate of the cash flows. The models that make use of these cash flows are referred to as FCFF models, and this cash flow is referred to as the free cash flow to the firm (FCFF). 14.2.2 Discount Rates The question “how much money would have to be invested presently, at a particular rate of return, to yield the anticipated cash flow, at its future date?” can be answered through the process of discounting future cash flows. In other words, discounting determines the present value of future cash flows by using a rate that is the cost of capital that most accurately reflects the risk and timing of the cash flows. This rate is called the discount rate. When doing an evaluation, we begin with the fundamental idea that the discount rate, that is applied to a cash flow, should represent the level of risk associated with that cash flow; cash flows with a higher level of risk should have higher discount rates. There are two different perspectives on risk. The first type of risk is known as default risk, and it refers to the possibility that a person or organisation may break a promise to fulfil some financial obligation, such as paying interest or the principal that is owed. When looking at debt, the rate that reflects the possibility of default is referred to as the cost of debt. Because interest expenses are deductible from taxable income, the cost of debt for most companies will be lower after taxes are taken into account. The second method to look at risk is to consider how it relates to the difference between actual returns and expected returns. The actual returns on an investment with a high level of risk, may be considerably


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0312.html -->
298 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT different from the predicted returns; the bigger the deviation, the higher the level of risk.When considering equity, we typically employ risk measures that are derived from the variation of returns. However, there are a few fundamental areas in which these models can reach a consensus, and that will be the topic of discussion in this chapter, which will focus on the various models that make attempts to do this. The first is that risk in an investment needs to be perceived through the perspective of the marginal investor in that investment (the investor who is most likely to be trading), and it is expected that this marginal investor has a good level of diversification across a number of other assets. Therefore, the risk of an investment,specifically the non-diversifiable or market risk of that investment,should be used to estimate discount rates for that investment. The second point is that the expected return on any investment may be calculated by beginning with the return that would be gained from a risk-free investment and then adding a premium that is proportional to the degree of market risk that is associated with the investment in question. The cost of equity is calculated based on this anticipated return. The cost of capital can be determined by taking the average of the cost of equity, which can be estimated using the method that was just presented, as well as the cost of borrowing money after taxes, which is determined by the default risk, and then weighting the average by the proportions that are used for each type of funding. When evaluating an existing corporation, our contention is that the weights applied should be determined by the current market values of the company’s debt and equity. There are some analysts who utilise book value weights, but doing so goes against a fundamental concept of valuation, which states that at a fair value, one ought to be indifferent between purchasing and selling an asset. If you breach this principle, you are in violation. The discount rates, that are applied in discounted cash flow valuations, ought to be reflective of the riskiness of the cash flows being valued. To be more specific, the cost of debt needs to include a default premium or spread to account for the risk of the debt going into default, and the cost of stock needs to include a risk premium to account for the risk of equity. The challenge in this case is to find out the ways in which we quantify the risk of defaulting on a loan or losing money on an investment, and more significantly, how do we determine the default and equity risk premiums. 14.2.3 Estimating future Growth We need estimates of the predicted growth rate for revenues and the expenses, in order to make projections about future cash flows. The rate of inflation, that is anticipated, is an essential component in the process of predicting the growth rate. The rate of anticipated inflation should be somewhat close to the pace of anticipated growth in cash flows in line with the expected state of affairs prevailing in the business environment and the economy. For example, if we take the case of real estate market and are looking at the valuation of a real estate firm, the anticipated growth rate of rentals may be higher than the anticipated rate of inflation, in tight markets with low vacancy rates. This trend is likely to continue at least until the market shortages are eliminated. In markets that have a high vacancy rate, it is more likely that the opposite will be true. The analysts are put in the most direct position to deal with uncertainty, when they are attempting to estimate the expected growth in cash flows in the future. There are three different methods that can be used to estimate growth. One strategy is to investigate the past of a company and employ the historical growth rate that was reported by that company. The danger is that growth in the past might not provide much of an indication of growth in the future.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0313.html -->
DISCOUNTED CASH FLOW VALUATION | 299 The second method is to get estimates of growth from sources that have a greater level of expertise. For some analysts, this means using the estimates provided by a company’s management, while for others, it takes the form of employing the consensus estimates of growth produced by others who follow the firm in question. The results of the appraisals should be questioned due to the inherent bias in both of these sources. The third method isto conduct a survey. The surveys are used to collect information on the expectations that investors have for predicted growth. It is noteworthy to note that even while investors have major disagreements over discount rates, the growth rates that are anticipated for cash inflows and cash outflows, fall within a rather narrow band. For example, it is possible that the expected rise in cash flows from real estate would be somewhere in the range of 4 percent to 6 percent. Further, what kind of impact could be there on these estimatesif rent control is anticipated soon or a hike in rentalsis expected. It is likely that the value would decrease and the predicted growth rate of cash flows over time will be reduced if there is a limit placed on how high the gains may be yet there is no limit placed on how low they can go. Estimation errors in the valuation will be compounded by the fact that the laws governing rent control cannot be predicted with regard to either the amount of the limit or whether or not the laws will be changed. 14.2.4 Estimating Growth Patterns When valuing a company in general, there are three main approaches, that may be taken, for the growth pattern: (a) we can assume that the company is already in stable growth; (b) we can assume a time of consistent high growth and subsequently lower the growth rate to stable growth (two-stage growth); or, (c) we can provide for an interim period to get to sustained growth (three-stage or n-stage models). When making this assessment, we need to take into account a number of different criteria. The level of current increase in earnings and revenues will be affected by the choice of growth pattern that is implemented. The rate of expansion of a company over the most recent time period allows us to divide businesses into three distinct categories. (a) Companies considered to have stable growth report earnings and revenue growth that is equal to or lower than the nominal growth rate of the economy in which they operate. (b) Companies are considered to have moderate growth if their earnings and revenue grow at a rate that is just slightly faster than the economy’s nominal growth rate. We would consider any growth rate to be moderate if it was within 8 to 10 percent of the growth rate of the economy. We can have this as the basic thumb rule for determining what constitutes a moderate growth rate. (c) The earnings and sales of high-growth companies expand at a rate that is significantly faster than the nominal growth rate of the economy as a whole. The steady state models that assume constant growth provide accurate estimates of value for businesses that are growing at a stable rate.The two-stage discounted cash flow modelshould give sufficient flexibility in terms of capturing changes in the underlying features of the firm for businesses that are developing at a rate that is considered to be moderate. It is possible that a model with three stages or even more stages may be required to reflect the longer transitions to steady growth that are characteristic of high-growth companies.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0314.html -->
300 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT The higher expected growth of a company can come from either broad competitive advantages obtained through time such as a brand name or reduced costs of manufacturing (from economies of scale) or particular advantagesthat are the consequence of legal barriersto entry,such aslicences or product patents. Both of these types of advantages can contribute to the company’s ability to compete successfully in the market. The former types of advantages are more likely to vanish suddenly when the legal impedimentsto entry are removed, whereasthe later are more likely to vanish gradually over time when new competitors enter the market. It is likely that a two-stage process will be followed by the anticipated growth rate of a company that has specific sources of growth. This process involves growth that is high for a certain period (for example, the period of the patent) and then abruptly drops to a stable rate after that period is over. It is more likely that, over time, the predicted growth rate of a company that has general sources of growth will gradually decrease because new competitors will be entering the market. The rate at which one can anticipate gaining this competitive advantage is dependent on a number of factors, including the following: A) Competitive Edge: Some competitive advantages, like a well-known brand name in a particular category of consumer goods, appear to be more challenging to overcome and, as a result, are more likely to create growth for an extended length of time. Other types of competitive advantages, such as the advantage of being the first mover, appear to be lost far more quickly. B) Management Competence: By developing strategies that seek out new markets in which to exploit the company’s existing competitive advantage as well as new sources of competitive advantage, management that is more capable of performing their duties will be able to slow the erosion of the company’s competitive advantage over time, but they will not be able to prevent it altogether. C) Entry Barriers: When there are more obstacles preventing competitors from joining a company’s market, whether those obstacles are financial or technological in nature, the rate at which the company’s advantage in the market erodes, will be slower. 14.3 APPROACHES TO DISCOUNTED CASH FLOW MODELS APPROACH I Is it possible to estimate Cash Flows? Yes No Type of Leverage Use Dividend Discount Model Static Dynamic Free Cash Flow to Equity Free Cash Flow to Firm


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0315.html -->
DISCOUNTED CASH FLOW VALUATION | 301 APPROACH II Current Earnings Positive & Normal Use Current Earning Base Yes Abnormal/ Negative Current Earnings No Survival is Possibile? Use Normalised Earnings in place of Current Earnings Yes No Firm Overburdened With Debt Margin Adjustment Needed Yes No Value Equity as an option to Liquidate Estimate Liquidation Value APPROACH III Present Growth Rate If less than Economy’s Growth Rate If more than Economy’s Growth Rate Use Stable Growth Model Is Competitive Advantage Short Lived? Yes No Use Three Stage/ n-stage Model Use Two Stage Model 14.4 VARIOUS DISCOUNTED CASH FLOW MODELS There are several Discounted Cash Flow Models which are widely used: (a) Enterprise DCF Model The enterprise DCF model applies a discount rate equal to the weighted average cost of capital to the free cash flow to the firm (FCFF). This has been discussed earlier in the book. (b) Equity DCF Model The equity discounted cash flow model can be implemented in two different ways: the dividend discount model and the free cash flow to equity model. The dividend discount model takes into account the cost of equity when calculating a discount on the expected dividend stream. The free cash flow to equity model also takes into account the cost of equity when calculating a discount on the free cash flow to equity. (c) Adjusted Present Value (APV) Model The APV model discounts the unlevered equity cash flow (which is the same as the free cash flow to the firm) at the unlevered cost of equity (the cost of equity assuming the firm has no leverage) and adds to it the discounted value of the interest tax shield on debt. This results in an estimate of the present value of the unlevered equity cash flow.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0316.html -->
302 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT (d) The Economic Profit Model In the economic profit model, the economic profit stream is discounted using the weighted average cost of capital, and then the currently invested capital is added to the resulting value. 14.5 DIVIDEND DISCOUNT MODEL The Dividend Discount Model is a quantitative method of valuing a company’s equity shares price based on the assumption that the fair price of share equals the present value of the company’s future dividends. Dividend discount models are among of the oldest discounted cash flow models still used in practice today. Despite the fact that numerous analysts have moved away from dividend discount models, on the grounds that these models produce estimates of value that are far too cautious, a lot of the key concepts, that come through with dividend discount models, apply when we look at alternative discounted cash flow models. The various dividend discount models used for valuation are as under: 14.5.1 Constant Growth Model One of the most common assumptions used by dividend discount models is that the dividend paid out per share would increase at a rate that is fixed (g). According to this hypothesis, the value of a share is computed as follows: P0 = D1 (1 + r) + D1(1 + g) (1 + r)2 +.... D1(1 + g)n (1 + r)n+1 + ...... Where, P0 is the current fair price of the share D1 is the expected dividend one year from now r is the rate of return required by the investor n represents any particular year and can be any number between 0 and infinity Following the use of the formula for the sum of a geometric progression, the preceding expression can be simplified to: P0 = D1 (r – g) Illustration: XYZ Ltd. is expected to pay a dividend of Rs. 3 per share one year from now. The dividend payments are expected to grow at 5% p.a. If an investor needs 12% rate of return on his investment, what price of the share will be considered to be fair, by him. Solution: Applying the formula, given above, P0 = D1 (r – g)


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0317.html -->
DISCOUNTED CASH FLOW VALUATION | 303 We get P0 = 3/ (0.12 – 0.05) = 3/ 0.07 = Rs. 42.86 The vast majority of stock valuation models are founded on the idea that dividends will increase over the course of time. 14.5.2 Zero Growth Model In the event that we make the assumption that the dividend per share stays the same from year to year at a value of D, the formula will look like this: P0 = D/r Illustration: XYZ Ltd. is expected to pay a dividend of Rs. 3 per share one year from now. The dividend payments are expected to remain constant at Rs. 3 per share. If an investor needs 12% rate of return on his investment, what price of the share will be considered to be fair, by him. Solution: Applying the formula for fair value, P0 = D1 (r – g) We get P0 = 3/ (0.12 – 0) = 3/ 0.12 = Rs. 25.00 14.5.3 Two Stage Model The most straightforward modification of the continuous growth model postulates that the exceptional growth—whether positive or negative—will last for a set number of years, after which the usual growth rate will take over and continue indefinitely. In the event where the dividends increase at a rate that is proportional to the rate of growth, the market price of the equity share will be: P0 = D1 (1 + r) + D1(1 + g1) (1 + r)2 + D1(1 + g1)2 (1 + r)3.... + D1(1 + g1)n–1 (1 + r)n + ...... Pn (1 + r) where P0 is the present price of the equity share, D1 is the dividend that is anticipated to be paid out one year from now, g1 is the extraordinary growth rate that is valid for n years, and Pn is the price of the equity share at the end of year n. It is assumed that the share will be sold at the end of year n at the price Pn. That is why its PV is included in the price P0. The current value of a growing annuity is represented by the first term on the Right Hand Side of the following equation: P D g r r g P r n n n 0 1 1 1 1 1 1 1 = − + +       −             + () +


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0318.html -->
304 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT Since the two-stage growth model assumes that the growth rate after n years remains constant, Pn will be equal to: Dn+1 r – g2 where Dn+1 is the dividend for year n+1 and g2 is the growth rate in the second period. Dn+1, the dividend for year n+1, may be expressed in terms of the dividend in the first stage. Dn+1 = D1(1 + g1)n–1(1 + g2) Substituting the above expression, we get P D g r r g D g g r g n n 0 1 1 1 1 1 1 2 1 1 1 1 1 = − + +       −             + + + − − () () 2 1 1       +       ()r n Illustration The current dividend on an equity share of PML Private Limited is Rs. 5. PML is expected to enjoy an above-normal growth rate of 25 percent for a period of 6 years. Thereafter, the growth rate will fall and stabilise at 15 percent. Equity investors require a return of 20 percent. What is the intrinsic value of the equity share of PML? The inputs required for applying the two-stage model are: g1 = 25 percent g2 = 15 percent n = 6 years r = 20 percent D1 = D0 (1 + g1) = Rs. 5(1.25) = Rs. 6.25 Plugging these inputs in the two-stage model, we get the intrinsic value estimate as follows: P0 = 6.25 1–(1.25/1.20)6 (0.20–0.25) + 6.25(1.25)5(1.15) (0.20–0.15) (1/1.20)6 P0 = 6.25 (1–1.278) –0.05 + 6.25(3.052)(1.15) 0.05 (0.334) = 34.6918 + 146.9165 = Rs. 181.6083 14.5.4 H Model The H model is another growth model that consists of two stages. The H model, as opposed to the traditional two-stage model, postulates that the exceptional growth rate in the beginning stage does not


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0319.html -->
DISCOUNTED CASH FLOW VALUATION | 305 remain constant but rather decreases linearly over time until it achieves a stable rate in the steady stage. This is in contrast to the conventional two-stage model. The H model, which was developed by Fuller and Hsia, makes the assumption that the earnings growth rate starts off at a high initial rate (ga), and then decreases at a linear rate over the course of 2H years to a stable growth rate (gn), which is maintained forever. This model also assumes that the earnings growth rate will remain stable forever, after the stability has been achieved. It operates under the presumption that the dividend payment rate and the cost of equity will not change over the course of time and will not be affected by the fluctuating growth rates. The equation for H model of valuation is as under: P D g H g g r g n a n n 0 0 1 = + + − − [() ()] where r is the rate of return needed by investors, P0 is the intrinsic value of each share, D0 is the current dividend per share, gn is the expected long-term growth rate, ga is the current growth rate, and H is the one half of duration during which ga levels out to gn. The above equation can also be written as P D g r g D H g g r g n n a n n 0 0 0 1 = + − + − − () () The H model can be understood in a straightforward manner by using the equation that wasjust presented. On the right-hand side of the equation, the first term indicates the value based on the normal growth rate, and D g r g n n 0 () 1+ − whereas the second term represents the premium due to the anomalous growth rate: D0 H (ga – gn)/ (r – gn) ILLUSTRATION The equity shares of ABC Private Limited presently generate a dividend payment of Rupee 1.00 every year. The growth rate as of right now is thirty percent. On the other hand, this will decrease in a linear fashion over the course of ten years, and then it will level off at ten percent. What is the company’s intrinsic worth per share, assuming that investors require a return of 15% on their investment? The following information is available: D0 = 1.00 ga = 30 percent H = 5 years gn = 10 percent r = 15 percent


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0320.html -->
306 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT Putting the above inputs in the H-model, we get the estimated intrinsic value as follows: P0 = 1 [(1.10)+5(0.30-0.10)] 0.15-0.10 = Rs. 42.00 The H model is more realistic than the two-stage model, which predicts that the growth rate would suddenly slow down after a given amount of time. Instead, the H model predicts that the growth rate will gradually slow down over time. On the other hand, presuming that the dividend pay-out rate will remain unchanged during all stages of growth appears to be an unrealistic expectation. Because of this, the model cannot be used by any company that pays out zero or very little dividends at the present time. The applicability of the model is severely restricted due to the requirement that it exhibit both rapid development and substantial pay-outs. 14.5.5 Three Stage Model Combining the two-stage model with the H model resulted in the creation of the three-stage growth model. It presupposes that there will be an initial time of stable high growth, a second period of growth that will decrease in a linear fashion, and a third era of steady low growth that will continue on indefinitely. The following equation illustratesthe growth that is anticipated throughout all the three time intervals. Because it does not place any limitations on the payout ratio, this model can be considered extremely generic. The value of the shares, as per this model, would be: P EPS g r DPS r EPS g o a t a h t t t n t tr t n n n 0 1 1 2 1 1 1 1 = + + + + + + = = ∑ () () () π ()π () r g () r s n s n t n t n = − + + = ∑ 1 2 1 1 2 where EPSt represents earnings per share in year t, DPSt represents dividends per share in year t, ga represents the growth rate in the high growth phase that lasts for n1 years, gn represents the growth rate in the stable growth phase, πa representsthe pay-out ratio in the high growth phase, πn represents the pay-out ratio in the stable growth phase, rh represents the cost of equity in the high growth phase, rtr represents the cost of equity in the transition phase, and rs is the cost of equity in the stable phase. ILLUSTRATION Alpha Limited has an Earnings per Share (EPS) of Rs. 10 currently. It is anticipated that the earnings per share will increase by 25% during the course of the subsequent five years, and the dividend payout ratio will remain unchanged at 25% throughout the high growth era. The rate of growth in earnings per share is expected to decrease linearly over the next five years, to a rate of 15 percent per year. During this time frame, the dividend payout ratio is expected to experience a linear growth that takes it from 25% to 50%. After the tenth year, the annual rate of increase in earnings per share will remain constant at 15% for the rest of time. The payout ratio will be set at 50 percent throughout the time with the consistent growth rate. The cost of equity will be 20 percent during the rapid growth phase; during the transition period, the cost of equity will reduce by 0.5 percent per year; and lastly, the cost of equity will be 17.5 percent during the steady growth period. According to the three-stage dividend discount model, what is the intrinsic value of each share of the company? The projected earnings per share, dividends per share, and present value of dividends during both the high growth phase and the transition phase are given ahead:


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0321.html -->
DISCOUNTED CASH FLOW VALUATION | 307 Year EPS Expected Growth Rate Payout Ratio DPS Cost of Equity Cumulated Cost of Equity Present Value of DPS Current 10 1 12.50 0.25 0.25 3.13 0.20 1.20 2.60 2 15.63 0.25 0.25 3.91 0.20 1.42 2.76 3 19.53 0.25 0.25 4.88 0.20 1.70 2.87 4 24.41 0.25 0.25 6.10 0.20 2.04 2.99 5 30.52 0.25 0.25 7.63 0.20 2.45 3.12 Present value of dividends in high growth phase 14.35 6 37.54 0.23 0.30 11.26 0.195 2.91 3.87 7 45.42 0.21 0.35 15.90 0.190 3.46 4.59 8 54.05 0.19 0.40 21.62 0.185 4.11 5.27 9 63.24 0.17 0.45 28.46 0.180 4.85 5.87 10 72.72 0.15 0.50 36.36 0.175 5.69 6.39 Present value of dividends in the transition phase 25.98 The values of the growth rate, the payout ratio, and the cost of equity change from those of the high growth period to those of the stable growth period in equal annual increments as the company moves from the high growth period to the transition phase. The earnings per share in year 11, a growth rate of 15 percent, a payout ratio of 50 percent, and a cost of equity of 17.5 percent can be used to compute the terminal value of the shares at the end of the 10th year. Terminal Value = 72.72 (1.15)(0.5) (0.175-0.15) = 1672.63 To obtain the present value, we divide the above value by the cumulated cost of equity in the 10th year (from the table). Present value of terminal value = 1672.63/5.69 = 293.81 The value of the Equity Share is: Amount (Rs.) Present value of dividends in the high growth phase 14.35 Add: Present value of dividends in the transition phase 25.98 Present value of terminal value at the end of the transition phase 293.81 Value of the Equity Share 334.14 14.6 APPLICABILITY OF THE DIVIDEND DISCOUNT MODEL There are mixed opinions about the applicability of the Dividend Discount Model. There are proponents who are staunch supporters ofthe model whereasthere are others who feel that there are several deficiencies in the model. This model, however, offers certain advantages as follows:


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0322.html -->
308 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT a) It is easy to understand and has a natural appeal. After all, the only source of cash flows that shareholders receive from the company is in the form of dividends. b) The forecasting of dividends requires a smaller number of assumptions than the forecasting of free cash flows. The growth rate estimate can be applied to the dividend that was paid out the previous year to provide a dividend forecast. A prediction of free cash flow involves assumptions to be made regarding net operating profit after taxes, capital expenditures, depreciation, and working capital. c) A smoothed dividend policy is typically implemented by companies. Therefore, despite the fact that a company’s earnings and reinvestments are frequently subject to uncertainty, dividends are more reliable. Therefore, a valuation that is based on dividends has a greater likelihood of being stable over time compared to a valuation that is based on cash flows. However, due to the fact that it treats dividends and cash flowsin the same manner, the dividend discount model is plagued by a significant drawback. In the actual world, many businesses keep considerable cash flowsthat they otherwise could give out to shareholdersin the form of dividends.As a direct consequence of this, they have amassed a significant amount of cash balances. Even while shareholders are unable to make a direct claim on these cash levels, they do have a stake in these cash balances as owners. Therefore, the value of the equity should reflect these claims. The dividend discount model has a tendency to result in an undervaluation of companies like these. On the otherside of the spectrum are the companiesthat pay dividends at a rate that is higher than their cash flows would suggest. Itshould come as no surprise that they will have to close the deficit by issuing more debt or stock. The dividend discount model typically results in an overvaluation of companies like these. LET US SUM UP In order to use the DCF approach, you need to have credible projections of the future cash flows as well as discount rates. When (a) present cash flows are positive, (b) future cash flows can be accurately forecast, and (c) the risk profile is steady, it is simple to apply to assets and companies. The discounted cash flow models that can be used to an investment come in a virtually infinite number of distinct flavours. It is common practice in investment banks and consulting firms to make the claim that their valuation models are superior to, or more sophisticated than, those used by their contemporaries in the sector. Thisis done in an effort to differentiate their own modelsfrom those used by their competitors. However, at the end of the day, discounted cash flow models can only be altered along a number of different criteria such as the estimation of inputs, projected cash flows, predicted growth, discount rates, growth flows, and growth patterns. In addition to the enterprise DCF model and the equity DCF model, the discounted cash flow (DCF) method can be used to calculate the value of a company’s equity using a number of other models, including the adjusted present value model, the economic profit model, the residual earnings model, and the maintainable profits model. Discounted cash flow models that take into account dividends are among the earliest models of this type that are still used in practice today. In spite of the fact that numerous analysts have moved away from dividend discount models on the grounds that these models produce estimates of value that are much too conservative, a lot of the key concepts that come through with dividend discount models apply when we look at alternative discounted cash flow models. This is because these models produce estimates of value that are far too cautious.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0323.html -->
DISCOUNTED CASH FLOW VALUATION | 309 The dividend discount model can be implemented in a variety of forms, the most frequent of which are the zero growth model, the constant growth model, the H model, the two-stage growth model, and the three-stage growth model. KEYWORDS Free Cash Flow to Equity, Free Cash Flow to Firm, Cost of Capital, Growth Flows, Growth Patterns, Entry Barriers, Constant Growth, Zero Growth, Two Stage Model, H Model, Three Stage Model CHECK YOUR PROGRESS 1. When making valuations, market-based approaches should not be used _____ (a) when the size of the company is insufficient (b) when a company’s assets are lower than its liabilities (c) in the event that there are significant and unprecedented shifts in the market price (d) when it is challenging to provide an accurate estimation of the realisable value in the event of a going concern. 2. The discounted cash flow method of valuation is based on ______ (a) a discount for the future that is anticipated and is likely to be earned. (b) the company’s valuation (c) Anticipated cash flows along with discount rates (d) a company’s potential for earnings 3. Under the discounted cash flow valuation, which of the following is typically employed as the discounting factor? (a) Cost of equity (b) Cost of debt (c) Annuity factor (d) Overall cost of capital 4. Present value indicates _________ (a) value in use (b) the value of cash flows expected in future (c) value computed using the internal rate of return (d) the present value which a buyer is willing to part with 5. The shares of company are quoted at Rs. 40 pershare. Last year, the firm had paid dividend @ Rs. 4 per share. The estimated growth of the company is approximately 16% per year. The required rate of return is 20%. What is the market value of equity shares as per dividend growth model? (a) Rs. 132 per share (b) Rs. 288 per share (c) Rs. 116 per share (d) Rs. 244 per share SOLUTIONS 1. (c) 2. (c) 3. (d) 4. (b) 5. (c)
