<!-- Page Start: text/part0325.html -->
STRUCTURE 15.0 Objectives 15.1 Introduction 15.2 Relative Valuation Model 15.3 Equity Valuation Multiples Model 15.4 Enterprise Value Multiples Model 15.5 Choosing the Right Multiples 15.6 Book Value Approach Model 15.7 Stock and Debt Approach Let Us Sum Up Keywords Check Your Progress Solutions U N I T 15 OTHER NON-DCF VALUATION MODELS


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0326.html -->
312 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 15.0 OBJECTIVES After studying this unit, you will have better understanding of: • Valuations methods other than DCF methods • Relative valuation model • Equity Valuation Multiples Model • Enterprise value multiples Model • Book value approach Model • Stock and Debt Approach Model 15.1 INTRODUCTION Valuation methods are broadly divided into two groups: The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) methods and Non-DCF methods. In previous Units, we have discussed in detail, various models involving DCF methods. In this Unit, we will discuss the valuation models which use Non-DCF methods for valuation. The models we will discuss, are: 1. Relative Valuation Model 2. Equity Valuation Multiples Model 3. Enterprise value multiples Model 4. Book value approach Model 5. Stock and Debt Approach Model 15.2 RELATIVE VALUATION MODEL In the process of valuing an asset using the discounted cash flow technique, the cash flow, growth, and risk characteristics of the asset are taken into consideration. A relative valuation places an asset’s worth in comparison to the prices at which other, comparable assets are selling on the market, at the moment. When it is difficult to quantify something’s value in a direct way, we compare it to other things, such as a similar product that is currently on the market or several iterations of the same product. When we compare things, we generate values that are relative to one another. Dan Ariely, a famous Behavioural Economist and author of the famous book ‘Predictably Irrational: The Hidden Forces That Shape Our Decisions’ has stated in his book that “humans rarely choose things in absolute terms. We don’t have an internal value meter that tells us how much things are worth. Rather, we focus on the relative advantage of one thing over another, and estimate value accordingly.” Similar assets should be sold for values that are comparable to one another, according to both common sense and economic reasoning. In accordance with this approach, one can determine the worth of an asset by analysing the price at which a comparable asset was sold to a buyer who was properly educated and to a seller who was similarly knowledgeable. For example, if you are thinking about selling your agricultural land, you may determine an appropriate asking price for it by researching similar properties on the market. Suppose your land measures 5 acres and recently a similar land was sold in neighborhood measuring 2 acres for Rs. 10 lakhs (at the rate of Rs. 5 lakhs per acre). In the first place, you will put a value of Rs. 25 lakhs for your agricultural land. i.e. 5 acres * Rs. 5 lakhs per acre.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0327.html -->
OTHER NON-DCF VALUATION MODELS | 313 In place of discounted cash flow valuation, relative valuation is frequently regarded an alternative. The DCF technique, on the other hand, serves as the conceptual basis for the majority of relative valuation indicators, as our study in this unit demonstrates. As a result, the two strategies ought to be regarded as being complementary to one another. 15.2.1 Steps Involved in Relative Valuation Relative valuation involves the following steps: 1. Conducting Research on the Concerned Organisation To get started, you need to perform a comprehensive analysis of the subject company, which is the company that will ultimately be evaluated. This analysis should focus on the subject company’s position within its industry and its financial standing. The following is a list of the most important points that will be discussed in this analysis: (a) The company’s product catalogue and the many market sectors that it serves (b) The availability of inputs and the prices of those inputs (c) Technological potential as well as manufacturing capacity (d) Reputation in the market, availability of distribution, and continued patronage (e) Differences in the products themselves, as well as their economic and competitive positions (f) Capability and motivation on the part of the manager (g) The competence and calibre of the available human resources (h) The dynamics of competitive markets (i) Access to capital, leverage, and liquidity in the market (j) Revenue, profit margins, and the rate of return on investment 2. Identifying Businesses That Are Analogous Following the analysis of the subject firm, the nextstage isto choose other businessesthat are comparable to the original company in terms of the types of industries they specialise in, the kind of customers they cater to, the scope of their operations, and so on. It can be challenging to discover organisations that are actually similar to one another due to the fact that most companies are involved in a diverse range of industries, cater to a variety of customer bases, and operate at variable capacity levels. Because of this, the analyst will typically have to settle with using organisations that are comparable to one another in some way. He ought to make every effort to look carefully at 10 to 15 companies in the same industry and choose at least three to four that come “as close as feasible” to the subject company. He should do this before selecting the companies to be analysed. It should come as no surprise that this procedure requires a significant amount of subjective evaluation. It can be difficult to find companies that are comparable to others. In point of fact, the capacity to do so is a defining characteristic ofsophisticated veteransthatsetsthem apart from novices. Following the creation of an initial list of comparables, the next step is to conduct additional research. Conduct an investigation into each organisation, making sure to ask pertinent questions. Why do multiples in the same peer group behave differently? Do some organisations have more desirable goods, a wider consumer base, more reliable revenue streams, or economies of scale than others? These advantages translate into stronger returns on invested capital and growth rates, and as a result, larger multiples are justifiable.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0328.html -->
314 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 3. Determining the Multiples Used in Valuation In reality, a variety of different value multiples are utilised. They can be organised into the following two major groups: (a) stock valuation multiples, such as the price-earnings ratio, the price-book value ratio, and the pricesales ratio, and (b) enterprise valuation multiples(EV-EBITDAratio, EV-FCFF ratio, EV-book value ratio, and EV-sales ratio). Given that none of the multiples used for valuation are ideal, it makes the most sense to employ two or three multiples that provide results that appear to be suitable for the task at hand. Enterprises are valued using a variety of valuation multiples, the most common of which are the EV-EBITDAratio, the EV-book value ratio, and the EV-sales ratio. 4. Figuring Out the Valuation Multiples for the Other Comparable Companies Calculate the valuation multiples for each of the similar companies by using the observed financial characteristics and values of the companies being compared. To illustrate this point, let’s imagine that there are two similar businesses, A and B, and their respective financial data is as follows: Rs. Crs. Company A Company B Sales 6,000 9,000 EBITDA 1,000 2,000 Book value of Assets 4,000 6,000 Enterprise Value 3,000 4,500 The valuation multiples for the companies are: Company A Company B Average EV-EBITDA 3 2.25 2.625 EV-Book value 0.75 0.75 0.75 EV-Sales 0.5 0.5 0.5 5. Establishing a Value for the Concerned Business It is possible to evaluate the subject company if one considers the valuation multiples that have been observed for comparable companies. You can accomplish this in a straightforward manner by applying the average multiples of the comparable companies to the pertinent financial attributes of the subject company. This will allow you to obtain multiple estimates (as many as the number of valuation multiples used) of the enterprise value of the subject company, and you can then take the arithmetic average of these estimates. The growth prospects, risk characteristics, and size of the subject company (the most important drivers of valuation multiples) should be compared with those of comparable companies, and after that, a judgmental view of the multiples that are applicable to it should be taken. This is a more sophisticated method of doing what needs to be done.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0329.html -->
OTHER NON-DCF VALUATION MODELS | 315 Illustration The following financial information is available for company M, an unlisted pharmaceutical company, which is being valued. • EBITDA: Rs. 500 crore • Book value of assets: Rs. 2,000 crore • Sales: Rs. 5,500 crore The pharmaceutical businesses E, F, and G have been identified to be comparable to company M based on an analysis of a number of other publicly traded pharmaceutical companies. The following information regarding the financial health of these companies is available: Rs. Crs. Company E Company F Company G Sales 1,000 2,000 3,000 EBITDA 400 500 600 Book value of Assets 900 1,000 2,000 Enterprise Value 3,000 4,000 6,000 Three valuation multiples, as shown below, have been considered Company E Company F Company G Average EV-EBITDA 7.5 8 10 12.75 EV-Book Value 3.33 4 3 5.165 EV-sales 3 2 2 3.5 The following estimations of Company M’s enterprise value can be derived by applying the average multiples to the company’s financial figures: EBITDA Basis Book Value Basis Sales Basis Average EV-EBITDA 12.75 Average EV-book value 5.165 Average EV sales 3.5 EBITDA of M 500 crore Book Value of M 2,000 crore Sales Value of M 5,500 crore EV of M 6,375 crore EV of M 10,330 crore EV of M 19,250 crore A simple arithmetic average of the three estimates of EV is: = 6,375 + 10,330 + 19,250 3 = Rs. 11,985 crore 15.3 EQUITY VALUATION MULTIPLES MODEL 15.3.1 P/E Multiple In the first edition of their book ‘Security Analysis’ which laid the intellectual foundation for what would later be called value investing and was published in 1934, Benjamin Graham and David L. Dodd presented equity valuation based on a price-to-earnings (P/E) multiple as the typical method of that time. This description was included in the book. Even in modern times, the price-to-earnings ratio remains


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0330.html -->
316 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT the most widely used multiple in valuation. The price-to-earnings multiple, or P/E multiple, is a popular valuation statistic that is typically defined as follows: P/E multiple = Market price per share Earnings per share The current market price per share is used to calculate the numerator of this multiple; the denominator of this multiple is the earnings per share (EPS) for the previous financial year or the EPS for the last 12 months or the expected EPS for the current year or the expected EPS for the following year. The numerator of this multiple is the current market price per share. It is typically measured as the predicted EPS for the current year, as this is the most common form of the metric. Therefore, one way to express the price-earnings multiple is as follows: P0/E1 where P0 is the current market price per share and E1 is the expected earnings per share a year from now. 15.3.1.1 Fundamental Determinants of the P/E Multiple From a fundamental point of view P0/E1 = (1-b) r – ROE*b where (1 – b) is the dividend payout ratio, r is the cost of equity, ROE is the return on equity, and b is the ploughback ratio. Example: V & S Company’s Return on Equity is 20 percent and its r is 15 percent. Company’s dividend payout ratio is 0.3 and its retention ratio 0.7. So, from a fundamental point of view, V & S Company’s P/E multiple is: P0/E1 = 0.3 0.15-0.20*0.7 = 30 15.3.1.2 Reasons for Using P/E Multiple The application of the P/E multiple is recommended for the following reasons: a) Earnings potential is a significant factor in determining investment value; as a result, earnings per share (EPS) plays a significant role in valuing securities. A survey conducted by the members of Association for Investment Management & Research identified book value as the most important variable to be considered when valuing a company’s equity from amongst earnings, cash flow, book value, and dividends. b) Empirical data indicatesthatstocks with a low price-to-earningsratio typically outperform the market. There are a few disadvantages associated with the use of P/E, which stem from the fact that if EPS is in the red, using P/E as a ratio makes no sense from an economic standpoint. Earnings that can be sustained over time, play a significant role in the determination of an asset’s intrinsic value. The job of the analyst is made more challenging by the fact that results frequently contain non-recurring and volatile components. c) Management has some leeway to alter EPS while still adhering to generally accepted accounting principles, but this leeway is limited. These kinds of adjustments might make it difficult to compare P/E ratios across different companies.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0331.html -->
OTHER NON-DCF VALUATION MODELS | 317 15.3.2 P/B Multiple Investment analysts have been using the price to book value (P/B) multiple for nearly as long as they have been using the price to earnings (P/E) multiple. The denominator of the P/E multiple is the earnings per share (EPS), which is a flow metric derived from the income statement. In contrast, the denominator of the P/B multiple, which is denoted by the letter B and stands for book value per share, is a stock metric that is derived from the balance sheet. The book value per share (B) = (Shareholders funds-Preference capital)/ Number of outstanding equity shares 15.3.2.1 The Most Important Factors That Determine the P/B Multiple When viewed from the most fundamental angle, P0 B0 = ROE (1-b) (r-g) where ROE is the return on equity, g is the growth rate, (1 – b) is the dividend payout multiple, and r is the rate of return required by equity investors. Example Vitthal Limited’s ROE is 25 percent and its r is 18 percent. Vitthal’s dividend payout ratio is 0.5 and its g is 15 percent. From a fundamental point of view, Vitthal’s P/B multiple is: P0 B0 = 0.25*0.5 0.18-0.15 = 4.17 15.3.2.2 Reasons for Using P/B Multiple There are a variety of factors that contribute to the widespread use of the P/B multiple. a) Despite the fact that earnings per share can be negative, book value will almost always be positive. b) Book value per share is more consistent than earnings per share (EPS). It is possible that the P/B multiple is more important than the P/E multiple in situations where EPS is extremely high or low or very variable. c) According to findingsfrom empiricalstudies, variationsin the price-to-book ratio (P/B multiple) are associated with variations in long-term average returns. The P/B multiple, on the other hand, has a few downsides, including the following: a) The traditional financial sheet does not take into account intangible assets such as human capital or brand equity. b) The difference between an asset’s book value and its market value is widened by factors such as inflation and technical advancements. c) Depending on the type of business strategy you choose, you can need a varied amount of assets. The P/B multiple has the potential to be deceiving when considerable disparities of this kind are present. 15.3.3 P/S Multiple The price-to-sales multiple, sometimes known as the P/S multiple, is a method of valuation that has garnered a lot of attention in recent years. The P/S multiple is determined by dividing the current stock price of a firm by the revenue per share that the company has generated over the course of the most


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0332.html -->
318 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT recent twelve months. Alternately, it can be calculated by dividing the company’s current market value of equity capital by its annual sales. This approach yields the same result. In essence, the P/S multiple shows the amount of money that the market is ready to pay for each rupee of sales. When employing the P/E multiple, investors may have a variety of issues and difficulties, including the following: earnings may be highly irregular; earnings may be negative; earnings may be defined in a variety of different ways; profits may be “managed.” When the P/S multiple is employed, these worries or problems are either completely eliminated or greatly alleviated. Researchers have examined a variety of investing strategies that are used to choose equities firms. These investment tools include book value, return on equity, P/E multiple, yield, and P/S multiple, amongst others. They discovered that the P/S multiple was a useful tool since portfolios consisting of firms with a low P/S multiple tend to outperform portfolios consisting of stocks with a high P/S multiple. It is generally accepted that a P/S multiple of 1.0 can serve as a benchmark for comparison purposes across all businesses. Therefore, equitiesthat are now trading at a P/S ratio that isfar lower than 1.0,such as 0.5, may be considered to be excellent deals.Any technique with thislevel ofsimplicity is certain to have very restricted applicability, as is obvious. P/S multiples are bound to vary from one industry and company to the next due to differences in parameters such as the net profit margin, growth rate, asset turnover, and so on. The P/S multiple should, therefore, be interpreted in reference to the factors that influence the P/S multiple if one wishes to take a more nuanced approach. Examining the P/S multiple in relation to the net profit margin makes sense given that the net profit margin is a primary factor in determining the P/S multiple. In addition, because P/S multiple is a method of relative valuation, the P/S multiple of a firm ought to be compared with that of the industry average in addition to the company’s own history. 15.3.3.1 Basic P/S Determinants P0 S0 = NPM (1+g) (1-b) (r-g) where NPM is the net profit margin ratio, g is the growth rate, (1 – b) is the dividend pay-out multiple, and r is the rate of return required by equity investors. Example: VLC Limited has a NPM of 10 percent and a growth rate 14 percent. VLC dividend pay-out ratio (1 – b) is 0.4 and its r is 0.19. From a basic point of view, VLC P/S multiple is: P0 S0 = 0.10 (1.14) 0.4 (0.19-0.14) = 0.912 15.3.3.2 Why Use Price to Sales Multiple The following are some of the explanations that analysts provide for adopting the P/S multiple. a) When compared to earnings per share and book value, sales are not as susceptible to manipulation in general. b) Even if EPS or book value are both in the red, sales will almost certainly be in the black. Therefore, the PS ratio can be utilised even in situations where EPS is in the red. c) In general, revenues are more constant than earnings per share, which can be affected by both operating leverage and financial leverage. As a result, the P/S multiple exhibits greater consistency over time in comparison to the P/E multiple.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0333.html -->
OTHER NON-DCF VALUATION MODELS | 319 d) There is some evidence from the real world that implies that variations in the P/S multiple may be associated to variations in long-term average returns. 15.3.4 PEG We discussed the Equity Multiples in the above paragraph. Let us have a look at the equations for P/E multiple, P/B multiple, and P/S multiple together. P0 E0 = (1-b) (r-g) P0 B0 = ROE (1-b) (r-g) P0 S0 = NPM (1+g) (1-b) (r-g) When we examine these equations, we discover that there is one variable that predominates when it comes to explaining each multiple. In the case of P/E, that variable is g, and in the case of P/B and P/S, those variables are ROE and NPM, respectively. This variable, which is the primary contributor to the overall level of explanation, is referred to as the companion variable. Investment professionalsfrequently make use of modified multiples, which are described in more detail below. These multiples take into account the significance of the companion variable. PEG Ratio is defined as the ratio of price to earnings divided by the anticipated annual growth rate in earnings per share. PEG ratio = PE ratio/Expected growth rate The multiple of the P/B to the ROE, is known as the value ratio. Value Ratio = P/B ROE The multiple of P/S to NPM is referred to as PSM. PSM = P/S NPM The PEG multiple is a popular choice among these modified multiples and is preferred by financial analysts. At first glance, it would appear that the company is undervalued if the PEG multiple is less than 1, whereas it would appear that the stock is overvalued if the PEG multiple is greater than 1. 15.3.5 Relative PE Ratio A company’s price earnings ratio is measured in relation to the average for the market using relative price earnings ratios. You can calculate it by dividing the current PE ratio of a company by the average PE ratio for the market: Relative PE = Current PE ratiofirm/Current PE ratiomarket The distribution of relative PE ratios is quite similar to the distribution of actual PE ratios, with one key distinction being that the average relative PE ratio is 1.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0334.html -->
320 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT The model that we use to estimate the PE ratio for a firm experiencing high growth, is also used to analyse relative PE ratios. When everything is considered, we get the following: Re () () () lative PE Payout ratio g g r r j j j j n j n j = × + × − + +         1 1 1 1 − + × + × + g − + Payout ratio g g r g r Payout j j n j n j n j j n j n ,, , () () ()() 1 1 1 ratio g g r r g Payout ratio m m m n m n m m m × + × − + +         − + () () (), 1 1 1 1 n m n m n m m n m n g g r g r × + × + − + () () ()() , , 1 1 1 Where j stands for firms and m stands for market. There are two different applicationsfor relative PEs when it comesto valuation. One method isto evaluate a company by contrasting its current relative PE ratio with its normsfrom the company’s past. For example, a company may be seen to be undervalued because its current relative PE ratio of 0.38 is lower than the relative PE at which it has traditionally traded. The second method is to examine the relative PE ratios of companies operating in various markets. This method is useful for making comparisons even when the relative PE ratios of companies operating in various markets are significantly different. The spread in price-to-earnings ratios, which results in a wider range for relative PE ratios, widens as the growth rate that is anticipated for the market climbs higher. Consider the relative PE of a company that grows at a rate that is half that of the market. This may be demonstrated in a fairly straightforward manner by doing so. This company’s price to earningsratio will be approximately 80 percent of the market PE when the growth rate of the market is 4 percent, and it will trade at that price. When the growth rate of the market reaches 10 percent, the company will trade at a PE that is equivalent to 60 percent of the PE of the market. This has repercussions for analysts who utilise PE ratios relative to other companies. When the market growth rate is high, the stocks of companies whose earnings grow at a pace that is significantly lower than the market growth rate will frequently look cheap on a relative PE basis. On the other hand, when the market growth rate is low, the stocks of same companies will often look costly. 15.4 ENTERPRISE VALUE MULTIPLES MODEL Enterprise value multiples put the emphasis on the value of the business itself, in contrast to equity multiples, which place the emphasis on the value of the equity. Typically, some measure of earnings, assets, orsales contributesto the determination of the enterprise value. The following are some examples of enterprise value multiples that are regularly used: • EV/EBITDA multiple • EV/EBIT multiple • EV/FCFF multiple • EV/BV multiple • EV/Sales multiple


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0335.html -->
OTHER NON-DCF VALUATION MODELS | 321 15.4.1 EV to EBITDA Multiple The Enterprise Value to Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization (EV-EBITDA) multiple is defined as follows: Enterprise value (EV)/ Earning before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortisation (EBITDA) The enterprise value to earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortisation multiple compares the overall market value of the company, minus any cash on hand, to the company’s earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortisation: So the Equation can also be restated as EV/EBITDA= (Market Value of Equity + Market Value of Debt-Cash)/EBITDA EV, or enterprise value, is calculated by adding the values of a company’s equity and interest-bearing debt together. To put it another way, EV stands for firm value (value of equity plus interest-bearing debt). The number of outstanding equity shares multiplied by the price per share is all that is required to calculate the market value of equity. Concerning the interest-bearing debt, the market value of the debt can be determined if the debt is issued in the form of debt securities that are traded on the market. If the interest-bearing debt is in the form of loans, then the value of such loans at the current market must be imputed. In most cases, one rupee of loan is considered to have the same worth as one rupee on the market. The reason for deducting cash from the worth of the company, while performing this calculation, is that if the cash is not netted out, the EV-to-EBITDA multiple will be overstated due to the fact that interest income from the cash is not included as part of the EBITDA. 15.4.1.1 Basic Determinants When viewed from the most fundamental angle EV EBITDA = (ROIC-g)* (1-DA)* (1-t) ROIC (WACC-g) where ROIC stands for return on invested capital, g is for growth rate, DA stands for depreciation and amortisation charges as a percent of EBITDA, t stands for tax rate, and WACC stands for weighted average cost of capital. Example JJ Company’s ROIC is 20 percent and its g is 11 percent.JJ’s DAis 9 percent and itstax rate is 30 percent. JJ’s WACC is 13 percent. Calculate JJ’s EV/EBITDA. EV EBITDA = (0.20-0.11)* (1-0.09)* (1-0.3) 0.20 (0.13-0.11) = 14.33 EBITDA is frequently used as a rough estimate for free cash flow by analysts. EBITDA, on the other hand, is not the same thing as a company’sfree cash flow. It isilluminating to draw comparisons between the two: FCFF = EBIT (1 – T) + Depreciation and amortization – Capital expenditure (CAPEX) – Change in net working capital (NWC) EBITDA = EBIT + Depreciation and amortization So, FCFF = EBITDA – (T x EBIT) – CAPEX – change in NWC


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0336.html -->
322 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT The information shown above makes it quite evident that FCFF has a greater propensity for volatility. This is the case due to the fact that FCFF takes into account new outlays on capital equipment (CAPEX) and net working capital (NWC), both of which are susceptible to change; for example, they expand when times are good and decrease when times are bad. Therefore, in years in which major investments are made, EBITDA is significantly higher than FCFF. In spite of the fact that EBITDA is a relatively inaccurate proxy for FCFF, EBITDA multiples continue to be the most popular valuation method. Why? These are a few of the possible explanations. To begin, earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortisation (EBITDA) provide a pretty accurate measurement of the pre-tax cash flows created by the company’s current assets. Therefore, EBITDAmultiples make a great deal of sense when the majority of the value of the company derives from its existing assets (as is the case in the case of stable and mature firms). Second, EBITDA is far more consistent when compared to FCFF, which, in addition to being very variable, is also likely to be negative when the company is expanding quickly. EBITDA, on the other hand, is much more reliable. 15.4.2 EV/EBIT Multiple EV/EBIT ratio is defined as: Enterprise value (EV)/ Earnings before interest and taxes (EBIT) Earnings before interest and taxes is earnings from operating assets, before taxes. A variant of this ratio is: EV/ EBIT (1 – Tax) The operating income after tax, also known asthe net operating income after tax,serves asthe denominator of this multiple (NOPAT) Basic Determinants When viewed from the fundamental angle EV0 EBIT1 = (1-t)(1-reinvestment rate) WACC-g where t is the tax rate, WACC is the weighted average cost of capital, and g is the growth rate. Example ABC Company has a tax rate of 30 percent and a reinvestment rate of 70 percent. ABC WACC is 15 percent and growth rate is 12 percent. Calculate EV/EBIT. EV EBIT = (1-0.3)(1-0.7) 0.15 - 0.12 = 7 15.4.3 EV/FCFF Multiple The EV/FCFF multiple can be defined as: Enterprise value (EV)/ Free cash flow to firm (FCFF) Basic Determinants When viewed from the fundamental angle EV0 FCFF1 = 1/ (WACC – g) where WACC is the weighted average cost of capital and g is the growth rate.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0337.html -->
OTHER NON-DCF VALUATION MODELS | 323 Example: XYZ Limited’s WACC is 16 percent and its g is 11 percent. Calculate EV/FCFF. EV0/FCFF1 = 1/(0.16-0.11) = 20 15.4.4 EV/BV Multiple EV/BV multiple is defined as: Enterprise value (EV)/ Book value of assets (BV) Basic Determinants When viewed from the fundamental angle EV BV = ROIC-g WACC - g where ROIC is the return on invested capital, g is the growth rate, and WACC is the weighted average cost of capital. Example Vivek Company has an ROIC of 18 percent, growth rate of 11 percent, and WACC of 13 percent. Calculate EV/BV. EV/BV= (0.18-0.11)/ (0.13 - 0.11) = 3.5 15.4.5 EV/Sales Multiple The definition of EV/Sales multiple is Enterprise value(EV)/ Sales Basic Determinants EV/S=[After tax operating margin(1+g)(1-reinvestment rate)]/ (WACC – g) where g is the growth rate and WACC is the weighted average cost of capital. Example: Planned Limited’s after-tax operating margin is 11 percent and growth rate is 9 percent. Its reinvestment rate is 70 percent and the WACC is 14 percent. Calculate EV/S. EV/S = [0.11(1+0.09)(1-0.70)]/ (0.14-0.09) = 0.72 15.5 CHOOSING THE RIGHT MULTIPLES The choice of multiple can have a significant impact on the valuations due to the fact that various multiples yield varying resultsin terms of value. The selection of the appropriate multiple to be employed becomes quite complicated and calls for specialist knowledge. Prof. Aswath Damodaran, a well-known authority in this area, holds the opinion that we can adopt the multiple that best reflects our prejudice (the cynical perspective), employ all of the multiples (the bludgeon view), or choose the “best” multiple. The Cynical View: We have the option ofselecting a multiple that conformsto our preconceived notions. If we are looking to sell (purchase) a company, we should select the multiple that provides the highest possible value. Despite the fact that this may give the impression of manipulation rather than analysis, it appears to be a practice that is rather widespread. Even if we don’t have such nefarious purposes in mind, we should nevertheless educate ourselves on how to protect ourselves from the effects of it. First,


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0338.html -->
324 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT if we give the job of valuing the company to an analyst, who will have a natural tendency to follow his own prejudices, we need to provide our approval to the multiple that should be used and the companies that are considered to be comparable. Second, when we look at a relative valuation report, we should ask ourselves what the value would be if we employed a different multiple or a different set of comparables. The Bludgeon View: We are able to arrive at a conclusion regarding the valuation of a corporation by first utilising a number of different multiples. There are three different approaches to taking care of this. To begin, we have the ability to arrive at a range of valuesthat is generated by the different multiples. The difficulty with this scenario is that the range is likely to be far too broad to be of any value for making decisions. Second, we have the ability to compute a straightforward average of the many valuesthat have been produced by the various multiples. The simplicity of this strategy is one of its primary benefits. On the other hand, it givesthe same significance to the values derived from each multiple, despite the fact that some multiples may be more pertinent than others. Third, we have the option of calculating a weighted average, in which the relative importance of each number is reflected by the weight that is assigned to it. The Best Multiple: Although it is likely that we would want not to get rid of any information, it is possible that the most accurate estimate of worth can be reached by choosing the one multiple that is most suitable for the company that is being appraised. There are three different approaches to selecting the optimal multiple. According to the fundamental method, we ought to make use of the variable that has the highest association with the worth of the company in question. For instance, there is a high degree of correlation between current earnings and value in consumer product firms, but not in cyclical companies. This is due to the fact that consumer product companies tend to have more stable revenue streams. Therefore, price-earnings multiples are appropriate for the first group, but not for the second group. In the statistical method, each multiple is regressed against the fundamentals that are thought to have an effect on the value, and the R-squared value of each multiple is compared against the other multiples until one has the highest value. The standard strategy comprises making use of the multiplier that has evolved to become the one that is utilised the most frequently for a certain setting or industry. In this context, the following points may be noted: a. The P/E multiple is more appropriate for companies that have (a) a demonstrated track record of positive earnings and (b) no large non-cash expenses. Thisis because these companies have a proven track record of being profitable. b. The PEG multiple is best utilised by companies that have low volatility in their earnings per share growth rates and risk characteristics. c. The P/B multiple is better suited for companies whose balance sheets represent the market worth of their assets in a manner that is at least somewhat accurate. Banks and other financial institutions are two good examples. d. Companies with significant non-cash expenses are better suited to use the EV/EBITDA multiple (depreciation and amortisation). Examples include companies that require a significant amount of capital investment, such as airlines, phone providers, and refineries. e. The EV/FCFF multiple is more suited for companies that have stable growth and predictable capital expenditures. f. The EV/sales multiple makes sense for companies that are still relatively young and have not yet established a track record of positive profitability.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0339.html -->
OTHER NON-DCF VALUATION MODELS | 325 15.6 BOOK VALUE APPROACH MODEL Relying just on the data shown in a company’s balance sheet is the least complicated method for determining the company’s market value.When determining the value of a company, there are two methods that are functionally equal to using the information from the balance sheet. To begin, a direct tally of the book values of the investor claims can be performed. Second, a complete inventory of the company’s assets can be compiled, and then from that inventory, claims from parties other than investors (such as accounts payable and provisions) can be subtracted. Let’s look at an example to further understand how this strategy works. The Balance Sheet of BML Limited as of the 31st of March, 2022 is given below. We can determine the worth of the company by utilising either the investor claim approach or the assetliability approach after looking at the balance sheet. Balance Sheet of BML Limited as on 31/03/2022 Rs. Crs. Liabilities Amount Assets Amount Share Capital 1600 Fixed Assets 3000 Equity 1600 Gross 5000 Reserve & Surplus 1130 Depreciation 2000 Secured Loans 3000 Investment 500 Term Loan 1000 Current Assets 3490 Debenture 2000 Cash & Bank 500 Unsecured Loan 660 Debtors 1690 Bank Credit 260 Stock 1200 Inter Corporate Loan 400 Other Assets 100 Current Liabilities & Provisions 600 6990 6990 Balance Sheet Valuation Investor Claims Approach Amount Asset Liability Approach Amount Share Capital 1600 Total Assets 6990 Reserve & Surplus 1130 Less: Current Liabilities & Provisions 600 Secured Loans 3000 Unsecured Loan 660 6390 6390 Values that are based on uncertain projections of the future are considered by some to be a less accurate assessment of value than values that are based on book values that are found on a balance sheet. In point


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0340.html -->
326 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT of fact, the initial intention behind accounting was for the company’s balance sheet to be able to produce a trustworthy assessment of the value of the firm’s assets and equity. Mortimer B. Daniels describes this ideal in the following way in an article on ‘Principles of Asset Valuation’that was published in The Accounting Review in June, 1934: “In short, the lay reader of financialstatements usually believesthat the total asset figure of the balance sheet is indicative, and is intended to be so of the value of the company. He probably understands this ‘value’ as what the business could be sold for, market value – the classic meeting of the minds between a willing buyer and a willing seller.” He has also stated that there is no consensus as to just what “sound value” means as applied to a business entity. The profession of accounting has had a hard time putting this principle into reality. They have determined certain principles by comparing the priority that must be given to the past cost of an asset to the estimated value of the item at the present time. For instance, the value of fixed assets is determined by their initial cost minus any depreciation that has occurred as a result of their use, while the value of current assets is determined by either their cost or their realisable market value, whichever is lower. Accountants, on the other hand, have not yet worked out how to properly value intangible assets such as the equity of a brand or technical know-how. They have consequently decided to disregard them for the most part. It is very difficult to say as to when the book value becomes a reasonable substitute for the actual worth. Book value is a good proxy for real worth when applied to a tangible asset that has no growth potential, very few growth opportunities, or no chances at all for producing greater returns. Book value and true worth will be very different for an organisation that has large chances for growth and the possibility of producing returns that are significantly higher than average. The degree of precision that may be achieved using the book value technique is directly proportional to the degree to which the net book values of the assets accurately represent their fair market values. There are three potential causes for a discrepancy between book prices and market values: a) The difference between the asset’s book value and its current value will widen as a result of inflation. The historical cost of an asset is subtracted from its total depreciation to arrive at its book value. As a result, it does not take into account inflation, which is unquestionably one of the factors that influences market value. b) As a result of advancements in technology, certain assets become obsolete and useless even before they have been completely written off in the books as having been depreciated. c) On the balance sheet, organisational capital does not appear, despite the fact that it is a very important asset. Value is added to an entity’s organisational capital when employees, customers, and suppliers are brought together in a connection that is mutually beneficial and productive for all parties involved. One of the most essential aspects of organisational capital is the fact that it cannot be readily disassociated from the company in its current state as a functioning entity. 15.6.1 Book Value Revision to Account for Replacement Cost Even though the earning power of an asset might not be related to the book value of the asset, particularly if the asset is old, it is likely to be related to the current cost of replacing the asset. Therefore, current replacement costs may be used in place of the asset’s book value. The following formula is used to assign values to each of the assets:


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0341.html -->
OTHER NON-DCF VALUATION MODELS | 327 Cash and Cash Equivalents Cash is highly liquid asset. As a result, assigning a value to it is not problematic in any way. In point of fact, having an asset that is so easy to value is something to be thankful for. Debtors: In most cases, the value of debtors is determined by their face value. When the creditworthiness of the debtors is in question, it is prudent to set aside money to cover possible defaulted debts. Inventories: Raw materials, work-in-process, and finished goods are the three distinct types of inventory that can be distinguished from one another. The most recent cost of acquisition may be used to determine the value of raw materials. Either the cost of work-in-process (which includes the cost of raw materials in addition to the cost of processing) or the selling price can be used as a lens through which to analyse work-inprocess (selling price of the final product less expenses to be incurred in translating work-in-process into sales). In most cases, an inventory of finished goods is valued by deducting the expenses that will be incurred in the process of packaging, handling, transporting, selling, and collecting receivables from the sale price that can be expected to be realised in the normal course of business. Other Current Assets: Deposits, prepaid expenses, and accruals are examples of other types of current assets that are valued at their book value. Non-Current Assets: Land, buildings and other civil works, as well as plant and machinery, make up the majority of a company’s fixed tangible assets. The value of land is determined as if it were uninhabited and on the market. It is possible to value buildings and civil works by subtracting the amount of physical depreciation and deterioration from the cost of replacement. It is possible to determine the value of plant and machinery by calculating the market price of comparable (used) assets and adding the cost of transportation and installation to that figure. Intangible fixed assets like software, licences, spectrum, leases etc. can be valued at their fair market price. Non-Operating Assets The assets of an organisation that are not essential to its day-to-day operations are referred to collectively as “non-operating assets.” The types of non-operating assets that are most frequently encountered are financial securities, excess land, and buildings that are only occasionally used. The assets in question are evaluated based on their true value in the market. 15.6.2 Adjusted Book Value Approach: An Illustration In December of 2021, the price of a share of ABC Limited’s equity on the market was fluctuating around Rs. 300. At that time, ABC had a paid-up equity capital of Rs. 2,000 crore, divided into Rs. 200 crore shares with a face value of Rs. 10 each.Apublic statement wasissued by the company stating that market conditions were significantly undervaluing the share. It was argued that the intrinsic value of each share was approximately Rs. 550. The reasoning behind this number is as follows:


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0342.html -->
328 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT Rs. Crore Replacement cost of ABC’s plants 50,000 Value of ABC’s 50 percent shareholding in MNP Limited 60,000 Cash Holdings 9,000 Value of Assets 1,19,000 Less: Outstanding Debt 9,000 Net Asset Value (NAV) 1,10,000 NAV per share (110000/200) Rs. 550 15.6.3 Adjusting Book Values to Reflect Liquidation Values Finding out how much cash the assets listed on a company’s balance sheet could bring in if the company were immediately put into liquidation isthe method that providesthe most accurate approximation of the fair market value of those assets. If there is a healthy secondary market for the assets, then the liquidation values will be the same as the prices on the secondary market. Nevertheless, there aren’t any active secondary marketsfor the majority of business assets. In situationslike these, the appraiser istasked with attempting to make an educated guess regarding the possible sales price of the assets. The fact that the liquidation value approach pays no attention to organisational capital isits mostsignificant shortcoming. The company is valued not as a going concern but rather as a collection of assets that can be sold separately from one another. This strategy is the only one that makes sense for a company whose value is greater in its dead state than in its living state. 15.6.4 Problems with Asset-Based Valuation The asset-based valuation approach, in essence, makes an attempt to recast the balance sheet by (a) determining the assets and liabilities that are included on the balance sheet based on their most recent values in the market and (b) determining which assets and liabilities are not included on the balance sheet and assigning a market value to each of those assets and liabilities. However, despite its apparent ease, it actually raises a number of very challenging issues: a. The assets that are included on the balance sheet might not be actively traded, which means that it might be difficult to obtain accurate market values for them. b. Even if the information is available, market values may not accurately reflect the true value of the asset due to inefficiencies in the market. c. The value that an asset has in the market may not be the same as the value that the asset has in the particular use that the company puts the asset to. It is possible that the cost of an asset’s current replacement or its selling price (its liquidation value) does not accurately reflect the value that the asset contributes when used in a particular going concern. d. The process of identifying and appraising omitted assets is fraught with difficulty, which is one of the issues concerning these assets. Intangible assets are typically what are considered omitted assets. It can be difficult to recognise and assess their worth.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0343.html -->
OTHER NON-DCF VALUATION MODELS | 329 e. Even if individual assets can be located and valued, it is likely that the total of the individual values of all identified assets will not equal the value of the assets as a whole. This is true even if individual assets can be located and valued. According to the words of Stephen Penman, “Assets are used jointly. Indeed, entrepreneurs found businesses with the intention of generating value through the novel combination of existing assets. It is difficult to put a price on the asset known as “synergy.” The question that needs to be answered in order to properly value the company is how much its individual assets are worth when added together.” 15.6.5 The Conclusion Only in extremely unusual circumstances, such as the valuation of regulated industries, does it make sense to use the unadjusted book value approach. It makes sense for businesses that derive the majority of their value from owning tangible resourcesto take one of three approachesto calculating their adjusted book value: the replacement cost approach, the liquidation value approach, or the adjusted book value approach. Due to the fact that most businesses have valuable organisational capital, situations like these are extremely uncommon. Therefore, the book value approach is only somewhat applicable in the vast majority of actual-life scenarios. 15.7 STOCK AND DEBT APPROACH When a company’ssecurities are traded on a public exchange, the value of the company can be determined by simply adding up the current market value of all of the company’s outstanding securities.Appraisers of properties use a straightforward methodology that they refer to as the stock and debt approach. Another name for this strategy is the market-based approach. An illustration of a stock and debt approach is provided by the analysis of BML Limited’s valuation. As of the 31st of March in 2022, the company had a total of 25 crore shares in circulation. The market value of BML’s equity was Rs. 3,000 crores at the day’s closing price of Rs. 120. In addition, the company had outstanding debt that had a market value of Rs. 1100 crore as of the 31st of March, 2022. When the market value of the firm’s equity and its market value of its debt are added together, the result is a total firm value for BML Limited as of March 31, 2022 that is equal to Rs. 4,100 crore. LET US SUM UP An analyst must pick between three different approaches—discounted cash flow valuation, relative valuation, and option pricing models—and several models within each approach when valuing a company, asset, or its equity. The qualities of the company or asset being valued—its earnings level, growth potential, sources of earnings growth, stability of debt, and dividend policy—will primarily determine this decision. As crucial to valuation as understanding the models and having the appropriate inputs is matching the valuation model to the asset or company being appraised. In relative valuation, the estimated worth of an asset is based on the pricing of similar assets. To do this comparison, you must first convert prices into multiples, i.e. standardise prices, and then compare these multiples among firms that you deem comparable. Earnings, book value, revenue, or industry-specific characteristics can be used to normalise prices.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0344.html -->
330 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT Earnings multiples can be applied to valuation in a number of different ways. One method is to compare earnings multiples across a carefully defined group of comparable organisations and to subjectively control for variations in growth and risk while also taking into account differences in cash flows. One alternative is to broaden the definition of a comparable firm to include the entire industry (for example, technology) or the market as a whole, and then use statistical methods to account for differences in the fundamentals of the businesses being compared. With equity multiples, we scale market value to earnings, book value, or revenues. The price-earnings ratio scales equities market value to net income. Analysts define this simple ratio differently. Equity multiples are established by predicted profits growth, equity risk, and cash flow possibilities. Greater growth, lower risk, and higher payout ratios should result in higher multiples of earnings, book value of equity, and revenues. Multiples vary depending on country, time, and company fundamentals. Failing to control for basic differences might lead to erroneous multiples comparison conclusions. Most investors don’t understand how price and book value relate.Afirm’s price–book value ratio depends on its predicted pay-out ratio, profits growth, and riskiness. Higher returns contribute to higher price–book value ratios, and lower returns lead to lower PBV ratios. High price–book value ratios with low returns on equity and low price–book value ratios with high returns on equity should draw investor attention. When a company’s stocks are publicly traded, its value can be calculated by summing the market value of all outstanding securities.Appraisers of property taxesrefer to thisstraightforward method asthe stock and debt method. This approach is also known as the market approach. KEYWORDS Relative Valuation, Equity Valuation Multiples, Relative Price Earning Ratio, Enterprise Value Multiples, Book Value, Market Approach CHECK YOUR PROGRESS 1. Relative valuation approach is also known as (a) Income approach (b) Asset approach (c) Liability approach (d) None of the above 2. The worth of an asset can be determined by analysing its pricing in relation to the pricing of comparable assets in terms of a common variable such as earnings, cash flows, book value, or sale is known as ___ (a) Discounted cash flow valuation (b) Relative valuation (c) Contingent claim valuation (d) Free cash flow valuation 3. A ratio that illustrates the stock market’s readiness to pay for one rupee of earnings per share is referred to as an earnings multiple. This is known as __________. (a) Ratio of Price to Net Profit (b) Ratio of Earnings to Price (c) Ratio of Price to Earnings (d) None of the above

STRUCTURE 16.0 Objectives 16.1 Introduction 16.2 Intangibles –Brand, Human Valuation etc. 16.3 Real Estate Firms 16.4 Start-up Firms 16.5 Firms with Negative or Low Earnings 16.6 Financial Service Companies 16.7 Distressed Firms 16.8 Valuation of Cash and Cross Holdings 16.9 Warrants and Convertibles 16.10 Cyclical & Non-Cyclical Companies 16.11 Holding Companies 16.12 E-commerce Firms Let Us Sum Up Keywords Check Your Progress Solutions U N I T 16 SPECIAL CASES OF VALUATION

<!-- Page Start: text/part0348.html -->
334 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 16.0 OBJECTIVES After studying this unit, you will have better understanding of: • Intangible assets and their valuation • Valuation of Real-estate firms, E-commerce firms and Start-up firms • Valuation of Holding companies • Valuation of distressed firms and loss making firms • Valuation of cyclical firms • Valuation of cash and cross holdings • Valuation of Warrants and convertibles 16.1 INTRODUCTION In the last few units, we concentrated on Corporate and Business Valuations and also discussed the various approaches and methodologies used by Valuers and Analysts for the purpose of valuation. In this unit, we shall be discussing some special cases of valuation. 16.2 INTANGIBLES –BRAND, HUMAN VALUATION ETC. It is in our nature, as human beings, to make a differentiation between the assets that are visible to us and the ones that we cannot see, and to feel somewhat more safe and secure about the items or assets that we can see and feel. However, the latter category encompasses a wide range of assets, some of which are: goodwill, a brand name, devoted employees, technological capabilities, and so on. One of the most prominent arguments made against valuation methodsin general and in particular against financial analysts, isthat we give intangible assets very little consideration, which leadsto an undervaluation of those assets. We can addressthis concern by taking a comprehensive look at intangible assets across a range of contexts. We start off by taking a look at intangible assets that can stand on their own and generate cash flows (commercially developed patents, copyrights, trademarks, and licences). These assets have monetary value and are shown in the balance sheet of the company. As per Companies Act 2013, the fixed assets are classified as tangible fixed assets and intangible fixed assets. For example, the software purchased by a company for its computer systems is an asset having monetary value but is intangible in form. Also, a Spectrum, purchased by a telecom company is an intangible asset with monetary value. This type of assets can be more or less accurately valued by using the traditional discounted cash flow (DCF) models. Next in line are the intangible assets like a company’s brand name and reputation, which are examples of intangible assets that collectively generate cash flows for the company that owns them, but which are far more difficult to single out and value on their own. In spite of this, we may contend that the traditional discounted cash flow valuation techniques are able to accurately capture their values, and that adding a premium for them after using the DCF valuation can be considered as double counting. In this unit, we will also examine some of the most enigmatic intangible assetsi.e., those that have the potential to generate cash flows in the future but do not do so right now. This group will consist of assets as diverse as undeveloped patents and operational flexibility, and due to the fact that there is an option available to the Company as to their use, there is a big challenge faced by the Company about the valuation of these intangible assets. 16.2.1 Importance of Intangible Assets Over the course of the past quarter of a century, intellectual capital has developed into the most important category of assets. The traditional forms of intellectual property assets, such as patents, trademarks, and copyrights, are what are meant to be referred to when using the term “intellectual capital.”


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0349.html -->
SPECIAL CASES OF VALUATION | 335 The majority of the value of the earliest publicly traded companies, which emerged as a result of the industrial revolution, was derived from their physical assets. These early global corporate giants, which includes companies like General Motors, Con Edison, Standard Oil, and AT&T etc. held land, buildings, and factoriesthat were straightforward for accountantsto measure and value. In India, we have examples of companies like Tata Steel, TCS, Infosys, Alembic, Indian Hotels, Godrej & Boyce and ITC. The last fifty years have given rise to a new breed of companies in the world, such as Apple, Coca-Cola, Pepsico, Amazon, Microsoft, Intel, Facebook, and Pfizer, and Indian companies like Bharat Biotech, Serum Institute, Wipro, Flipkart etc. that derive the majority of their value from intangible assets. These assets include brand recognition, customer loyalty, and intellectual property. Intangible assets might take the form of a company’s brand name (as is the case with Pepsico, Coca-Cola etc.), patents (as is the case with Serum Institute, Bharat Biotech, Pfizer etc.), or technological competence (as is the case with Infosys, Intel and Microsoft etc.), but they all have certain things in common. The first problem is that conventional accounting standards either grossly underestimate their value or ignore it entirely; as a result, the balance statements of these companies provide little indication in thisregard. The second reason is that these intangible assets contribute significantly to the market valuations of these organisations; there is evidence, for instance, that brand name alone may explain more than half of the value in many consumers’ product companies. Last but not least, the failure to value these intangible assets causes a distortion not just in accounting measures of profitability like return on equity and capital, but also in market measures of value like P/E ratios and EV/EBITDA multiples. Between the years 1975 and 2015, the proportion of market value attributable to intangible assets rose from 17% to 84% according to OceanTomo, a Management Consultancy firm providing advice focused on mattersinvolving intellectual property (IP) and other intangible assets. The firm brought their Intellectual Asset Market Value (IAMV) Study up to date in July 2020 in order to explore the implications the novel coronavirus would have on the economy. Intangible assets, according to them accounted for 90% of the market value of the S&P 500, which indicates that COVID-19 accelerated the trend of increasing IAMV share. When one looks at the chart below, which illustrates the market capitalization of the S&P 500, one can see how the value of intangible assets has increased over the years. VALUE OF ASSETS* IN SELECTED GROUP FOR S&P 500 COMPANIES INTANGIBLE ASSETS TANGIBLE ASSETS TOTAL YEAR 2009 2014 0.00 5.00 10.00 15.00 7.50 3.00 4.10 32.50 5.90 30.00 35.00 20.00 25.00 2019 $ TRILLIONS 10.50 15.40 19.50 26.60 Source: Company reports, PwC Analysis *Implied market value of intangibles = Market cap + Book value of total liabilities - Book value of intangibles (net PP&E)


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0350.html -->
336 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT Have valuation experts been able to significantly improve upon the work that has been done by accountants in determining the value of intangible assets? Given that we derive such a significant portion of the information that we use in valuation from accounting statements, one could argue that the valuation of intangible assets has suffered from many of the same limitations as the accounting measures. This is because accounting statements are one of the primary sources of information that we use in valuation. In point of fact, the pressure that is being put on accountants to more accurately reflect the value of intangible assets like brand name on financial statements has provided an impetus for valuation analysts to take a closer look at how they have valued or failed to value the very same assets. These intangible assets include brand names. 16.2.2 Independent & Cash-Flow-Generating Intangible Assets Those intangible assets that are attached to a particular product or product line and create cash flows, are the ones that are the least complicated to evaluate. These assets typically have finite lifespan, over which the cash flows have to be estimated, but otherwise, they are not qualitatively distinct from the many tangible assets that create cash flows over finite periods of time. In this part, we will look at a few examples of assets that fit under this category. 1. Trademarks, Copyrights, and Licenses The owner of a trademark, copyright, or licence has the sole authority to manufacture or sell the associated goods or render the associated service. As a direct result of this, their value is determined by the cash flows that can be produced as a result of holding the exclusive right. To the extent that there are expenses connected with production, the value is derived from the additional returns that are received as a result of possessing the exclusive right. The value of copyrights and trademarks can be determined in one of two ways, just like the value of other assets. A discounted cash flow valuation of the asset can be obtained by first estimating the expected cash flowsthat will result from owning the asset, then applying a discount rate to these cash flowsthat isreflective of the uncertainty associated with them, and finally taking the present value of this value. Alternately, we have the option of attempting a relative value, which is when we apply a multiple to the revenues or income that we believe can be earned by the copyright or the trademark. The multiple is often estimated by taking a look at the prices at which assets of a comparable nature have been sold in the past. It is possible that, in the process of creating these estimates, we will come into estimating challenges that are specific to these assets. First and foremost, we have to take into consideration the fact that a copyright or trademark grants us exclusive rights for a predetermined amount of time. As a consequence of this, the cash flows that we will estimate will only be for this period, and there won’t be a terminal value in the majority of cases. Second, we have to consider the potential financial impact of infringing upon the copyright as well as the trademark. At least two different things could be included in these prices. The first expense is the cost associated with enforcing exclusivity, which includeslegal and monitoring costs. The second factor isthat the value of the right will be decreased because it isimpossible for usto guarantee that there will be no violations of the right, regardless of how meticulously we monitor it; as a result, any lost income (profits) will make the right less valuable. 2. Franchises The owner of a franchise is granted the right to promote and sell a company’s branded good orservice under the franchise’s name. The thousands of Domino’s Pizza or McDonald’sfast-food shopslocated all over the world are two examples of franchises. Other examples include the Chain of Delhi Public


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0351.html -->
SPECIAL CASES OF VALUATION | 337 Schools, Dealerships for the automobile industry, and even Ola, an app-based taxi service. In either scenario, the individual who buys the franchise, known as the franchisee, is responsible for paying the franchisor (McDonald’s or Maruti Suzuki), either an initial charge or an ongoing annual cost in order to operate the franchise. In exchange, the individual receives the power of the brand name, as well as the support of the corporation and advertising backing. 16.2.2.1 Approaches for valuation It is typically challenging to isolate and put a monetary value on intangible assets that both garner the most attention and are considered to have the greatest value. They do not provide cash flows on their own, but they enable a firm to charge higher prices for its products, which in turn produces a greater amount of cash flows for the company. However, there are three distinct approaches that we can take in order to arrive at an estimate of the worth of these intangible assets, despite the fact that it is more difficult to do so. The 3 approaches are as under: (a) Capital Investment: The amount of money that a company has put into an asset over the course of its existence can give us a good idea of how much that asset is worth on paper. To do this with a brand name, for example, you would need to look at advertising expenditures throughout time, capitalise these expenses, and then look at the balance of these expenses today that has not yet been amortised. This method may not match or even come close to the asset’s market value, despite the fact that it isthe leastsubjective of the three approaches. However, it follows the pattern that accountants use to determine the value of other tangible assets that are recorded in the books. (b) Discounted Cash Flow Valuation: We have the ability to discount the anticipated increase in cash flows that will be brought to the company as a result of the intangible asset in question. This will require isolating the percentage of an organization’s aggregate cash flows that can be attributable to its brand name or its level of technological expertise and then discounting back these cash flows at a rate that is appropriate for the situation. (c) Relative valuation: Comparing the market value of a company (with the intangible asset) to the market value of companies that are similarly similar but do not have the intangible asset is one approach to isolating the effect of an intangible asset such as a brand name. This disparity can be traced back to the existence of the intangible asset. 16.3 REAL ESTATE FIRMS The concepts of asset valuation that were originally established for financial assets can also be applied to real assets. The most important part of real asset investments is often made up of properties that are purchased asinvestments. When it comesto determining the worth of real estate, analystsin the real estate industry have, for a significant amount of time, relied on their own unique takes on various valuation models. They believe that the techniques that were established to value publicly traded stocks cannot be used to determine the value of real estate since it belongs to a completely different asset class. We may look at things from a different perspective, which is that even if stocks and real estate belong to separate asset classes, the fundamentals of valuation shouldn’t change depending on the asset class.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0352.html -->
338 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT The approaches of intrinsic and relative valuation, that we used to analyse equities, ought to be applicable to the analysis of real estate as well. Having said that, there are significant estimating challenges to overcome that are specific to real estate, and we can attempt to look at the problem of valuing real estate enterprises from the standpoint of addressing this challenge. 16.3.1 Real Versus Financial Assets Both real estate and financial assets share a number of aspects in common, including the fact that their values should be decided by the cash flows that they create, the degree of uncertainty associated with those cash flows, and the predicted growth in those cash flows. When all other factors are held constant, the value of an asset is proportional to the volume of cash flows, their growth rate, and the level of risk associated with those cash flows. The asset’s value rises in proportion to the risk level. The two categories of assets also differ significantly from one another in major ways. Many people believe that the risk and return models, that are utilised in the analysis of financial assets, cannot be utilised in the analysis of real estate due to the differences in the levels of liquidity that exist between the two markets as well as the types of investors that participate in each market. This Chapter will look at some non-conventional models of risk and return in addition to the more standard ones. There are additional distinctions to be made between the kinds of cash flows that are produced by investments in real estate and financial markets. Specifically, real estate investments often have limited lifespans and must be appraised appropriately because of this fact. Stocks are only one example of the many types of financial assets that might exist forever. The value that is allocated to each asset at the conclusion of the estimating period reflects these differences in asset lifetimes and how long they have been owned. Because of the anticipated increase in the cash flows and the fact that these cash flows are anticipated to continue indefinitely, the value of a stock at its terminal stage, which may be five or ten years in the future, is often significantly higher than its current value. There is a possibility that the terminal value of a building will be less than its current value. This is because the worth of the building may decrease as it is used. On the other hand, the land component will have an endless life and, in certain circumstances, it may be the most important factor in determining the final value. 16.3.2 Discounted Cash Flow Valuation The value of any asset that produces cash flows is equal to the asset’s predicted cash flows multiplied by their present value. It is possible to use discounted cash flow valuation models, such as the dividend discount model, to determine the value of cash-flow producing real estate investments in the same way that these models can be used to determine the value of financial assets. In order to properly evaluate real estate investments by using discounted cash flow valuation, the following steps need to be taken: a) Determination of the level of risk associated with real estate investments and basing your estimate of the appropriate discount rate on this level of risk. b) Determination of an estimate of the anticipated cash flows which are expected from the real estate investment over the course of the asset’s life. These issues are discussed in detail as follows: 1. Estimating Discount Rates We went over the methods that are utilised to estimate the costs of equity, debt, and capital for an investment in Module B as well as the earlier chapters of Module C. Do those models also apply to transactions involving real estate? In that case, do any adjustments have to be made to them? If not, what should we do in its place? In this part of the article, we investigate whether or not risk and


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0353.html -->
SPECIAL CASES OF VALUATION | 339 return models can be applied to real estate investments. During this phase of the process, we consider if the assumption that the marginal investor is well diversified is a justifiable one for real estate investments. If thisisthe case, we next determine the most effective way to measure the parameters of the model—risk-free rate, beta, and risk premium—in order to estimate the cost of equity. Traditional risk and return models don’t take into account all of the potential dangers associated with real estate investments; thus, we investigate how these hazards might be factored into value calculations. 2. Cost of Equity The risk of any asset, whether real or financial, is defined as the percentage of that asset’s variance that cannot be diversified away by the fundamental modelsthat are used to estimate the cost of equity for financial assets. These models are used to calculate the cost of equity. This non-diversifiable risk is quantified in the capital asset pricing model (CAPM) by the market beta, as well asin the arbitrage pricing model and the multifactor model. In order for these modelsto arrive at these results, the most important assumptions that they make are that the marginal investor in the asset has a good level of diversification and that risk is assessed in terms of the variability of returns. If one were to make the assumption that these models are applicable to real assets as well, then one would calculate the risk of a real asset based on its beta in relation to the market portfolio in the CAPM, and based on its factor betas in multifactor models. If we do this, on the other hand, we are making the assumption, just like we did with publicly traded stocks, that the average real estate investor has a diverse portfolio. 3. Are the Marginal Investors in Real Estate Well Diversified? Analysts are of the opinion that due to the size of the required investments, real estate investors may not be able to diversify their portfolios as well as they would like. In addition, they point out that investing in real estate necessitates having localised expertise, and that those who acquire this information opt to participate in real estate either largely or exclusively as a form of investment. As a result of this, they note that the use of the capital asset pricing model or multifactor models, both of which assume that only non-diversifiable risk is rewarded, is not an appropriate method for estimating the cost of equity. This is because these models assume that risk can only be rewarded in one direction. This argument contains a grain of truth, but it is not without a flaw, and it is pretty simple to debunk it by pointing out that: The concentration of their investment in real estate is a strategic decision made by many investors. They view it as an opportunity to capitalise on the particular knowledge they have gained in the field of real estate. As a result, we would think of them in the same way that we think of investors who choose to have their portfolios consist solely of technology stocks. Even substantial real estate investments can be subdivided into smaller parcels, giving investors the opportunity to hold both financial assets and real estate investmentsin their portfoliossimultaneously. This is especially true in light of the fact that real estate is currently being securitized. In the same way that the marginal investor in stocks is frequently an institutional investor who has the resources to diversify and the ability to maintain low transaction costs, the marginal investor in many real estate markets in today’s market has sufficient resources to diversify. If real estate developers and private investors continue to insist on higher expected returns because they are not diversified, then real estate investments will increasingly be held by real estate investment trusts, limited partnerships, and corporations. These entities attract more diversified investors because they require lower required returns from those investors. This pattern is clearly seen in the United States and has the potential to emerge in other nations as well over the course of time.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0354.html -->
340 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 16.3.3 Measuring Risk for Real Assets in Asset Pricing Models Even if it is conceded that a real asset’s risk is equal to its market beta in the CAPM and its factor betas in multifactor models, there are a number of concerns pertaining to the measurement and utilisation of these risk parameters that need to be investigated. These concerns include a variety of issues that need to be examined. Consider the conventional method for measuring betas in the capital asset pricing model applied to a publicly traded company in order to gain some understanding of the measurement issuesthat are associated with real assets. At first, the prices of the stock are gathered from the company’s historical records, and then returns are computed on a regular basis (daily, weekly, or monthly). Second, in order to calculate the beta, the returns on these individualstocks are compared to the returns on an index covering the same time period. These tasks are not as simple to do when dealing with real estate. 16.4 START-UP FIRMS It is not difficult to assign a value to publicly listed and traded companies that have been in business for some time. But the approach cannot be the same for valuation of new businesses that have only recently started their operations and especially those firms that are using new technologies. There are many analysts who contend that it is impossible to assign a value to these companies because they do not have a history and, in certain cases, do not have any goods or services that they can sell. This section offers an alternative viewpoint to the one presented previously. Although we acknowledge that putting a value on a new company is more challenging than putting a value on a company that has been around for a while, we maintain that the principles of valuation have not changed. The current value of the predicted cash flows from a young company’s operations is what determines its worth; nevertheless, making accurate estimations of these expected cash flows may require us to look beyond the typical sources of information that we consult. 16.4.1 Information Constraints When determining the worth of a company, we have to will look at information from three different sources. The first thing to look at is the company’s most recent set of financial statements. We may establish how profitable a company’s investments are or have been by using them, as well as how much the company reinvests back into the businessto stimulate future growth and for all of the other inputsthat are required in any valuation. The second factor is the company’s history, both in terms of its earnings and the prices it has been selling its products for. The profits and revenue history of a company over time can be used to make judgements about how cyclical a company’s business has been and how much growth it has showed, whereas the price history of a company can help us estimate the risk associated with that company.
