Finally, we can estimate crucial inputs on risk, growth, and cash flows by looking at the firm’s competitors or peer group in order to acquire a measurement of how much better or worse a firm is than its competition. This can be done by comparing the firm to its peers. Although it would be ideal for usto have substantial knowledge from all three sources, we may frequently be forced to choose between increasing the amount of one sort of information and decreasing the amount of another if we have no other option. Therefore, the fact that there is a significant amount of background information available on each of India’s major automobile manufacturers makes up for the fact that there are relatively few of them. In comparison, there may only be a few years’worth of information regarding the Toppers Academy, but the company operates in a market (specialised online coaching) that contains


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0355.html -->
SPECIAL CASES OF VALUATION | 341 a large number of other enterprises that are comparable to it. The absence of history at the company is compensated for by the simplicity with which industry averages can be obtained and the precision with which these averages can be calculated. There are some companies, particularly those operating in emerging parts of the economy, in which you can experience informational difficulties. To begin, most of these businesses have not been around for more than a year or two, which results in a history that is quite limited in scope. Second, the existing disclosures of their financial position provide relatively little information regarding the aspect of their assets—expected growth—that isthe single most important contributor to the value of those assets. Third, these companies are frequently the pioneers in the industry in which they operate. In many instances, there is neither a competition nor a peer group against whom they can be evaluated in order to determine their standing. When it comes to the valuation of these companies, you may, as a result, find that you are confined on all three counts in terms of the information. How have investors reacted to the lack of information that has been provided? Some people have come to the conclusion that certain equities cannot be evaluated, and as a result, they should not be included in a portfolio. Others have claimed that even while these stocks cannot be valued using conventional methods, the problem rests with the models rather than with the stocks themselves. On the basis of the scant information that was made available to them, they have developed novel and creative explanations to justify the costs that were paid for them. 16.4.2 The Analysis Framework in General The processes required to evaluate a company that has negative earnings, little or no historical data, and few comparable companies are, for the most part, the same stagesthat are involved in any other valuation. When appraising fledgling or startup enterprises, we are going to take a look at some of the challenges that are likely to appear at each level. Step 1: Assessment of the Enterprise’s Current Standing: When valuing companies, it is normal practice to collect the inputsfor the current year by using data from the most recent fiscal year as a point of reference. The numbers have a tendency to shift drastically from one period to the next for businesses that have declining earnings despite experiencing rapid expansion in revenue. As a consequence of this, it is prudent to consider the most recent information that one is able to acquire, at the very least with regard to revenues and profits. For example, utilising the revenues and earnings for the last 12 months will produce a significantly better estimate of value than using the earnings from the most recent financial year. This is because the last 12 months includes more recent data. It is possible that some items, including operating leases and options outstanding, are not updated asregularly as others. Thisissomething that isreal. Despite this, we would propose that estimatesshould be used for these inputs, and that more recent data should be used when valuing companies. Step 2: Revenue Growth Estimation Young companies often bring in a relatively modest quantity of revenue, but it is reasonable to anticipate that this amount will increase at a significant rate in the foreseeable future. It should come as no surprise that this is a crucial component in these appraisals; hence, it is recommended that data is drawn from a variety of sources. Historical percentage increase in the company’s total revenue is also quite important. It will become increasingly challenging to sustain very high growth rates as the company expands because the size at which it operates will increase as it does so. Therefore, a company that increased its size by three times just two years ago and by two times the previous year is likely to expand at a rate that is lower this year.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0356.html -->
342 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT Rate of expansion in the general market that this company operates in needs to be examined in detail. It is a great deal simpler for businesses to sustain high growth rates in markets that are expanding at high rates themselves than it is for them to do so in markets that are not changing much at all. Barriersto entry and competitive advantages held by the company must be examined in detail. To be able to maintain high growth rates over time, a company needs to have some sort of competitive edge that can be maintained over time. This can result from having legal protection (asisthe case with a patent), having a product or service that is superior, having a brand name, or being the first person to enter a market. It is far more likely that rapid growth will continue for an extended length of time if the competitive advantage appears to be sustainable. If this is not the case, the decline will occur considerably more quickly. Step 3: Stable-growth Associated Operating Margin Estimation High revenue growth will only make a losing firm’slosses worse over time.Ayoung firm’s value depends on the anticipation that its negative operating margin will turn positive. The key test in valuation is estimating a young, high-growth company’s operating margin when its growth stabilises. This assignment is harder without comparables. However, the following suggestions may help in this regard: a) Consider this company’s genuine competitors by analysing its business. Tesla is an electric car and a technological company, yet it’s an automaker. Tesla’s margins may approach those of other automakers, at least from the margins’ viewpoint. b) Analyse the company’s income statement to determine the operating margin. Many early start-up enterprises that declare losses do so because a big amount of operational expenses is incurred in anticipation of future growth and should be considered capital expenses. Since many of these charges are included as selling, general, and administrative expenses in the statement of profit and loss, predicting margins and profitability without taking these expenses into account, helps determine how profitable a company’s products are. Step 4: Estimate Reinvestment to Generate Growth When examining a new company, one cannot ignore the importance of reinvestment because it is necessary for the company’s growth and cannot be ignored under any circumstances. In contrast to a mature company, however, it is unlikely that there is much in the past of the company that can assist in determining how much the company will need to reinvest in the future. It is likely that the form of the company’s reinvestment and the quantity of money that is put back into the business will shift as the company expands, and the task at hand is to determine this amount. The following equation can be used to describe the relationship that exists between increasing levels of operational income, increasing levels of reinvestment by a company, and the quality of those reinvestments (as evaluated by the return on capital): Expected Growth = Reinvestment rate * Return on Capital Despite the fact that this equation is utilised in the majority of valuations to estimate growth, its use can become problematic in certain circumstances. For example, when valuing startups, we are typically in the position of having negative operating earnings, which renders this equation inoperable. In such situations, it is necessary to first estimate the growth in revenues, and then the reinvestment must be based on the growth in revenues. In order to establish this connection, we utilise a sales-to-capital ratio, which is a ratio that indicates the number of additional rupees of revenue that will be earned for each additional rupee of capital.:


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0357.html -->
SPECIAL CASES OF VALUATION | 343 Expected Reinvestment = Expected Change in Revenue/ (Sales/Capital) For example, if you have a sales-to-capital ratio of 5, then you will need to make an additional investment of Rs. 2 lakhs in order to expand your revenues by Rs. 10 lakhs. The sales-to-capital ratio is the most important input that must be provided for this formulation. This ratio can be estimated by looking at the history of the company, despite the fact that this history may be limited, as well as the averages of the industry, with the industry being defined in a broad sense to reflect the business that the company is in. Expected reinvestment rate (Stable) = Expected Growth (Stable)/ ROC(stable) In a stable growth state, on the other hand, it is possible to compute the reinvestment needs by using the expected growth rate in conjunction with the expected return on capital. To estimate cash flowsfrom a business, an additional method can be applied to use the typical reinvestment rates of the industry, which are then subdivided into capital expenditures and working capital requirements. Step 5: Risk and Discount Rate Estimates We estimate beta using the conventional methods, which involve performing a regression ofstock returns versus market returns. Even when they are publicly listed, young companies just starting out have very little historical data, which means we cannot use the traditional way to estimate risk parameters. However, there are alternative methods for estimating betas that can be used to help bridge this gap in knowledge. The first method is called the bottom-up approach. If there are comparable companies that have been listed for at least two years, then it is possible to determine the current risk parameters for the company by looking at the averages of these comparable companies. Even if there are no companies that fit this description, risk factors can be determined by looking at the firm’s financial characteristics, such as their size, the nature of their cash flow, and the degree to which they are leveraged financially. When attempting to estimate the cost of debt for a new company that is in debt, we run into a different dilemma. Because the company won’t typically be rated, we won’t have the opportunity to calculate an estimate of the cost of debt based on the rating. It is possible that we could attempt to estimate a synthetic rating, however the company’s negative operating income will result in a negative interest coverage ratio and a default rating for the company. It may be noted that these forecasts have already been established in steps 2 and 3, therefore one option would be to estimate a synthetic rating for the company based on a predicted interest coverage ratio for the company, which would be calculated based on the expected operational income for future periods. One further method would be to calculate a synthetic rating using the predicted interest coverage ratio. No matter which method we choose to employ to estimate the costs of equity and debt, we must ensure that these costs do not remain unchanged throughout the period that is being estimated. The risk parameters should also approach those of an average firm asthe company grows and progressestoward itssustainable margin and stable growth. Specifically, the betas should move closer to 1 and the cost of debt should change toward a mature firm’s cost of debt in order to achieve this. We need to make an estimate not only of the cost of equity for these companies, but also of how the firms’ leverage will develop over the course of time. Once more, aiming for the industry average or finding the best debt ratio for this company (based on how it will appear after it has reached a steady state) should produce fair estimates for the cost of capital over time. Step 6: Firm valuation This valuation starts to resemble a traditional valuation if the inputs on profits, reinvestment rates, and risk characteristics over time are applied. In many instances, the cash flows in the early years will be


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0358.html -->
344 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT negative as a result of negative profitability and significant reinvestment requirements. However, the cash flows will turn positive in the later years as margins increase and reinvestment demands decrease. The terminal value will typically contain the majority of the value in its entirety. It is important to keep in mind, however, that the size of the terminal value is largely dependent on the growth and goal margin assumptions that you make throughout the high growth phase. After determining the value of the company’s operational assets, the next step in valuing the company is to examine two additional aspects: the likelihood that the company will fail to continue as a going concern and the value of the company’s non-operating assets. Survival When we use the discounted cash flow method to value companies, we almost always make the assumption that the company will continue to exist as a going concern and produce cash flows into the foreseeable future. When valuing young companies, it is possible that this assumption is flawed due to the fact that many of these organisations will not be able to withstand the challenges that they will face over the next few years. If we exclude the potential of this happening and focus only on the optimistic scenario in which these companies continue to grow and generate profitsindefinitely, we will calculate an incorrect value for them. When it comes to coping with this situation, we have two options available to us. (i) The first step is to factor in the risk of bad results when calculating the predicted growth rates and earnings. Therefore, the growth rate that will be utilised in calculating revenue will be the predicted growth rate across all scenarios, both optimistic and pessimistic, and will include the possibility that the company will not be successful. As time goes on, fledgling companies will find it ever more challenging to achieve this goal. The deeper into the future one looks, the more difficult it will be. (ii) The second option is to estimate a value for the firm’s discounted cash flow across only those scenarios in which the company continues to operate as a going concern, and then to apply a probability that the company will continue to operate as a going concern to this value. When we have calculated the likelihood of continuing operations as a going concern, we can next proceed to assess the worth of a company using the following formula: Value of Firm = Probability of surviving as a going concern * Discounted Cash Flow of Firm + (1- Probability of surviving as a going concern) * Distress or Liquidation sale value Value of Non-Operating Assets When valuing a company, it is necessary to take into account its liquid assets, marketable securities, and holdings in other businesses, just as you would when valuing any other type of business. The one word of caution that we would add is that younger companies, due to the nature of their activities, tend to burn through considerable cash holdings in a relatively short amount of time. This is because their operations consume cash rather than producing it. Therefore, the cash balance that was reported in the most recent financial accounts, particularly if those statements are older than a few months, may not be reflective of the cash balances that are present at the moment. Young companies frequently have holdings in other young companies; consequently, there is a risk that investments in other firms may be shown on the books at values that are not reflective of their true value. This risk is compounded by the fact that young companies frequently have holdings in other young companies. If there are only one or two major holdings, you should still use cash flow–based methodologies to value such holdings even though there are fewer of them.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0359.html -->
SPECIAL CASES OF VALUATION | 345 Step 7: Estimating Equity Value and Per-Share Value In order to go from the value of the firm to the value of the equity, we typically deduct all claims on the firm that are not equity claims. When companies reach a certain level of maturity, the non-equity claims take the form of outstanding bank debt and bonds. When determining the value of the common equity, it is necessary to first determine the value of any preferred equity claims that may exist in new companies and then subtract those values from the total. When converting equity value to value pershare, it is necessary to take into account the number of equity options that are currently outstanding on the company. This is something that needs to be done for all companies, but it becomes particularly important with young start-up companies because the value of the options that are outstanding can be a much larger share of the overall equity value. Although this is something that needs to be done for all companies, it becomes particularly important with young start-up companies. Given the significance of these claims, we would propose that an option pricing model be used to determine the value of the options, both vested and non-vested, and that this value be deducted from the value of the equity to determine the value of the equity represented by common stock. After reaching this total value, the equity value per share can be calculated by dividing it by the actual number of shares that are currently in circulation. Because a newly established industry that is capital intensive, such as a port, will most likely need to issue more shares in the next few years to cover its reinvestment needs, a dilution discount to current value may need to be considered. Including the additional shares in the denominator, on the other hand, would be considered as double counting. 16.5 FIRMS WITH NEGATIVE OR LOW EARNINGS So far, we have only considered companies’ worth based on whether or not they have positive earnings. In this section, we look at a selection of companies that have negative earnings or earnings that are abnormally low, and we investigate the most effective way to evaluate these companies. We start by looking into the reasons why companies have negative earningsin the first place, and then we investigate the various ways in which valuation needs to be modified to reflect these fundamental factors. For companies that are experiencing issues that are only temporary, such as a strike or the recall of a product, we contend that the adjustment process can be a straightforward one. In this case, we remove from the company’s current earnings the portion of the expenses that is associated with the temporary problems. It may be argued that normalised earnings should be used in valuation for cyclical companies, where negative earnings are caused by a deterioration in the overall economy, and for commodity companies, where cyclical movements in commodity prices can affect earnings. This is because both of these types of companies are susceptible to fluctuations in earnings. The process of valuation is made more difficult for companies that have long-term strategic or operational problems (such as obsolete plants, a workforce that is not adequately trained, or poor investments made in the past). This is because we have to make assumptions about whether or not the company will be able to outlive its problems and restructure itself. Finally, we examine companiesthat have negative earnings because they have borrowed an excessive amount of money and discuss the most effective way to cope with the possibility that the company would default on its loans. 16.5.1 Valuing Loss Making Firms The reasons why a company has negative earnings in the first place will dictate how we approach the problem of negative earnings in the company. In the next section, the various options for dealing with businesses that have a poor profit history are discussed.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0360.html -->
346 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 16.5.1.1 Companies Facing Momentary Challenges When earnings are low due to issues that will only last for a limited time or are just transitory, it is reasonable to anticipate that earnings will improve in the not-too-distant future. Therefore, the solutions that we come up with will be ratherstraightforward, and the majority of them will consist of replacing the present earnings, which are in the red, with the normalised profits (which will be positive). The manner in which we standardise earnings, will change according to the specifics of the challenge. 16.5.1.2 Concerns Unique to the Company It is possible for a company to have a bad year in terms of earnings, but the problems may be unique to the company and be of a sort that’s just temporary. If the loss can be traced back to a particular occurrence— for example, a strike or a verdict in a legal case—and the financial records disclose the cost that is linked with the occurrence, the answer is quite straightforward. We need to make an estimate of the earnings before these costs, and we should not just utilise these earnings for forecasting cash flows but also for computing basics like return on capital. When we are making these estimations, it is important to keep in mind that we should deduct not just the expense but also any and all tax benefits that were achieved as a result of the expense, presuming that the expense was tax deductible. We will have a more difficult time accomplishing our goal if the source of the loss is less clear or if the cost of the event that caused the loss is not broken out from the cost of any other expenses. First things first, we need to be sure that the loss is really transitory and not a sign of more serious issues with the company in the long run. The following step is to make an estimate of the typical earnings of the company. A comparison of each spending item for the company for the current year with the same item in prior years, scaled to sales, is the method that is the quickest, easiest, and most straightforward way to accomplish this goal. Any metric that, in comparison to other years, appears to have an abnormally high value should be normalised (by taking the average of values from earlier years). Alternately, we could calculate an operating income to use in the valuation by applying the operating margin that the company achieved in previous years to the revenues of the current year. This would give we an estimate of the operating income. In general, we will be required to think about making adjustments to the earnings of companies after the yearsin which they have made major acquisitions. Thisis because the accounting statementsin these years will be skewed by large items that are generally one-time occurrences and are related to the acquisition. 16.5.1.3 Industry-Wide or Market-Driven Issues Earnings at cyclical companies are characterised by their inherent instability and sensitivity to changes in the status of the economy. Earnings at these companies are expected to rise during times of economic expansion, but they will fall to lower levels when the economy is in a recession. The same is true for commodity companies that experience price cycles, which consist of periods of high prices for the commodity being frequently followed by periods of low pricesfor the product. In both the circumstances, we can acquire deceptive estimations of value if we use the current year’sresults as our base year earnings. 16.5.1.4 Companies with Long-Term Issues In each and every one of the valuesthat have been discussed till now, the earnings were either immediately changed to represent normal levels or very quickly changed to reflect our expectation that the negative earnings will cease to exist in the near future. However, in some instances, the negative earnings are a


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0361.html -->
SPECIAL CASES OF VALUATION | 347 symptom of larger systemic issues that have been present at the company for a longer period of time. In these kinds of circumstances, we will be compelled to make judgements asto whether or not the problem will be solved, and if it will, as to when this will take place. This section offers a variety of potential options for businesses who find themselves in this predicament. 16.5.1.5 Strategic Issues When it comes to the product mixes, they provide the marketing methods they use, or even the target audiences they decide to go for; businesses are prone to making mistakes on occasions. They frequently find themselves in the position of having negative or decreased profitability, and in some cases, even experiencing a permanent loss of market share or even bankruptcy as a result. Think on it in light of the following examples: The Kodak Case: Kodak spent over 10 years arguing with Fuji Films, its greatest competitor, that examining a digital camera image was a usual procedure and people liked the feel of a printed image. Kodak felt Americans would always select it over Fuji Films. Fuji Films and other companies focused on photography and videography rather than arguing with Kodak. Again, Kodak promoted film cameras instead of imitating competition. It disregarded media and market feedback. Kodak squandered 10 years by promoting film over digital cameras. Kodak also lost foreign finance. Digital photography surpassed traditional film photography, too. Better image quality and cheaper than film. A publication said Kodak was falling behind because it ignored emerging technology. Kodak’s marketing team tried to persuade managers to change the company’s essential ideals. Kodak’s management committee continued to rely on film cameras, claiming the magazine reporter lacked the competence to support his allegation. Kodak didn’t recognise its once-effective strategy was now hurting it. Technology and market changes derailed the strategy. Kodak spent money on modest acquisitions instead of promoting digital camera sales. When Kodak eventually figured out digital cameras, it was too late. Kodak couldn’t compete with the big companies at the time. In the year 2004, Kodak eventually declared it would end the sales of classic film cameras. This move made 15,000 workers(one-fifth of the company’s workforce) redundant. Kodak fell off the S&P 500 index, which ranks the 500 largest U.S. corporations by stock performance, before 2011. Kodak stock hit $0.54 per share in September 2011. The shares fell over 50% that year. Kodak’s Graph Year 1973 1980 1990 2000 2012 Share Price, $Employees 100 150 80 120 60 90 40 60 20 30 0 0 Source: https://startuptalky.com/kodak-bankruptcy-case-study/#:~:text=Kodak%20failed%20to%20 realize%20that,the%20sales%20of%20digital%20cameras.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0362.html -->
348 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT The IBM Case: The development of the personal computer industry in the 1980s was a problem for IBM since it threatened the company’s dominant position in the mainframe computer sector as well as the unprecedented profitability of that business. Despite the fact that IBM had the capability to develop an operating system for personal computers early on in the process, the company instead chose to cede that business to a newcomer named Microsoft. By the year 1989, more than half of IBM’s market value had been erased, and the company’s return on equity had fallen into the single digits. The company has since made a comeback but the valuable lessons learnt remain. The Case of the Xerox Corporation: Xerox was so successful in the photocopier market for so many years that its brand name eventually became synonymous with the actual product. During the 1970s and 1980s, competition for the market came from Asian companies such as Ricoh and Canon that had cost structures that were less expensive. Following an early period of losses, Xerox was successful in recovering a portion of its market share. Xerox’s fortunes, on the other hand, have been steadily deteriorating since the latter half of the 1990s due to the impact that technology (in the form of e-mails, faxes, and low-cost printers) has had. By the end of the year 2000, many people were beginning to wonder whether or not Xerox had a bright future. The failure of senior management at Xerox to notice emerging technologies that would eventually replace Xerox’s copier technology precipitated the company’s downfall. The unfortunate issue is that Xerox invented the majority of the technology that changed the world, but they failed to make use of it. When companies’ low or negative earnings can be traced back to strategic blunders, the question of whether or not the shift is a permanent one needs to be asked and answered. If this is the case, we will need to determine the company’s value based on the presumption that it will never regain the ground it has lost and adjust our forecasts for revenue growth and expected margins accordingly. On the other hand, if we have a more positive outlook regarding the company’s ability to recover or its expansion into new areas, we can safely expect that the company will be able to return to the high growth and margins that it was accustomed to experiencing. 16.5.1.6 Operating Issues Companies which are able to provide their customers with goods and services in a more timely and cost-effective manner than their rivals will experience increased profitability and market value. But how and why can businesses gradually lose their competitive edge? There are several instances in which the causes can be linked back to a failure to stay up with the times, replace assets, and keep up with the most recent technologies. In general, the expenses of producing one metric tonne of steel will be greater for an older steel firm that has factories that date back several decades, and equipment that was manufactured in an earlier era, than what is currently used in the industry. In other circumstances, the issue may be the price of labour. A steel company that operates in the United Kingdom is subject to labour expenses that are significantly greater than those of an equivalent company in Asia. The operational margin is the variable that most accurately evaluates the efficiency of the operating system, and companies that have problems with their operating systems typically have margins that are substantially lower than those of their competitors. Increasing the margin until it reaches the average of the industry is one strategy to account for the influence of operating improvements that occur over time. However, the rate at which the margins will converge will be determined by a number of factors, including the following:


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0363.html -->
SPECIAL CASES OF VALUATION | 349 16.5.1.7 The Company’s Size In most cases, the amount of time required to eradicate inefficiencies is proportional to the size of the company. In large businesses, not only is inertia a considerably more powerful factor, but the absolute scale of the changes that need to be implemented is also far greater. If we assume that the costs of operation are the same, then a company that has revenues of Rs. 1000 crore will need to reduce its costs by Rs. 50 crore in order to achieve a 5 percent improvement in its pre-tax operating margin. On the other hand, a company that has revenues of Rs. 100 crore will only need to reduce its costs by Rs. 5 crore in order to achieve the same goal. 16.5.1.8 Aspects of the Inefficiency There are some inefficienciesthat can be corrected significantly faster than others. For instance, a company can swiftly replace ageing machinery or an inefficient inventory management system, but retraining an existing workforce will take far more time. 16.5.1.9 External Limitations Contractual constraints, Government restrictions and social pressure frequently place limitations on the scope and velocity of change that companies can implement in order to address inefficiencies in their operations. For an organisation that has more workers than it needs, the most obvious option may appear to be to lay off a significant section of the personnel; however, union contracts and the possibility of receiving unwanted press may make businesses unwilling to take this course of action. 16.5.1.10 The Quality of Management A management team that is willing to embrace changes is one of the most important ingredients for a successful turnaround. It is possible that an organisation will need to make changes to its senior management in order to be successful in resolving the operational issues it is facing. 16.6 FINANCIAL SERVICE COMPANIES Financialservice firms provide financial products and servicesto corporate and non-corporate customers and businesses. How financial services create money divides them into four groups. A bank makes money through the margin between the interest it pays funders and the interest it charges borrowers, and from other services it offers depositors and lenders. Insurers make money two ways. One is premiums from people who buy claims from them, and the other is investment portfolio revenue. An investment bank advises and helps non-financial enterprises raise funds or complete acquisitions or divestitures. Wealth Managers advise and manage client assets. Their income derives from advising fees, portfolio management, and sales. Non-financial service firms and financial service firms share many similarities. They must negotiate the trade-off between profitability and risk, are concerned with competition, and desire rapid growth over time. If they are publicly traded, companies are evaluated based on the total return they provide to their stockholders, just like other corporations. We shall, however, focuses on the characteristics of financial service enterprises that distinguish them from other businesses and explores the value implications.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0364.html -->
350 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT The special features of financial services companies include: a) Use of debt for earning income b) Heavily Regulated Sector c) Difficulty in measuring reinvestment d) Capital Adequacy Norms e) Difficulty in choice of multiples f) Issues faced for income estimation due to Provisioning for Losses g) Government directives about the choice of financial mix An analyst who is attempting to value a financialservices company,such as a commercial bank, insurance company, or other financialservice organisation, faces a unique set of obstaclesfor two reasons. The first reason isthat the nature of their enterprises makesit challenging to characterise both debt and reinvestment, which in turn makes the assessment of cash flows considerably more complicated and muddled. The other issue is that they are typically subject to a great deal of regulation, and it is necessary to take into account the impact that regulatory requirements have on value. In this section, we will discuss the factors that set apart financial service organisations from one another, as well as strategies for navigating these disparities. After that, it examines how best we might adapt discounted cash flow modelsto evaluate financialservice organisations, and it doesso by looking at three different alternatives: a conventional dividend discount model, a cash flow to equity discount model, and an excess return model. We will also take a look at a wide range of instances pertaining to the field of financial services for each. Now that we’ve covered the basics of relative valuation, let’s move on to discussing the many multiples that may be applied to financial service companies and how they should be used. There may also be certain issuesthat, even if they are not unique to financialservice firms, are emphasised in those firms. These issues could range from the impact that shifting regulatory requirements have on risk and value to the most effective way for banks to evaluate the quality of their loan portfolios. 16.6.1 Discounted Cash Flow Valuation The present value of the anticipated cash flows that will be generated by an asset is what is taken into consideration when developing a discounted cash flow model to determine the asset’s worth. In this part of the discussion, we will begin by analysing dividend discount models, then move on to cash flow to equity models, and then finish up by looking at excessreturn models. These models are used to determine the worth of banks and other financial sector companies. 16.6.1.1 Dividend Discount Models In Unit 14, we looked at different ways that dividend discount models might be used to determine the value of a company’s equity. We evaluated equity as the present value of the anticipated dividends by using the rationale that dividends are the only source of cash flows that a stakeholder in a publicly traded company is entitled to receive. We studied a variety of dividend discount models, ranging from those with low to large growth, and thought about the most accurate way to estimate the variables. Although much of what was discussed in Unit 14 is relevant to this portion as well, in this part of the lesson, we will focus on some of the characteristics that are specific to financial service companies.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0365.html -->
SPECIAL CASES OF VALUATION | 351 16.6.1.2 Basic Models According to the fundamental dividend discount model, the value of a stock is equal to the present value of the dividends that are anticipated to be received from that specific stock. If we make the assumption that a publicly listed company’s equity has an endless life, then we get the following results: Value per share of equity = DPS k t e t =1 () 1+ =∞ ∑t t where DPSt = Expected dividend per share in period t ke = Cost of equity t can vary from 1 to infinity This model collapses into the Gordon growth model in the rare scenario where the expected growth rate in dividends is constant forever. The key variable in the Gordon growth model is the expected growth rate in perpetuity. In the more generalscenario, where dividends are growing at a rate that is not anticipated to be sustainable or constant forever for a period of time (this period is referred to as the extraordinary growth period), we can still make the assumption that the growth rate will be constant forever at some point in time in the future. This enables us to then estimate the value of a stock using the dividend discount model, which is the sum of the present values of the dividends over the extraordinary growth period and the present value of the terminal price, both of which are estimated using the Gordon growth model. In other words, the value of a stock is calculated as the sum of these two present values. 16.6.1.3 Inputs to Model This relates to attention to the estimation problems that are specific to financial service companies in terms of the inputs that these models require. In general, we require estimates of the cost of equity, the expected payment ratios, and the estimated growth rate in profits per share over time in order to evaluate a stock via the dividend discount model. 1. Cost of Equity In line with how we estimate the cost of equity for companies, the cost of equity for a financial services company needs to reflect the share of the risk in the equity that the marginal investor in the stock is unable to diversify away. In the capital asset pricing model, a beta or betas are used to evaluate this risk. Multifactor pricing models and arbitrage pricing models also use betas. If regulatory limitations have not changed throughout the course of the time in question and are not anticipated to do so in the foreseeable future, then this may be one of the few industries in which regression betas can still be utilised with at least some degree of certainty. The proviso that we should avoid employing regression betas is something that must be adhered to even during times when the regulations are being rewritten and the regulatory landscape is being reshaped. There is a second point of differentiation between financial and non-financial companies. When we estimate betas for non-financial service companies, we place a strong emphasis on the importance of unlevering betas(regardless of whether they are historical orsector averages) and then re-lever them by using a company’s current debt to equity ratio. This is done in order to ensure that the estimates are accurate. When dealing with companies that provide financial services, we bypass this stage for two reasons. To begin, the capital structures of companies that provide financial services have a tendency to be rather


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0366.html -->
352 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT similar to one another. These companies are almost always highly levered. Secondly, it is challenging for companies that provide financial services to accurately measure debt. In everyday language, this indicates that we will calculate the bottom-up beta for the company under consideration based on the average levered beta of enterprises that are considered to be comparable. 2. Payout Ratios The estimated earnings per share in a future period can be multiplied by the expected payout ratio to get at an estimate of the expected dividend per share that will be paid out in that future period. The practice of extracting dividendsfrom anticipated earnings hastwo distinct advantages. The first advantage isthat it makesit possible for usto concentrate on the anticipated growth in earnings, which is more closely linked to the fundamentals of the company than dividends are. The second advantage isthat the payout ratio can be adjusted over time to take into account shifting patterns of growth and opportunities for investment. The payoutratio of a bank, like the payoutratio of any other company, is calculated by dividing the dividend by the firm’s earnings. Having said that, financial service corporations have traditionally distributed a greater amount of dividends than the vast majority of other companies operating in the market. When compared to comparable statistics for the rest of the market, the dividend payment ratios and dividend yields for financial institutions such as banks, insurance companies, investment banks, and investment businesses are significantly higher. Why do companies that provide financial services pay higher dividends than other types of companies? The fact that they are involved in industriesthat are far more developed than others,such asthe technology industry, is an apparent reaction; yet, thisis only a portion of the tale. Two factors explain why companies that provide financial services pay out a far larger portion of their profits as dividends compared to other types of businesses, and this is true even when predicted growth rates are taken into account. One reason for this is that, in comparison to other types of businesses, financial institutions and insurance companies are required to make a lower level of “net capital expenditures.” This, in turn, indicatesthat a much larger portion of these companies’ net income can be given out as dividends compared to a manufacturing company’s ability to do so. The second consideration is historical context. It is common knowledge that financial institutions such as banks and insurance firms are consistently reliable dividend payers. They have, throughout the course of time, attracted investors who have a preference for payouts, which makes it difficult for them to alter their dividend policy. In recent years, in accordance with a pattern that can be observed in other industries as well, financial service organisations have raised the amount of capital that they return to their stockholders through the use of stock buybacks. In this scenario, concentrating solely on the dividends that were distributed can give an inaccurate image of the amount of cash that was returned to stockholders. The simplest approach is to compute the composite payout ratio after adding the amount of money paid out in dividends to the amount paid out in stock buybacks each year. If we do this, however, we need to look at the number across a number of years because stock buybacks vary greatly over time. For example, a repurchase of shares costing Rs. 10,000 crore in one year may be followed by three years of extremely few or no buybacks. 3. Expected Growth If the amount of dividends is determined by earnings, then the predicted growth rate in earnings is the one that will be used to calculate the value of the stock. As is the case with other types of businesses, financial service companies’ earnings growth can be estimated in one of the following three ways:


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0367.html -->
SPECIAL CASES OF VALUATION | 353 (a) Historical Earnings Growth Rate: There are a lot of banks and insurance firms that have been around for a very long time, and it is typically possible to estimate their historical growth. In addition, the correlation between financial service companies’ historical earnings growth and their anticipated future earnings growth used to be far higher than it was for other types of companies. This would imply that the growth in earnings over the course of the company’s history is a significantly better predictor of the company’s future earnings. However, we need to exercise extreme caution when extrapolating past growth into the future if the regulatory environment is going through a period of change. (b) Analyst estimates in growth in earnings: Growth rate of Earnings projected by analysts are provided for a large number of publicly traded companies, despite the fact that the breadth of coverage varies greatly. You are able to obtain these projections of future growth since there are a lot of significant banks and insurance businesses that are well monitored. There is no clear answer to the topic of whether or not the long-term projections made by experts are a more accurate indicator of future growth than looking back at historical growth. (c) Fundamental growth: The anticipated increase in earnings pershare is a function that may be described in terms of the retention ratio and the return on equity (ROE). Expected growthEPS = Retention ratio × ROE With the use of this equation, we are able to determine the growth rate that should be anticipated for businessesthat have consistentreturns on equity.When looking at payout, if we incorporate stock buybacks in addition to dividends, the retention ratio should be defined consistently as well. In the event where it is anticipated that the return on equity would shift throughout the course of time, the predicted growth rate in earnings per share can be expressed as follows: Expected growthEPS = Retention ratio × ROEt+1 + (ROEt+1 – ROEt )/ROEt The predicted growth rate is a function of both the retention ratio and the return on equity in both of these equations. The retention ratio measures the amount of reinvestment, while the return on equity reflects their quality. The extent to which the financial service companies are able to benefit from fundamental growth modelsisremarkable. The retention ratio in a bank is a measurement of the equity that isreinvested back into the firms. This, in turn, decides, in large part, how much these firms can expand in the future given the regulatory focus on capital ratios. The return on equity is also a more significant measure of investment quality since financial assets are far more likely to be marked up to market value. This makes the ROE a more accurate indicator of investment quality. Stable Growth When using dividend discount models, we have to make the assumption that the financial service companies you are valuing will be experiencing stable growth at some point in the future. Stable growth is defined as growth that is either lower than or equal to the growth rate of the economy. If analysts want to get closure with dividend discount models, they must assume this(or the risk-free rate). The anticipated growth rate may already be at a stable level in some circumstances, particularly with larger companies operating in industries that are further along in their development. It is necessary to take into consideration these three aspects before arriving at a conclusion on the time at which a financial services company will transition into a stable growth firm. The first factor to consider


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0368.html -->
354 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT is how big the company is in comparison to the market that it serves. It will be challenging for larger financialservice companies, particularly in more developed economies, to maintain high growth ratesfor extended periods of time. The nature of the competition is the second consideration. If there is a lot of competition, then steady growth will occur much sooner rather than later. If there are restrictions placed on competition, rapid growth and surplusreturns can be maintained for a significantly longer period of time. Because regulation can work both as a benefit and as a barrier, the manner in which financial service firms are regulated can have an effect on the convergence to stable growth. This is because regulation can work both ways. Regulations may assist financial service companies in sustaining high growth rates over extended periods of time by limiting the number of new competitors. At the same time, however, legislative constraints may hinder companies from joining new businesses that have the potential to be lucrative; as a result, the duration of the high growth era may be shortened. The growth rate is not the only variable that shifts during periods of stable growth. The payout ratio needs to be adjusted so that it accurately reflects the steady growth rate (g), and the following are some estimates that can be derived from the payout ratio: Payout ratio in stable growth = 1 – g/ROEstable growth The assumption ofstable growth needsto be reflected in the firm’srisk profile, which needsto be adjusted. In particular, if betas are being used to determine the cost of equity, then in stable growth they should converge toward the value 1. 16.6.2 Asset-based Valuation When conducting an asset-based valuation, we determine the value of a financial services company’s existing assets, deduct the amount of debt and any other claims that are still due, and then report the difference as the equity’s value. In the case of a bank, for instance, this would entail estimating the value of the bank’s equity by first assessing the loan portfolio of the bank (which would represent the firm’s assets) and then deducting the value of any outstanding debt. Estimating the worth of the equity in a company requires first valuing the policies that are now in effect at the company (in the case of an insurance provider), then deducting the predicted claims that would come from these policies as well as any other debt that is currently outstanding. How would you go about valuing the policy holdings of an insurance business or the loan portfolio of a financial institution? Estimating the price at which the loan portfolio could be sold to another financial service provider is one method, but valuing the portfolio based on the cash flows that are anticipated to be generated by the loans is a more accurate method. Take for example a financial institution that has a loan portfolio worth Rs. 5,000 crore and a weighted average duration of seven years. From these loans, the financial institution generates an interest revenue of Rs. 400 crore. Furthermore, let’s assume that the default risk on the loans is high enough that the fair market interest rate on the loans would be 5.50 percent. The fair market interest rate issomething that can be estimated by either having a ratings agency rate the loan portfolio or by measuring the potential for default risk in the portfolio. The following formula can be used to get an estimate of the value of the loans: Value of Loans = Rs. 400 crore(PV of Annuity, 7 years, 5.50%) + Rs. 5,000 crore/1.0557 = Rs. 5,710.37 crore Due to the fact that the bank applies an interest rate that is higher than the going rate in the market, this loan portfolio has a fair market value that is greater than its book value. If the bank charged an interest


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0369.html -->
SPECIAL CASES OF VALUATION | 355 rate that was lower than the market rate, the situation would be viewed from the other point of view. To calculate the value of the equity in this book, you must first deduct the deposits, debt, and any other claims that are placed on the bank from the total amount. This method hassome value if you are trying to determine the worth of an established financial institution or insurance firm that haslittle to no room for future expansion, but it does have two important drawbacks. To begin, it does not give any weight or significance to the anticipated future growth and the additional profits that will result from that expansion. If, for example, a bank is able to lend at interest rates that are continuously higher than what is warranted by the risk of default, then the bank should be able to collect value from future loans as well. Second, it is challenging to implement when a financialservices company expandsinto variousindustries at the same time. It would be challenging to assign a value to a company such as Citigroup due to the fact that it isinvolved in a number of different lines of business. The assets in each of these lines of business—insurance, commercial banking, investment banking, and portfolio management—would need to be evaluated independently, with different income streams and different discount rates applied. 16.7 DISTRESSED FIRMS We have previously studied how discounted cash flow models may be modified to assess companies with negative earnings. The majority of the solutions evaluated future cash flows and anticipated that an increase in margins or profitability would result in positive cash flows and an increase in company value. In the exceptional circumstance where the company has large debt, we argued that the prospect of defaulting on the debt and declaring bankruptcy is quite plausible. In such circumstances, discounted cash flow valuation may not be an appropriate method for determining value. This section examines companies with negative earnings, substantial assets, and substantial debt. Given limited liability, we argue that the equity investors in this company have the choice to dissolve the company and pay off the debt. This call option on the underlying firm can increase the value of the shares, particularly when there is substantial uncertainty over the value of the assets. 16.7.1 Equity In Highly Levered Distressed Firms The majority of publicly traded companies have two aspects of equity. The first advantage is that the equity investors run the company and have the power to decide at any time whether or not to liquidate its assets and pay off any other claim holders. The second benefit is that the liability of equity investors in certain privately held companies and virtually entirely in publicly listed companies, is limited to the amount of equity that they have invested in those companies. The availability of a liquidation option, in conjunction with limited liability, gives equity the opportunity to have the characteristics of a call option. It is possible for the option value of equity to be higher than the discounted cash flow value in companies that have big obligations and negative earnings. 16.7.2 Payoff on Equity as an Option The equity in a company is a residual claim, which means that the holders of equity have a right to any cash flows that remain after all other financial claimholders have been paid off (including debt holders, preferred stock holders, and so on).The same rule appliesin the event that a company is put into liquidation:


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0370.html -->
356 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT equity owners are entitled to the cash that remains in the company after all of the company’s outstanding debt and other financial demands have been satisfied. With limited liability, equity investors can neverlose more than the amount that they initially invested in the company, even if the value of the company falls below the value of the debt that is currently outstanding. When the company is liquidated, the return on equity investors’ investments can be expressed as: Payoff to equity on liquidation = V – D if V >D = 0 if V < D where V = Liquidation value of the firm D = Face value of the outstanding debt and other non-equity claims Thus, equity can be thought of as a call option on the company, the exercise of which requires that the company be liquidated and the face amount of the debt (which is equivalent to the exercise price) paid off. The company itself serves as the underlying asset, and the option will become void once the obligation has been satisfied. 16.7.2.1 Implications of Viewing Equity as an Option When the equity in a company begins to exhibit the characteristics of a call option, we will need to adjust the way we think about the value of the equity and the factors that determine the value of the equity. Let us discuss a number of possible repercussions that this characteristic could have on bondholders and equity investors in the company. 1. Worth of the Equity In discounted cash flow valuation, we argue that equity is worthless if what you own (the value of the assets) is less than what you owe on the debt. This situation occurs when the value of the assets is less than the value of the debt. The first implication of considering equity as a call option is that equity will have value, even if the value of the assets falls to a level that is far lower than the face value of the debt that is outstanding. Even though investors, accountants, and analysts will perceive the company as having problems, the equity in the company is not completely worthless. Because of the time premium on the option (the length of time remaining until the bonds mature and become due) and the possibility that the value of the assets may increase above the face value of the bonds before they become due, equity actually commands a value. This is because of the amount of time remaining until the bonds mature and become due. This is analogous to the way in which deep outof-the-money traded options command value due to the fact that it is feasible for the value of the underlying asset to climb to an extent that is greater than the strike price during the time that the option still has to live. 2. Risk vis-à-vis Equity Value When using a traditional valuation technique known as discounted cash flow, a higher level of risk almost invariably results in a lower value for equity investors. We should not anticipate that this relationship will continue to hold if the stock begins to exhibit the characteristics of a call option because it will not. If a person is an equity investor in a struggling company, risk can become his buddy and help him make money. He will have, in essence, very little to lose and a lot to gain from fluctuations in the value of the company.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0371.html -->
SPECIAL CASES OF VALUATION | 357 16.8 VALUATION OF CASH AND CROSS HOLDINGS The majority of businesses, both publicly traded and privately held, hold assets on their balance sheets that fall under the category of nonoperating assets. The first and most prominent example of such assets is cash and near-cash investments, which are investments in riskless or very low-risk investments. These types of investments are made by the majority of businesses that have high cash holdings. The second strategy involves making investments in the equities and bonds of other companies, which can be done either for investment purposes or forstrategic ones. The third component isthe company’sstakesin other businesses, both private and public, which accountants classify using a wide range of criteria. Last but not least, there are assets that do not generate cash flows but could nevertheless have value, such as land in Mumbai or Pune that has not been developed or a pension plan that has excess assets. When companies are being valued, these assets receive little to no real attention, despite the fact that the repercussions might be rather severe. Let’s take a look at some of the difficulties that come along with valuing nonoperating assets, as well assome of the common mistakesthat can be made while evaluating these assets. 16.8.1 Cash and Near-Cash Investments There is a line item for cash and marketable securities on the balance sheet of every company. This line item refers to the company’s holdings of cash and assets that are nearly equivalent to cash. Investments in short-term governmentsecurities or commercial paper are examples of near-cash investments. Both of these types of investments can be easily turned into cash at a very cheap cost and in a very short amount of time. As a first step, let’s analyse the various reasons why businesses keep cash on hand and how much of it they actually have.After that, we talk about the many methods that are utilised to classify cash holdings and how to deal with cash holdings in the most effective manner when doing relative valuations and discounted cash flow analysis. Why Hold Cash? Every company hassome cash sitting on its books, and many of them have extremely sizable cash balances in relation to the overall value of their companies. The famous economist,John Maynard Keynesidentified three motivations for individuals to hold money. He urged that they maintain cash for transactions, as a protection against unplanned expenses, and for speculative purposes. One could make the case that businesses amass riches for the same reasons, but there is one more motivation for them to do so. At large publicly traded corporations, the separation of management and stockholders can produce an incentive for firms (or at least the managers in these firms) to amass cash reserves. This can be beneficial to the stockholders. Operating (Transactions) The main reason why businessesrequire a constantsupply of liquid assetsis proper functioning, although these requirements are likely to vary from one company to the next. For instance, in order for retail enterprises to function properly, the cash registers of the locations where they are located need to always have some cash on hand. In addition to this, small businesses require access to cash in order to replenish depleted inventory and make their weekly payroll obligations. A computer software company, on the other hand, might be able to get away with a substantially smaller operational cash balance if they play their cards right. The following factors are likely to have an effect on the amount of cash that is required for business operations:


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0372.html -->
358 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT Cash vs. Credit-oriented businesses Companiesthat operate in cash-oriented businesses,such as quick-service restaurants and grocery stores, will need a greater amount of liquid assets in order to continue business operations than companies that engage in credit-oriented businesses. Small versus large transactions Companies that produce their revenues through a large number of smaller transactions are less likely to demand cash for their business than companies that get their revenues through a limited number of more substantial transactions. It is quite improbable that a company like Boeing, which makes its profits from a small number of extremely big transactions, will receive or pay cash for the majority of the transactions it engages in. There should be some economies of scale that allow larger enterprises to keep lower (proportional) operating cash balances than smaller firms. Banking system Astime goes on, fewer and fewer transactions will be conducted using currency as a medium of exchange. As a direct result of this, we anticipate that the need for cash will reduce as technological advancements in the banking industry make it possible for customers to make payments using credit cards or through various digital modes. Differences between Cash Valuation Approaches Consolidated Valuation Separate Valuation Objective Value firm as a whole with cash as part of the assets. Value non-cash assets separately from cash. Earnings Should include interest income from cash and marketable securities Should exclude interest income from cash and marketable securities. (If using net income to estimate cash flows to equity, you need to remove after-tax interest income.) Reinvestment Should consider reinvestment in both operating assets and cash. Reinvestment should be only in operating assets Unlevered Beta Should be the weighted average of the unlevered beta of operating assets and the beta of cash (generally zero). Weights should be based on estimated values of operating assets and cash. Unlevered beta of just the operating assets. Accounting returns Should be measured using total earnings (including earnings from cash) and capital inclusive of cash. Should be measured using non-cash earnings, and cash should be netted from capital measure. Growth rate Growth rate should reflect growth in consolidated earnings (including earnings from cash). Growth rate should be only in operating earnings. Final Valuation The present value of the cash flows will already include cash. Do not add cash to it. The present value of the cash flows is the value of the operating assets. Cash has to be added to it.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0373.html -->
SPECIAL CASES OF VALUATION | 359 16.9 WARRANTS AND CONVERTIBLES Convertible bonds and stock are a kind of traditional debt and preferred stock that additionally include a conversion mechanism, often known as a call option, that allows the holder to convert the bonds or stock into shares of common equity at a predetermined price per share. Because the value of such debt is equivalent to the value of the common stock, it is not independent of the enterprise value of the company; as a result, it cannot be subtracted from the enterprise value of the company in order to determine the equity value of the company. The assumption that all of a target company’s debt and preferred stock will be converted into equity upon acquisition is one strategy for determining the value of such debt and stock. This makes the most sense in situations in which the price being offered for the target is higher than the price per share at which the debt can be converted. The methods of valuation of warrants and convertible bonds has been dealt with in an earlier chapter. 16.10 CYCLICAL & NON-CYCLICAL COMPANIES Valuing Cyclical Firms The amount of earnings generated in the base year can have a major impact on the valuation of cyclical companies. There are two potential remedies to this problem: the first is to alter the predicted growth rate in the near term to reflect cyclical swings, and the second is to value the company based on normalised rather than current earnings. Both of these adjustments are possible answers. Adjust Expected Growth Cyclical businesses typically record poor profitability at the bottom of an economic cycle, but those earnings typically rebound swiftly once the economy begins to improve. If profits are not negative, one option would be to adjust the predicted growth rate in earnings, particularly in the near term, to reflect expected changes in the economic cycle. This would be done notably if earnings are positive. If the company’s earnings and the economy are both struggling at the moment but are forecast to improve in the near future, then this would entail using a higher growth rate in the next year or two. In the event that present earnings are overstated (as a result of an economic boom), and in the event that it is anticipated that the economy will slow down, the strategy would be switched around. This strategy has the drawback of basing the accuracy of the estimate of value for a cyclical company on the accuracy of the macroeconomic predictions made by the analyst who is performing the valuation. However, it is impossible to value a cyclical company without making assumptions about the direction the economy will go in the future, thus it is possible that this criticism cannot be avoided. The real growth rate in earnings in turning-point years, which are years when the economy enters or exits an economic downturn, can be predicted by looking at the experience of this company (or similar firms) in earlier recessions. The years in which the economy is either entering or leaving a recession are referred to as turning-point years. Normalise Earnings The problem of cyclical companies’ earnings being erratic over time and having negative earnings in the base period can be easily remedied by normalising earnings, which is the quickest approach. When we normalise the earnings of a company that has reported a loss in previous years, all we are trying to do is find an answer to the question, “What would this company earn in a typical year?” This remark makes the assumption that the present year is not a typical year and that earnings will swiftly recover to normal levels. This assumption is implicit in the statement. Because of this, this strategy is best suited


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0374.html -->
360 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT for use by cyclical enterprises that are already well established. Earnings can be normalised in a variety of different ways, including the following: Earnings in Rupees for the company, averaged over the course of past time periods Utilizing the earnings average from the periods before the current one isthe easiest method for normalising earnings. How many different time periods should you travel back through? When looking at cyclical businesses, you need to go back far enough to encompass a complete economic cycle, which is between five and ten years. Although this method is straightforward, it is most appropriate for companies that have maintained the same scale (or size) during the period in question. When it is applied to a company that has grown larger orsmaller over time (in terms of the number of unitsitsells or total revenues), it will result in a normalised estimate that is wrong. This can happen either in terms of the total revenues or the number of units sold. Average return on investment (ROI) or profit margins for the company across the preceding time periods. This method is quite similar to the preceding one, with the exception that the averaging is performed not on dollar earnings but rather scaled earnings (such as operating margins or return on capital). The advantage of using this method is that it makes it possible for the estimated normalised earnings to take into account the present size of the company. Therefore, a company that had a normalised operating income of Rs. 120 crore over the course of the past periods and had an average return on capital of 12% over those periods would have Rs. 1,000 crore in current capital. A normalised net income can be calculated by taking the average return on equity and the book value of equity. It is possible to arrive at a normalised operating or net income by first estimating the average operating or net margin in preceding periods and then applying this margin to current revenues. This is a close variant of the approach described above. Working with revenues, on the other hand, has the advantage of being less susceptible to being manipulated through accounting. When it comes to normalising profits, there is one last question that we need to answer, and that question concerns the time frame in which earnings will be normalised. To assume that normalisation would take place instantly when comparing current earnings to normalised earnings is, in essence, the same thing as replacing current earnings with normalised earnings(i.e., in the very first time period of the valuation). If it is expected that it will be several periods before profits return to their normalised levels, the value that is generated by normalising current earnings will be excessively high. A straightforward adjustment that may be made is to reduce the value by the number of time periods that will need to pass before earnings return to their regular level. 16.11 HOLDING COMPANIES Holding companies can be appraised using a variety of different approaches, including those based on free cash flow and dividend discount models. Holding companies need to be valued for their ability to provide superior investment opportunities and to make sound strategic decisions.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0375.html -->
SPECIAL CASES OF VALUATION | 361 16.12 E-COMMERCE FIRMS E-commerce enterprises are a brand-new sort of business that have only been around for the past twenty years. Because new technology is always being developed in different parts of the world, it can be challenging to assign a value to these businesses. Still today, valuers will determine a price for these businesses by taking a number of different criteria into consideration. These aspects include Changes in Future Technology, Habits of Individuals (buyers), the Advantages and Disadvantages of Conducting Business via E-Commerce, and Competitors, amongst other things. LET US SUM UP Till the late 1970s, the majority of the stock market was controlled by companies involved in the production of goods, the provision of utilities, the transportation of goods, and the extraction of oil and gas. These companies derived the majority of their value from their physical assets, such as land, plant and machinery, vehicles, oil reserves, and so on. Technology and service industries, which rely heavily on intangible assets such as technological know-how, human resources, and brand names, have been the most successful types of businesses during the past few decades. An analyst who is tasked with appraising a company that places a high premium on intangible assets must work around one obstacle: the incorrect accounting for intangible assets. Capital expenses and operating expensesshould always be kept completely separate in an accounting system, as this is one of the fundamental principles. A capital expense is one that delivers benefits over a number of years, while an operating expense is one that only gives benefit in the current financial year. When applying the fundamental principles and methodologies of corporate valuation to various types of businesses, it is necessary to take into consideration the distinctive qualities of the businesses being evaluated. For instance, a multi-business firm has specific characteristics that make it more difficult to value than a single-business company does. This is because a single-business company only has one line of business. Similarly, the challenges involved in assigning a value to a financial institution (such as a bank or insurance firm), as opposed to those involved in assigning a value to a manufacturing company, are extremely different. Financial institutions, such as banks and insurance companies, are some of the most difficult companies to value. This is especially true for independent analysts, who often lack access to essential information (such as asset-liability mismatch) regarding the companiesthey are valuing (such as financial institutions). In addition, because these organisations have significant levels of debt, their valuations are extraordinarily susceptible to even slight shifts in the factors that matter the most. It is important to consider, in addition to the nature of a company’s operations, the environment in which the valuation is performed. For instance, the valuation of businesses in emerging markets presents its own unique set of challenges. This is due to the fact that emerging economies are characterised by high levels of macroeconomic uncertainty, considerable volatility in exchange rates, inflation, and interest rates, control over capital flows, and somewhat relaxed disclosure norms. In a similar vein, the valuation of a certain categories of business like real estate firms, startups, companies with negative earnings, cyclical companies, distressed firms, holding companies, e-commerce companies is a specialised job and needs to be done very carefully.


---

362 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT KEYWORDS Intangible Assets, Relative Valuation, Financial Assets, Cost of Equity, Asset Pricing Models, Start-up Firms, Operating Margin, Non-operatingAssets, Negative Earnings, Pay-out Ratio,Asset Based Valuation, Distressed Firms, Cross-holdings, Near cash investments CHECK YOUR PROGRESS 1. (b) Net interest income and fee income are the least important sources of income for a bank. U N I T 17 MERGER, ACQUISITION & RESTRUCTURING STRUCTURE 17.0 Objectives 17.1 Introduction 17.2 Types of Transactions 17.3 Reasons for Merger 17.4 Mechanics of a Merger 17.5 Costs and Benefits of a Merger 17.6 Exchange Ratio in a Merger 17.7 Purchase of a Division/ Plant 17.8 Takeovers 17.9 Leveraged Buyouts 17.10 Acquisition Financing 17.11 Business Alliances 17.12 Managing Acquisitions 17.13 Divestitures 17.14 Holding Company 17.15 Demergers Let Us Sum Up Keywords Check Your Progress Solutions


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0378.html -->
364 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 17.0 OBJECTIVES After studying this unit, you will have better understanding of: • The meaning of corporate restructuring, acquisitions and divestitures • Purpose and benefits of acquisitions and divestitures • The process of merger and the regulatory aspects • Valuations involved in acquisitions and divestitures • Various types of acquisitions and divestiture • Uses of demergers • Tax aspects of mergers and demergers 17.1 INTRODUCTION The reorganisation of a business is an integral aspect of modern business operations. As a result of globalisation and the loosening of constraints and controls, new waves of competition and free trade have emerged. This requires a restructuring and reorganisation of the corporate organisation in order to produce new synergies to adapt to the altered market conditions and competitive environment. Reorganizational restructuring typically involves significant organisational changes, such as a shift in business strategies. Internal restructuring may involve additional investments in plant and machinery, research and development of goods and processes,spinning off non-core enterprises, divestiture,selloffs, demerger, etc. A form of external restructuring is the sale of non-core businesses. External restructuring can also occur through mergers and acquisitions(M&As), joint venture formation, and strategic alliances with other companies. Corporate re-structuring refers to any modifications made to a company’s assets, financial structure, or ownership structure, as well as any expansion or contraction of the company’s operations.
