Mergers, acquisitions, and takeovers; financial restructuring and reorganisation; divestitures, de-mergers, and spinoffs; leveraged buyouts; and management buyouts are the most prevalent types of corporate restructuring. However, corporate reorganisation can take numerous shapes. Mergers and acquisitions (M&A) is the subject that has garnered the greatest discussion nowadays. Mergers and acquisitions are frequent occurrencesin economiesthat have evolved substantially. Hundreds of mergers and acquisitions take happen annually in Japan, the United States, and Europe. M&A activity is generally seen as typical business strategic practice worldwide, as also in India. In India, the business of corporate restructuring, which includes mergers, acquisitions, and other related activities, has exploded in the previous decade. The market for corporate control has expanded from 2004, when it was worth around $4.5 billion, hitting a record high of $13 billion in 2005 and $56.2 billion in 2016.Astheir home countriesstruggled under the weight of a recession at the time, international investors sought out an alternate location, preferably one with a rising economy. This is a result of the adverse macroeconomic climate caused by the Euro Zone crisis and other internal factors, such as inflation, the fiscal deficit, and currency depreciation. According to data collated by Bloomberg and revealed in June, the second quarter of the year 2022 saw the biggest amount of mergers and acquisitions activity ever recorded in India, totalling $82.3 billion in deals that were either in the process of being done or had already been finalised. The previous record, which was set in the third quarter of 2019 at $38.1 billion, has been surpassed by more than twice that


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0379.html -->
MERGER, ACQUISITION & RESTRUCTURING | 365 amount. The HDFC Ltd. merger with the HDFC Bank for a total transaction value of $58.5 billion in April was the driving force behind the spike in India. In May, 2022, there was yet another significant transaction that exemplified how the technological landscape had evolved, with assistance from the volatile financial markets. The agreement resulted to the merger of two software companies, Mindtree Ltd and Larsen & Toubro Infotech Ltd, in an all-stock transaction valued at $3.3 billion. Both companies were controlled by the engineering conglomerate Larsen & Toubro Ltd. In everyday conversation, the phrases “mergers,” “acquisitions,” and “takeovers” are frequently used synonymously with one another. However, there are distinctions. Acquisition is the process of one entity buying out another and absorbing it into itself, as contrast to merger, which refers to the combination of two separate entitiesinto one. In the context of Indian law, the term “merger” is more accurately rendered as “amalgamation.” The amalgamations can be by merger of firms within the limits of the Companies Act, and acquisition through takeovers. The Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) oversees takeovers, although the CompaniesAct governs mergers and acquisitions(M&A) deals. In cross border transactions, international tax considerations often arise. According to Halsburry’s Laws of England, an amalgamation is described as the combination of two or more pre-existing businesses, with the shareholders of each amalgamating company becoming largely the shareholders in the amalgamating company. As a result, the coming together of two or more businesses into a single entity is known as a merger. When two or more businesses combine into a single entity, when one business merges with another, or when one business is acquired by another, this process is referred to as “amalgamation.” In the case Inland Steam Navigation Workers Union vs. R.S. Navigation Company Ltd., it was determined that in the event of an amalgamation, the rights and liabilities of one company are merged into those of another company, making the transferee company vested with all of the rights and liabilities of the transferor company. This was one of the observations made in this case. ATake-over occurs when both, the company doing the take-over and the company being taken-over, are able to continue operating independently following the completion of the deal. If the acquisition results in consolidation, it means the legal dissolution of both of the companies involved and the creation of a new company into which the prior entities are combined. When a merger results in the legal dissolution of only one of the corporations involved, it is called absorption. Lord Swaraj Paul is credited as having initiated the practice of corporate takeovers in India when he attempted to acquire Escorts. The othersignificant takeoversinclude Hindujas’Shaw Wallace’s purchase of Ashok Leyland, the Chabbria Group’s acquisition of Dunlop and Falcon Tyres, the Goenka family’s purchase of Ceat Tyres, and Tata Tea’s purchase of Consolidated Coffee. The BIFR was responsible for coordinating the acquisition of smaller businesses by industry titans such as ITC, McDowells, Lakshmi Machine Works, and the Somani Group. As a result of the rapidly accelerating process of industrialization taking place in a nation whose primary economic activity is agriculture, a great number of new businesses are being established. There has been a rise in the amount of interaction between businesses and people from one country and those from other countries as a result of the new tendencies of globalisation, which have been observed not only in this country but also on a global scale. Today, corporate restructuring is gaining steam, and corporations and undertakings in India and elsewhere are merging, demerging, divesting, and acquiring or taking over other companies and undertakings, both unregistered and registered.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0380.html -->
366 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT Given the current state of affairs in the business world, it is imperative that mergers and acquisitions be supported, both in the interest of the general public and for the purpose of promoting industry and trade. On the one hand, the government isresponsible for protecting the interests of its citizens, consumers, and investors. On the other hand, the government isresponsible for protecting the interests of itsshareholders, creditors, and employees/workers. The provisions on “Compromises,Arrangements, andAmalgamations” are found in Chapter XV (Section 230 to Section 240) of the Companies Act, 2013. These provisions cover corporate debt restructuring, demergers, fast track mergers for small companies/holding subsidiary companies, cross-border mergers, takeovers, amalgamation of companies in public interest, and more. Special reorganisation proceduresinclude the “Reconstruction” ofsick industrial companies as envisioned by the Sick Industries(Special Provisions)Act, 1985, and the “Revival” of financially unviable companies as envisioned by section 72A of the Income Tax Act, 1961. Both of these reorganisation procedures were enacted in the United Kingdom. However, all of these mergers and acquisitions are also governed or controlled through relevant provisions of the Foreign Exchange Management Act of 1999, the Income TaxAct of 1961, the Industries(Development and Regulation)Act of 1951, the CompetitionAct of 2002, and the restrictions imposed by other relevant Acts including the SEBI Act of 1992, depending on the circumstances. In general, a reconstruction, reorganisation, or scheme of arrangement is the term used to describe a situation in which only one firm is involved in a scheme and the rights of the owners and creditors are altered. In the process of amalgamation, two or more businesses are combined into a single entity by the use of mergers or by one business purchasing another. An amalgamation is the process of combining two or more existing businesses into a single entity. As part of this process, the shareholders of each of the companies that are merging into a single entity become substantial shareholders in the company that will operate the blended business. Either the transfer of one or more undertakings to an existing company, or the transfer of two or more undertakings to a new company, can lead to the formation of a new business through the process of amalgamation. Strictly speaking, the term “amalgamation” does not cover the simple acquisition of the share capital of another company by one company, provided that the other company continues to exist and carry out its business as usual; however, the context in which the term is used may show that it is intended to include such an acquisition. 17.2 TYPES OF TRANSACTIONS The various types of transactions have different connotations and are explained in detail in this chapter. These transactions are as under: 1. Merger Acommon definition of a merger isthe coming together of two separate businessesinto one. The process of dissolving one or more businesses, corporations, or proprietorships in order to construct another company through absorption into it, is what is meant by the phrase “merger.” The combined business would be significantly larger after the transaction was completed. The following are some of the most common types of mergers: a) Horizontal Merger: The two businesses that have recently merged are both operating in the same market sector. As a result, the newly consolidated company will likely have a larger market share than its predecessors,


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0381.html -->
MERGER, ACQUISITION & RESTRUCTURING | 367 and it is possible that it will move closer to becoming a monopoly or a near monopoly in order to eliminate competition. b) Vertical Merger: This type of merger takes place when two organisations that have a “buyer-seller” relationship (or potentially have a buyer-seller relationship) come together to form a single entity. c) Mergers Between Conglomerates: Mergers of this kind include companies whose lines of business are completely unconnected to one another. To put it another way, the acquirer and the target do not produce the same or rival products, which means that their respective commercial endeavours are not tied to one another in either a horizontal or vertical sense (having relationship of buyer and supplier). There are no significant aspects that are shared between the companies in the areas of manufacturing, marketing, research and development, or technology in a conglomerate merger that is pure in nature. There is, however, the possibility that one or more of these common elements will share some degree of overlap with one another. These types of mergers actually involve the consolidation of several distinct types of enterprises into a single parent organisation. The utilisation of financial resources, the expansion of debt capacity, and the integration of managerial activities will continue to be the primary goals of any merger. d) Congeneric Merger: The acquiring company and the company it is merging with are connected in some way, whether it is through fundamental technologies, industrial methods, or market segments. The acquirer will benefit from an expansion of their product range, market participation, or technological capabilities as a result of the acquired company. These mergers represent an outward shift by the acquirer from its existing business environment to other similar business activities within the overall structure of the industry as a whole. e) Reverse Merger: Areverse merger occurs when a smaller, unlisted company acquires a larger, publicly listed company This allowsthe unlisted company to avoid the lengthy and complicated processthat would be necessary to be followed in the event that it desired to issue its shares to the public through an Initial Public Offering. 2. Acquisition: This term refers to the purchase of a controlling interest in the share capital of an existing firm by one corporation from another corporation. This could happen by: (i) An arrangement with the person who holds the majority of the interest. (ii) The acquisition of fresh shares through a confidential agreement. (iii) Acquisition of shares through the open market (open offer). (iv) The acquisition of a portion of a company’s share capital by the payment of cash and the issuing of shares. (v) Making an offer to buy out the general body of shareholders in the company. When one company acquires another, the acquiring firm has two options: either it can combine both businesses into a single entity and operate as a single entity, or it can continue to run the taken-over company as an independent entity but with new management and different policies. A “merger” refers to the coming together of two separate businesses on an equal footing. When a firm is “acquired,” it means that it has been bought out by another corporation, and the acquired company typically loses its identity. This method is normally done in a cordial manner.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0382.html -->
368 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT If the transfer of business is to be completed without allotting shares in the transferee company to the shareholders of the transferor company, then the acquisition of one of the businesses of a company, as a going concern by an agreement does not necessarily need to be routed through the court system. This would amount to nothing more than a straightforward acquisition. In this scenario, the transferor firm will most likely continue to operate, and there will not be any changes to the shareholding structure. The sale is referred to as a slump sale if it is completed for a single sum of money and no specific valuations are assigned to any of the asset categories included in the transaction. As a result of a judgement made by the Supreme Court of India, any capital gains that were realised as a result of slump sales were not subject to income tax. 3. Purchase of Division or Plant It is possible for one corporation to purchase a division or factory from another company. For instance, SRF India acquired CEAT Limited’s nylon cord division in a recent acquisition. In a typical business acquisition, the acquiring company purchases the relevant division’s assets, assumes responsibility for the division’s liabilities, and makes a monetary payment of compensation to the selling company. For instance, Abbott Laboratories paid $3.72 billion to acquire the pharmaceuticals business of Piramal Health Care. Abbott Laboratories was a competitor of Piramal Health Care. It is important to keep in mind that only a fraction of the assets and liabilities of one company are taken over by another firm when a transaction is carried out in this manner. 4. Takeover A takeover often comprises the acquisition of a specified interest in the equity capital of a company (typically between 50 and 100 percent), which grants the acquirer the ability to exert control over the operations of the company. For instance, HINDALCO was able to acquire control of INDAL after purchasing a 54 percent stake in the company from its international parent company, Alcan. INDAL, on the other hand, was ultimately absorbed into HINDALCO after some time. A takeover, in contrast to a merger or the purchase of an existing division, does not include the transfer of assets and obligations. Takeover and acquisition are same, technically. However, the term takeover generally is used when the transaction is without the consent of the shareholders of the target company or, in other words, it is hostile takeover. Acquisition, on the other hand, refers to an amicable agreement or consent of the majority shareholders of the target company. 5. Leveraged Buyout A takeover or the purchase of a division can also be referred to as a leveraged buyout, which differs in that it is mostly accomplished with the assistance of loan financing. 6. Divestitures When compared to divestitures, acquisitions lead to an increase in control and a larger asset base, while divestituresresult in a smaller asset base and a loss of control. The following is a list and brief description of the most typical types of divestitures: a) Partial Selloff A partial selloff is when one company sells a portion of its operations, like a facility or a business division, to another company. A sale of a business division might be thought of as its inverse in this context. b) The Transfer of Ownership A sale of equity stake occurs when one investor (or a group of investors) sells an equity stake to another investor. This equity stake typically represents a controlling block in the company. As an


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0383.html -->
MERGER, ACQUISITION & RESTRUCTURING | 369 illustration, Alcan has parted ways with HINDALCO by selling the latter 54 percent of its stock holding in INDAL. A takeover is essentially what this deal is doing, just in reverse. c) Demerger A demerger is the process by which a corporation transfers one or more of its business divisions to another company that is being formed at the same time as the original firm. For instance, the Great Eastern Shipping Business split off its offshore operations and created a new company known as The Great Offshore Limited to house those operations. Both the firm whose business division is moved and the company to which it is transferred are referred to as the demerged company and the resultant company, respectively. d) Equity Carveout When a parent firm engages in an equity carveout, it is selling a portion of its ownership stake in a wholly owned subsidiary. It’s possible that a strategic investor or the general investing public will buy the equity. e) PSU Disinvestment Individuals and organisations that are not affiliated with the government, might acquire ownership stakesin previously state-owned businessesthrough a process known as privatisation. This can either be a partial or complete transfer of ownership. 17.3 REASONS FOR MERGER The following list contains the most typical reasons for mergers and acquisitions (M&A): 1. Synergistic operating economics: One possible definition of synergy is as follows: V (AB) >V(A) + V (B). To put it another way, the total value of two separate businesses or organisations must be greater than each of their individual values. According to Mark L. Sirower of Boston Consulting Group, who wrote “The Synergy Trap,” synergy is the increase in performance of the combined firm over what the two firms are already expected or required to accomplish as independent firms. Synergy is the result of combining the resources of two or more companies. This could be due to complementary services, economies of scale, or both of these factors. One useful illustration of activities that are complementary is the possibility that one company may have an effective production system while another company may have an effective networking of branches. Therefore, the amalgamated enterprises will have a higher productivity level than their respective predecessors. Along the same lines as the previous point, one of the reasons for the benefits of synergy is the economics of huge scale. The primary reason for this is that production on a larger scale, results in a lower average cost of production. This can be seen, for example, in a reduction in overhead costs brought about by the sharing of central services like accounting and finances, office executives, top level management, legal, sales promotion and advertisement, and so on. These economies can be considered “real” since they result from a reduction in the amount of factor input required for each unit of output, whereas pecuniary economics result from paying cheaper prices for factor inputs when they are transacted in bulk. 2. Taxation: It is possible that the provisions of the Income Tax Act that allow for losses to be offset against other income or carried forward are yet another compelling argument for the merger and acquisition. As a


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0384.html -->
370 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT result, the amalgamated company will see tax savings as well as a reduction in its tax liabilities. In a similar vein, in the event of an acquisition, the target business’s losses will be permitted to be set against the earnings of the company that is doing the acquiring. 3. Growth: A company is able to grow at a faster rate using the mode of mergers and acquisitions as opposed to the other mode, which is organic growth. The reduction in “Time to Market” was the driving force behind this decision. The acquiring company avoids delays that would have been caused by the purchase of a building and site, the establishment of the plant, and the hiring of people, among other things. 4. The Consolidation of Production Capabilities and the Enhancement of Market Power: The decrease in total number of competitors results in an increase in marketing power. In addition, the merging of two or more plants can boost the output capacity of the overall operation. The table that follows provides an explanation of the primary reasonsforseveral of the well-known transactions that have taken place in India in recent times. 5. Economies of Scale Due to the increased volume of business conducted by the combined corporation, some efficiencies can be realised when two or more companies merge into a single entity. These cost savings are the result of a more intensive utilisation of manufacturing capabilities, distribution networks, engineering services, research and development facilities, data processing systems, and so on and so forth. In the case of horizontal mergers, where there is the potential for a more intensive utilisation of resources, economies ofscale are at their most obvious and evident. Vertical mergerstypically result in significant benefits, the most important of which are greater activity coordination, decreased inventory levels, and increased market power for the merged firm. Last but not least, even in the case of mergersinvolving conglomerates, there is potential for the reduction or elimination of some overhead expenses. Are there ever times when economies of scale work against you? If the scope of operations and the size of the organisation become excessively large and unmanageable, then the answer is yes. Economists frequently discuss the optimum size of an operation in terms of the lowest possible unit costs. After reaching this ideal position, the unit cost has a tendency to continue rising. 6. Economies of Scope A company might broaden the range of its activities by making use of a certain set of capabilities or assetsthat it already owns. For instance, if Proctor and Gamble were to purchase a consumer product firm that could make use of their well-respected consumer marketing expertise, they would be able to reap the benefits of economies of scope. 7. Economies of Vertical Integration Vertical integration can lead to cost savings by combining the resources of multiple enterprises operating at various stages of a value chain or production process. A firm that is involved in oil exploration and production, such as ONGC, and a company that is involved in oil refining and marketing, such as HPCL, may be able to increase coordination and control by merging their operations. However, vertical integration isn’t alwaysthe beststrategy in every situation. If a business decides to handle all of its operations in-house, it runs the risk of missing out on the benefits of outsourcing to third-party providers who might be more effective in certain aspects of the value chain. 8. Complementary Resources A merger could make sense for two companies if those companies’resources are complementary to one another. For instance, a small company that is developing a revolutionary product could require


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0385.html -->
MERGER, ACQUISITION & RESTRUCTURING | 371 the engineering capabilities and marketing reach of a large company. It is possible that the unique product can be successfully manufactured and marketed if the two companies that created it decide to merge into one. As a result, the value of the two companies is increased when they work together because of the complimentary resources that the two possess. Brown Boveri and ASEA merged to form ASEA Brown Boveri, which is a notable example of a merger between organisations that were strong fits for each other. This merger led to the formation of ASEA Brown Boveri (ABB). Brown Boveri operated on a globalscale, in contrast toASEA, which did not.ASEAperformed exceptionally well in management, in contrast to Brown Boveri, which did not. The two firms, in terms of their technologies, markets, and cultures, were a good fit for one another. 9. Utilisation of Surplus Funds Although a company operating in an established market might make a lot of cash, it might not have many optionsfor making lucrative investments.Acompany of this nature ought to pay outsignificant dividends and even purchase back some of its own shares, assuming such actions are viable options. However, the majority of management teams have a propensity to make more investments, despite the fact that these expenditures might not be lucrative. In circumstances like these, a merger with another company that involves cash compensation isfrequently the case that reflects a more effective utilisation of surplus capital. 10. Managerial Effectiveness An increase in management efficiency is one of the potential benefitsthat could result from a merger. It is possible for this to happen if the current management team, which is not doing well, is replaced with a new management team that is more effective.Acompany that has been plagued by managerial shortcomings can frequently benefit enormously from the superior management that is expected to emerge as a sequel to the merger of the two companies.Amerger may have further ancillary benefits, one of which is an increased degree of congruence between the interests of the company’s managers and those of its shareholders. The idea that creating an atmosphere that isfavourable for mergersimposes a certain level of discipline on management is a popular justification for doing so. Existing managers will work diligently and persistently to improve their performance if it is determined that a company’s poor performance makes it more susceptible to the possibility of being acquired by another business. 11. Industry Consolidation The necessity of consolidation has been a key factor in the formation of mergers across a wide variety of business sectors all over the world. Consolidation is required for boosting efficiency whenever there are an excessive number of players and surplus capacities. This has happened (and is continuing to happen) in several industries all over the world, including but not limited to banking, telecommunications, pharmaceuticals, cement,steel, autos, and so on. Even in India, we are seeing a pattern that is very similar to this one. Given the dispersed capacity acrossso many different domains, consolidation appears to be an unavoidable necessity. 12. Dubious Reasons for Mergers There are instances when the desire to diversify, achieve a cheaper cost of financing, and achieve a greater rate of earnings growth will lead a company to pursue a merger. At first glance, these goals appear to be worthy; nonetheless, it is highly unlikely that they will increase value. 13. Diversification One of the most frequently cited goals of mergersisto lessen overall risk by increasing diversification of operations. The degree to which risk is minimised is, of course, contingent on the degree to


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0386.html -->
372 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT which the revenues of the merging firms are correlated with one another. The presence of a negative correlation is associated with a bigger risk reduction, whereas the presence of a positive correlation is associated with a smaller risk reduction. What kind of value does the elimination of such risk bring to investors? Investors do not receive any benefit from the proposed merger if they are able to diversify their portfolios on their own by purchasing the stocks of companies that intend to combine operations. Any investor who wants to reduce risk by diversifying between two companies, for example, Kappa Company and Gamma Limited, can easily do so by purchasing the stocks of both of these companies and “merging” them into a single investment portfolio. It is not required for him to ‘merge’ these companies in order to reap the benefits of diversification in his portfolio. In point of fact, his “home-made” diversification affords him a great deal more flexibility than other options. Because there is not a “set” proportion that is the consequence of the merger, he is free to combine the stocks of Kappa and Gamma in any proportion he sees fit. However, there are at least two exceptional circumstances in which corporate diversity might be beneficial: (i) If a firm is beset by issues that threaten its very existence and a merger with another company is the only way to prevent the company from going bankrupt, then the corporation should pursue the merger. (ii) If an investor does not have the opportunity to engage in ‘home-made’ diversification because one of the companies is not traded in the marketplace, then corporate diversification may be the only viable avenue to risk reduction that is available to them. 14. Lower Financing Costs Many people believe that a reduction in the cost of borrowing money for the merged company will occur as a result of the company’s larger size and more consistent earnings. The rationale for this is that the creditors of the combined company are afforded greater protection than the creditors of either of the merging firms operating on their own. If two companies, A and B, decide to merge, the equity of both companies will serve to safeguard the creditors of the newly formed firm, which we will refer to as firm AB. In spite of the fact that this additional protection lowers the cost of debt, it places an additional burden on the shareholders of the companies involved. The shareholders of company A are required to support the debt of company B, and vice versa. In a market that functions effectively, the advantage accruing to shareholders as a consequence of a decrease in the cost of debt would be cancelled out by the greater load that would need to be carried by shareholders; as a result, there would be no net gain. 15. Growth in Earnings It is indeed possible that a merger will provide the impression that earnings are growing. If investors are misled, a price increase could result from this. An illustration of this phenomena might be provided in the form of an example. Imagine that A Limited has purchased B Limited. The column headings 1 and 2 of the table that follows present an overview of A Limited and B Limited’s respective financial situations prior to the merger. The price-earningsratio forALimited isset at 20, reflecting the company’s exceptional growth prospects. On the other hand, B Limited has subpar growth prospects and is trading at a price-earnings ratio of 10 times its current price. It is not anticipated that the merger will result in the creation of any new value. The exchange ratio isset at 1:2, which indicatesthat one share ofALimited will be transferred in exchange for two shares of B Limited. This ratio was determined based on the market prices that existed prior to the merger. After the merger, the financial position of A Limited, assuming the market is “smart,” will be as illustrated in column 3 of the table that is presented below. Even when earnings per share go up, the


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0387.html -->
MERGER, ACQUISITION & RESTRUCTURING | 373 price-earningsratio goes down because the market realisesthat the growth prospects of the combined company will not be as bright as those of A Limited alone. This causes the price-earnings ratio to reflect a lower value. Therefore, the price of one share on the market has remained stable at sixty yen. Therefore, the market value of the combined firm can be calculated by adding the market values of the two companies that merged to form the combined company. If the market continuesto behave in an “inefficient or foolish” manner, it may consider the 33 percent increase in earnings per share to be reflective of genuine growth. As a result, the price-earnings ratio will not experience a decline. The market price per share of A Limited will increase to Rs. 80 as a result of an increased earnings per share and a price-earnings ratio that has remained steady. As a consequence of this, the overall market worth will rise from Rs. 9 crore to Rs. 12 crore. Financial Position of A Limited & B Limited Particulars A Ltd before Merger B Ltd before Merger A Ltd after Merger Market is Smart Market is Inefficient Earnings per share 3 3 4 4 Price per share 60 30 60 80 PE Ratio 20 10 15 20 No. of shares 10 lakhs 10 lakhs 15 lakhs 15 lakhs Total Earnings Rs. 30 Lakhs Rs. 30 Lakhs Rs. 60 Lakhs Rs. 60 Lakhs Total Value Rs. 6 crore Rs. 3 crore Rs. 9 crore Rs. 12 crore Therefore, if the market is “inefficient,” it may be susceptible to being hypnotised by the allure of earnings growth. In a market that is inefficient, such an illusion might be successful for a while. The false profits are sure to vanish once the market reaches its optimal level of efficiency. 17.4 MECHANICS OF A MERGER The Companies Act is the primary legislation that governs the process of mergers and acquisitions in India. New provisions were included in the Companies Act 2013 to take the place of the provisions that were previously included in the CompaniesAct 1956. The older laws, on the other hand, are still in effect because the newly enacted ones have not been made public as of the current date. In addition to company law, the Securities and Exchange Board of India Act and the Competition Act also have provisions that regulate mergers and acquisitions. The CompaniesAct is primarily concerned with protecting the interests of creditors, the SEBI Act is primarily concerned with protecting the interests of minority shareholders, and the Competition Act is primarily concerned with protecting the interests of consumers from being harmed as a result of diminished competition resulting from mergers. A merger is a sophisticated transaction that involves fairly complex legal, tax, and accounting factors. Mergers typically include two or more companies. When considering a proposed merger, it is important to keep the following legal, tax, and accounting provisions in mind.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0388.html -->
374 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 17.4.1 Legal Procedure of Amalgamation- A fairly long process The following procedures are typically involved in amalgamation as part of the process: a) An In-Depth Analysis of Object Clauses To determine whether or whether the companies have the authority to merge into one another, the memorandums of association of both businesses need to be reviewed. In addition, the business of the amalgamating company shall be allowed to continue under the object clause of the transferee company, which will be the company that results from the merger (transferor company). In the event that such terms do not exist, it is necessary to obtain consent from the shareholders, boards of directors, and the Company Law Board. b) Communication with Stock Exchanges The proposed merger should be brought to the attention of the stock exchanges that are now home to both the companies that will be merged and those that will be amalgamating. Copies of all notices, resolutions, and orders should be sent through regular mail to the stock exchanges that are relevant to the situation at various intervals. c) Board Approvals of the Draft Amalgamation Proposal The preliminary draught of the proposal to merge should be accepted by the separate boards of directors, and those boards should then issue a resolution that gives their directors and executives permission to take the subject further. d) Application to the NCLT/s Following approval from the board of directors, each company should submit an application to the National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT) of the state in which itsregistered office islocated. This will allow the companies to call meetings of their shareholders and creditors in order to vote on whether or not to proceed with the proposed merger. In the event of a merger or amalgamation involving a domestic company and a foreign corporation, or vice versa, the consent of the RBI must be sought before the National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT) can be contacted. Certain criteria apply. e) Notice to the shareholders and creditors of the company After obtaining the approval ofthe NCLT, each company isrequired to send a notice and an explanatory statement to its shareholders and creditors at least 30 days in advance, along with a copy of the scheme and the prescribed details. In addition, the notice must include a copy of the scheme and the details. Additionally, the notice of the meetings ought to be published in two newspapers — one English and one vernacular — as well as on the websites of the company, SEBI, and the appropriate stock market. Additionally, different papers including financials, an order from the NCLT, scheme information, an auditor’s certificate on accounting treatment, and so on should be made accessible for inspection by the shareholders and creditors. The National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT) should be provided with an affidavit certifying that all of the necessary processes for convening the meetings have been met with. f) Conducting Meetings of Shareholders and Creditors Each company needs to call a meeting of their shareholders in order to vote on whether or not to proceed with the merger. The scheme of amalgamation needs to receive approval from at least 75 percent (in value) ofshareholdersin each class who vote in person, by proxy, or through postal ballot. These shareholders can vote in any of these three ways. In a similar vein, the scheme of amalgamation must first receive approval from the company’s creditors during a separate meeting. Equally, at least seventy-five percent (in value) of the creditors who vote in favour of the plan, either in person or through proxies, must be in agreement with it. Only anyone who own more than 10 percent of the


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0389.html -->
MERGER, ACQUISITION & RESTRUCTURING | 375 company’s shares or creditors who have an outstanding debt of more than 5 percent are allowed to voice their opposition to the plan. Notifications of the meetings should be distributed to the Central Government, the Registrar of Companies, the Income Tax authorities, the Reserve Bank of India, the Securities and Exchange Board of India, the Competition Commission of India, the respective stock exchanges, and any other authorities, as directed by the NCLTs. These authorities have thirty days to submit their representations. If they do not send in any objections, it will be assumed that they are okay with it. The creditors’ meeting is not required if at least 90 percent of those owed money agree to or affirm the plan by signing an affidavit. g) Petition to the National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT) for the Confirmation and Passing Orders After the shareholders and creditors have voted in favour of the scheme to merge the companies, the companiesinvolved should submit a petition to the NCLT requesting that it confirm the scheme. The NCLT will schedule a time for the hearing. It is required that a notice of the same be published in newspapers. The NCLT will send notices of the hearing to the shareholders and creditors who have expressed opposition to the scheme, as well as to the Central Government and any other regulators who have expressed interest in the matter. The NCLT will issue an ordersanctioning the amalgamation scheme once it has listened to the parties involved, determined that the scheme is equitable and reasonable, and then passed an order sanctioning the arrangement. Nevertheless, the NCLT has the authority to make changes to the scheme and issue orders that reflect those changes. There is also the possibility of a streamlined and expedited process for mergers and demergers involving two or more minor companies meeting certain criteria, as well as holding corporations and the fully owned subsidiaries of those holding companies. In the simplified method, there is no need to contact the National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT) if the official liquidator, the shareholders, and the creditors do not have any objections to the scheme, and the scheme is approved by the Central Government. h) Filing the Order Copy with the Registrar of Companies The Registrar of Companies requires that certified authentic copies of the order issued by the NCLT be submitted. i) Transfer of Assets and Liabilities After the final orders have been issued by both of the NCLTs, the merged business will be required to take on all of the assets and liabilities of the company that was merging into it as of the appointed date. j) Issue of Equity and Debentures After complying with the requirements of the legislation, the merged company is required to start selling shares and debentures of the merged firm (cash payment allowed in some cases). After then, the stock exchange will begin trading with the newly issued debentures and shares of stock. 17.4.2 Competition Issues The CompetitionAct of 2002 isthe primary legal document in India that governs antitrust and competition concerns. In accordance with the Competition Act, the Competition Commission (hereafter referred to as the “Commission”) has the authority to investigate potentially monopolistic or restrictive business practices. The Competition Act outlaws any business combinations that have the potential to have a discernible impact on the level of competition in the relevant market in India. Within a period of seven days after the board of directors of concerned enterprises approve the combination or after any agreement or other document for acquisition is executed, any person or enterprise that proposes to enter into a combination can approach the Commission for approval of the combination. This must be done within


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0390.html -->
376 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT seven days of the board of directors of concerned enterprises approving the combination. The Commission has the authority to ban the combination if it deems that it already has or is likely to have an appreciably negative effect on the level of competition inside India. In the event that pre-acquisition approval is not obtained, the Commission has a year to investigate the matter either on its own initiative or in response to an application submitted by a third party. 17.4.3 Tax Aspects An amalgamation is the process of merging one or more firms into an already existing company or merging two or more companiesinto a new company that has been founded particularly for the purpose of carrying out the amalgamation. The companiesthat are coming together to form a new entity are referred to as amalgamating companies, and the new company is referred to as the amalgamated company. If the following conditions are met, the amalgamated company will be eligible for a number of tax benefits: (a) all of the properties and liabilities of the amalgamating company immediately prior to the amalgamation will become the properties and liabilities of the amalgamated company by virtue of the amalgamation; (b) shareholders holding at least 75 percent in value of the amalgamating company’s shares will become shareholders of the amalgamated company because of the amalgamation. Only in the case where the amalgamating company was an Indian corporation will the resulting company be eligible for certain tax advantages. The combined firm will have access to the following deductions, to the extent that they were available to the company that was merging, and to the extent that they are still unabsorbed or unfulfilled: a) Investments in fixed assets devoted to scientific research b) The cost of purchasing or acquiring patent rights, copy rights, and technical know-how c) The cost of acquiring a licence to provide telecommunications services as an operating expense d) Depreciation of the costs incurred in the planning stage e) The ability to carry forward losses as well as unused depreciation We are also required to take into consideration stamp duties in addition to income tax. Some states, such as Maharashtra and Gujarat, have enacted their own stamp duty legislation, while other states have adopted the Indian stamp duty and made the necessary modifications to it. 17.4.4 Accounting for Business Combinations Atransaction or other event that resultsin an acquirer gaining control of one or more firms can be referred to as a business combination. An acquirer could take control of an acquiree in a number of different methods, such as the following examples: (a) through the transfer of cash, cash equivalents, or other assets (including the net assets that make up a firm); (b) by taking on financial obligations; (c) by selling ownership stakes in the company; or (d) by offering more than one kind of consideration to take into account. A business combination can be formed in a variety of different ways for a number of different reasons, including legal and taxes considerations. The application of Ind AS 103 is found in commercial combinations. Both the acquisition technique and the pooling approach are utilised for the purpose of accounting for business combinations in accordance with Ind AS 103. The acquisition procedure, which is applicable to all business combinations with the exception of common control business combinations, entails the following steps:


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0391.html -->
MERGER, ACQUISITION & RESTRUCTURING | 377 (a) Establishing who the acquirer is, (b) Figuring out when the property was acquired, (c) Recognizing and measuring goodwill or a gain from a bargain purchase; and (d) Recognizing and measuring the identifiable assets acquired, the liabilities assumed, and any noncontrolling interest in the acquiree on the date of the acquisition at fair values.Abusiness combination involving entities or businesses in which all of the combining entities or businesses are ultimately controlled by the same party or parties both before and after the business combination and where that control does not transitorily change hands is an example of a transaction known as a “common control business transaction.” The pooling of interests method is the one that is utilised for corporate mergers that include common control. The following is an example of what is involved in the pooling of interests method: (i) The assets and liabilities of the companies that are being combined are recorded at the carrying amounts of those assets and liabilities. (ii) There are no modifications made to reflect fair values, and there is no recognition of any new assets or liabilities. The only changes that are performed are those that are necessary to synchronise the accounting policies. (iii) An adjustment is made in the reserves to account for the difference in capital that was caused by the share swap ratio (also known as the exchange ratio). 17.4.5 The Role of Investment Bankers There are multiple ways in which investment banks are involved in mergers and acquisitions. To begin, they assist in the coordination of mergers. Second, when a corporation istargeted by an adversarial raider, it will create and put into practise defensive strategies in order to protect itself. Thirdly, they contribute to the process of valuing potential targets. In the fourth step, they organise funding for the transaction. Fifth, they put their money into possibilities to arbitrage. a) Arranging Mergers The majority of investment banksinclude a department dedicated to mergers and acquisitions known as an M&Agroup. This department istasked with finding companiesthat would be desirable to others as well as companies that have surplus capital and the desire to buy other companies. The mergers and acquisitions team is working to bring about a merger. b) Developing Defensive Strategies If the target company does not wish to be purchased, it will typically seek the assistance of an investment banking business as well as a law firm that specialises in mergers and acquisitions in order to fend off the possible acquirer in a variety of ways. c) Determining Fair Value It is essential, when working out the details of a friendly merger, to prove that the price that has been agreed upon is a reasonable one. If this is not done, the stockholders of either or both companies may challenge the merger in a court of law. Therefore, in the majority of cases involving substantial mergers, each party will retain an investment-banking firm to assist in determining the appropriate price for the combined company. d) Financing Mergers Investment bankers need to be able to provide clients with a financing package if they want to be successful in the mergers and acquisitions (M&A) market. Acquirers may need funds for purchases.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0392.html -->
378 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT Target companies who are interested in thwarting a takeover threat may require funds for stock repurchases in order to do so. e) Arbitrage Operations In finance, an arbitrage refers to the practice of simultaneously buying and selling a securities (or currency) on two or more markets in order to profit from a difference in the prices of those markets in a risk-free way. However, investment bankers and other investors may engage in a different form of arbitrage when they speculate on the stocks of firms that are possible takeover targets. This kind of arbitrage is known as “tail risk arbitrage.” 17.5 COSTS AND BENEFITS OF A MERGER When companyAdecidesto buy company B, it is making a decision that amountsto a capital investment, whereas company B is making a decision that amounts to a capital divestment. What does the decision to go with firm A mean in terms of its net present value? What does the decision to go with firm B mean in terms of its net present value? Before we can determine firm A’s net present value as a result of the merger, we must first determine the benefits and the drawbacks of the combination. The advantage of the merger is the difference between the present value (PV) of the combined entity PVAB and the present value of the two entities if they are kept separate (PVA + PVB). Hence, Benefit = PVAB – (PVA+PVB) 17.5.1 Compensation in Cash If we assume that the compensation to firm B will be paid in cash, then the cost of the merger, when viewed from the perspective of business A, is equal to the cash payment made for purchasing firm B, less the present value of firm B when viewed as its own distinct organisation. Thus, Cost = Cash – PVB According to the explanations provided above, the difference between the benefits and the costs constitutes the net present value (NPV) of the merger from the perspective of firm A. So NPV to A = Benefit – Cost or PVAB – PVA – Cash The cost of the merger, as seen from the perspective of firm A, is all that is required to determine the net present value of the merger from the point of view of firm B. Hence, NPV to B = Cash – PVB Let us illustrate this with an example. Firm A has a value of Rs. 100 crore and firm B has a value of Rs. 25 crore. If the two firms merge, cost savings with a present value of Rs. 25 crore would occur. Firm A proposes to offer Rs. 30 crore cash compensation to acquire firm B. Calculate the net present value of the merger to the two firms. In this example PVA = Rs. 100 crore, PVB = Rs. 25 crore, PVAB = Rs. 150 crore, Cash = Rs. 30 crore. Therefore, Benefit = PVAB – (PVA+PVB) = Rs. 25 crore Cost = Cash – PVB = Rs. 5 crore NPV to A = Benefit – Cost = Rs. 20 crore NPV to B = Cash – PVB = Rs. 5 crore


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0393.html -->
MERGER, ACQUISITION & RESTRUCTURING | 379 17.5.2 Compensation in Stock In the previous discussion, we made the assumption that the acquiring company would provide the acquired company with monetary compensation. However, in practice, compensation is typically delivered in the form of shares rather than cash. When anything like this occurs, the “cost” component of the calculation for the current value needs to be done so with extreme caution. Let’s look at an example to see how this can be illustrated. The company M intends to acquire the other company N. Prior to the announcement of the merger, the pertinent financial details of the two companies are as follows: M N Market price per share Rs. 100 Rs. 40 Number of shares 20,00,000 10,00,000 Market value of the firm Rs. 20 crores Rs. 4 crores The merger is expected to bring gains which have a present value of Rs. 200 lakhs. Firm M offers 500,000 shares in exchange for 1000,000 shares to the shareholders of firm N. The apparent cost of acquiring firm N is: 500,000 × 100 – 4,00,00,000 = Rs. 1,00,00,000 The true cost, however, is greater than Rs. 1,00,00,000.While calculating the true cost, you must recognise that N’s shareholders end up owning a fraction of the share capital of the combined firm. The true cost, when N’sshareholders get a fraction α of the share capital of the combined firm, is equal to: Cost = αPVAB – PVB In our example, the share of N in the combined entity will be: α = 5,00,000 20,00,000 + 5,00,000 = 0.20 Assume that the market values of the two firms just before the merger announcement are equal to their present values as separate entities and the benefit of merger is Rs. 2 crores. Then, PVAB = PVA + PVB + Benefit 20 + 4 + 2 = Rs. 26 crores Cost = α PVAB – PVB 0.20 * 26 – 4 = Rs. 1.2 crores NPV to A = Benefit – Cost = 2 – 1.2 = Rs. 0.8 crores NPV to B = Cost = Rs. 1.2 crores There is, without a doubt, a significant distinction between cash compensation and stock compensation. If the remuneration is paid in cash, then the cost of the acquisition is not related to the gains that were realised as a result of the acquisition. Conversely, if the compensation is paid in stock, rather than cash, then the cost of the acquisition is contingent upon the gains that are realised as a result of the acquisition. 17.5.3 Cash vs. Stock Compensation The decision of whether to pay for an acquisition with cash or with shares should not be taken lightly. When a purchase is paid for in cash, it is crystal clear who the buyer and seller are; but, when an acquisition is paid for in stock, it is somewhat less obvious who the buyer and seller are. The decision of whether to be compensated in cash or shares is primarily determined by four different criteria.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0394.html -->
380 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 1. Overvaluation If the stock of the acquiring company is overvalued in comparison to the stock of the company being bought, then making payments in stock rather than cash can be the more cost-effective option. 2. Taxes In the eyes of the shareholders of the acquired company, a transaction including cash compensation constitutes taxable income, whereas a transaction involving stock compensation does not. 3. Sharing of Risks and Rewards If the shareholders of the acquired firm are given financial compensation instead of other benefits, they will neither shoulder the risks nor experience the benefits of the merger. On the other hand, shareholders of the acquired firm take part in the risks as well as the profits of the merger if stock compensation is given out. 4. Discipline The empirical evidence revealsthat cash-financed acquisitions have a higher rate ofsuccess compared to stock-financed purchasesin terms of overallsuccess. Why? It is possible that cash buyers evaluate properties with a greater sense of discipline, circumspection, and rigour. 17.6 EXCHANGE RATIO IN A MERGER In a merger, the acquiring company would often offer the shares of its own company in exchange for those of the target company. The offer is presented in the form of an exchange ratio, also known as a swap ratio, which is the number of shares that the acquiring company is prepared to give up in return for one share of the target company. For instance, if the exchange ratio is set at 1, it indicates that the acquiring company is willing to trade one share of its own stock for each share of the target company. 17.6.1 Factors involved in Determining the Exchange Ratio What factors go into determining the exchange rate, and what are those factors? Book value per share, earnings pershare, market price pershare, dividend discounted value pershare, and discounted cash flow value per share are the typical bases that are utilised when determining an exchange ratio. 1. Book Value Per Share It is possible to calculate the exchange rate by comparing the book values of each share held in each of the two companies. For instance, if the book value pershare of the firm that is purchasing the target company is Rs. 25, and the book value pershare of the company that is being acquired is Rs. 50, then the book value based exchange ratio would be 0.5 (25/50). Those who support using book value as a basis for valuation argue that it offers a very objective foundation. This is an argument that does not hold much water due to the fact that book value is affected by accounting practices, and these policies represent subjective judgments. More significant arguments can be made against the utilisation of book value, including the following: (a) The valuations of books do not take into accountshiftsin the relative purchasing power of money. (b) Book values and genuine economic values frequently diverge significantly from one another. 2. Earnings Per Share Let’s say that the earnings per share of the firm that is doing the acquiring are Rs. 10.00, while the earnings per share of the firm that is being acquired are Rs. 5.00. The exchange ratio, calculated based on earnings per share, will be 0.5, which is written as 5/10. This indicates that ten shares of the target company will be acquired in return for five shares of the acquiring company.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0395.html -->
MERGER, ACQUISITION & RESTRUCTURING | 381 There are certain issues with an exchange ratio that is entirely based on the present profits per share of the merging companies. This is due to the fact that the ratio does not take into consideration the following: (a) The disparity in the pace of increase in earnings that the two companies have experienced. (b) The increases in earnings that were a direct result of the merger. (c) The distinct risks that are connected with the financial outcomes of the two businesses. In addition, there is the issue of determining the usual level of current profits, which presents a measurement challenge. The current earnings per share could be affected by a number of one-time circumstances,such as a windfall profit, an extraordinary labour crisis, or a significant tax relief. Last but not least, the manner in which the company is going to deal with negative earnings per share is still a question that needs to be answered. 3. Market Price Per Share The exchange ratio could be determined by looking at how the share prices of the acquiring company and the target company compare to one another on the market. For illustration purposes, if the target company’s equity shares are selling for Rs. 25 and the acquiring company’s equity shares are selling for Rs. 100, the market price-based exchange ratio would be 0.25 (25/100). This indicates that one share of the firm that is doing the acquiring will be traded for four shares of the firm that is being acquired. Prices determined by the market have a lot of value when both the shares of the acquiring company and the shares of the target company are actively traded in a competitive market. They are an accurate reflection of the company’s existing profitability, growth potential, and risk characteristics. However, market prices may not be very trustworthy when trading is light, and in the worst case scenario, there may be no market prices at all if the shares are not traded at all. This would be an extreme instance. Another issue with market prices is the possibility that they will be influenced by people who have a financial stake in the outcome of the market. 4. Dividend Discounted (DD) Value Per Share The current value of the anticipated stream of dividends is what is meant to be represented by the dividend discounted value per share. It is possible to calculate the exchange ratio by comparing the relative DD values per share of the two companies that are merging. For instance, if the DD value per share of the firm that is purchasing the other firm is Rs. 80, and the DD value per share of the firm that is being acquired is Rs. 50, then the DD value based exchange ratio is 0.625. The DD value technique only makes sense in situations in which it is possible to estimate the dividend streams of the merging firms with a decent degree of accuracy; in other situations, it is not very helpful. 5. Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Value Per Share The DCF value per share is equal to: (Firm value using DCF Method – Debt Value)/No. of Equity shares The exchange rate can be calculated using the relative DCF values per share of the companies that are merging. For instance, if the DCF value per share of the purchasing firm is Rs. 40 and the DCF value per share of the target company is Rs. 30, then the DCF value based exchange ratio is 0.75. When fairly solid business plans and cash flow estimates are provided for a period of five to ten years for the merging companies, the DCF value approach is a good choice for determining the worth of the combined company. This strategy, on the other hand, ignores the value of the possibilities that are already built into the firm.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0396.html -->
382 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 17.7 PURCHASE OF A DIVISION/ PLANT The purchase and selling of divisions or plants is becoming increasingly widespread as a result of the increased activity surrounding corporate reorganisation. Listed below are some notable examples from the most recent years: Listed below are some notable examples from the most recent years: (a) TISCO’s cement mill was purchased by Lafarge. (b) SRF Limited acquired the nylon tyre cord division of CEAT. (c) Heinz India Limited was successful in its acquisition of Glaxo India Limited’s food business. (d) In 2010, Abbot Laboratories paid 17000 crore, which was equivalent to $3.72 billion, to acquire the domestic formulations division of Piramal Healthcare. The opposite of purchasing something is known as divesting. A divestiture occurs in the case where one company, firm B, sells a plant, factory, or business division to another company, firm A. In this scenario, firm B is the seller. As a result of the more liberalised economic environment, it is anticipated that purchases (as well as divestitures) will become increasingly important in the years to come. This is because businesses will have more leeway to restructure themselves. How should an acquisition of this nature be valued? In general, the value of an acquisition is equivalent to the advantages that will be received in the future (free cash flow plus horizon value), which are then discounted using the opportunity cost of capital. Thissection examines a method for pricing a purchase in accordance with this principle, provides a numerical example to demonstrate the method, and investigates other alternative ways of gaining a better understanding of the horizon value (which is often the most significant component of valuation). 17.7.1 Valuing a Purchase When one company buys the division of another company or when one company takes over another company by gaining a controlling equity share, the acquiring company needs to put a value on the ownership position that it has obtained. In the event of the purchase of a division, the bidder company obtains one hundred percent ownership, however in the case of a takeover of a firm, the bidder firm frequently acquires a smaller ownership position (usually 30 to 80 percent). Our discussion will centre on estimating the enterprise value of the target entity because determining the value of the target entity’s debt and the proportion of ownership that was acquired are both rather straightforward. The evaluation of an acquisition follows the same general framework as the evaluation of any other company. On the other hand, because control premiums and synergy premiums exist, the situation frequently becomes more complicated. The worth of an acquisition target can be accurately determined by beginning with the value of the status quo, then adding the value of control, and finally adding the value of synergy. 1. Status Quo Value The discounted cash flow (DCF) method and the market multiple method are the two most popular approaches that are taken when determining the value of the status quo. 2. DCF Method The present value of the free cash flows that will be generated in the future is what is meant by the DCF value of a company (or business division). In actuality, the future is broken up into two distinct time periods known as the explicit prediction period and the balance period. The explicit forecast


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0397.html -->
MERGER, ACQUISITION & RESTRUCTURING | 383 period is the time frame (often between 5 and 10 years) during which the company is anticipated to see significant growth. The balance period is the remaining time during which it is anticipated that the company will be operating in a “steady state.” There are three primary stages involved in the DCF technique of company valuation. First, determine the value, in present terms, of the free cash flow that will result from the transaction. In the second step, the horizon value is calculated and then discounted to the current time. The third step involves calculation of the worth of the transaction, by adding together the “present value” of “free cash flow” and the “horizon value.” 3. Market Multiple Method Similar assetsshould be sold for valuesthat are comparable to one another, according to both common sense and economic reasoning. On the basis of this theory, you can determine the value of a firm by looking at the value placed on other businesses that are comparable to it. Let’s say you want to determine the fair market value of the cement division of a conglomerate that is presently generating a NOPAT of Rs. One thousand crore. When looking at the valuation of cement firms, you discover that businesses that have comparable cement facilities are trading on the market at 5 times their NOPAT. This is the case when looking at the valuation of cement companies. In light of this, a rough estimate would put the worth of the cement segment under consideration somewhere around Rs. 5,000 crore. In the last illustration, we examined the market value-net operating profit multiple (NOPAT). Other multiples, such as the market value to sales multiple, the market value to PBDIT multiple, and the market value to book value multiple, are also taken into consideration by financial analysts. It is a well-known fact that the market multiple approach is not without its flaws. One could argue that the conceptual underpinnings of multiples are weak and ill-defined; that there are challenges in assessing genuine profit; that there are distortions generated by arbitrary accounting rules; that there are difficulties in discovering comparable companies; and so on and so forth. In spite of these restrictions, the insights offered by the market multiples are extremely helpful. To do so would be the same as ignoring evidence that is pertinent. 4. Value of Control The importance of Control Acquiring is that Companies are frequently willing to pay a price that is greater than the worth of the status quo in order to get the right to control the management of the businesses that they are acquiring. The value of control derives from the fact that it enables one to make adjustments that can improve performance. It is possible to make investments in the capacity of debottlenecking, it is possible to liquidate redundant assets, it is possible to streamline operations, it is possible to change the structure of financing, it is possible to strengthen managerial systems and processes, it is possible to bring in more competent people, and so on and so forth. The importance of control can be understood by considering the following: Value of Control = Value of Firm, if it is optimally managed – Value of firm with current management If the company is currently being managed in a very ineffective manner and there is much room for improvement, then obviously the benefit of having control of the company is great. On the other hand, if the company is being managed effectively, the value of control is essentially null and void. 5. Value of Synergy The vast majority of acquisitions have the potential to result in synergy, which can manifest itself in one or more of the following ways:


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0398.html -->
384 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT a) Decreased overhead expenses as a result of economies of scale. b) Decreased costs associated with research and development, advertising, marketing, and other shared services. c) A faster rate of growth as a result of the combined entity’s increased influence in the market. d) A more extended period of growth as a result of improved competitive advantages. e) A decrease in the cost of capital as a result of increased debt capacity f) Increased efficiency in the use of tax havens. It may not be simple to place a value on synergy because synergy is simple to conceptualise but challenging to achieve in practice. 6. Biases in Valuation The process of valuing an acquisition is vulnerable to biases since the management of both the firm being acquired and the firm being acquired as a target seek to justify their views to their respective shareholders. The bidder company is attempting to persuade its shareholders that it is purchasing the target company at a reasonable price. When an acquisition is conducted amicably, the target company makes every effort to assure itsshareholdersthat they are receiving a reasonable price (i.e., it is getting at least what it is worth). In a hostile acquisition, the roles of the parties involved switch places. The firm that is making the bid seeks to persuade the shareholders of the company that is the target of the bid that they are getting a fair price, while the target company argues that they are not. Variables such as return on invested capital, growth rate trajectory, and cost of capital are all susceptible to modification in the context of the DCF approach, which is used to validate a particular valuation figure. It is possible to rationalise the conclusion that one wishesto reach by tailoring both the choice of the comparable firm and the choice of the market multiple in the event that the market multiple approach is adopted. 17.8 TAKEOVERS A takeover often entails the acquisition of a certain block of a business’s equity capital, which grants the acquirer the ability to exert control over the operations of the company. In order to obtain full control of the acquired firm, an acquirer typically needs to purchase more than fifty percent of the paid-up equity of the target company. In actuality, however, effective control can be exercised with a smaller holding; typically, this range is between 30 and 40 percent. This is possible due to the fact that the remaining shareholders are dispersed and poorly organised, making it unlikely that they will challenge the acquirer’s control of the company. A takeover is referred to as friendly when the current management agrees to be acquired by the acquiring company, and hostile when the management disagrees. In the realm of Indian business, takeovers have evolved into an everyday occurrence. Takeovers of INDAL by HINDALCO, IPCL by Reliance Industries, VSNL by Tatas, BALCO by Sterlite Industries, and Ranbaxy Laboratories by Daichi Sankyo are examples of notable transactions that have occurred in recent years. A takeover may be done through the following ways: 1. Open market purchase The shares of the publicly traded company are purchased on the stock market by the acquirer. This is the typical starting point for hostile takeovers of a company.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0399.html -->
MERGER, ACQUISITION & RESTRUCTURING | 385 2. Negotiated Acquisitions In a transaction that has been arranged, the acquirer purchases shares of the target firm from one or more current owners, the majority of whom are likely to be promoter shareholders. 3. Preferential allotment Through a process known as a preferential allotment of equity shares, the acquirer will purchase shares of the target company. This type of purchase is obviously a friendly acquisition that is carried out with the intention of providing the acquirer with a strategic position in the firm as well as injecting finances into the organisation. 17.8.1 Arguments for and Against Takeovers are frequently accompanied by a great deal of controversy. According to Donald M. DePamphilis, “The corporate takeover has been dramatised in Hollywood as being motivated by excessive greed; reviled in the press as a destroyer of jobs and of local communities; sanctified on Wall Street as a means of dislodging incompetent management; and often heralded by shareholders as a source of windfall gains.” The truth isthat corporate takeovers could involve a mix of all of these factorsto varying degrees. Takeover supporters say that mergers and acquisitions increase the quality of management, facilitate forward and backward linkages with the other activities of the acquirer, and give the opportunity to realise synergistic benefits. T. Boone Pickens Jr., who is a very eloquent votary of takeovers, views them as a device for punishing weak managements and safeguarding the interests of small shareholders. Takeovers are seen as a method for punishing weak managements and protecting the interests of small shareholders. The central argument of a book that he authored is that corporations need to be operated in the interest of their shareholders rather than the interests of management or, as some executives like to argue, “the society at large.” Takeovers, in his view, are a necessary mechanism for safeguarding shareholders’ rights and interests. Naturally, Pickens’point of view has been aggressively contested by those who are opposed to takeovers. Warren Law, in a highly perceptive criticism, attacked the notion that takeovers help in rescuing poorly managed companiesfrom inadequate managers. This was Pickens’main argument for the use of takeovers. According to Law, managers do significantly more for their organisations and shareholders than raiders (or predators), who are largely motivated by a desire to earn a “quick buck” and see their photographs on magazine covers. Managers are more concerned with the long-term success of their companies. Even Peter Drucker was vehemently against mergers and acquisitions. He stated vehemently that if shareholders have a responsibility towards managers, then managers should likewise have a reciprocal responsibility towards shareholders for the greater good of society. If shareholders have a responsibility towards managers, then this should be the case. However, research from the scientific community is in favour of takeovers. The scientific evidence has been summed up by MichaelJensen and Richard S. Ruback, who say the following: “In brief, the evidence seems to indicate that corporate takeovers generate positive gains, that target firm shareholders benefit, and that bidding firm shareholders do not lose. In addition, the gains created by corporate takeovers do not appear to be the result of the creation of market power.” Last but not least, it is uncommon to find managerial acts connected to corporate control that are detrimental to shareholders. 17.8.2 Regulation of Takeovers of Existing Companies Takeovers have the potential to be viewed as a legitimate tool in the market for corporate control provided that they are appropriately controlled in accordance with the following principles:


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0400.html -->
386 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 1. Transparency of the Process A takeover has the potential to effect the interests of a wide variety of parties and components, including shareholders, employees, consumers, suppliers, competing acquirers, and creditors, to name a few. As a result, it ought to be carried out in an open fashion. Takeovers will be viewed as a legitimate mechanism in the market for corporate control by multiple constituents if the process is transparent, which is why takeovers need to be transparent. 2. Interest of Minority Shareholders When a company is acquired, the “controlling block,” which typically ranges from 30 to 60 percent of the company’s shares, is typically purchased from a single seller (occasionally it may be acquired from several sellers through market purchases). In most cases, the “controlling block” is acquired at a negotiated price that is greater than the price that is currently being offered on the market. When it comes to the other shareholders, what happens to them? The takeover rules should ensure that other shareholders do not face any kind of disadvantage as a result of the takeover. 3. Realisation of Economic Gains The key economic justification for takeovers ought to be the enhancement of operational efficiency and the promotion of more effective utilisation of available resources. The acquirer needs to have a reasonable amount of leeway to restructure the operations, expand the product range, redeploy the resources, and so on in order to enable the realisation of these economic gains. This will allow the acquirer to facilitate the realisation of these economic gains. In addition, appropriate economic incentives need to be provided, particularly in situations where takeovers assist to the rehabilitation of failing units. 4. No Undue Concentration of Market Power While the regulatory environmentshould be designed in a way that makesit easier to realise economic gains, it should also work to keep market power from becoming too concentrated. As a result of the takeover, the acquirer shall not be allowed to enjoy an excessive amount of market power that can be exploited to the harm of customers and other parties. An index such as the Herfindahl – Hirchman Index (HHI), which is utilised by the Department of Justice in the United States, may be investigated within the scope of this discussion. Thisindex takes into account the proportion of the market that each of the companies in the sector has. For instance, if three companies possess a market share of 20 percent each and ten companies hold a market share of 4 percent each, the HHI would be calculated as follows: 3 (20)2 + 10 (4)2 = 1,360. On the other hand, the HHI would be calculated as follows if one company held a market share of 52 percent and 12 companies each held a market share of 4 percent: (52)2 plus 12 (4)2 equals 2,896. Both the degree of inequality and the concentration of wealth are reflected by the HHI. 5. Financial Support If takeovers are to be viewed as valuable toolsfor enhancing the level of management and the efficacy of operations, then they should not be the exclusive domain of those who are in a position to make the necessary financial investments.
