It is imperative that suitable financial mechanisms be built in order to provide competent individuals, regardless of their financial resources, with the opportunity to engage in takeover exercises. To be able to back their takeover offers, successful business owners and managers who have demonstrated capabilities and a track record of achievement ought to have access to money that are provided by financial institutions or investors through the capital market.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0401.html -->
MERGER, ACQUISITION & RESTRUCTURING | 387 17.8.3 SEBI Takeover Code The following is a list of the most important provisions of the SEBI Takeover Code: 1. Disclosure Any acquirer that purchases stock or voting rights in a company (henceforth referred to as holdings) that, when combined with the acquirer’s already-held holdings in the company, total more than 5 percent of the company’s total stock, is required to make full disclosure of their holdings to both the company and the relevant stock exchange at each stage of the acquisition process (s). The stock exchanges are obligated to instantly make this information visible to the general public. 2. Trigger Point No acquirer shall be permitted to acquire holdings that, when combined with the acquirer’s already existing stock in the company, would equal or exceed 25 percent of the total, unless the acquirer first makes a public announcement to acquire shares through a public offer to the extent that is specified in the code (currently 26 percent). 3. Merchant Banker The acquirer is required to hire a Category I merchant banker who is registered with SEBI before making an announcement about a public offer. The merchant banker is responsible for ensuring that the public announcement of the offer is made in accordance with the regulations, that the acquirer is able to put the offer into action, and that there are concrete arrangements for finances to satisfy the responsibilities outlined in the offer. 4. Public Announcement Within four business daysfollowing the agreement or the decision to purchase shares or voting rights in excess of the stipulated percentages, the merchant banker is obligated to make a public notification about the transaction. The information regarding the minimum offer price, the object of acquisition, the date by which the letter of offer will be posted, the opening and closing dates of the offer, and the number of shares that are proposed to be bought will be included in the public announcement. 5. Offer Price The pricing that will be offered to the general public is contingent on meeting a number of criteria. 6. The Responsibilities of the Acquirer The acquirer is responsible for ensuring that the letter of offer is delivered to shareholders within forty-five days of the date of the public announcement, and that shareholders who have accepted the offer receive payment within a period of thirty days beginning on the date that the offer period comes to a close. 7. Obligations of the Board of the Target Company During the time period of the public offer, the Board of Directors of the target company is not allowed to dispose of assets, issue capital, enter into significant contracts, or appoint additional directors unless they first obtain the approval of the general body of shareholders, which can only happen after the public announcement of the offer has been made. 8. Bids from Competitors Within a period of twenty-one days of the public release of the initial offer, competitive bids can be made, and in response, the acquirer who made the earlier offer can revise the offer. However, there are some conditions that must be met before competitive bids can be made.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0402.html -->
388 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 9. Establishment of an Escrow Account The acquirer is obligated to place at least 25 percent of the consideration payable for the public offer up to Rs. 100 crore and 10 percent of the consideration above Rs. 100 crore in an escrow account. This is done to ensure that the acquirer will fulfil their obligations. The acquirer is obligated to place at least fifty percent of the price payable for the public offer in an escrow account when the offer is subject to a minimum level of acceptance. The funds for the escrow account should be deposited in the form of cash, a bank guarantee in favour of the merchant banker, or a deposit ofsuitable securities along with the necessary margin. 10. Creeping Acquisition No acquirer, together with persons acting in concert, is allowed to acquire more than 5 percent of holdings in any financial year ending on the 31st of March without complying with the open offer requirements if the existing holdings are between 15 percent and 75 percent. This restriction applies if the existing holdings are between 15 percent and 75 percent. To put it another way, an acquirer who already owns more than 15 percent can engage in a “creeping acquisition” of up to 5 percent per year without triggering the open offer requirements by doing so. However, any such purchase or sale transaction that amounts to 2 percent or more of the share capital of the target company or more must be reported within two days in the same manner as described in the previous point concerning disclosure. This must be done in accordance with the guidelines outlined in the aforementioned point. The purpose of the SEBI guidelines is to (i) impart greater transparency to takeover deals, (ii) ensure a greater amount of disclosure through public announcement and offer document, and (iii) protect the interest of small shareholders. 17.8.4 Pricing Guidelines Under SEBI Takeover Code The highest price out of following will be considered as the minimum price for the open offer: a) The highest price that was agreed for each share of the target company as part of any acquisition that wassubject to the terms of the agreement and triggered the requirement to make a public notification of an open offer. b) The volume-weighted average price paid or due for acquisitions made during the fifty-two weeks immediately before the date of the public announcement. These acquisitions could have been made by the acquirer himself or by any person working in concert with him. c) The highest price paid or payable for any purchase during the twenty-six weeks immediately before the date of the public announcement, whether by the acquirer himself or by any other person acting in concert with him. The highest price paid or payable for any acquisition. d) The volume-weighted average market price of such shares for a period of sixty trading days, immediately preceding the date of the public announcement as traded on the stock exchange where the maximum volume of trading in the shares of the target company, are recorded during such period, provided thatsuch shares are frequently traded. This market price should be calculated using the stock exchange where the maximum volume of trading in the shares of the target company, are recorded during such period. Shares of a target company are considered “frequently traded” if their turnover on any stock exchange during the twelve calendar months prior to the calendar month in which the public announcement is made constitutes at least ten percent of the total number of shares of such a class of shares held by the target company. This percentage must be at least ten percent in order for the shares to be considered “frequently traded.”


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0403.html -->
MERGER, ACQUISITION & RESTRUCTURING | 389 In cases in which the shares are not actively traded, the price of the open offer will be decided by the acquirer and the manager after taking into account various valuation parameters. These parameters may include book value, comparable trading multiples, and any other parameters that are customary for the valuation of shares in companies of this type. After purchasing 25 percent of the company’s equity, acquirers are required by the new takeover law to make an open offer for an additional 26 percent of the company’s shares. This makes it much simpler for potential acquirers to receive a chance to take control of target organisations. Therefore, one should prepare for an increase in hostile takeovers in the years to come. Companies that contain hidden values, such as intellectual property rights (IPR), massive amounts of real estate, or unlisted subsidiaries, may be more susceptible to hostile takeovers than other types of businesses. However, adversarial bids would have a difficult time succeeding in a country where the majority of businesses are managed by promoters who use a variety of strategies to maintain their dominant position in the company. For instance, the current promoters of Escorts and DCM were able to foil the first attempt of this kind made by Lord Swaraj Paul, who was attempting to seize control of those companies. Recently, EIH prevented ITC from mounting a takeover bid by selling an interest in the company to Reliance Industries Limited. ITC had accumulated EIH shares over the past few years and was planning to use those shares in a bid to acquire EIH. In addition to these kinds of roadblocks, finances for hostile bids are currently difficult to come by. 17.8.5 The Acquisition of Ambuja Cements & ACC 82 percent of the equity voting stock of ACC was held by Ambuja Cement India Ltd (ACIL), a division of Gujarat Ambuja Cements Ltd (GACL). Through its Mauritius subsidiary Holderind Investments Ltd., Holcim initially acquired the 40% of the equity in ACIL that was held by three local private investment firms. Then, ACIL made a preferred stock allocation to Holcim’s Indian investment business Holdcem Cements Private Limited, reducing GACL’s interest from 60 to 33 percent and increasing that of the Holcim group to 67 percent. Additionally, Holderind Investments Ltd. received cumulative redeemable preference sharesfromACIL for around 8100 million in order to finance its purchase ofACC shares. The shareholders of ACC were then given an open offer at 370 rupees per share with the intention of raising their total equity voting stock in ACC to 52.66 percent. Due to the acceptable response to this matter, ACIL increased its equity voting position to 34.71 percent, thereby giving it and Holcim through them control over ACC. However, as of now, Ambuja Cements and its subsidiary ACC Ltd were have been purchased for $6.4 billion by TheAdani Group in September 2022 from the Holcim Group in Switzerland. 17.8.6 Anti-takeover Defences Target organisations have utilised a vast array of anti-takeover defences in order to ward off potential bidders, notably in the United States, which has a rich and eventful history of takeovers. The following is a concise explanation of both pre-offer and post-offer defences, which are the two primary groups that comprise this type of legal protection. 1 Pre-Offer Defences a) Staggered board The board of directors consists of three sections of equal number of directors each. One group is selected to lead each year.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0404.html -->
390 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT b) The provision of Super Majority Clause In order for a merger to be approved, an extremely high percentage of votes, typically over 80 percent, is required. c) Poison pills Existing shareholders are given the right to buy bonds or preference stock that, in the case of a merger, are converted into stock of the acquiring firm on very favourable terms. This right is extended to existing shareholders at no additional cost. d) Poison Puts A poison put is an anti-hostile takeover defence tactic. It entails the target company issuing redeemable bonds that can be purchased before they mature. A pre-offer defence technique that is similar to the poison pill tactic is the poison put defence. e) Dual class A new class of equity shareholders, that has better voting rights, is created. These shareholders are given preference in the voting process. f) Golden parachute If the current management isretained after a takeover, they are eligible to obtain a very generous remuneration package. 2 Post-offer Defences a) Greenmail The target firm makes a deal with the bidder in which it promises not to engage in a hostile takeover in exchange for the target company agreeing to buy the shares acquired by the bidder at a premium. b) Pacman defence The stock that was being bid on is then subject to a counterbid from the company that is being targeted. c) Litigation The corporation being targeted initiates legal action against the company doing the bidding, alleging that the latter broke anti-trust or securities laws. d) Restructuring of Assets The bidder demands that the target firm sell its most valuable assets, also known as the “crown jewels,” and/or that the target company buy assets that the bidder does not want or that may cause anti-trust issues for the bidder. e) Restructuring of the liabilities Either the target firm buys back its own shares at a significant premium or it issues new shares to a third party who is friendly with the company. At least one anti-takeover defence is utilised by most companies in the United States. The staggered board and the poison pill are two common types of defences that players use. The first method is utilised by oversixty percent of the large businessesin order to stop an acquisition from arbitrarily replacing the entire board of directors. The latter makes a corporation an unfeasible and excessively expensive target; it is utilised by around fifty percent of the large companies. 3 Anti-takeover Defences in India When compared to their American counterparts, businesses in India have a more limited selection of anti-takeover defences at their disposal. Currently, businesses in India will use any combination of the following defences in the event that a hostile takeover attempt is made against them.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0405.html -->
MERGER, ACQUISITION & RESTRUCTURING | 391 Currently, businesses in India will use any combination of the following defences in the event that a hostile takeover attempt is made against them. a) Make Preferential Allotment In order to increase the promoter group’s equity holding, a corporation could provide the promoter group preferred treatment when it comesto the allocation of equity shares or convertible securities. b) Creeping Enhancement The promoter group is permitted by the guidelinesissued by SEBI to increase its equity ownership through creeping upgrades; however, they are subject to limits and cannot invoke the clause that allows them to make an open market offer. c) Amalgamate Group Companies A larger company could be formed by the combination of two or more businesses that were all fostered by the same organisation. When all other factors are taken into account, a larger company is in a better position to withstand a hostile takeover than a smaller company. d) Sell the Crown Jewels In the event that the raider is enticed by certain valuable assets held by the target company, the target company can decide to sell such assets in order to make itself less desirable to the raider. e) Search for a White Knight A business that is under attack may turn to its allies for moral and financial assistance. It is possible for it to ask a white knight to come to its help and save it from the grasp of the raider. 17.9 LEVERAGED BUYOUTS A leveraged buyout is the process of transferring control of a company while primarily using debt as financing. While some leveraged buyouts entail the acquisition of an entire company, others focus on purchasing only a portion of an existing company’s operations. A management buyout is the term used to describe a transaction in which the management of a business unit purchases the business unit outright (MBO). Following the completion of the buyout, the company (or the business unit) will in all likelihood transition into a private company. A straightforward illustration is all that is required to convey the essential nature of a leveraged buyout transaction. The A division, the B division, and the C division are the three different departments that are under the purview of Vipul Limited. Vipul Limited is considering selling off theAsection of the company. If these assets were sold, they would only bring in 100 crore rupees, despite the fact that the division’s replacement cost for its assets is 160 crore rupees. If it receives Rs. 120 crore, Vipul is open to the idea ofselling the division. Four important executives working for theAdivision are interested in carrying out a leveraged buyout in order to acquire the division. They are open to making an investment of 10 crores of rupees. They make a request for assistance to the commercial banking company known as Financial Engineering Limited (FEL). FEL produces estimates for the A division based on the assumption that the four top executives will run it as an independent firm in the future. According to FEL’s calculations, the cash flows generated by this venture would be sufficient to cover a debt of Rs. 120 crore. FEL is successful in locating a financial institution that is willing to contribute debt financing to the project in the amount of Rs. 95 crore. In addition to this, FEL is successful in finding a private investor who is willing to contribute Rs. 8 crore in the equity of this project. The A division of Vipul was purchased by a separate business, which is headed by the four senior executives, and was financed by debt in the


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0406.html -->
392 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT amount of Rs. 95 crore and equity in the amount of Rs. 18 crore (Rs. 10 crore is contributed by the four key executives and Rs. 8 crore by the private investor). 17.9.1 What Does Debt Do? A leveraged buyout includes high dependence on loans. What does that clue up to? On the other hand, equity tends to have a sedative effect on management, whereas debt tends to provide a jolt of energy. The presence of debt provides management with an incentive to perform, but the presence of equity encourages management to kick back, relax, and take it easy. “Debt is a ‘just-in-time’ financial system,” G. Bennett Stewart III and David M. Glassman described it. Another technique to squeeze operational inefficiencies is the precise duty to repay it. When there is limited room for error, the risks associated with committing a mistake are increased, which leads to fewer errors being committed. In a similar vein, they continued by saying, “Equity is easy, and debt is difficult.” When compared to debt, equity is more forgiving. Debt is a sword, whereas equity is a pillow. The yin and yang of corporate finance are represented by equity and debt, respectively. 17.9.2 Risks and Rewards With the assistance of significant debt financing, the sponsors of a leveraged buyout are enticed by the possibility of owning a firm or a division of that company in its whole (or to a significant extent). They are willing to put themselves through a lot of trouble in the pursuit of potentially lucrative payoffs. Their capacity to improve the performance of the unit, contain the business risks associated with it, apply cost controls, and dispose assets that are no longer needed is critical to the success of the entire operation. In the event that they are unable to do so, the large fixed financial costs may put the endeavour in jeopardy. 17.9.3 How Is Value Created? There appear to be three primary variables that contribute to the creation of value in an LBO: a) The value of the firm is increased as a result of operational improvements. b) Cash generated from operations is put toward paying down debt, which results in an increase in the proportion of enterprise value held by equity shareholders. c) A tax shield is provided by the interest on the debt. 17.9.4 What Makes a Good LBO Candidate When a company or division possesses the following qualities, it indicates that it is a strong candidate for an LBO. a) Good management team. b) Consistent Cash flows. c) The opportunity for enhancing operational effectiveness. d) Adaptability with regard to the sale of assets that are no longer needed. e) Requirements for capital expenditures are quite low. f) Easy Exit. 17.9.5 LBO of Tetley The story of David and Goliath in the business world, the acquisition of Tetley Tea by Tata Tea, generated quite a bit of publicity in the 2000s. Tata Tea is an Indian company. The negotiation for the acquisition began in February of 2000, and on March 10th, 2000, Tetley Tea was finally put up for sale. This


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0407.html -->
MERGER, ACQUISITION & RESTRUCTURING | 393 takeover deal would be remembered as one that witnessed a “global shark” being purchased by a “Indian minnow” in the annals of business history. This was not an exaggeration because the situation truly was as described. When Tata wanted to buy Tetley, the company’s net worth was only $114 million, but the acquisition cost $450 million, which was equivalent to 271 million pounds. The fact that this was the first acquisition made by an Indian company, utilising the strategy of a leveraged buyout, contributed to the sensationalism of this deal. 17.10 ACQUISITION FINANCING In recent years, the businesssector in India has been quite active in its pursuit of acquisition opportunities worldwide. Some of the most notable transactions that have taken place recently include the following: Dr. Reddy’s Laboratories’purchase of Betapharm for $570 million; Suzlon Energy’s purchase of Hansen Transmission for $565 million; Tata Steel’s purchase of Corus for $12.9 billion; Hindalco’s purchase of Novelis for $6 billion; and Bharti Airtel’s purchase of Zain’s African assets for $10.7 billion. The acquisition finance offered by banks such as Citi, Deutsche Bank, Stanchart, and ICICI Bank, all of whom have established specialised teams solely for the purpose of providing acquisition financing, is a major factor in the success of such transactions. The following is a list of characteristics that are typical of purchase financing: a) Either the overseas subsidiary of the Indian company or a special purpose entity formed overseas for the purpose of acquisition receives the loan. The loan is issued for the purpose of acquisition. b) On the basis ofthe guarantee offered by the Indian parent company, financing is offered by international financial institutions and by overseas branches of Indian banks. c) When deciding whether or not to provide financial assistance, lenderslook at the acquirer’s cash flow. The amount of financing is typically between three and six times EBITDA, however this number can vary widely based on a variety of criteria including the quality of the products, the quality of the management, the regulatory environment, and the viability of cash flows. d) The cost of financing is determined by a number of factors, including the robustness of the acquisition target, the ingenuity of the parent company, the availability of legal recourse to the lenders, and the type of debt that the bank is participating in (senior debt, mezzanine debt, subordinated debt, or junk debt, respectively) (this is yet to catch on). The cost of acquisition finance is typically about 200 basis points more than the standard financing charges, with a range of 25 basis points to 500 basis points possible. 17.10.1 Tata’s Acquisition of Jaguar Land Rover Tata Motors completed the acquisition of the Jaguar and Land Rover companies from Ford Motor Company on June 2, 2008, as was initially announced on March 26, 2008, in an all-cash transaction. The net consideration for the acquisition was $2.3 billion, which is equivalent to Rs. 9,200 crore. Ford made a contribution to the pension schemes ofJaguar Land Rover amounting to around $600 million US. Following the completion of the deal, Tata Motors discovered that it had accumulated a debt of Rs. 21,900 crore. The corporation reported its first loss in seven years, which came in the form of a Rs. 2,500 crore loss for the fiscal year ending in 2009 (FY2009). This was a striking departure from the earnings of Rs. 2,200 crore that the company had raked in just the single preceding year (2008). Not only had the worldwide recession of 2008 caused a decrease in vehicle sales (particularly in the luxury and SUV segments), but it also made it more difficult for the automotive sector to obtain adequate


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0408.html -->
394 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT credit from financial institutions. Because Tata Motors and Jaguar Land Rover both catered to wholly distinct markets in the passenger automobile sector, there was reason to be concerned about the overall scope of the transaction. Tata and JLR had to first confront the reality that they were wholly independent corporations, each with their own underlying value chain and business plan. This was an initial challenge for the two companies. The first thing that Tata Motors did was ensure that the management structure that was already in place at JLR would not be altered by the addition of Tata’s own executives. Tata latersupported JLR in establishing an effective cash management system within the company, which marked the first time in JLR’s history that the corporation was accountable for its own money. JLR was well positioned to achieve profitable growth thanks to the support it received from the management consultant Roland Berger. At JLR, ten to eleven different teams with cross-functional responsibilities were established. Tata invested approximately one billion pounds(equivalent to Rs. 5,728 crore at the then prevailing exchange rate) in order to meet the requirements for product development, the upgrading of technology, and capital expenditures. In order to maintain a consistent flow of cash, it even resorted to selling off a portion of its equity in Tata Steel and other subsidiaries of the parent firm. JLR has rebuilt, integrated, and enlarged its line-up as a result of investments totalling more than 22 billion pounds (Rs. 196,249 crore). This was accomplished by utilising flexible, shared platforms as well as new powertrains and proprietary technology. Today, the venerable British company boasts a workforce of more than 43,000 people across its many locations. 17.11 BUSINESS ALLIANCES There has been a considerable increase in the number of different types of business alliances,such asjoint ventures,strategic alliances, equity partnerships, licencing, franchising alliances, and network alliances.A well-designed business alliance can be a viable alternative to mergers and acquisitions in many different types of scenarios. It should come as no surprise that they have become standard practice in a variety of industries including high technology, media and entertainment, autos, pharmaceuticals, oil exploration, and financial services. Here are some conspicuous examples: a) In the 1980s, General Motors and Toyota came to an agreement to form a joint venture, which was at the time unheard of. b) Over one hundred different companies and organisations are Merck’s partners in research and development. c) In 1999, IBM established business collaborations with firms such as Cisco and Dell PCs that were worth a combined total of $30 billion. d) Over 15,000 alliances have been formed between Oracle and its many business partners. The nature of business partnerships can take many different shapes. Joint ventures, strategic alliances, equity partnerships, franchising alliances, and network alliances are the most prevalent types of business partnerships. Other types include licencing and network alliances. 17.11.1 Rationale for Business Alliances The desire to share risks and gain access to new markets, cut expenses, achieve favourable regulatory treatment, or purchase (or exit) a business is one of the primary drivers behind the formation of business partnerships.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0409.html -->
MERGER, ACQUISITION & RESTRUCTURING | 395 a) Risk and Resource Sharing The creation of brand new technological capabilities can be a highly high-stakes and pricey endeavour. In addition, undertakings of this nature necessitate the pooling of the technical resources of other organisations.Therefore, businesses operating in high technology sectors often form business alliances in order to facilitate the pooling of a variety of know-how, the acquisition of sufficient capital, and the development of risk-sharing arrangements that are deemed acceptable. b) Gaining Entry into Emerging Markets Because of the enormous outlaysthat are required for advertising, promotion,storage, and distribution, the cost of entering a new market may be prohibitively expensive. To find a solution to this issue, a company can consider forming a strategic partnership with another businessin order to sell its goods or services through the sales force, distribution channels, or Internet site of the other company. c) Reduction in Expenditure The sharing or combination of facilitiesin joint production activities as well asrelationships between purchasers and suppliers that are favourable to both parties can contribute to cost savings and be achieved through the formation of business partnerships. d) Favourable Approach of Regulators Joint ventures are often viewed in a more favourable light than mergers or acquisitions by regulatory bodies like RBI and SEBI in India. e) Basis for Acquisition or Exit There is a possibility that acquiring another company will begin with the formation of a strategic alliance or joint venture. As an alternative, one might utilise it as a way to get out of running a firm. 17.11.2 The Success Mantra The following attributes are essential to the growth and prosperity of a business partnership: a) The partners’ respective strengths are complementary to one another. b) For a single company, the expense of producing a new product can be too expensive. c) The partners are capable of working together to achieve their common goals. d) There is an unmistakable understanding of the purpose, the roles, and the obligations. e) All of the persons involved consider the distribution of responsibilities and potential benefits to be fair. f) Both the partners’ time horizons and their financial expectations are comparable to one another. 17.12 MANAGING ACQUISITIONS Quite a few studies have found that mergers and acquisitions more frequently than not result in a loss of value for the shareholders of the company which acquires another company. A brand’s value can be increased through mergers and acquisitionsfor a variety of reasons, including the improvement of existing products orservices, a change in personality or direction, a gateway to marketsin other countries, and the purchase of brilliant individuals or intellectual property. In spite of the persuasive arguments in favour of engaging in a transaction, it is surprising how few of them actually result in value creation. According to the findings of a study conducted by KPMG, more than fifty percent of mergers end up decreasing shareholder value, while thirty percent have no effect at all. Failures in accounting and operations are two of the more tangible causes of disastrous mergers; however, the most complex explanations have to


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0410.html -->
396 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT do with people, culture, and the emotions that humans experience. These are also the errors that are the most challenging to fix. 17.12.1 Collective Wisdom on Mergers and Acquisitions The following is a hypothesis that emerges from the substantial study done on mergers and acquisitions: a) When the stock market is performing well, it is beneficial for businesses to engage in mergers and acquisitions. b) In most cases, acquirers pay prices that are excessive. This is to the advantage of the shareholders of the company that is being acquired, but it is to the detriment of the shareholders of the company that is being acquired. c) Chief Executive Officers fall in love with transactions and fail to walk away from them when they ought to. d) Repeat purchasers have a better chance of becoming successful compared to infrequent consumers. e) When compared to the purchase of publicly traded companies, the acquisition of privately held businesses is a method that is more trustworthy for creating value and producing higher returns for purchasers. f) It is more likely that connected buys will yield larger returns than purchases that are not related. g) Integration might be difficult to achieve successfully. 17.12.2 Five Sins of Acquisitions It would appear that acquisitions are afflicted with five sins: unable to integrate successfully, a) Getting Way Too Far Away From It All Extremely few companies are capable of efficiently managing a wide variety of business types. It is common for there to be a strong urge to wander off into unconnected regions that give off the impression of being very exotic and very promising. However, the fact of the matter isthat endeavours of this nature frequently involve a great deal of danger. b) Striving for Greatness The majority of organisations, whether they are businesses or not, measure themselves primarily based on theirsize. This may be a very essential yardstick. Because of this, there is a tremendoustemptation on the side of managers to develop large empires because their income is strongly influenced by the size of their companies. The preoccupation with scale could cause rash decisions on acquisitions. c) Plunging without thinking It is rather typical for prospective buyers to not conduct sufficient research on the seller’s company. The issues here are as follows: I the seller may overestimate the worth of intangible assets (such as brand image, technical know-how, patents and copyrights, and so on), (ii) the financial statements may be skillfully window-dressed, and (iii) the buyer may not be able to accurately evaluate the possible problems and contingent liabilities, or it may casually dismiss them aside because of its infatuation with the target company. d) Overpaying When there is a competitive bidding environment, those that are naive have a tendency to place higher bids. Most of the time, the highest bidder is the one who grossly misjudge the value due to their lack of experience. Even though he comes out on top, it may be said that he is the winner of the most unfortunate contest. The “winner’s curse” idea is another name for this phenomenon.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0411.html -->
MERGER, ACQUISITION & RESTRUCTURING | 397 e) Inability to Integrate Well Even the most brilliant ofstrategies can be rendered uselessif they are not carried out effectively. The effective integration of two distinct organisations after an acquisition is necessary for its completion successfully. This is a prerequisite for success. This is a difficult endeavour, and it is possible that it will not be completed successfully. 17.12.3 A Disciplined Acquisition Programme Because there are no dry runs, the entire amount of money has to be paid up front, the exit costs after integration are prohibitively high, and managing a synergy is in many ways similar to managing a complex new venture, acquisitions are a special kind of investment decision that cannot be made in any other way. It should come as no surprise that acquirers have recklessly wasted billions of dollars in their pursuit of acquisitions that are poorly understood. In light of these empirical facts, Warren Hellman, an experienced investment banker, expresses his scepticism regarding mergers in the following manner: “There should be a presumption of failure because so many mergers fail to provide what they claim.” Because acquisition premiumstypically range from 20 percent to 60 percent of the pre-acquisition market value, a company that is acquiring another company needs to devise a strategy that is both transparent and consistent so that it can earn back the premium that it will almost certainly be required to pay. A corporation needs to fulfil the following requirements in order to improve its chances of creating value: a) Keep your attention on the Right Targets. b) Ensure that synergies are accurately estimated. c) Negotiate in a disciplined manner d) Organize and exercise command over the integrating Focus on Right Targets Pay attention to the areas in which you have the opportunity to produce value. For instance, if your company is the best in its class when it comes to operations, you should think about under-performing companies that have the potential to be turned around. If your company has a unique sales channel, you should look for companies that have great products but weak sales capabilities. Create a database of potential targets for acquisition, and make sure to keep it up to date on a regular basis. Applying appropriate screening criteria can help you narrow down the list. For instance, an acquirer might decide not to pursue a deal with a company because it is either too large, too small, involved in an unrelated business, quoting at a high price-earnings multiple, not amenable to acquisition, culturally alien, or any combination of these factors. Estimate Synergies Each potential acquisition should be evaluated based on the most accurate information possible. Wishful thinking and an unhealthy fixation with acquiring the target firm are both bad for valuation, and both should be avoided at all costs. Most importantly, concerns of “synergistic benefits,” which may be illusory rather than real, must not lead to an overestimation of value. Mark L. Sirover contends in his well-researched book, The Synergy Trap, that the synergy trap opens for eight out of ten companies involved in big acquisitions due to inadequate pre-acquisition planning. Negotiate in a Disciplined Manner Even when synergies are accurately predicted, acquirers nevertheless face the temptation to pay too much for a target company. It is well known that the process of negotiating purchases can easily lead to


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0412.html -->
398 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT increasing obligations being made. According to Warren Buffett, the excitement of the pursuit can cause the acquirer to be oblivious to the results of the pursuit. Due to the fact that this inherent risk is present, it makes perfect sense to delegate the duty of acquisition analysis and negotiation to two separate teams. The first team should conduct an analysis of the acquisition target from the perspectives of strategy, operation, and organisation, and then design a game plan outlining the stepsthat need to be taken in order to improve the overall performance. In an ideal scenario, it should be made up of operations managers who, in the event that the deal is finalised, would be charged with the job of actually running the business. The second team should be made up of people with expertise in finance and law, and they should be given the responsibility of actually carrying out the discussions. It is imperative that it maintain the mental fortitude to back out of the transaction even if the financial and legal parameters become more favourable. Plan and Control the Integration The road to acquisition is littered with obstacles at every turn. Differences in visions, strategies, values, cultures, production processes and standards, accounting systems, administrative procedures, and so on can all give birth to these kinds of conflicts. Personality conflicts and the responses of those whose lives have been changed by the acquisition are the primary causes of these problems. It’s possible that the merging companies, who are likely consumed by their excitement for the merger, will brush such issues off as unimportant. On the other hand, what should be done is the complete opposite of that. A conscientious effort needs to be made to think through the consequences of the merger, predict problems that may occur, comprehend the nature of these problems, and work out a solution to these problems that is reasonable and mutually acceptable. It pays to perform proper due diligence and avoid getting swayed by instincts. Better safe than sorry. 17.13 DIVESTITURES It refersto the act of a corporation selling one of its divisions or undertakingsto another firm or forming an entirely new company out of the remaining elements of its business. There are many different motivations behind divestiture and demerger, including the following: a) To focus on the most important aspects of the business; b) It is possible that the Division’s or Business’ contributions to the total income are insufficient; c) The size of the company can be too great to be managed effectively d) The company might have an immediate need for funds since it is considering alternative investment opportunities. 17.13.1 The Viewpoint of the Seller It is essential to keep in mind that there must always be a seller in order for there to be a buyer. The process of selling something is known as divestiture, despite the fact that the processes of analysis used for buying and selling are identical. Although the decision to sell a firm is at least as essential as the decision to purchase one, selling typically does not involve the same level of forethought as purchasing does.Alot of the time, the decision and the choice of the buyer are completely arbitrary, which resultsin a poor bargain for the owners of the firm that is being sold. It is essential to have the awareness that selling something requires the same set of skills as purchasing something. It’s possible that the executives of a firm will have to decide to sell off a certain section at some point in the future. There is no impropriety in selling a division to another party if doing so results in a higher overall value. The decision to sell could have been spurred by dismal growth forecastsfor a particular division or by industry-wide consolidation.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0413.html -->
MERGER, ACQUISITION & RESTRUCTURING | 399 It is prudent for executives to be ready to make a sale at any moment, taking into account the reality that the necessity to do so could occur at any time. To be more specific, executives are the ones who need to be aware of the value of their organisation. It is possible to take into account both the organization’s strengths and its weaknessesin terms of production, marketing, general management, the value ofsynergy to prospective purchasers, the value of brand equity, the organization’s skill base, and so on. In conclusion, the following are some of the most important “sell-side” imperatives: 1) The pressure from competitors is getting higher. 2) It appears that the company will have to be sold because it is dealing with a number of major issues, including the following: a) Lack of access to emerging technologies and latest advancements. b) Strong market entry obstacles. There was no way to improve our geographical presence. c) Poorly positioned on either the supply or demand side of the market. d) It was not possible to reach the critical mass. e) No efficient usage of distribution capacities. f) It was not possible to build new strategic business divisions in preparation for future expansion. g) Lack of sufficient financial resources to finish the project. h) An opportunity knocking in the form of the possibility of selling the company at a price that is desirable. i) Focus on core competencies. If a major and well-known company approaches the target company with the idea, the latter may be more than likely to give in, as this would be in the best interest of the shareholders. 17.13.2 Different Forms The following is a list of the various ways in which divestment, demerger, or divestitures can be accomplished: a) Complete or Partial Sale of Assets A sell off is the process by which one organisation transfers ownership of an asset, factory, division, product line, or subsidiary to another organisation in exchange for a purchase consideration paid in the form of cash or securities. Partial sell off is a type of divestment in which the corporation sells a business unit or a subsidiary to another entity because the entity is determined to be incompatible with the primary business strategy of the parent company. Typically, a subsidiary is sold off when it is determined that it cannot be integrated into the primary businessstrategy of the main company. It’s possible that the market is giving the combined businesses a lower value than they deserve since there isn’t enough synergy between the parent company and the subsidiary. Therefore, the management and the board come to the conclusion that the subsidiary would do better under the ownership of a different party. The sale of a subsidiary not wanted by the parent company generates cash, which can then be used to settle outstanding financial obligations. Corporate raiders utilised debt as a source of financing for acquisitions during the late 1980s and early 1990s. Then, after completing a purchase, they would sell off its subsidiaries in order to obtain cash to pay off the debt that they had incurred. If the total is bigger than the sum of its parts, then the invaders’ manner of operation makes perfect sense. Deals fall through when this condition is not met.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0414.html -->
400 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT b) Spin-off In this scenario, a portion of the business is segmented off and spun off into its own independent company. The company’s current stockholders will each get a proportionate ownership stake in the business. Therefore, there is no change in ownership, and the same shareholders continue to own the newly established business in the same proportion as they did in the original company. This means that there is no change in ownership. The management of the spin-off subsidiary, on the other hand, has been replaced. A spin-off does not result in new financial gain. One possible motive for spinning off an entity is to: (i) provide a component or division its own distinct identity. (ii) prevent a hostile takeover of the company by a potential acquirer by making the company less appealing to the potential acquirer by spinning off a profitable segment. (ii) split regulated and uncontrolled business lines into their own distinct entities. c) Split-up This necessitatesthe division of the entire company into a number of independentspin-off companies (by creating separate legal entities). Only the newly established entities continue to exist today, as the parent company does not exist legally any longer. For example, a large corporation might have four different divisions labelled A, B, C, and D. These four divisions are going to be broken up into four separate corporate businesses, each of which will have complete autonomy and a distinct legal position. The company that was founded as a corporation will soon be dissolved. As a result of their lowersize, de-merged units are significantly easier to organise and handle from a logisticalstandpoint. Therefore, it is recognised that spin-offs and split-ups have the potential to increase the value of the company to its shareholders while also bringing about increased efficiency and effectiveness. d) Equity Carve Outs This is similar to a spin off; however, in this case, some shares of the new firm are sold in the market by making a public offer, which results in cash being brought in. To increase their value to shareholders, an increasing number of businesses are turning to equity carve-outs. An initial public offering (IPO) of shares is essentially a partial sell-off and is used by a parent company to bring a subsidiary company into the public eye. The parent business establishes a new publicly traded subsidiary while maintaining a controlling share in the newly traded company itself. When one of a parent company’s subsidiaries is expanding at a faster rate and carries a greater valuation than other firms owned by the parent, the parent company may choose to pursue a strategic option known as a carve-out. The sale of shares in the subsidiary to the general public results in the generation of cash from a carve-out. However, the issuance also unlocks the value of the subsidiary unit and increases the value of the parent company’s shareholders. Although a carve-out creates a new legal corporation with its own board of directors, the parent company typically maintains some level of control over the new entity. In situations like these, it’s possible thatsome members of the board of directors of the parent company willserve on both boards. The relationship between the two companiesislikely to be strong given that the parent company holds a controlling stake in the subsidiary, which indicates that the shareholders of both companies are the same. Having said that, businesses may decide to spin off a subsidiary not because it is profitable but rather because it is a liability for the parent company. Even if the subsidiary was previously a part of the parent company and does not have a proven track record of increasing revenues and profits, having such an intention will not lead to a successful outcome, especially if the subsidiary that was carved out is already too burdened with debt or problems.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0415.html -->
MERGER, ACQUISITION & RESTRUCTURING | 401 e) The Demerger of Two Family-Owned Businesses or Their Division Family run businesses are responsible for the management of over eighty percent of India’s private sector enterprises.As a result of the evolving picture of a liberalised economy, the capital markets are spreading, which is accompanied by attendant growth incentives. As a result of this, family-owned businesses are facing enormous pressure to hand control of their businesses over to professional managements. As a result of this, many of these corporations are in the process of formulating plans to spin off their unproductive operations or divisions in order to address a number of succession issues. Even if this is not the case, a collection of enterprises that are run by the same family can decide to reorganise their business in order to concentrate on just their most important activities. To do this, the first thing that will likely need to be done within the group is to differentiate between core and non-core processes. The second phase may involve lowering the burden of interest payments by reorganising the debt and selling any assets that are in surplus. The money gained by selling assets could be invested in the business’s future growth through new acquisitions and the reinvigoration of its existing operations. The bottom line isthat an acquisition needsto develop and promote synergies, increase economies of scale, and cut manufacturing costs in order to be successful. Therefore, in addition to making acquisitions, the business might be required to unavoidably take effortsto increase the productivity of its existing activities. In one way or another, expansion can be achieved through corporate mergers, asset purchases, and takeovers. They are founded on the synergy principle, which states that the sum of two and two is equal to five. The opposite of thisistrue for divestitures, which require some form of reduction. They are founded on the anergy principle, which states that the sum of two and two is equal to three. The equity carveout, the partial sell-off, and the demerger (spinoff and split-up) are the three most important techniques of divestment among the many different methods that are available. It is important to keep in mind that the definition of divestitures offered by certain academics is fairly restricted.We use a more all-encompassing definition of “divestitures” that includes “partialselloffs,” “demergers,” “selling of equity stake,” and so on. f) Partial Selloff Apartialselloff, also known as a slump sale, isthe process by which one company transfers ownership of a business unit or asset to another company. It is the equivalent of selling a company unit or a plant, but in the opposite direction. It is a form of contraction when viewed from the standpoint of the seller, but it is a form of expansion when viewed from the perspective of the buyer.As an illustration, the size of Coromandal Fertilisers Limited decreased after the company sold its cement division to India Cement Limited. On the other hand, the size of India Cement Limited increased. 17.13.3 Motives for Selloff Many different factors can contribute to the decision to sell an asset. The following is a discussion of the more significant ones: a) Raising capital One of the most prevalent reasons for selling off assets is to accumulate more capital. Companies that are short on cash often turn to divestiture as a means of improving their liquidity. CEAT, for instance, was able to settle its outstandings and obtain finances by selling its nylon tyre cord business in Gwalior to SRF for the price of 3,250 million rupees. This allowed CEAT to concentrate more on the production of tyres.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0416.html -->
402 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT b) Reduction in monetary losses One of the primary motivations for selling off assets is to minimise monetary losses. In a broader sense, it may suggest that the unit that is being considered for sale is generating a rate of return that is below average. c) Adjustments to the strategic alignment The buyer may decide to sell a unit that is no longer compatible with the seller’s overall strategic plan. A unit like this typically operates in a line that is unrelated to the rest of the company, and it may require a significant amount of time and attention from management. Following the completion of the divestment, the seller will be able to focus on the operation of its primary company. It would appear that ICI is an excellent example. It sold its seed business to Hysum India, its fibre business to Terene Fibres India, and its fertiliser business to Chand Chap Fertilisers and Chemicals. The purpose of these sell-offs was to hone in on ICI’s focus on paints and industrial chemicals, which is in accordance with the overall strategy of its parent company. d) Focus The company is able to sharpen itsfocus when activitiesthat are not directly related to its core business are sold off. Investment bankers are under the impression that conglomerates sell at a discount due to the fact that stock analysts find it difficult to monitor them. A counterargument was presented by Michael Sheriman, who stated, “It’s not that conglomerates are difficult for analysts to understand. We are concerned that management will have a hard time grasping the nature of conglomerates. e) Increased productivity A sell-off might result in an increase in efficiency when the unit that is being sold would be worth more as a component of another company or as a standalone enterprise. This occurs when there is a reverse synergy, which is sometimes referred to as a “anergy” in some circles. This indicates that the value of the individual components is higher than the value of the total. It follows, according to elementary rules of mathematics, that 5 minus 3 equals 3! It is important to keep in mind that the possibility of synergistic benefit is what drives a merger, which means that it is anticipated that the whole would be more valuable than the sum of the parts: 2 + 3 = 6! 17.13.4 Financial Evaluation of a Sell-off When a corporation sells a division (or plant) to another company, the company will typically transfer the assets of the division along with the liabilities of the division, with the approval of the division’s creditors. This is standard procedure. This essentially means that the selling company, which will be referred to in the following paragraphs as the parent company, will transfer its ownership position in that division. The following step-by-step process can be carried out in order to determine whether or not it is financially beneficial to proceed in this manner: 1. First, formulate an estimate for the after-tax cash flow of the division. The parent company needs to make an estimate of the cash flow after taxes that is associated with the operations of the division in addition to the rest of the operations of the parent company. The question that needsto be answered in this scenario is this: What happens to the post-tax cash flow of the parent firm with the division, and what happens to it without the division? The disparity between the two figures shows the division’s after-tax contribution to the cash flow of the company. 2. Establish the discount rate for the division asthe second step. The discount rate thatshould be applied to the division’s post-tax cash flow should be reflective of the risk that the division faces as a separate


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0417.html -->
MERGER, ACQUISITION & RESTRUCTURING | 403 enterprise. The cost of capital of a company (or a group of companies) that is approximately the same size as the division in question and that is engaged solely or substantially in the same line of business can be used as a proxy for the division’s cost of capital, according to one method that has been proposed. 3. Determine the value of the division in the present. Calculate the post-tax cash flow that was created in Step 1 and use the discount rate that was obtained in Step 2 to determine its present value. This reflects the current value of the cash flow generating capacity that can be generated by the division. 4. Determine the fair value of the liabilities that are specific to each division The present value of the obligationsthat have arisen as a result of the division’sliabilitiesis all that isrequired to calculate the market value of the division-specific liabilities. Keep in mind that the market value of the divisionspecific liabilities will differ from the book value of the division-specific liabilities if the contracted interest rates on those liabilities differ from the current interest rates. This will cause the market value of the division-specific liabilities to differ from the book value of the division-specific liabilities. 5. Estimate the Value of the Parent Company’s Ownership Position in the Division of the Business Simply put, the value of the ownership position (VOP) that the parent company enjoysin the division is equal to the following: The value of the division – The current value of the cash flow liabilities that are specific to each division = (Step 3) – (Step 4) 6. Evaluate the value of ownership position (VOP) in relation to the proceeds from the divestiture (DP) As remuneration for relinquishing its ownership position in a subsidiary, a parent company is entitled to divestiture proceeds (DP) in the event that it is the party responsible for the transfer of a division’s assets in addition to the liabilities associated with that division. The following would thus be the decision rule for the divestment of assets: DP > VOP Put the division up for sale. DP = VOP Be indifferent DP < VOP Maintain the separation. 17.13.5 Tax Aspects of a Sell-Off A sell-off, also known as a slump sale in the tax jargon, refers to the transfer of one or more undertakings as a result of the sale for a lump sum consideration without assigning values to the individual assets and liabilities of the undertaking being sold. This occurs when an undertaking is sold for a lower price than it was originally purchased for. Any profits or gains made as a result of a slump sale carried out in the year before to the one in which the transfer takes place will be subject to taxation as long-term capital gains and will be considered to be income earned during the year prior to the one in which the transfer takes place. However, if any capital assets, comprising one or more undertakings, which the assessee has owned and held for not more than 36 months, are transferred as part of a slump sale, then the capital gains that follow from this transaction are considered to be short-term capital gains. The difference between the sale consideration and the net worth of the undertaking that was transferred is what is referred to as the capital gain. The sum of a person’s total assets is referred to as their “net worth.” The value of any fixed assets must be recorded at their adjusted cost basis in order to comply with income tax regulations. In the case of long-term capital gains, you will not be able to take advantage of the benefits of indexation. On the other hand, the advantage of a reduced rate of taxation for long-term capital gains [of 20%] would be accessible.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0418.html -->
404 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT 17.13.6 Rationale It is generally accepted thatspinoffs and split-ups are valuable toolsfor improving the value of a company’s operations by increasing their level of efficiency and productivity. The following is a list of the primary sources that contribute to gains in efficiency and improvements in performance: a) Clearer focal point By removing a bad “fit” for the firm, a spinoff can help promote a better focus for the business. K.M. Sheth, Executive Chairman of GE Shipping, made the following statement at the time that the company demerged its offshore oilfield services business in 2005: “The entire restructuring of the business through the demerger route is aimed at providing a greater focus to each of the businesses of the company as well as to unlock shareholder value.” b) Enhanced incentives, along with greater responsibility Aspinoff improves managerial incentives and raisesthe level of accountability of the parent company. As an illustration, the chief executive officer of Peabody International provided the following justification for the spinoff of GEO International: “Speaking from personal experience, one of the most exciting benefits has been a rekindling of the entrepreneurial spirit and initiative within both Peabody and GEO.” Managers in both businesses now have the perception that the contributions they make individually can have a major impact on the bottom-line performance. c) Carveouts of equity When a parent firm engages in an equity carveout, it is selling a portion of its ownership stake in a wholly owned subsidiary. It is possible that a strategic investor or the general investing public will buy the equity. The following are some of the ways in which an equity carveout is distinct from a spin off: (a) In the case of a spinoff, the current shareholders of the parent business receive a portion of the newly created company’s shares, but in the case of an equity carveout, the newly created company’s shares are offered for sale to new investors. (a) In contrast to a spin-off, an equity carveout provides the parent business with an influx of cash while a spin-off does not. Carveouts of equity are transactions that are carried out with the purpose of bringing cash to the parent company and luring a strategic investor into a subsidiary. 17.14 HOLDING COMPANY A holding company is a corporation that owns the equity of multiple other companies in order to exert influence over those companies. A holding company does not necessarily have to own one hundred percent of the investee business’s equity. In many cases, it is possible for it to exercise effective working control over the activities of another firm with an ownership stake that is as little as 25 percent (or even sometimeslower than this), provided that it is able to manage itsrelationship with the othershareholders. The following are some of the other benefits that come with running a holding company: 1. Central control 2. Flexibility for growth 3. Continuity and succession planning 4. Tax planning 5. Risk mitigation 6. Legal protection for valuable assets of the group 7. Optimum utilisation of funds


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0419.html -->
MERGER, ACQUISITION & RESTRUCTURING | 405 17.15 DEMERGERS A demerger is the process by which one business transfers one or more of its undertakings to another company. This can involve more than one company. It is common practice to refer to the company whose undertaking is transferred as the demerged company, and the business (or companies) to whom the undertaking is transferred as the resultant company. It’s possible for a demerger to look more like a spinoff or a split-up. A spinoff is the process through which an enterprise or department within a company istransformed into a standalone business. Following the completion of the spinoff, the parent firm and the newly formed company are each considered to be independent corporate entities. For instance, in the late 1980s, the Information Technology Division of WIPRO Limited was separated out into its own firm so that it could operate independently. A firm may undergo a split-up, which involvesthe division of the businessinto two or more separate entities. Following this event, the parent company ceases to exist as a distinct corporate entity, and in its stead, two or more independent businesses are formed. For instance, the Ahmedabad Advance Mills was subdivided into two distinct businesses, namely the New Ahmedabad Advance Mills and the Tata Metal Strips. Both of these businesses continue to operate independently. The economic substance of spinoffs and split-ups is identical, despite the fact that their forms of organisation are distinct. 17.15.1 Division of a Business Empire A demerger is an efficient method for splitting a large company empire and finding a solution to the problem of succession. Demerger of Reliance Industries Ltd (RIL) is the biggest such example in Indian corporate history. After the death of Dirubhai Ambani, many divisions of Reliance Industries Ltd (RIL) were spun-off in the form of new companies. It was not a split-up, but a spin-off, as RIL continued to exist. Anil Ambani received all the companies which were spun- off, while his brother Mukesh Ambani retained control of RIL. The non-promoter shareholders of RIL got allotment of shares in all the spun-off companies in proportion to their holding in RIL The potential for value creation is one of the primary motivating factors for proponents of demergers, despite the fact that demergers are a practical approach to work out settlements. 17.15.2 Tax Aspects of a Demerger In the context of companies, the term “demerger” refers to the transfer of one or more of a demerged company’s undertakings to the company that is created as a result of the scheme of arrangement implemented by the demerged company. In order for the demerger to be eligible for tax incentives, it is necessary for it to satisfy the following conditions: a) The firm that is formed out of the demerger should be given ownership of all of the assets that are being transferred from the company that is being demerged. b) It is necessary for the business that is formed after the merger to take on all of the obligations associated with the undertaking that is being transferred from the firm that was demerged. c) Both the assets and the obligations of the business that are being taken over by the newly formed company are being taken over at the values that were recorded in the demerged firm’s books of account immediately prior to the demerger. d) As a form of payment in exchange for the demerger, the newly formed business will distribute shares on a proportional basis to the shareholders of the firm that will be demerged. e) Those shareholders who owned at least three-quarters of the value of the demerged company’sshares are automatically transferred over to the newly formed business as shareholders.

<!-- Page Start: text/part0420.html -->
406 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT LET US SUM UP Mergers & Acquisitions (M&A) refer to the combination of two companies. M&A have different legal meanings while being used interchangeably. Mergers combine two similar-sized enterprises into one. M&A Transaction Types can be Horizontal or Vertical merger, Conglomerates, Congeneric Merger, Reverse Merger, Acquisition, Takeover, Leveraged Buyout, Divestiture, Demerger, Equity Carveout and Disinvestment. M&A can be caused by synergies which reduce costs or increase revenues. Cost synergies are achieved by economies of scale, and revenue synergies by cross-selling, gaining market share, or raising prices. Inorganic growth through mergers and acquisitions (M&A) is frequently faster than organic growth. A company can acquire or merge with a company with the latest skills without creating them internally. Horizontal mergersincrease marketshare and price-setting power. Vertical mergersincrease market power because the corporation has better control over its supply chain, avoiding external supply disruptions. One company’s tax benefits are considered when another has carried forward losses. Acquisition of a company with tax losses reduces the acquirer’s tax liability. Stock and cash are the normal payment modes. Mixed offerings are also used in many M&Atransactions. In an IPO, the acquirer paysthe target’s stockholders with new shares. Due to stock price variations, the exchange ratio determines the amount of shares to be exchanged. The acquirer pays cash for the target’s shares in a cash offer. Valuation is a crucial aspect of mergers and acquisitions and a primary topic of conversation between the acquirer and the target. KEYWORDS Merger, Horizontal & Vertical Merger, Conglomerates, Congeneric Merger, Reverse Merger,Acquisition, Takeover, Leveraged Buyout, Divestiture, Demerger, Equity Carveout, Disinvestment, Synergy,Anergy, Diversification, Economies of Scale, Exchange Ratio, Minority Interest, Creeping Acquisition, AntiTakeover Defence, Spin-off, Split-up, Amalgamation CHECK YOUR PROGRESS 1. Which of the following statements is correct? (a) In a reverse merger a smaller company acquires a still smaller company (b) In a reverse merger a smaller company acquires Larger company (c) In a reverse merger a larger company acquires a smaller company (d) In a reverse merger an NBFC acquires a Non-Financial company 2. A merger is said to be synergistic if the combined value of the companies is greater than (a) the aggregate value of the individual companies before the merger (b) values of the individual companies following the merger (c) the worth of the company as a whole (d) none of the above 3. Which of the following entails dividing a large corporation, such as a conglomerate, which is comprised of various divisions, into multiple independent businesses? (a) Stock split (b) Amalgamation
