Principles for Responsible Investment (PRI), Equator Principles (EP), United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP), and Statement of Commitment by Financial Institutions on Sustainable Development are some examples of major flagship programmesthatsuggest waysforsignatoriesto implement green finance.Anumber of organisations based in India have put their names on these programmes. Nevertheless, it is possible to ensure a steady flow of finance into sustainable projects only if there is any reliable source of information on the entities’ overall management of environmental and social risks, track record on the entities’identification of opportunities that bring both a decent rate of return and environmental benefits, and so on. (UNEP). In this context, Sustainable Stock Exchange is an initiative that recommends to the signatory countries’stock exchanges that they develop stock price indices that track the stock performance of a set of companies operating in these countries. These companies are leaders in recognising the Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) principlesinto their financing aspects. These indices would track the stock performance of a set of companiesthat would be included in the Sustainable Stock Exchange. These indices are designed to serve as a guide for investors who have an interest in investing in environmentally friendly endeavours. Both the Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE) and the National Stock Exchange (NSE), two of the most important stock exchangesin India, are participating in this project and publishing their own individual ESG indices.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0590.html -->
576 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT The regulatory infrastructure that exists around the world can be broken down into four basic categories. The first type of disclosure is known as sustainability disclosure, and it applies to both financial and non-financial enterprises. Under this type of disclosure, businesses are required to report on a periodic basis their exposure to ESG-related risks that are caused by their operations. Following the push that the G20 gave in the form of promoting the voluntary adoption by corporations of the proposals made by the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures, such disclosures have begun to gain momentum (TCFD). Sixty percent of the world’s 100 largest public corporations have committed to supporting the TCFD recommendations as of June 2020. The leading stock exchanges, financial market regulators, and ministries dealing with corporate affairsin China (2008), Hong Kong (2012), the United Kingdom (2012), India (2012), the Philippines (2013), Vietnam (2013), and Singapore (2016) were among the nations that implemented a framework for the disclosure of environmental, social, and governance-related risks by listed companiesin their respective jurisdictions(Volz, 2018). In 2008, Bangladesh made it mandatory for commercial banks to report their Corporate Social Responsibilities (CSR) on a biannual basis. In 2011, the country encouraged banksto conductsystematic environmental risk assessments as part of their credit appraisals. In 2013, the country extended this norm to non-banking financial institutions (NBFIs), and in 2017, it included assessments of social risks (Volz, 2018). As early as 2013, Bangladesh made available to the banking sector a standardised reporting format for potential risks. In 2015, France implemented a law called the Energy Transition for Green Growth Law, which made it mandatory for asset owners and managers to report on how physical and transition risks affect their activities and assets. The purpose of this strategy is to establish a connection between disclosures and broader efforts being made in France to decarbonize the energy sector. The second type of financing is known as directed and concessional lending, and it is utilised in a number of different nations. For instance, as part of the green re-financing strategy in Bangladesh, in 2009 a “revolving fund” with a total of US$26 million was established so that low-interest loans could be distributed to over 50 different green and renewable energy businesses. In addition, in 2016, an extra fund in the amount of US$200 million was established for the leather and textile industries in order to facilitate the transition to environmentally friendly technologies. Since 2015, Bangladesh has required its commercial banks to devote at least 5% of their credit to the field of renewable energy and other environmentally friendly technology (Volz, 2018). The third category isthe micro- and macro-prudential rulesthat are placed on varioustypes of institutions, including financial and non-financial ones. In 2006, China started applying credit restrictionsto businesses based on how well they were complying with environmental regulations. Since 2010, Lebanon’s commercial banks have been subject to a policy known as differential reserve requirement. Under this policy, banksthat have a greater proportion of loansfor environmentally friendly projectsin their portfolio are required to keep a smaller amount of reserves (Dikau and Volz, 2018). In 2011, Brazil integrated environmental factors into the Internal Process of Capital Adequacy Assessment of the country’s banks by taking into account the possibility of lending money to projects that posed environmental and social dangers (Dikau and Volz, 2018). In the end, Brazil suggested that banks include an explanation of the risk assessment procedures they use as well as their exposure to social and environmental damages in their annual reports for the year 2017. The formation of environmentally responsible financial institutionsisthe fourth step.The United Kingdom Green Investment Bank plc was created in 2012 as a government-owned financial company with a capitalization of 3 billion British pounds (3.9 billion US dollars) (Geddes et al., 2018). Since 2013, the


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0591.html -->
GREEN AND SUSTAINABLE FINANCING | 577 countries of the Nordic Region have established coalitions of local and regional governments. These coalitions have issued bonds on the financial market and distributed the proceeds to their sub-national members(such as Kommuninvest in Sweden, Kommunalbanken and KLPKommunekredittAS in Norway, KommuneKredit in Denmark, and MuniFin in Finland) (Nassiry, 2018). Asian Development Bank and United States Agency for International Development provide partial credit and bond guarantees to the partner institutions and countries in their respective jurisdiction for green finance. In accordance with these practices that are common internationally, India has, over the course of the past decade, put into place a number of policy instruments, which are outlined in the sections that follow. 24.5 PUBLIC POLICY IN INDIA Since 2007, India has placed a significant emphasis on green financial practices. Within the context of sustainable development, the Reserve Bank of India issued a notification in December 2007 titled “Corporate Social Responsibility, Sustainable Development and Nonfinancial Reporting – Role of Banks.” Within this notification, the Reserve Bank highlightsthe significance of issues pertaining to global warming and climate change. The National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC) was developed in 2008 with the goal of outlining the general policy framework for reducing the effects of climate change. This plan was formulated with a vision. Within the Ministry of Finance in India, the Climate Change Finance Unit, often known as the CCFU, was established in 2011 to serve as a coordinating agency for the many institutions in India that are responsible for green finance. Since 2012, the most significant strategic step has been the introduction of the criteria for sustainability disclosure. Since 2012, the Security and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) has made it required for the top 100 listed businesses based on market capitalization at BSE and NSE to publish annual business responsibility reports. SEBI has also updated this requirement on occasion. In May of 2017, SEBI published guidelines for the issuance of green bonds, in which the disclosure criteria were specified. In addition, the Companies Act of 2013 requires businesses to report annually on their progress toward fulfilling their Corporate Social Responsibilities (CSR). This requirement was enforced by the Ministry of Corporate Affairs. 9 The Report of the Committee on Corporate Governance, which was released in October 2017, suggested that the board of directors should gather together at least once a year to particularly discuss succession planning, ESG, risk management, and strategy. In recent years, India has implemented a number of different economic and financial incentives. These incentives are in line with the commitmentsthat India made under the ParisAgreement in 2015 to reduce the intensity of its greenhouse gas emissions by 33 to 35 percent below the levels that they were at in 2005 and to achieve 40 percent of itsinstalled electric power capacity from non-fossilsources by 203010. In the majority of states, the Government of India (GoI) provides a subsidy that covers thirty percent of the cost of installing solar panels on rooftops. This subsidy is available to institutional, residential, and social sectors. The subsidy can cover up to seventy percent of the total cost of installation in some of the states that fall under the special category. In addition, recipients have the opportunity to take advantage of a generation-based incentive, which entitles them to receive Rs. 2 per unit of generation if their annual generation is greater than or equal to 1100kWh1500kWh. In addition, the surplus power can be sold to customers at a price determined by the government. In addition, the Government of India launched two phases of the Faster Adoption and Manufacturing of Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (FAME) scheme in 2015 and 2019, respectively, with the goals of improving the flow of credit, lowering the initial purchase price of all vehicles, and developing the necessary infrastructures (such as charging stations)


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0592.html -->
578 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT to encourage the production and sale of environmentally friendly vehicles. In order to combat the high initial cost of such vehicles, the State Bank of India has introduced a ‘green car loans’ programme for electric vehicles. In comparison to the existing auto loans, this scheme features an interest rate that is 20 basis points cheaper and a longer repayment window. In addition to this, the government has initiated a Production Linked Incentive Scheme (PLI) to encourage the production of high-efficiency modules within the realm of renewable energy. Additionally, the Reserve Bank has been proactively promoting and supporting green financing initiatives by means of policy measures that it has taken. In 2015, it expanded its Priority Segment Lending (PSL) programme to cover the microgeneration sector of the renewable energy industry. Under the terms of this programme, businesses operating in the renewable energy sector are qualified to receive loans of up to 30 crore (increased from 15Crore since September 4, 2020), and individual households are qualified to receive loans of up to 10 lakh for the purpose of investing in renewable energy. India made the announcement in September 2019 that it intendsto reach 450 GWof renewable energy generation by the year 2030.Through its regular publications and many other communications, the Reserve Bank is raising awareness among the general public, investors, and financial institutions about the necessity of green financing as well as the potential and difficulties associated with it. For instance, the Reserve Bank mentioned the findings of the G20 Green Finance Study Group (GFSG14) in itsAnnual Report (2015-16), which discussed the need for the development of local green bond markets, the facilitation of cross-border investments in green bonds, the sharing of knowledge on environmental risks, and the improvement of overall green finance activities. The annual report goes on to discussthe more general concerns of green finance that will require further examination in the future. Among these are the definition of “green activities,” various aspects of intellectual property rights in the process of developing and transferring technology from industrialised countries, and the assessment of environmental risk by financial institutions. The Reserve Bank of India highlighted the risk that climate change poses to financial assets in its Report on Trend and Progress of Banking in India (2018-19), as well as the necessity of accelerating the growth of green finance in order to achieve environmentally friendly sustainable development. It recognises the difficulties associated with the growth of green finance, such as “greenwashing,” which refers to exaggerated claims of environmental compliance; a variety of definitions; and maturity mismatches between long-term green investments and short-term investor interests. It goes on to underline the necessity of policy action to develop a framework that promotes the green finance ecosystem in India by creating awareness through coordinated efforts. This is another point that is brought up in the report. In the context of green financial institutions, the Indian Renewable Energy DevelopmentAgency (IREDA), an agency sponsored by the government that promotesinvestmentsin clean energy, revealed plansin May 2016 to become India’s first Green bank. For the purpose of funding sustainable infrastructure projects with bond tenors of more than five years, India Infrastructure Finance Corporation Limited (IIFCL) has also introduced a specific programme that is known as the “credit enhancement plan.” In light of these developments, in the next paragraph, we will evaluate the state of green finance in India, including the level of general awareness on the importance of environmental sustainability. 24.6 PROGRESS OF GREEN FINANCE IN INDIA a. Improvement in General Awareness There is a dearth of information available from traditional sources that may be used to evaluate the level of awareness regarding green finance and sustainable development. When viewed in this


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0593.html -->
GREEN AND SUSTAINABLE FINANCING | 579 light, Google Trends has the potential to be a very useful instrument for gaining a knowledge of the pattern of Google searches conducted in various places and at various times. It is possible for us to have a better understanding of the interest in a particularsubject by analysing the amount ofsearches conducted on Google. In Google Trends, the information on the number of searches made in Google on any topic is normalised as the proportion of the total number of searches made in an area during the given time period on all topics. These proportions are based on the total number ofsearches made in Google. The bias that was introduced as a result of shifts in the general search activity on Google over time has been eliminated thanks to this normalisation process. For instance, the total volume of online searches is significantly higher now than it was in 2004 as a direct result of the widespread increase in the number of people who have access to the internet all over the world. As a result, the absolute number of searches in any given subject could not provide an accurate comparison of the changing level of general interest in thatsubject over time. Evidence based on Google Trendsreveals that more people are becoming aware of green finance and the role it plays in the development of sustainable economic practices. b. Green Lending As part of the green finance initiative, the Reserve Bank has included the small renewable energy sector under its Priority Sector Lending (PSL) scheme in 2015. As at end-march 2020, the aggregate outstanding bank credit to the non-conventional energy sector was around Rs. 36,543 crore, constituting 7.9 per cent of the outstanding bank credit to the power generation compared to 5.4 per cent in March 2015. The commercial banks’ exposure to the non-conventional energy sector varied among bank groups and the major states in India. c. Green Bonds Green bonds are the bonds issued by any sovereign entity, inter-governmental groups or alliances and corporates with the aim that the proceeds of the bonds are utilised for projects classified as environmentally sustainable. For India, we first extracted most bonds issued by the corporate and government since January 21, 2015, irrespective of whether they are green bonds or not. In this regard, we have taken those bonds where the country of risk has been marked as India, irrespective of the issuers’ country of incorporation. Our data includesthe initial issuance amount in US$, coupon rate, debt to total assets ratio, and we then looked at whether the bond proceed was to be utilised for green projects or not, for over 5000 bonds issued in Indian market since 2015. India started issuing green bonds since 2015. As of February 12, 2020, the outstanding amount of green bonds in India was US$16.3 billion India issued green bonds of about US$8 billion since January 1, 2018, which constituted about 0.7 per cent of all the bondsissued in the Indian financial market.Although the value of green bonds issued in India since 2018 constituted a very small portion of the total bond issuance, India maintained a favourable position compared to several advanced and emerging economies. 24.6.1 SEBI Guidelines on issue of Green Bonds in India In 2016, SEBI wasresponsible for establishing the green bond criteria in India. During the time that SEBI was working to finalise the guidelines, it solicited feedback on the consultation paper in order to have a conversation about the challenges associated with defining what makes a bond “green.” The Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) on 30th May, 2017 came out with a circularstating the disclosure requirements for issuance and listing of Green Debt Securities in India. Earlier in December, 2015, SEBI had come out with a Concept Paper for issuance of Green Bonds in India. The Concept


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0594.html -->
580 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT Paper brought out the need for enhanced disclosures for issuance of green bonds so as to differentiate it from other form of debt securities issued and listed in India and the Circular is largely in line with the concept paper. Definition of “Green” The SEBI Circular defines the term “Green” or “Green Debt Securities” in the following manner: A Debt Security shall be considered as “Green” or “Green Debt Securities”, if the funds raised through issuance of the debt securities are to be utilised for project(s) and/or asset(s) falling under any of the following broad categories: 1. Renewable and sustainable energy including wind, solar, bioenergy, other sources of energy which use clean technology etc. 2. Clean transportation including mass/public transportation etc. 3. Sustainable water management including clean and/or drinking water, water recycling etc. 4. Climate change adaptation 5. Energy efficiency including efficient and green buildings etc. 6. Sustainable waste management including recycling, waste to energy, efficient disposal of wastage etc. 7. Sustainable land use including sustainable forestry and agriculture, afforestation etc. 8. Biodiversity conservation 9. Any other category as may be specified by SEBI, from time to time. Internationally, for all issuance of Green Bonds or Climate Bonds, an independent reviewer/ certifier has to be appointed mandatorily who certifies whether the targeted project assets qualify to be eligible assets or not. However, as per the SEBI Circular for any issuance of Green Debt Securities in India, the issuer may use its discretion to appoint an independent reviewer/ certifier and it is not a mandatory requirement. Disclosure requirements In order to issue Green Debt Securities, the provisions of SEBI (Issue and Listing of Debt Securities - ILDS) Regulations, 2008 are to be complied with. Therefore, the disclosure requirements provided therein in ILDS Regulations have to be adhered to. Additionally, the following details also have to be disclosed in the offer document: 1. A statement on environmental objectives of the issue of Green Debt Securities; 2. Brief details of decision-making process that the issuer has followed/would follow for determining the eligibility of project(s) and/or asset(s), for which the proceeds are been raised through issuance of Green Debt Securities. Indicative details to be provided is as under: a. Process followed/ to be followed for determining how the project(s) and/or asset(s) fit within the eligible green projects; b. The criteria, making the project(s) and/or asset(s) eligible for using the Green Debt Securities proceeds; and c. Environmental sustainability objectives of the proposed green investment. 3. Details of the system/procedures to be employed for tracking the deployment of the proceeds of the issue.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0595.html -->
GREEN AND SUSTAINABLE FINANCING | 581 4. Details of the project(s) and/or asset(s) or areas where the issuer, proposes to utilise the proceeds of the issue of Green Debt Securities, including towards refinancing of existing green project(s) and/ or asset(s), if any. 5. If the issuer appoints an independent third-party reviewer/certifier, for reviewing /certifying the processesincluding project evaluation and selection criteria, project categories eligible for financing by Green Debt Securities, etc. then such appointment of such reviewer/ certifier shall have to be disclosure in the offer document. These disclosures requirements are similar to that prescribed under international principles. Apart from the initial disclosures, there are some details which have to be disclosed on a continuing basis in the Annual Report or financial results and these are: 1. Details to be provided in the half-yearly and annual financial statements: a. Utilisation of the proceeds of the issue, as per the tracking done by the issuer using the internal process as disclosed in offer document/disclosure document. The utilisation of the proceeds shall be verified by the report of an external auditor, to verify the internal tracking method and the allocation of funds towards the project(s) and/or asset(s), from the proceeds of Green Debt Securities. b. Details of unutilized proceeds. 2. Details to be provided in the Annual Report: a. List of project(s) and/or asset(s) to which proceeds of the Green Debt Securities have been allocated/invested including a brief description ofsuch project(s) and/or asset(s) and the amounts disbursed. However, where confidentiality agreements limit the amount of detail that can be made available about specific project(s) and/or asset(s), information shall be presented about the areas in which such project(s) and/or asset(s) fall into. b. Qualitative performance indicators and, where feasible, quantitative performance measures of the environmental impact of the project(s) and/or asset(s). If the quantitative benefits/impact cannot be ascertained, then the said fact may be appropriately disclosed along with the reasons for non-ascertainment of the benefits/impact on the environment. c. Methods and the key underlying assumptions used in preparation of the performance indicators and metrics. In addition to the above, if the issuer of the Green Debt Securities or any agent appointed by the issuer follows any globally accepted standards, then such standards also have to be disclosed in the offer document of the securities. Responsibilities of the issuer In addition to the responsibilities entrusted on the issuer of debtsecurities under SEBI (ILDS) Regulations, the following additional responsibilities have been entrusted upon the issuers of Green Debt Securities under the Circular: a. The issuer must maintain a decision-making process which it uses to determine the continuing eligibility of the project(s) and/or asset(s), which would include, without limitation, a statement on the environmental objectives of the Green Debt Securities and a process to determine whether the project(s) and/or asset(s) meet the eligibility requirements.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0596.html -->
582 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT b. The issuer must ensure that all project(s) and/or asset(s) funded by the proceeds of Green Debt Securities, meet the documented objectives of Green Debt Securities. c. The issuershould utilise the proceeds only for the stated purpose(s), as disclosed in the offer document. (Source: SEBI website) With the guidelines released, it was seen as an incredible step forward to provide policy directions to the industry. The following deductions can be made after carefully analysing the recommendations that were supplied by the regulator: 1. The SEBI Guidelines are in accordance with the three most fundamental global principles - The Green Bond Principle, the Social Bond Principle, and the Sustainable Bond Principle all have to do with environmental and social responsibility. For this reason, it is absolutely necessary to build a domestic framework that enhances the rules that are offered by SEBI. 2. The SEBI criteria don’t apply to every project, including “green” ones, and here’s why: It is vital that the specified projects for which the bond is funded be ‘overwhelmingly green’ when investors come to a ‘decision for certainty’ regarding the raising of finance for green bonds. This is because green bonds are a kind of alternative financing. The majority of the time, fundraising efforts for initiatives dealing with forestry, clean water, and the adaptation to climate change are unsuccessful here. 3. Not all kinds of environmentally friendly projects may be directly compared to one another: For example, the impact on emissions per unit of investment that results from the establishment of renewable energy projects is not the same as that which results from the utilisation of low-carbon public transportation, despite the fact that both may result in an influence on the environment. As a result, some weightages along the various categories stated by SEBI will be required in order to provide evidence of their impact to investors who are interested in the topic. This would help guide the investors in the direction of the impact category that they have picked. 4. Some industries are quite complicated: Because some of the businesses covered by the SEBI guidelines are very complicated, possessing a solid foundation of environmental understanding is absolutely necessary in order to model the effect that green bonds would have on the climate in relation to the aforementioned fields of business. 5. It is essential to have a good understanding of climate risk and the life cycle: It is not enough to simply report on a building’s emissions for there to be complete transparency on its green standards; one must also take into consideration urban planning, which includes the kind of materials used and how resilient they are to natural disasters and extreme weather. A simple yes/ no checkbox does not convey any information about the long-term viability of initiatives, nor does it take into consideration the varying degrees of danger that each nation faces. 6. The absence of a local context: Although there are many benefits to having global verification and assessment procedures, it is possible to develop a globalstandard for evaluation, despite the fact that it does not take into account differences across countries. There is concern among some stakeholders that, in light of the fact that India has requirements that are distinct from those of other countries, a certification standard should be adapted to the conditions of the Indian context rather than internationalstandards. In this context it will be useful to benchmark an India-specific green rating framework against internationalstandards (perhaps those provided by Sustainanalystics and CICERO) will help provide the international investors with needed transparency and confidence to invest in Indian green bonds.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0597.html -->
GREEN AND SUSTAINABLE FINANCING | 583 24.6.2 SEBI guidelines on reporting on sustainability by listed companies In recent years, ESG (Environmental, Social and Governance) investing has slowly and steadily made its way to becoming an investment strategy whereby investors put their money in companies that are looking to make the world a better place to live in. ESG investing requires balancing the consideration of environmental, social and governance factors alongside financial factors in the investment decision-making process. Investors are increasingly applying these non-financial factors as part of their analysis process to identify material risks and growth opportunities. ESG metrics were not commonly part of mandatory financial reporting, though of late several countries are increasingly making these disclosures mandatory for companiesin their annual report or by way of a standalone sustainability report. Several ESG conscious companies are also voluntarily opting to make ESG disclosures available to the public. Numerous institutions, such as the Sustainability Accounting Standards Board (SASB), the Global Reporting Initiative (GRI), and the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) are working to form standards and define materiality to facilitate incorporation of these factors into the investment process. ESG reporting in India started in 2009 with the Ministry of CorporateAffairs(MCA) issuing the Voluntary Guidelines on Corporate Social Responsibility. Since then, the reporting landscape has come a long way with the introduction of Business Responsibility Reporting (BRR), Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR), IR, National Guidelines on Responsible Business Conduct (NGRBC). SEBI was one of the early adopters of sustainability reporting for listed entities amongst its global peers. The filing of the BRR containing ESG (Environment, Social and Governance) disclosures was first introduced for listed entities in 2012. Since then, a number of developments have taken place. With the adoption of the Paris Agreement on Climate Change and UN Sustainable Development Goals, adapting to and mitigating climate change impact and transitioning to sustainable economies have emerged as major issues globally. The COVID pandemic had also accelerated the relevance of ESG considerations to investors resulting in increased awareness of investors and a shift towards sustainable investing. The same was reflected in the spurt in new launches of ESG themed mutual funds and growth in assets of such schemes, including in India. As ESG investing becomes more mainstream, a need was felt that disclosure requirements should keep pace with these changes and thisled to introduction of the Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) is a significant step towards this direction. Accordingly, SEBI issued a Circular on 10th May, 2021 containing the format of the Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (“BRSR”). Under BRSR, the top 1,000 listed entities need to provide an overview of the entity’s material ESG risks and opportunities, approach to mitigate or adapt to the risks along with financial implications of the same. BRSR was introduced with the aim of making it mandatory for the top 1000 listed companies to report their sustainability performance in order to maintain transparency with stakeholders. BRSR is a questionnaire-based reporting that is divided into 3 sections as follows: Section A: General Disclosures This section contains details of the listed entity; products/services; operations; employees; holding, subsidiary and associate companies (including joint ventures); CSR; transparency and disclosure compliances.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0598.html -->
584 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT Section B: Management and Process Disclosures This section contains questions related to policy and management processes, governance, leadership and oversight. Section C: Principle-Wise Performance Disclosures Companies are required to report upon KPIs in alignment with the nine principles of the National Guidelines on Responsible Business Conduct (NGRBC). The section classifies KPIs into two subcategories that companies are required to report upon, viz., • Essential Indicators (Mandatory), and • Leadership Indicators (Voluntary). While SEBI introduced the requirement of BRSR vide its May 2021 circular, in order to give time to companies to adapt to the new requirements, SEBI mandated that the reporting would be on a voluntary basis for the financial year 2021-22. However, from the financial year 2022-23 onwards, BRSR is mandatory for the top 1,000 listed entities. The pandemic along with the adoption of the Paris Agreement and other initiatives to combat climate change, have been the major contributors that accelerated the relevance of ESG considerations to investors. ESG related reports provide stakeholders with comparable information of the top companies to help them make informed investment choices. ESG criteria are used by socially conscious investors to screen potential investments. Listed below is an overview of the three criteria used to evaluate companies for ESG investing: Environment: What kind of impact does the company have on the environment? How does it protect/ safeguard the environment? This can include a company’s carbon footprint, toxic chemicals involved in its manufacturing processes, its corporate policies addressing climate change and sustainability efforts that make up its supply chain. Social: How does the company improve its social impact, both within the company and in the broader community? Social factors include everything from LGBTQ+ equality, racial diversity in both the executive suite and staff overall, and inclusion programs and hiring practices. Social criteria examine how a company managesrelationships with employees,suppliers, customers, and the communities where it operates. Governance: How does the company’s board and management drive positive change? Governance includes everything from issues surrounding a company’s leadership, executive pay, diversity in leadership, how well that leadership respondsto and interacts with shareholders’audits, internal controls, and shareholder rights. With ESG investing becoming more mainstream, SEBI’s above disclosure requirements through BRSR have been introduced to keep pace with such investmentstrategies, and growing concerns aboutresponsible corporate governance and climate change. (Source: SEBI website) 24.6.3 Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) submitted by India after Paris Agreement As evidence of the growing recognition of the risks posed by climate change, in the Conference of Parties (COP21) held at Parisin December 2015, nearly 200 governments agreed to strengthen the global response to the threat of climate change by “holding the increase in the global average temperature to well below


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0599.html -->
GREEN AND SUSTAINABLE FINANCING | 585 2°C above pre-industrial levels and to pursue efforts to limit the temperature increase to 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels.” This is commonly referred to as the Paris Agreement. In the lead up to the 2015 abovementioned United Nations Climate Change Conference held in Paris, the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) requested all countries to codify their proposed contributions on the reduction in Green House Gases(GHG) emissions and overall enhancement in climate protection. These submissions would include information about the country’s emission reduction plans, pragmatic timelines required to achieve such a deadline, scope of emission reduction, type of approach to be adopted for the same, a base year from which emission reductions will be measured and a robust adaptation plan. By India’s Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC), the Government pledged to undertake the following activities: • To put forward and further propagate a healthy and sustainable way of living based on traditions and values of conservation and moderation. • To adopt a climate-friendly and a cleaner path than the one followed hitherto by others at corresponding level of economic development. • To reduce the emissions intensity of its GDP by 33 to 35% by 2030 from 2005 level. • To achieve about 40% cumulative electric power installed capacity from non-fossil fuel-based energy resources by 2030, with the help of transfer of technology and low cost international finance, including from Green Climate Fund. • To create an additional carbon sink of 2.5 to 3 billion tonnes of CO2 equivalent through additional forest and tree cover by 2030. • To better adapt to climate change by enhancing investments in development programmes in sectors vulnerable to climate change, particularly agriculture, water resources, Himalayan region, coastal regions, health and disaster management. • To mobilize domestic and new and additional funds from developed countries to implement the above mitigation and adaptation actions in view of the resource required and the resource gap. • To build capacities, create domestic framework and international architecture for quick diffusion of cutting-edge climate technology in India and for joint collaborative R&D forsuch future technologies. By implementing the objectives set forth in the NDC, India aimed to pursue a low-carbon emissions pathway, while simultaneously endeavouring to meet all the developmental challenges that the country faces today. The eight key parameters highlighted in the NDC were: • Sustainable Lifestyles, • Cleaner Economic Development, • Reduce Emission Intensity of GDP • Increase the Share of Non-Fossil Fuel Based Electricity, • Enhance Forests Carbon Sink, • Adapt to Climate Change, • Mobilise Finance, and • Technology Development and Transfer. India’s NDCs were designed to create a blueprint for enhancing the overall climate performance of the country in its developmental perspective.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0600.html -->
586 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT The objectives revolved around India’s policies and programmes on promotion of energy access, sustainable development, clean energy, low-carbon and resilient urban centres,following a waste-to-wealth culture, developing safe, smart and sustainable green transportation network, abatement of pollution and making efforts to enhance its carbon sink through creation of forest and tree cover. It also captured citizens’ and private sector’s contribution to combating climate change. (Source: 24.CDP India Climate Change Report 2017) Comparative Resolutions – COP21 and COP26 Paris Agreement COP21 in 2016 COP26 in 2021 1 Reduce emissions intensity of its GDP by 33- 35% by 2030 from 2005 levels Reduce the carbon intensity of the economy to less than 45% by 2030 2 Increase the share of non-fossil fuel-based electricity to 40% by 2030 Meet 50% of the country’s energy requirements from RE sources by 2030 3 Increase renewable energy generation to 175 GW by 2022 Raise the non-fossil fuel-based energy capacity of the country to 500 GW by 2030 4 — Reduce the total projected carbon emission by one billion tons by 2030 5 — Become carbon neutral and achieve net zero emissions by the year 2070 24.6.4 Task Force set up by the Government In January 2021, a Task Force on Sustainable Finance has been set up by the Department of Economic Affairs, Ministry of Finance, Government of India. The Terms of Reference of the Task Force include: • defining the framework for sustainable finance in India, • establishing the pillars for a sustainable finance roadmap, • suggesting draft taxonomy of sustainable activities, and • suggesting a framework of risk assessment by the financial sector. The task force will also recommend ways to strengthen the resilience of the country’s financial sector against risks emanating from various climate and environmental, social and governance (ESG) issues. It will lay out waysto grow sustainable finance in India and set a clear time frame for their implementation. As part of thisinitiative, a survey is also being undertaken with the help of the United Nations Development Programme (India) to assess climate and ESG resilience in India’s financial sector. According to the UNDP estimate made before the pandemic, India requires an estimated USD 2.6 trillion to realise targets under the sustainable development goals by 2030. Since the government alone cannot generate or deploy such massive resources for this purpose, it requires collective efforts of both the public and private sectors. InAugust 2020, the Department of EconomicAffairs had launched the Sustainable Finance Collaborative in partnership with the UNDP. It included dialogues on variousrelated issues, including barriersto deploying new and innovative modes of financing,such asimpact investing, the role of blended finance instruments, green finance instruments for sustainable development and sustainability-related disclosures by companies.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0601.html -->
GREEN AND SUSTAINABLE FINANCING | 587 24.7 CHALLENGES AND WAY FORWARD Existing literature and global experiences suggest that integrated policy approach towards green finance is gradually gaining momentum. In India, while there have been improvements in public awareness and financing options, the major challenges could be high borrowing costs, false claims of environmental compliance, plurality of green loan definitions, maturity mismatches between long-term green investment and relatively short-term interests of investors. In thissection, we elaborate some of these challenges and discuss a few possible policy measures. a. Borrowing Cost The cost of issuing green bonds has generally remained higher than the other bonds in India. The average coupon rate for green bonds issued since 2015, with maturities between 5 to 10 years, have generally remained higher than the corporate and government bonds with similar tenure. The pattern for the INR denominated green bonds is similar. For the US$ denominated green bonds with tenure of more than or equal to 10 years, the coupon rate was, however lower than the corporate bonds. It may be mentioned that most of the green bonds in India are issued by the public sector units or corporates with better financial health. It is evident from the fact that the private sector issuers of green bonds, on average, reported lower debt-to-assets ratio compared to the non-issuers of green bonds. Their better financial health is also evident from their stock prices. The stock prices of the entities that generally focussed in ESG-related initiatives are the onesthat outperformed othersin the market. Despite being relatively secured, the higher borrowing cost of green bonds in India could be on account of asymmetric information higher risk perception and other governance issues. Existing literature suggests that green projects often have a high up-front cost with some cost-saving features only applicable in the long-term. Maturity-mismatches in green projects and their financing increase the cost of borrowing (G20 GFSG, 2016). Absence of a universal definition of green finance and information asymmetry often result in “green-washing”, wherein the investors end up receiving false signals about the green bonds (Berensmann and Lindenberg, 2016). Several of these issues are also likely to be present in India, where the financial market is still emerging as compared to the developed markets. b. Borrowing Cost and Information Asymmetry High borrowing cost has been perhapsthe most important challenge and our analysisindicatesthat it could be due to the asymmetric information. Therefore, developing a better information management system in India may help in reducing maturity mismatches, borrowing costs and lead to efficient resource allocation in this segment. It may be mentioned that to overcome such information gap, several countries including Australia, China, India and the United States have database related to green building projects in the country. While India does monitor greenhouse gas emissions through variousreporting mechanismsincluding PAT (perform-achieve trade) and RPO (renewable purchase obligations), like many other countries it does not have a national measurement, reporting and verification platform for tracking climate finance. c. Market Infrastructure Development Given the large size of domestic market and much smaller penetration of green instrumentsso far, there remain vast opportunities to be tapped. In this context, some of the studies noted the importance of (a) increased coordination between investment and environmental policies and (b) an implementable policy framework for both national and state levels in addressing the existing frictions. In this vein,


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0602.html -->
588 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT some of the policy measures such as deepening of corporate bond market, standardisation of green investment terminology, consistent corporate reporting, and removing information asymmetry between investors and recipients can make a significant contribution in addressing some of the shortcomings of the green finance market (RBI, 2019). d. Other Public Policies Other approach could be engaging with industry bodiesthat have taken initiativestowards expanding ‘green buildings’that are designed to consume less water and energy resources, maintain better waste management and provide healthier spaces for living. The Government at all levels can possibly engage with these bodies to better assess their financial and operational needs. It may also undertake policies that make production and distribution of nonconventional energy profitable, especially for smaller firms. There is a scope of well calibrated policies for providing fiscal incentives for green projects in India, while taking cognisance of their impact on supply-chain, inflationary pressure and fiscal consolidation. (Source: Article prepared by Saurabh Ghosh, Siddhartha Nath and Abhishek Ranjan of the Strategic Research Unit of Department of Economic and Policy Research) 24.8 GROWTH OF REGULATORY FRAMEWORK After several months focusing on the impacts of COVID-19, regulators are returning to climate change risks and sustainable finance. International bodies are issuing analyses, EU ESG (environment, social, governance) rules are increasing, and supervisors are setting out their expectations. The pandemic has highlighted that businesssectors are deeply interconnected across borders, thatsocieties of all types and wealth levels are vulnerable, and that the environment is under increasing strain. Labour inequality and human rights are to the fore. Around the globe, investor and customer demand remains a key driver of change, but the regulatory initiative that started in the EU is spreading. 24.8.1 Impact of Climate Change Risk on Equity Markets The IMF Global Financial Stability Report of April 2020 said “Disasters as a result of climate change are projected to be more frequent and more severe, which could threaten financial stability.” The report finds the impact of large physical disasters on equity markets generally to have been modest over the past 50 years. It notes that high levels of insurance penetration and sovereign financial strength can help preserve financial stability in the face of climatic disasters. However, aggregate equity valuations as of 2019 did not reflect the predicted changes in physical risk under various climate change scenarios, which suggests that investors do not pay enough attention to these risks. The report arguesthat better disclosures and stresstesting for financial firms can help preserve financial stability and should complement policy measures to mitigate and adapt to climate change. 24.8.2 NGFS Recommendations to Supervisors a) Determine how climate-related and environmental riskstransmit to the economy and financialsectors in the jurisdiction and identify how they are likely to be material for supervised entities. b) Develop a clearstrategy, establish an internal organisation and allocate adequate resourcesto address these risks. c) Identify the exposures of supervised entities that are vulnerable to these risks and assess potential losses should they materialise.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0603.html -->
GREEN AND SUSTAINABLE FINANCING | 589 d) Set supervisory expectations to create transparency for financial institutions in relation to the supervisors’ understanding of a prudent approach to these risks. e) Ensure adequate management of these risks by financial institutions and take mitigating action where appropriate. 24.8.3 Prudential Risks for Banks InApril 2020, the Basel Committee published a stocktake report prepared by its high-level Task Force on Climate-related Financial Risks. The report noted that most BCBS members are undertaking regulatory and supervisory initiatives on climate-related financial risks, and that future work includes analytical reports and developing effective supervisory practices. In May 2020, the Central Banks and Supervisors Network for Greening the financial System (NGFS) published a guide for supervisors on integrating climate change into prudential supervision. It provides a snapshot of the state-of-play in several countries and sets out five non-binding recommendations for supervisors, intended to co-ordinate a common regulatory response to climate-related and environmental risks. Significant institutions are expected to review and, where needed, adapt their practices. As part of the supervisory dialogue, from end-2020 significant institutions are asked to inform the ECB of any divergences of their practices from the supervisory expectations described in the guide. The ECB acknowledges that the management and disclosure of climate-related and environmental risks, and the methodologies and tools used to address them, are currently evolving and are expected to mature over time. The UK regulators are also requiring firmsto reflect ESG factorsin their risk frameworks and disclosures, but are not proposing new rules and are leaving detailed guidance to the industry. 24.9 NATIONAL EFFORTS TOWARDS GREEN AND SUSTAINABLE FINANCING India has started along the path of becoming carbon neutral and has proposed a “Green Deal” with the goal of accomplishing this by the year 2070. Green financing has been designated by the Green Deal as a key facilitator for accelerating the decarbonization process. It places a strong focus on the necessity of an increased flow of funds from both the national government and commercial businesses in order to construct environmentally friendly infrastructure. It has developed four primary areas of focusin order to assist in accelerating the development of environmentally friendly finance in India. To begin, a transparent and well-defined taxonomy pavesthe way for the creation of environmentally friendly projects and helps to keep transaction coststo a minimum. Second, the establishment of a system for the valuation of carbon in India. The introduction of a price on carbon will make it more likely that the costs of climate change adaptation and mitigation strategies will be factored into mainstream investment decisions. Thirdly, the use of national investments by including so-called “Green Infrastructure Investment Trusts” (InvITs), which include markets for green bonds and instruments for green finance. Last but not least, expanding into international markets while minimising the costs of prevarication, establishing standardsfor external borrowing, and removing any other legislative obstaclesthatstand in the way of green financing in India. The Indian economy’s banking industry, in particular, has been at the forefront of the race to produce green financing. The Reserve Bank of India (also known as “RBI”) published a notification highlighting the significance of financial institutions and the imperative nature of putting forth effortsto achieve sustainable


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0604.html -->
590 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT development in the year 2007.Against this context, Equator Principles were mentioned. These principles offer a framework for recognising, managing, and evaluating risks related to environment and society while monetizing projects. In 2016, the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) had the opportunity to produce a study on the lines of sustainable financial systems in partnership with the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) and India. In this paper, numerous aspects of India’s financial systems, as well as the country’s part in fostering the growth of green finance, are investigated. The Companies Act of 2013 includes a provision that requireslarge capital companiesto donate 2% of their annual profitsto Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) initiatives. These initiatives cover topicssuch as environmentalsustainability, ecological protection, healthcare rural development, and educational opportunities. The government of India has established a number of programmes and funds in an effort to encourage businesses and organisations to adopt more environmentally friendly practises. Through a programme called “Perform Achieve and Trade,” the government of the country hasincorporated carbon trading into its overall policy framework. In its objective to make the transition to green energy, the government has also placed a strong emphasis on the growth of specialised industries, in particular the renewable energy sector. Additionally, the RBI has authorised lending for renewable projects by corporations up to 15 crores of Indian rupees, while the ceiling for private people is up to Rs. 10 lakhs. In 2021, the Reserve Bank of India became a member of the Network for Greening the Financial System, also known as NGFS. NGFS is a collection of nationalised banks that encourage the transition to a green economy by engaging in practises that advance the reduction of environment and climate-related risks in the financial sector. Repeatedly, the Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) has issued a number of different sustainable reporting requirements in order to encourage businesses to operate in accordance with sustainable practises. The “Annual Business Responsibility Reporting” wasthe very first reporting structure ever created, and it was mostly based on the national voluntary criteria. The establishment of a method for the management of the environment by commercial enterprises that is capable of discouraging the causing of damage to the environment while carrying out official operations was one of the essential components of the principle. Another framework for reporting exists, and it is called the “Business Responsibility Report.” Its purpose isto encourage organisationsto run their businessin an ecologically responsible manner. The framework offers a disclosure model that companies may use to evaluate their overall performance in the reporting categories. These evaluations can be carried out with the assistance of the framework. The entity is responsible for adhering to all eight of the requirements that make up the principle titled “environment.” These include the percentage of recyclable materials, the total amount of energy consumed, the use of energy saving methods and the amount of energy saved, the amount of water consumed and the amount of water saved and recycled, the emission and reduction of greenhouse gases, the treatment of water before it is discharged, and the rebuilding of biodiversity. The Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) made a bold move in 2021 when it introduced the “Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report” (BRSR) in an effort to improve the level of disclosure in the “Environment, Social, and Governance” (ESG) requirements. The BRSR is applicable to the top one thousand firms and will become required beginning in the fiscal year 2022-2023. The data that is presented is qualitative as well as quantitative, and the BRSR framework makes use of worldwide ESG standards like those developed by the Global Reporting Initiative (GRI). It is estimated that ESG statistics are included in the NIFTY 50, of which 40% of private enterprises have a large favourable record. The World Economic Forum estimates that by 2023 the market for green bonds might be worth more than two trillion dollars. The earnings from the sale of green bonds, which are issued in the same manner as traditional bonds by both public and private entities, are put toward the funding of environmentally


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0605.html -->
GREEN AND SUSTAINABLE FINANCING | 591 responsible and net-zero energy generating projects. In 2015, India started investing in green bonds with the intention of meeting the capital demand for green development through these investments. The Securities Exchange Board of India, also known as SEBI, is the major body responsible for regulating the issue of green bonds as well as the conditions for listing them. Even while the share of green bonds in the Indian financial market is only 0.7% from 2018-2020, this percentage is more than it is in affluent countrieslike the United States, the United Kingdom,Australia, etc. The issuance of green bondsin India has reached over six billion dollars in the first half of 2021, indicating that the country’s green bond market isseeing exponential expansion. India isinvestigating the possibility of obtaining money through offshore green bonds, which may assist in bridging the gap necessary to attain net zero. According to projections made by a highly regarded think group based in India, in order for the nation to reach its goal of becoming carbon neutral by the year 2070, it will require an investment of 10.103 trillion dollars. The goal is planned to be accomplished by the implementation of two strategies: switching from coal to renewable energy sources and investing in technology that is powered by green hydrogen. Green bonds with maturities of more than 10 years have been issued by leading renewable energy businesses in India such as Adani and Power Finance Co. The World Bank has also made multiple investments in Indian green bonds at a variety of different times. There are a number of different approaches that may be used in order to improve the Indian Green Bonds market segment. In the Indian Budget for 2022, several measures as follows have been announced by the Finance Minister: a) The scope of the Parivesh portal for Green Clearances, which waslaunched in 2018, will be expanded, to provide information to the applicants. b) Green Energy, and Clean Mobility Systems which have immense potential to assist sustainable development at scale and modernize the country are included among Sunrise Opportunities. Supportive policies, light-touch regulations, facilitative actions to build domestic capacities, and promotion of research & development will guide the government’s approach. For R&D in these sunrise opportunities, in addition to efforts of collaboration among academia, industry and public institutions, government contribution will be provided. c) Energy Transition and Climate Action have been emphasised along with Solar Power. d) As a part of the government’s overall market borrowings in 2022-23, sovereign Green Bonds will be issued for mobilizing resources for green infrastructure. The proceeds will be deployed in public sector projects which help in reducing the carbon intensity of the economy. To summarise, on the basis of research conducted by various agencies, we may conclude that the following actions are the ones that are most likely to hasten the growth of the market. Each of these prerequisites calls for additional in-depth analysis as well as the execution of pilot projects in order to establish the frameworks, procedures, and deployment strategies that are the most effective. a) There is an undeniable requirement for support from the government in order to propel the sector to reach itsfull potential. The government, acting through a variety of its agencies and departments, has already begun to stimulate the market by entering it and issuing green bonds. However, there isroom for direct interventionsto increase the disclosure and transparency aspects with a level of consistency in order to further grow the market. This might be done in order to further grow the segment. Other potential interventions of this type include the establishment of a guarantee fund, which would be put to use for credit enhancement in a variety of ways in order to make priority green bonds more appealing; the use of certain concessions,such astax-exemptions, could also be leveraged in order to


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0606.html -->
592 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT increase domestic demand for green bonds; and the possibility of the government issuing a sovereign green bond, which would send a clearsignal of the government’ssupport and interest in green bonds. b) There is potential for the involvement of national development banks,such as SIDBI and NABARD, in the creation of projects that include components of aggregation and credit improvement, with the goal of channelling money toward areas that have not yet been exploited. c) The new international exchange established in India might begin operations with a concentration on green bonds in order to entice a greater number of international investors who are interested in assisting climate change initiatives in emerging economies. In order to accomplish this goal, we will need assistance in the form of national rules and a transparent and solid framework for assessing green bonds. d) Develop business models that would give price benefits to issuers of green bonds by combining financial support from the Green Climate Fund with other sources of funding (GCF). e) The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) might think about broadening its priority sector lending criteria to include some of the industries that are already covered by SEBI’s standards in order to make such industries more profitable for bond issuers. f) Policymakers may want to think about using existing domestic fundsforsecuritization, which might include funds like the National Disaster Relief Fund (NDRF), in order to issue catastrophe bonds for operations based on disaster resilience and adaptation at the national level. 24.10 RBI VIEWS ON CLIMATE RISK AND SUSTAINABLE FINANCE In order to learn from and contribute to the global effort towards enhancing the role of the financial system to manage risks, and in the broader context of environmentally sustainable development, the RBI joined the Central Banks and Supervisors Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) as a member in April 2021. Further, the RBI is also represented in the G-20 Sustainable Finance Working Group, Financial Stability Board’s Working Group on Climate Risk and Work Stream on Climate-related Disclosures, Task Force on Climate-related Financial Risks set up by the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS) and the International Platform on Sustainable Finance. In May 2021, the RBI set up a Sustainable Finance Group (SFG) within its Deptt of Regulations to lead the efforts and regulatory initiatives in the area of climate risk and sustainable finance. The SFG would be instrumental in suggesting strategies and evolving a regulatory framework, including appropriate disclosures, which could be prescribed for banks and other regulated entities(REs) to propagate sustainable practices and mitigate climate-related risks in the Indian context. The Sustainable Finance Group (SFG) in the Department of Regulation (DoR), Reserve Bank of India (RBI), carried out a survey in January 2022 to assess the status of climate risk and sustainable finance in leading by scheduled commercial banks. The survey covered 34 leading scheduled commercial banks comprising 12 public sector banks, 16 private sector banks and 6 leading foreign banks in India. The objective of the survey was to assess the approach of these banks, their level of preparedness, etc., with respect to climate risk and sustainable finance. The responses indicated that although banks have begun taking steps in the area of climate risk and sustainable finance, there remains a need for concerted effort and further action in this regard.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0607.html -->
GREEN AND SUSTAINABLE FINANCING | 593 The Survey’s results were revealing and observations from the results were as follows: Board-level engagement and responsibility: Board-level engagement on climate risk and sustainable finance is inadequate. In about a third of the banks that were surveyed, responsibility for overseeing initiativesrelated to climate risk and sustainability was yet to be assigned. Furthermore, only a few banks have included climate risk/ sustainability/ environmental, social and governance (ESG) related Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) in the performance evaluation of their top management. Strategy: A majority of the banks did not have a separate business unit or vertical for sustainability and ESG-related initiatives. Only a few banks had a strategy for embedding ESG principles in their business, scaling up their sustainable finance portfolio and incorporating climate change risks into their existing risk management framework. Risk management:Almost all the surveyed banksrecognized the urgency of the issue, and most of them considered climate-related financial risks to be a material threat to their business. Physical and transition risks were seen as the main sources of climate-related risks. Some of the banks are not just considering climate and environment-related risks, rather they are also focusing on the social and governance aspects while evaluating credit proposals above a certain amount. A few banks are also attempting to quantify the amount of their loan and investment portfolio that is susceptible to such risks. Transition to low-carbon exposure: Most of the surveyed banks have decided to gradually reduce their exposure to high-carbon emitting/polluting businesses in the coming years. A few banks have either mobilized new capital to scale up green lending and investment or set a target for incremental lending and investment for sustainable finance. Most banks have launched a few loan products to tap the opportunities from climate change. A few banks have also launched green deposits to scale up lending to environment-friendly businesses. Climate-related financial disclosures: A majority of the banks have not aligned their climate-related financial disclosures with any internationally accepted framework. Moving towards a low-carbon environment in banking operations: Most banks have either taken some measures or have plans to decrease the absolute carbon emissions arising from their operations and increase the proportion of renewable energy in their total sourced electricity. A few banks have either announced time-bound plans or plan to come up with a roadmap over the next 12 months to become carbon-neutral. Capacity building and data gaps: Most banks are looking at capacity building to better understand the financial implications of climate risk. Further, most banks felt that the available data was insufficient for an appropriate assessment of climate-related financial risks and the processes and methodologies to measure and monitor climate-related financial risks were also not sufficiently developed. 24.10.1 Important Takeaways and Recommendations of the Survey a) Governance: It is imperative that banks put in place, either at the board level or at the senior management level, a system that allows for the monitoring and expansion of programmes that are related to climate risk and sustainability. They ought to give some thought to incorporating key performance indicators (KPIs) on climate risk, sustainability, and ESG into the process by which they evaluate the senior management’s work. b) Risk Management: In order to make their loan and investment portfolios more resistant to the effects of climate change, financial institutions need to not only have a complete understanding of the physical, transition, and liability risks associated with climate change, but they also need to begin


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0608.html -->
594 | ADVANCED BUSINESS & FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT actively managing these risks. In addition, financial institutions need to devise a plan for mitigating climate risk and incorporate this plan into their overall framework for risk management. c) Climate-related financial disclosures: To increase the comparability and consistency of their climate-related financial disclosures with those of their peers around the world, banks need to align their climate-related financial disclosures with a framework that is internationally acknowledged. d) Possibilities arising from the shift toward a greener future: The expansion of green lending and investment could be facilitated by banks by either the mobilisation of fresh capital or the establishment of a target for the incremental lending and investment needed for sustainable financing. e) Building capability and strengthening human resources (HR): Banks need to make major investments in the capacity building of their employees with regard to climate risk, environmental, social, and governance issues, as well as sustainable finance. In addition, in order for financial institutions to successfully capitalise on the opportunities presented by climate change, sustainable finance, and the expanding emphasis on ESG, they will need to allocate specific resources to this field. f) Taking steps in the direction of a lower carbon footprint in banking operations: It is possible for financial institutions to devise a plan to cut emissions caused by their own operations. In accordance with the pledge that India made at the COP26Summit, financial institutions may also take into consideration the possibility of collaborating on a schedule to get toward net-zero emissions. The Reserve Bank of India, on 27th July 2022, released a Discussion Paper on Climate Risks and Sustainable Financing. The Discussion Paper lists out the following six questions for which it has called for opinion from relevant stake holders: 1. What should be the immediate priorities in shaping the policy discourse on climate risk in India? What actions would help foster a more sustainable and resilient financial system? 2. What should be the way forward for the regulatory policy framework for climate risk in an emerging market like India keeping in view its aspects such as demography, geography, etc.? Is there any plan/ contemplation in the REs to integrate climate and environmental considerations in their core activities of lending and investment? 3. What are the main challenges in integrating the climate risk framework in Governance, Strategy and Risk Management? What is needed to overcome these?Are there plansin place/ being contemplated regarding the same by the REs? 4. What are the potential challenges in developing climate risk stress testing and a scenario analysis framework for REs? How do you think the potential effects arising out of such exercise should be analysed? 5. What could be the overall timeline for implementation of disclosure/ TCFD framework, and which recommendationsshould be prioritized? Should it begin with disclosure around the qualitative aspects first, followed by disclosure around quantitative aspects or both together? 6. What measures would you suggest that the Reserve Bank of India could consider with respect to climate risk and sustainable finance? Note: Risks to Banks emanating from climate change are of the following three types, viz., physical risks, transition risks and liability risks. Physical risks refer to the economic costs and financial losses resulting from the increasing severity and frequency of: • Extreme climate change-related weather events (or extreme weather events) such as heatwaves, landslides, floods, wildfires and storms (i.e., acute physical risks);


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0609.html -->
GREEN AND SUSTAINABLE FINANCING | 595 • Longer-term gradual shifts in climate such as changes in precipitation, extreme weather variability, ocean acidification, and rising sea levels and average temperatures (i.e., chronic physical risks or chronic risks); and • Indirect effects of climate change such as loss of ecosystem services (e.g., desertification, water shortage, degradation of soil quality or marine ecology). Transition risks are the risks related to the process of adjustment towards a low-carbon economy. Liability risks are the risks arising from people or businesses seeking compensation for losses suffered from physical and transition risks. (Source: RBI Report of the Survey on Climate Risk and Sustainable Finance – July 2022) Based on the responsesreceived from the stakeholders, RBI is expected to issue comprehensive guidelines on the issues of Climate Risk and Sustainable Finance. This step is considered to be very important because Sustainable Finance is a critical part of the entire gamut of Climate Change. LET US SUM UP As industrial sectors such as renewable energy, energy efficiency, green building and recycling have grown, investors have been attracted by the ethos of ethical and environmental investments, as well as the promise of healthy financial returns in a booming sector of the economy. ISO 32210, Framework for sustainable finance: Principles and guidance, is the first standard by ISO/TC 322, designed to provide both a foundation and overarching structure in the field of sustainable finance. ISO 14008, Monetary valuation of environmental impacts and related environmental aspects, is the world’s first internationally agreed reference for assessing the economic value of an organization’s environmental aspects and impacts. ISO 14008 is complemented by ISO 14007, Environmental management – Guidelines for determining environmental costs and benefits. ISO 14097, Greenhouse gas management and related activities – Framework including principles and requirements for assessing and reporting investments and financing activities related to climate change, will offer much-needed support to organizations reporting in accordance with the recommendations of the Task Force for Climate-related Financial Disclosures. There are several instruments by which governments, foundations, banks, and private investors can fund ‘green’ projects. They can be categorized into grants, risk-mitigation products, equity, and debt. Debt instruments are largely classified as two types – green loans and green bonds. To enhance the transparency and integrity of the green bonds market globally, there are a set of voluntary process guidelines – the Green Bond Principles (GBP) – issued by the International Capital Market Association. In India, while there have been improvements in public awareness and financing options, the major challenges could be high borrowing costs, false claims of environmental compliance, plurality of green loan definitions, maturity mismatches between long-term green investment and relatively short-term interests of investors. India has commenced its journey for carbon neutrality and put forward a ‘Green Deal’ to be achieved by 2070.


---

<!-- Page Start: text/part0610.html -->

ISO 14001 is (a) An extremely dangerous greenhouse gas (b) A European Union law on the reduction of pollution (c) A water quality regulation established by the Environment Act. (d) An internationalstandard thatspecifies auditing best practisesfor organisations’environmental management systems and policies. 2. “Greenwash” is a company’s attempt to improve its environmental image as a means of promoting itself and its products and gaining customers, without necessarily having any genuine substance to back up its claims. “Greenwashing” is thus: (a) Anecessary economic activity thatshould be supported if conducted positively and responsibly. (b) A prohibited action that is unpleasant. (c) A sort of advertising that is ignorable (d) A practice employed by multinational firms to avoid paying tax 3.
